vii
Summary
The Coffee Guide to Better Health
Introducing Circular Health Coffee for Wellness, 
Sustainability and New Coffee Standard
What if your daily cup of coffee wasn’t just a ritual, but one of the most powerful 
choices you make for your health?
Coffee is one of the most widely consumed beverages in the world, yet it’s rarely 
appreciated for its full health potential. The Coffee Guide to Better Health presents 
a paradigm-shifting approach to coffee consumption, introducing the concept of 
Circular Health Coffee (CHC) that prioritizes health benefits throughout the entire 
production chain from seed to cup. This comprehensive guide bridges the gap between 
emerging scientific research on coffee’s health benefits and practical application for 
producers, roasters, and consumers.
Coffee is not merely a caffeinated beverage but a complex agricultural product 
containing powerful bioactive compounds that can significantly impact human health 
when properly cultivated, processed, and prepared. The book challenges traditional 
quality metrics focused on taste and appearance, proposing instead a health-centered 
evaluation system. This includes:
•
The key bioactive compounds in coffee, like chlorogenic acids, trigonelline,
diterpenes, and even caffeine, and how they contribute to liver support,
cognitive protection, metabolic health, and more.
•
How farming, processing, and roasting methods can enhance or degrade
coffee’s health-promoting properties, and understand the rigorous testing
required to safeguard consumers from potential contaminants.
 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 

The Coffee Guide to Better Health
•
Why organic regenerative agriculture is foundational to producing coffee
with real wellness value.
•
Practical insights for choosing, preparing, and enjoying coffee that aligns
with your health goals.
While food safety remains essential, the emphasis of Circular Health Coffee is on 
maximizing the good, preserving and elevating the compounds that make coffee one 
of the most studied and promising functional beverages available today. This evolving 
category is focused not on perfection, but on continuous improvement through 
transparency, innovation, and better decision-making at every stage.
Featuring helpful photos, charts and graphs, and deeply researched but accessible 
science, The Coffee Guide to Better Health is an essential read for anyone who wants 
their daily brew to be as good for the body as it is for the soul.
Whether you’re a coffee drinker, producer, wellness professional, or curious skeptic, 
this book will change the way you think about what’s in your cup.

xi
Foreword
There probably isn’t a single coffee lover, or coffee professional for that matter, who 
came to coffee because of its health benefits. The notion of coffee as a health food is 
almost nonexistent, in fact. Rhetoric and messaging around coffee’s impact on health 
has been cautionary at best, sometimes verging on alarmist, and downright shaming at 
its worst. We have only ourselves to blame, again, for not doing the work necessary to 
investigate and correct the record on coffee—and for coffee. Caffeine is typically the 
scapegoat of coffee’s story, as if that is the only component of thousands to research. 
Coffee can be so complex in flavor, so craved and coveted, that it often serves as the 
elixir sparking verbal debate, inspiring action, and transforming our lives daily; yet it 
can also be sorely misunderstood. The history of coffee’s influence on the world has 
been written about many times. And if this is to be truly holistic, we must consider 
what we can bring to this story as coffee lovers. This book will serve as the author’s 
substantive contribution to that narrative. Ildi Revi has taken on the challenge here.
I met Ildi when we were both members of the Specialty Coffee Association of America 
(SCAA). Having been in the industry for my entire adult life, I was more than 20 
years in before I met someone so driven as Ildi was by the work we did as volunteers 
for the SCAA. I was thrilled when she accepted the job of Education Manager for 
their education program. As an educator, Revi stands as one of the best the specialty 
coffee industry has ever known. Her thirst for information and relentless curiosity set 
her apart from her contemporaries, so it follows that she would run at this topic. She 
delved deep into the science of coffee and submitted this compelling case for health 
and, by extension, the sustainability of coffee. The data are relatively available to all of 
us, but it took the intellect and wisdom of Revi to compile it. Her drive is borne out 
of a desire to serve more than just the coffee industry. This book is offered in service 
to coffee itself. 
 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 

In late 2002, I wrote down my thoughts on the specialty coffee landscape, proposing 
that there are three waves in coffee consumption and that we are currently experiencing 
the “third wave.” These waves define our goals, behavior, and the industry’s engagement 
with consumers. While I wasn’t sure that the concept would be interesting to anyone 
besides myself, I eventually saw some third wave concepts play out easily, and the 
community began using the term to define quality and an elevated presentation of 
coffee. Other ideas attached to the third wave did not catch on. Originally, I was sure 
that the industry was ready to level the playing field for all actors along the value 
stream. For example, I assumed we would increasingly embrace Certified Organic 
and Biodynamic production. Back then, I was encouraged by the attention coffee 
growers were starting to get, and I predicted the trend would continue toward better 
prices in accordance with the level of quality coming from specialty producers. While 
we saw some good examples of that, the assumptions I made around our collective 
commitment to sustainability didn’t materialize. Our industry, and to some extent, 
our consumers, have become more individualistic than ever. We seek out exclusive 
and somewhat selfish experiences in coffee, and as a result, the common good has 
become the least of our concerns.
When people ask me what I think will happen after the third wave, I’m sure I will 
disappoint some with my answer. They might expect me to predict even rarer coffees 
with more esoteric flavor profiles, or fancier exclusive offerings at every local café. I 
am sure they expect me to be excited about higher prices for competition lots that 
few can drink and even fewer can afford. No, that’s not my dream of post-third-wave 
coffee. Instead, the potential I see is in the group effort to raise all boats. 
The hope is for what we can achieve together—to value coffee holistically. I can 
imagine an educated consumer base who knows where their coffee comes from, knows 
xiv ♦ Ildi Revi
xii

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 
the care with which it’s grown and processed, understands the cost it takes to get it to 
them, and the fragile complexity of the entire coffee value stream. In a holistic circular 
coffee world, we realize the benefits of coffee to our own health, and producers’ health 
and well-being. We enjoy prosperous yields, commerce, trade, and a thriving and 
healthy community from farmers all the way through to café guests. 
This book is one crucial building block to a more harmonious coffee world. Whether 
you read it cover-to-cover or keep it on the shelf for reference, hold this holistic goal 
in your mind’s eye. Let’s start with this comprehensive guidebook to wellness. Coffee 
has taken such good care of us and it’s time to return the favor.
Cheers, 
Trish Rothgeb
December 2024
xiii


1
Introduction
Imagine a world where your morning coffee does more than wake you up. It supports 
your health and the health of the environment.
With each sip, you’re taking in a mixture of antioxidants—compounds that support 
your immune system, calm inflammation, aid your liver, and help you feel better from 
the inside out. The coffee company has done its due diligence, obtaining a certificate 
of analysis (COA) from a third-party lab to validate its claims, giving you peace of 
mind that the coffee meets its quality and safety goals.
You also feel at ease knowing your coffee is organic and produced in ways that 
regenerate soil and support ecosystems. The farmers’ focus on health outcomes ensures 
that your coffee is as beneficial as possible for everyone involved. Your daily ritual 
becomes more than a habit. It becomes a catalyst for change.
Could these factors inspire you to switch to a different kind of coffee? That simple 
shift might support your health in real, measurable ways. For many people, coffee 
is the most consistent source of antioxidants in their daily diet. What if it could be 
even more?
If you are curious (perhaps skeptical) about coffee’s health benefits, please read on as 
we explore coffee through a new lens. While we may confirm some of what you know, 
this book is a journey into a new category of coffee, empowering you with knowledge 
that can transform your coffee experience.
This book is for coffee lovers, wellness experts, farmers, roasters, and importers. You all 
contribute to a movement grounded in “One Health,” a concept that originated in early 
20th-century collaborations between veterinary and public health professionals and 
has since evolved into a global framework recognizing the interdependence of human, 
animal, and environmental health. Sustainable farming, protecting biodiversity, and 

2 ♦ Ildi Revi
ensuring fair value are key. Every decision prioritizes health, leading to better coffee 
production. The guide cites research and highlights health-focused choices at each 
stage. With this approach, every cup benefits your well-being and our world.
The journey begins with a question: “What if every choice in making this cup of 
coffee was focused on health?” Roasted coffee is incredibly complex, with hundreds 
of compounds. What if we could enhance the most beneficial ones? To explore this, 
I’ve used mostly peer-reviewed scientific studies. I’ve occasionally referred to official 
sources, such as the United Nations, the United States Food and Drug Administration 
(FDA), and the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA).
Endangered 
Coffea arabica
After over two decades of research, scientists at the Royal Botanic 
Gardens, Kew, conducted an assessment of all 124 coffee species 
for the first time for the International Union for Conservation 
of Nature (IUCN) Red List of Threatened Species™. The results, 
published in the journals Science Advances and Global Change 
Biology, highlight significant concerns for the future of global 
coffee production.
The research indicates that 60 percent of wild coffee species 
are at risk of extinction due to deforestation, climate change, 
and the increasing prevalence of fungal pathogens and pests. 
This includes the wild relative of Coffea arabica, which is highly 
favored and extensively traded worldwide, now classified as 
‘Endangered’ on the IUCN Red List, primarily due to climate 
change projections.
“The climate change impacts on Arabica coffee (Coffea 
arabica) revealed by Kew’s research findings and IUCN Red 
List assessment raise serious environmental, economic and 
social concerns, particularly for the millions of smallholder 
farmers that rely on this crop for their livelihood… numerous 
wild relatives of the commercially grown crops, such as Arabica 
coffee, are essential to ensure the resilience of cultivated coffee in 
the face of climate change and other threats. …The situation is 
alarming and is an important reminder of the need for effective 
species conservation.” 
- Craig Hilton-Taylor
Head of IUCN Red List Unit
(Kew.org 2019, Davis 2019, Moat 2019)

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 3
WHAT IS CIRCULAR HEALTH COFFEE (CHC)?
Circular Health Coffee (CHC) is a category of coffee defined by health integrity, 
ecological accountability, and scientific verification. It goes beyond flavor by requiring 
measurable contributions to human and environmental well-being.
CHC is not just “better coffee.” It’s a completely different kind of coffee. It’s produced 
purposefully for health, using regenerative methods, verified by lab testing, and 
designed to support the wellness of the drinker, the grower, and the planet.
What Makes CHC Different? 
CHC coffee is:
•
Produced intentionally to support human health, not just to develop flavor.
•
Tested in third-party labs to verify beneficial compounds and ensure the
absence of harmful contaminants.
•
Backed by transparent data derived from third-party lab testing, which helps
consumers select coffees aligned with their health goals.
•
Farmed using practices that enhance soil health, support biodiversity, and
avoid synthetic inputs.
•
Roasted with data-informed and monitored protocols targeting key
compounds while honoring the natural character of each lot.
•
Built on long-term, equitable relationships that prioritize trust and
accountability across the supply chain.
As the book unfolds, you’ll begin to see how each of these elements comes together, 
what makes CHC unique, and how it’s changing the way we think about coffee.
DEFINING CIRCULAR HEALTH COFFEE 
(CHC) OR HOLISTIC COFFEE.
CHC is grounded in three intersecting frameworks: circular economy, circular 
agriculture, and circular health. The circular economy and agriculture aim to reduce 
waste and improve resource efficiency through recycling and closed-loop systems. 

4 ♦ Ildi Revi
Circular agriculture enhances soil health through crop rotation, composting, and the 
use of integrated pest management. This promotes biodiversity and reduces the use 
of chemicals. Circular health, linked to One Health, takes a comprehensive view of 
health that connects the well-being of humans, animals, plants, and the environment.
Since the start of the 2020s, coffee organizations have increasingly embraced circular 
economy concepts (ICO 2023). By merging circular agriculture and circular health 
(Capua 2020), we define CHC as follows:
Circular Health Coffee is a holistic approach to coffee production, from farm 
to roaster, that integrates principles of circular agriculture, circular economy, and 
circular health to enhance the overall wellness of people, plants, and ecosystems. 
This approach emphasizes organic regenerative farming practices, waste reduction, 
and resource efficiency, ensuring health benefits for all elements within the coffee 
value chain. Continuous improvement is driven by peer-reviewed research, field 
innovation, and feedback from supply chain partners and consumers.
We also refer to CHC as holistic coffee. Both terms are interchangeable; however, I 
use “CHC” formally and “holistic coffee” informally. CHC offers a new reason to 
choose coffee: while it must taste good and meet high-quality standards, it must also 
promote well-being. The CHC approach examines the plant’s health and environment 
together, and how both factors influence the development of beneficial compounds.
When I mention “category,” I mean it in the retail sense: like energy drinks or 
gluten-free foods, CHC offers consumers a specific purpose and identity. People 
are increasingly seeking coffees with antioxidant activity and features that align with 
personal health goals.
People are now choosing coffee for health benefits, reshaping the industry and 
consumer expectations. Billions worldwide enjoy coffee daily for taste and caffeine; 
CHC suggests adding wellness to the list.
TRUST, TESTING, AND TRANSPARENCY IN CHC
CHC is built on trust, but also on verification. That verification isn’t about suspicion; 
it’s about alignment. When we test coffee, it’s not to catch someone doing something 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 5
wrong. It’s to confirm what producers are doing right. It’s also about learning together, 
noticing patterns, tracking improvements, and gaining a better understanding of 
how farming, processing, and roasting choices impact the final chemistry of the cup. 
As an agricultural product, not a pharmaceutical or lab-created supplement, coffee 
is naturally subject to some variation, which is expected and even beneficial in a 
biologically diverse system.
Across the CHC supply chain, producers, exporters, importers, and roasters work 
closely together. Many producers look forward to lab results as a kind of feedback 
loop, or an opportunity to validate the impact of a new soil amendment, fermentation 
protocol, or harvest practice. These trusted relationships allow us to grow together 
while protecting public health.
What sets CHC apart is that testing isn’t hidden or optional; it’s foundational. Lab 
testing is one of the clearest differentiators between CHC and other coffees. It verifies 
compound levels, such as CGA, trigonelline, and caffeine, and confirms the absence 
of harmful substances, including mycotoxins, heavy metals, and pesticide residues. 
But most importantly, it empowers consumers. While full lab documents are typically 
reserved for compliance and internal verification, producers can communicate key 
compound data that helps consumers choose coffees aligned with their health goals.. 
There is no single “healthiest” coffee, although circular health coffees are demonstrably 
“cleaner” and more nutritious than others. However, ultimately, every person has 
different health needs, and lab-verified data helps match the right coffee to the right 
body. What is the healthiest for one person may not be for another.
We currently do not have a way to third-party lab test every bag or batch of coffee 
as it comes out of the roaster. That would be cost-prohibitive and impractical. 
Rather, we focus on doing each part of the process correctly, including product 
development, research, monitoring green coffee moisture, following roast curves and 
roast benchmarks with technology, test-tasting, adhering to good manufacturing 
practices, record-keeping, training employees, and visually inspecting finished 
products. Combine that with verifying results through lab testing for nutrients and 
contaminants at critical supply chain points, and you have a coffee that is intentionally 

6 ♦ Ildi Revi
produced for both health and taste. This approach gives us the confidence that what 
we’re offering is safe, consistent, and aligned with our health-focused values.
Healthy coffee implies coffee that’s more than just free of mold and contaminants. 
Toxin-free should be the standard for all food, not a special feature. Yes, we want to 
avoid the “bad stuff,” but coffee is a remarkable plant with numerous health benefits. 
Focusing only on it being “mold-free” misses the bigger picture. We need to recognize, 
highlight, and develop these health aspects.
To be clear, I’m referring to pure coffee, not coffee blended with supplements or 
added ingredients like mushrooms or adaptogens. If a company is adding ingredients 
to coffee for alleged health reasons, shouldn’t it start with a coffee that has already 
been vetted for its health benefits? Has the company tested or verified the safety and 
quality of the ingredients it is adding? And if coffee is already a personal and complex 
beverage, adding other ingredients and calling it healthier because of them may make 
things more confusing rather than more effective.
WHAT THIS BOOK IS, AND WHAT IT PROPOSES
This book introduces the evolving standards behind Circular Health Coffee (CHC) 
and offers a snapshot of where this emerging category stands today. It does not dictate 
rules or enforce compliance. Instead, it invites passionate collaborators, farmers, 
researchers, roasters, scientists, and consumers, to help shape a new kind of system 
centered on health: the health of coffee drinkers, ecosystems, and the endangered 
Coffea arabica plant itself.
CHC is not a critique of any coffee or company. It is doing something different. 
Its focus is health. Where earlier systems emphasized taste, visual quality, and trade 
logistics, CHC prioritizes health integrity at its core. That includes verified regenerative 
practices, lab-tested bioactive compounds, food safety, and transparency. This is not 
just a shift in flavor preference; it is a redefinition of what quality means in the coffee 
industry.
A key aim of this book is to initiate a formal conversation about establishing trusted, 
verifiable standards within the coffee industry. Globally recognized standard-setting 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 7
processes, such as those defined by the International Organization for Standardization 
(ISO) and the World Trade Organization (WTO), require a scientific foundation, 
stakeholder engagement, transparency, and consensus-based development. In that 
spirit, this book calls for serious, collaborative work to build a credible framework for 
CHC that earns trust not through branding, but through transparent, measurable, 
and science-based methodologies.
Chapter 5 proposes a starting point: a health-grade green arabica coffee standard 
that incorporates safety, sustainability, and thresholds for key bioactive compounds. 
It is not intended as a final word, but as an open invitation to begin the process with 
rigor and integrity, for the benefit of both consumers and supply chain partners. For 
those interested in the broader systems-level model behind CHC, see Appendix 2: 
The Circular Health Coffee Cycle, which outlines the nine interconnected stages of 
production and participation that define this category.
A PERSONAL NOTE BEFORE WE BEGIN: FULL DISCLOSURE
You might wonder why a book called The Coffee Guide to Better Health begins with 
a personal story that includes science classrooms in Zimbabwe, life on a coffee farm, 
and the accidental opening of a café. Fair question. Although this book isn’t about me, 
I begin with my story for a reason. For nearly 30 years, I worked in almost every part 
of the coffee industry (farming, importing, roasting, retail, and education) without 
ever considering coffee’s potential health benefits beyond caffeine. 
It wasn’t until I had a conversation with Andrew Salisbury, a newcomer to the world 
of coffee from the telecommunications industry, that I began to see coffee through 
an entirely new lens. That conversation eventually led to my involvement with Purity 
Coffee. It launched a journey I hadn’t expected, one that included hundreds of hours 
of reading scientific literature and trying to make it both accurate and accessible for 
real-world use.
I share my story because many in the industry, especially those skeptical of health-
focused claims, may see themselves in it. My early doubts and assumptions mirrored 
what I’ve since heard from countless others. By retracing the steps that changed how 

8 ♦ Ildi Revi
I think about coffee, I hope to offer a credible and relatable entry point into this new 
category. 
While I’m not a scientist, I’ve spent years as an educator working at the intersection 
of science and practice, trying to bridge the gap between research and application 
for farmers, roasters, healthcare professionals, and coffee lovers alike. Presenting this 
information clearly, realistically, and responsibly is of great importance to me.
This book feels like my final lap and may be one of the last big projects I’ll contribute 
before stepping back. I’ve been asked many times to share my story, and it seemed 
like the right time to put it all down, reflect, give back, and share what I’ve learned 
along the way.
That said, if personal stories aren’t your thing, don’t hesitate to skip ahead to Chapter 
3. My feelings won’t be hurt. You don’t need to know my backstory to understand the 
science. It’s a bit of a detour, but sometimes it helps to know who’s driving the bus.
HOW THIS BOOK IS STRUCTURED
This book follows the journey of coffee through the CHC lens from seed to cup. 
It begins with the health-promoting compounds found in coffee, then moves into 
farming, processing, roasting, and brewing practices that influence their presence or 
degradation. It also explores food safety, decaffeination, and transparency, offering 
practical guidance for producers, roasters, and consumers. Along the way, you’ll see 
how each stage can be optimized to protect both people and ecosystems, and how 
collaborative standards can support that transformation. 
We begin to examine the effects of coffee on various diseases in Chapter 3, drawing 
on peer-reviewed studies and authoritative sources such as the United Nations and the 
FDA. Through concise literature reviews, it explores pressing health topics, including 
liver diseases, type 2 diabetes, neurodegenerative conditions, heart health, and various 
cancers. 
Next, we look at the major compounds in coffee, guided by the question: “How can 
we produce coffee by making every decision based on health?” This chapter outlines 
some of the most extensively researched bioactive compounds, including caffeine, 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 9
chlorogenic acids, melanoidins, diterpenes, and others, as well as contaminants and 
compounds of concern. Understanding these substances helps both producers and 
consumers make more informed, health-supportive choices.
Following that is a primer on coffee production, with a focus on how producers can 
impact the health profile of the beans that eventually get roasted and brewed. Circular 
agriculture and health in this category emphasize organic and regenerative farming 
practices to combat soil degradation, a growing global concern. Projections suggest 
that, without major changes, many farmlands may have fewer than 50 harvests 
remaining before irreversible damage occurs. This chapter examines models that utilize 
organic methods and agroforestry to improve soil fertility, promote biodiversity, and 
manage pests naturally, ultimately enhancing the health of humans, animals, and 
plants. 
This leads to a discussion of standards for CHC, including a review of existing systems 
and the rationale for setting new baselines. The economics and business practices of 
CHC diverge meaningfully from the standard market-plus-differential approach to 
coffee trade. Collaboration, fair pricing, and community trust help build a resilient 
and equitable supply chain that supports long-term well-being for all involved.
Next, a chapter on decaffeination describes various methods: methylene chloride, 
ethyl acetate, supercritical carbon dioxide, and water processing. Each has distinct 
health and environmental implications. High-quality decaffeinated coffees can retain 
many beneficial compounds and have been linked to a reduced risk of type 2 diabetes, 
improved liver function, lower colorectal cancer risk, and neuroprotection. The best 
decaf coffees are a vital and valued part of the holistic coffee category.
After that, the chapter on roasting coffee for health is one of transparency and challenge. 
It begins as a koan, a paradoxical concept that resists simple understanding. Coffee 
roasting can be vague in its grasp of what exactly happens at each phase of the roast, 
yet it can still yield exceptional and repeatable outcomes. Roasting remains a complex 
and partly mysterious craft, even as a growing body of scientific research reveals how 
different roasting conditions can influence the health benefits of the final cup.
The complexity of coffee as an agricultural product, subject to natural cycles and 
countless variables, can make us feel like we are chasing unicorns. And yet, there 

10 ♦ Ildi Revi
is real evidence to build on. The holistic coffee category is growing because we are 
actively shaping it and seeing results. This enigma that is coffee challenges us to bridge 
science, technology, and food production in new ways. Roasting for health may be 
demanding, but it merges tradition and innovation in a pursuit that makes a tangible 
difference in people’s lives. 
Food safety in coffee production and manufacturing is a pillar of health and 
quality. It should not be viewed as a set of tedious compliance tasks, but as an area 
of ongoing research and improvement focused on the healthfulness of the final 
product. Throughout the book, I cover food safety practices from farm to roastery, 
guided by standards such as the U.S. Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA) of 
2011. A comprehensive safety approach addresses biological, chemical, and physical 
hazards across the supply chain, with good manufacturing practices being essential. 
Understanding these practices gives everyone perspective on the healthfulness of the 
foods they buy, including coffee, which still seems to be an afterthought by many.
The final chapter examines healthy brewing and the sensory experience of coffee. 
Flavor perception is influenced not only by brewing variables but also by genetics, 
health status, and the human microbiome. We examine water quality, which comprises 
98–99% of coffee, as well as brewing methods and storage practices that align with 
individual taste preferences and health goals.
For those with limited time or looking for an overview, each chapter begins with a 
summary of key points before diving into the full content.  

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦11

12
Technical Notes and Intention
While science can’t predict exactly how any one person will 
respond to coffee, it does reveal consistent patterns and 
outcomes that help us understand its broader impact.
HOW SCIENCE LOOKS AT COFFEE
Scientific research helps us understand the effects of coffee on human health. One 
important tool is epidemiology, which studies patterns of disease in large populations. 
Originally focused on infectious diseases, epidemiology now also investigates non-
communicable diseases. These studies are observational, and they track real-life 
behavior over time, without controlling variables like in a lab experiment.
This method is especially useful for identifying long-term trends. For example, 
researchers linked smoking to lung cancer by comparing health outcomes between 
smokers and non-smokers. Epidemiological studies often adjust for confounding 
factors, enabling scientists to isolate the relationship between a specific behavior, such 
as drinking coffee, and a health outcome.
While these studies can’t prove causation, they often reveal strong associations. In 
the case of coffee, epidemiology has consistently shown benefits: lower risk of type 2 
diabetes, liver disease, and some neurodegenerative conditions. These patterns show 
up across cultures, diets, and lifestyles. The consistency suggests that coffee is not just 
a complement to other healthy behaviors, but is likely playing an active role.
Genetics also plays a role. Some individuals may be genetically predisposed to benefit 
more from coffee. Genome-wide studies have demonstrated that variations in genes 
such as CYP1A2, which influence caffeine metabolism, can impact both tolerance 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 13
and consumption patterns. People with the C allele metabolize caffeine more slowly, 
leading to prolonged physiological effects. As a result, they often consume less coffee 
to avoid overstimulation or adverse effects.
Despite individual variability, science offers broad insights into how coffee functions 
in the body. Researchers continue to explore which compounds drive these benefits 
and how they can be retained or enhanced through farming, processing, and roasting. 
The goal isn’t to treat coffee like a pharmaceutical, but to better understand how 
something consumed daily, often habitually, can be aligned with individual and public 
health goals.
A WORD ABOUT CITATIONS 
I find reading texts with numbered citations challenging because I constantly have 
to flip to the back of the chapter or book to find the reference, making it hard to 
remember which citation corresponds to which number. Instead, I prefer to use the 
first author’s last name and the year (Author 2025). Considering that the majority 
of citations involve multiple authors, please assume “et al.” in such cases. The only 
exception is when there are just two authors for a citation (Author and Author 2025).
CHLOROGENIC ACIDS (CGA)
Throughout the book, I refer to chlorogenic acids as CGA, a common abbreviation 
used in scientific literature. Chlorogenic acids encompass a group of related 
compounds, not just a single entity. “CGA” is commonly used in scientific literature 
to refer to these compounds collectively in both singular and plural contexts. Although 
“CGA” appears singular, it is also understood to be plural (although some also use 
“CGAs,” which my scientific advisor told me not to do), and thus, the verbs associated 
with it are plural as well. For example, one can say, “CGA are phenolic compounds” 
and “CGA help regulate glucose.” I’ve included a footnote in the CGA section as a 
reminder of this grammar point.

14 ♦ Ildi Revi
INTENTION
This book is for anyone who has individual wellness objectives and wants to have 
the information needed to choose the best coffee for their health journey. For this 
to happen, we need people in the coffee and wellness industries to join in creating 
standards and developing these coffees. I hope that food scientists, coffee enthusiasts, 
individuals with health concerns, and those interested in other food products will find 
the book’s structure and content useful. More importantly, I hope to inspire you, shift 
your paradigm of coffee, and help you see that “the sky is in the jar.” I hope you see 
how coffee can be wondrous to behold.
If you already drink coffee or decaf, I hope to guide you toward making the healthiest 
choices aligned with your own goals. This is also the intention behind Circular Health 
Coffee.
Everyone reacts to coffee differently. Just as not every medicine is suitable for everyone, 
coffee may not be right for everyone to consume. Individuals metabolize coffee 
compounds differently and have varying tolerances to them. The presence of a specific 
compound in coffee does not guarantee it will produce the same effects for everyone. 
As we begin our journey, I want to emphasize that individual experiences with coffee 
are very personal, and I do not intend to pressure anyone into drinking it. 
Each person’s experience with coffee is unique. Through my own journey, I learned 
to see coffee differently, not merely as a morning ritual or caffeine source, but as 
an intentional wellness decision. Circular health coffee reveals possibilities I never 
imagined. I’m sharing my story to help you explore this new perspective on coffee’s 
role in wellness.

15
2
The Sky is in the Jar:
Where Wonder Begins, and Perspective Shifts
From the Zambezi to Purity: A Journey into Coffee and Health
From living and working on an African coffee farm to founding a roaster-retail café, designing 
global coffee education programs, and creating a learning system for an import company, I spent 
two decades immersed in coffee without once considering its potential as a health-promoting 
beverage.
This chapter traces the beginning of my relationship with coffee long before I knew it would 
become the focus of my life’s work. It started when I took a volunteer teaching job in a remote part 
of Zimbabwe and met the farmer who first taught me how coffee is grown and roasted. Years later, 
I returned to southern Africa, where I met my future husband, a coffee producer in the Vumba 
Mountains of Zimbabwe. I went on to live and work on the farm, gaining firsthand experience 
with every stage of coffee cultivation and processing.
After relocating to South Carolina to build a roasting business, my background as a science teacher 
and workplace learning educator laid the foundation for designing education programs in the 
specialty coffee industry. Through industry connections, I was recommended to help launch 
what would become Purity Coffee before the first bean was ever roasted. That moment marked 
the beginning of a profound shift in how I understood coffee: not just as a beverage, but as a 
functional, health-supporting plant.
This is the story of how that shift happened and an invitation for anyone in healthcare, the coffee 
industry, or simply someone who drinks coffee to wake up to see it not just as a habit, but as a 
powerful, health-supporting plant. If a lifelong coffee professional could be transformed by this 
perspective, I hope everyone can look at coffee with fresh eyes. The sky is in the jar, and it is 
wondrous to behold.

16 ♦ Ildi Revi
The first time I experienced the intoxicating aroma of coffee being roasted was in 
the Vumba Mountains of Zimbabwe in the kitchen of a coffee farm, where traces of 
countless earlier roasts clung in the gray canvas curtains and rough wooden ceiling. The 
farmer making the coffee was John Wisdom, a robust man with a booming, infectious 
laugh as we exchanged stories on my first morning on his farm. John was not using a 
sophisticated coffee roaster. Instead, he heated green (raw) coffee beans in a large iron 
wok, stirring them steadily with a wooden spoon until they transformed into deep, 
dark brown. At just the right moment, with a perfectly timed flourish, he flipped the 
beans out of the wok and into a large, thickly woven basket made from palm leaves. 
He tossed the beans to cool them, flicking his wrists with such finesse that the mass 
of beans stayed together, not a single one veering off course towards the floor. I was 
both mesmerized and impressed, and I felt a sense of being let in on a secret that few 
other people knew about a beverage they drank every morning.
Once the coffee was cooled and ready to be brewed, John put an ancient iron kettle of 
water on his little white stove to boil and heated the milk he had collected earlier that 
morning from the elegant, fawn-colored cow in his front yard. He scooped the coffee 
from the basket and transferred it into the hopper of an antique, heavy hand-crank 
grinder attached to the wall, catching the grounds in a little silver tray as they fell out 
while he rotated the grinder arm around and around. 
The potent fragrance of the ground coffee filled the air. It propelled me back into a 
scene from my childhood that I had long forgotten, and I was grateful to relive it. For 
an instant, I was with my father back at the neighborhood A&P Supermarket, which 
used to have electric coffee grinders. My father would take a bag of whole-bean coffee 
off the shelf and grind it right there, diffusing coffee fragrance into the aisle. 
The scent of freshly ground coffee was not just a familiar comfort, but it also made 
breathing easier. As a child, I occasionally suffered from asthma. When I started to 
wheeze, my father would brew me a strong cup of coffee, a remedy he had learned from 
his mother, a pediatrician and apothecary in Hungary. With my father’s hand rubbing 
my back in circles, I would sip the coffee, and within about an hour, the bronchi in 
my lungs would relax, allowing me to breathe freely again.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 17
I was snapped out of this memory as John poured the ground coffee and steaming hot 
water into a stainless steel plunge pot, which I had never seen before (but years later 
learned was a French press). The coffee grounds bubbled up fiercely, nearly spilling 
over the brim. John stirred it, added more water, stirred again and pressed the plunger 
screen down, filtering the grounds out of the finished brew.
He filled two large mugs halfway with the coffee and topped them with creamy, warm 
milk. We left the kitchen and settled on the verandah, his dogs,  a white bull terrier 
and a black Newfie,  trotting behind us. With my first sip of “café au lait,” I fell in love 
with coffee, as if my world had transformed. The morning was cool, and the steam 
from our cups drifted outward, mingling with the mist rising from the coffee lands 
and dense forests dotted with tree ferns. The sounds of birds seemed amplified, and 
the sun cast a shimmering glow on Chikamba Dam, visible across the mountains in 
Mozambique. We could see the road that led directly to Beira on the coast, and in 
the distance, a plume of smoke rose from the lone Gorongosa Mountains. I would 
visit that mountain years later, but not then–civil war still gripped Manica and Sofala 
provinces.
Like most pivotal moments, this one felt unremarkable at the time. I didn’t know 
I was choosing a path that would become a profound, spiral-shaped journey with 
coffee, altering everything. A year out of university with a history degree, I saw few 
prospects beyond waitressing—being a barista wasn’t even a thing back then. Then 
a random moment shifted my trajectory: a friend of my mother’s sent her an article 
about an organization seeking volunteers to teach in remote Zimbabwe. With no 
grand ambitions except to move my life forward, I applied and was soon placed at 
Manjolo Secondary School, about 15 kilometers from Binga, sharing a house with 
teachers from England.

18 ♦ Ildi Revi
Manjolo Secondary School, 1990
I met John through this teaching post. His mother, Doris Lessing, who would go on 
to win the Nobel Prize for Literature in 2007, was a close friend of the director of the 
volunteer program that had brought me to Zimbabwe. When Doris planned to visit 
Binga while researching her book African Laughter, the director reached out to me. 
She asked if I could prepare the house and help show them around during the holiday 
break. When Doris and the director arrived, I gave them the full tour of Binga. We 
visited the Rest Camp, with its hot spring-fed pools, and the craft center, where local 
Tonga artists sold exquisitely woven baskets, beadwork, and wood carvings.
After a few nights in Binga, they invited me to join them on a three-day journey to 
Harare. Before we parted ways, Doris introduced me to her son, John, who would 
become a close friend of mine. He welcomed me into his home during school breaks 
and took me on trips to places I never would have seen otherwise.
I looked forward to my holidays in Vumba with John, but my everyday life was on the 
other side of the country in Binga, which was off the main tar road from Bulawayo 
toward Victoria Falls, then onto a narrow, winding strip of tarmac that threaded 
through the hills to Kamativi. Beyond Kamativi, the pavement disappeared, and the 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 19
final two-hour stretch to Binga unfolded along a rough, bone-shaking dirt road, deeply 
corrugated in places, with ruts and hollows carved by rural buses and heavy trucks.
Each morning, my roommate Andrea, a teacher from Manchester, and I drove the 
organization-issued pickup truck to Manjolo. Students traveled from various villages 
to arrive at assembly before the bell. We stopped to pick up as many students as we 
could fit in the back seat and truck bed. During the ride, those in the cab with us 
taught us Tonga phrases, correcting our pronunciation, but on arrival at school, only 
English was spoken.
The Tonga people, original inhabitants of the Zambezi Valley, were displaced in the 
1950s when the Zambezi River was dammed to create Lake Kariba. As the reservoir 
rose, it swallowed their ancestral lands. They were relocated to higher ground along 
the Zambezi escarpment, characterized by mopane woodlands, poor soils, and a short, 
unpredictable rainy season, which bore little resemblance to the fertile riverbanks 
they had once cultivated (Tremmel 1994). We were near Chizarira National Park, but 
wildlife still moved freely beyond its borders. When I lived there, rhinos and elephants 
were a part of the landscape. We could hear lions roaring at night, and sometimes 
elephants wandered right through the school grounds. Government electricity had 
not reached the area, and running water was only available in Binga town. It was a 
bitter irony, since Kariba Dam had been built to produce hydroelectric power, and the 
vast lake now shimmered in the valley below, yet the people who had paid the highest 
price for its creation lived without power or water.
Our house on the outskirts of town was modest but fortunate by comparison: it had 
a small solar panel wired to a car battery, which powered a couple of fluorescent tube 
lamps. When the battery died, we lit candles to read or work after dark, and I did 
plenty of both. Although I was meant to teach English and history, when I arrived at 
Manjolo, I was told I’d instead be teaching maths and science to the first- and second-
year students. I objected, but the headmaster insisted it was the only option.
While it was not what I expected, it was not a disappointment, since I had enjoyed 
science and had taken biology, botany and ecology at university. I needed to review 
the maths I was supposed to teach, since I had not done it since high school. I also 
had to learn the Zimbabwe Junior Certificate (ZJC) and O-Level curricula for each 

20 ♦ Ildi Revi
grade, develop lesson plans, and ensure I was at least a couple of steps ahead of my 
students each day. 
The most challenging thing was organizing science class projects. Not only was there 
no electricity or water at Manjolo, but when I first arrived, there was no dedicated 
science room, just a closet with tubs of supplies randomly thrown together and a rusty 
metal cabinet stacked with brown bottles and beaten-up cardboard boxes filled with 
random items. I stayed late every day during the first week to complete an inventory 
of chemicals, equipment and materials. Only then could I figure out what parts of 
the curriculum I could teach and what to order from the supply center in Bulawayo. 
Luckily, I had everything I needed for the first month of each grade level, and I bought 
a 30-liter plastic tank at the Binga Supermarket with a tap on it, which I filled with 
water every morning and secured in the back of the truck. 
Drawing by Moffat Mutale, 1990. Andrea and I driving to Manjolo.
The first experiment in the ZJC science curriculum was designed to spark students’ 
curiosity and encourage them to observe the world through a scientific lens. It also 
introduced the fundamentals of the scientific method, including how to accurately 
record and report experimental observations using a standard lab report format.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 21
Following the curriculum’s guidelines, I prepared the “blue bottle” experiment by 
combining glucose, sodium hydroxide, and methylene blue in a sealed jam jar, leaving 
a few centimeters of headspace. Initially, the mixture settled into a clear, colorless 
solution. When the jar was shaken, oxygen from the trapped air reacted with the 
methylene blue, oxidizing it and turning the solution blue. As the solution stood 
still, the glucose gradually reduced the methylene blue, returning the solution to a 
colorless state.
When I presented the jar of solution to the class, I placed it on my desk at the front 
of the room. Pointing to it, I asked, “What is in the jar?”
As expected, the majority of the students responded, “Water.”
I then picked up the jar and gave it a firm shake. Instantly, the liquid inside transformed 
into a deep indigo blue. The students gasped in surprise. A few moments later, the 
blue faded, and the solution returned to its clear state. They gasped again, but this 
time with more wonder than shock.
The transformation sparked an animated discussion. We talked about how the world 
isn’t always what it seems, and how science helps us uncover the hidden forces at work 
in everyday things. Sometimes, what we’re sure we know (like presuming it’s water in 
the jar) turns out to be something else entirely. Science gives us the tools to test those 
assumptions and explore what’s really happening beneath the surface. I introduced 
the idea that what we observe can change depending on how we interact with it, and 
that experiments like this are a way to test ideas, not just memorize facts.
Using this moment of curiosity, I introduced the structure of a basic lab report, 
explaining how to describe an observation, formulate a hypothesis, outline the steps, 
and record the results. I gave the students time to write a short report on the blue 
bottle experiment in their own words.
Later, I read through their reports and shared them with Andrea so she could assess 
their English language levels and adjust her class planning accordingly.
One report that has stayed with me all these years was written by a student named 
Jimmy Musambazi. During class, Jimmy often sat silently, his face unreadable, eyes 

22 ♦ Ildi Revi
distant. He never raised his hand, never spoke up, and to be honest, we weren’t sure 
he was understanding anything we were teaching.
But when we read his lab report, it took our breath away. He had written:
We were dumbfounded, not just by the poetry, but by the clarity and wonder in his 
words. In just a few lines, Jimmy had captured the essence of the experiment more 
beautifully than we could have explained it ourselves. And in doing so, he ended up 
teaching us the very lesson the experiment was intended to teach the students: that 
what we think we know, which seems obvious or certain, can turn out to be something 
completely different.
Jimmy’s phrase became a metaphor among us. If someone revealed a fresh insight into 
something ordinary or saw something we had overlooked, we’d say, “The sky is in the 
jar.” And when something truly unexpected and beautiful happened, we’d smile and 
say, “It is a wondrous thing to behold.”
By the time my teaching contract was over, as with many who travel as volunteers to 
far-off lands, I was beyond grateful to the students and the people I had met. There 
is a Tonga proverb that once you drink the waters of the Zambezi, you can never stay 
away from it. I made my next life choices, knowing I needed to return someday. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 23
I wanted to stay in education, but I was determined to do something different 
from formal school teaching. When I returned to the United States, I enrolled in 
an innovative adult education program, focusing my degree on workplace learning, 
skills training, and job performance. Upon finishing, I ran an education program for a 
social service agency in Chicago for three years. I taught and trained volunteer teachers 
for adult basic education, English as a second language and job readiness programs. 
An increasing number of teenagers who had been expelled from public schools for 
various reasons began attending my adult learning classes, which were not a good fit 
for them. In response, I applied for and received a $250,000 grant from the Chicago 
Public Schools. With that funding, I launched an alternative high school and built a 
team of professional teachers to serve these students more effectively. 
All the while, I kept an eye out for a chance to return to southern Africa. That 
opportunity came in an unexpected way. I had sent a couple of teachers to a conference, 
and when one of them returned, she handed me a card she had picked up from 
the open jobs board. As I read it, I couldn’t help but shake my head at fate’s way 
of “throwing down the gauntlet.” It had information about a workplace learning 
US Information Service teaching fellowship in Mozambique. The civil war had 
finally ended, and they needed to expand their business capacity to help rebuild their 
economy. They were challenged by the fact that Portuguese was the official language, 
as English-speaking countries surrounded them. They needed to communicate with 
all the Southern African Development Community (SADC) countries, and Angola 
was the only other Portuguese-speaking country in the region. SADC’s goals, both 
then and now, were to unite and share resources among its member countries and to 
ensure regional cooperation and effective policy implementation. 
I got the position after a brief interview, and although I was wistful about leaving the 
education program I had built, I left it in capable hands. The executive director said 
she would always have an open spot at the agency for me, but like in Robert Frost’s 
poem “The Road Less Traveled,” I doubted that I would ever return.
When I arrived at my assignment in the port city of Beira, I was shocked by what I saw. 
The war had left deep scars, and in 1996, Mozambique was considered the poorest 
country in the world. There were hundreds of aid workers, many of whom organized 

24 ♦ Ildi Revi
food programs, while others undertook the dangerous work of clearing land mines 
from the fields where crops needed to be grown. 
A major plus, though, was its 3-hour drive to the Vumba mountains via a road close 
to Gorongosa National Park and past Chikamba Dam – the same road I first saw from 
John’s verandah five years earlier. After spending four months in Beira, I had a few 
days off, and a friend driving to Zimbabwe to go shopping offered to give me a lift to 
Mutare, the city closest to the Vumba.
It was a quick ride to the border, although the crossing was rough with hundreds of 
people and dozens of vehicles. We made it through eventually, and my friend dropped 
me off at the club John used to belong to. Over the years, I had tried to phone him 
a few times and sent him several letters, but I received no reply. Unfortunately, I was 
not in contact with anyone who could pass a message on to him, but as soon as I 
walked through the club door, I ran into Lynn Perkins, an old friend of John’s. We 
quickly caught up on the paths our lives had taken, and then I asked about John. She 
explained that he had passed away suddenly a few months after I had left. They held 
his funeral, and his ashes were scattered into the stream that coiled through the forest 
on his farm. Lynn offered to take me there the following day.
When we arrived at John’s place the next morning, it was almost exactly as I 
remembered it…peaceful, familiar. The only difference was that his friend Tackie 
now lived there and had transformed it into a guesthouse. Tackie was a botanist who 
specialized in tree ferns, cultivating them in the forest surrounding the coffee lands, 
which the neighbors now managed. We walked to the stream where the ashes had 
been strewn, and it was enchanting, wondrous to behold, with tree ferns punctuating 
the path, birds singing dynamically and water flowing past rocks and across the road. 
We sat on a fallen munjerenji tree trunk alongside the stream for an hour, telling our 
favorite John stories.
Tackie suggested we go to Piet Snyder’s Club for drinks to raise a glass to John. As we 
drove up to the club, we heard gunshots. We parked and went inside, asking Amon, 
the bartender, what was happening. 
“Oh, Robert and Andrew are skeet shooting,” he said, pointing outside. We looked 
through the club’s huge picture windows to see a clay disc launched from a station at 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 25
the fence. It whizzed high above the 
dam. We heard a shot and saw the 
disc burst into fragments.
“Who are Robert and Andrew,” I 
asked. 
“Neighbors,” said Tackie, “Rob has 
been expanding Crake Valley Farm’s 
coffee operation since he returned 
from college in 1991. Andrew is his 
cousin, who is visiting from South 
Africa.” 
Rob walked through the door, 
ordered a Castle Lager and said 
hello to me. Game over. I felt a jolt 
of electricity surge through me, and 
I knew I had just met the man I 
would marry. We talked for hours, 
completely absorbed in each other, 
and when Tackie invited me to stay at John’s house that night, Lynn smiled at me as 
she said her goodbyes and returned to town. When we got to John’s, I cooked spaghetti 
Bolognese for supper for Tackie, Rob and Andrew. After the meal, Rob explained he 
had to leave early since his work started at 5:30 a.m. but before he left, he invited me 
to Crake Valley. I said yes without hesitation. 
The next day, Rob joined me for breakfast, and afterward, we toured his farm 
until lunchtime. As a remarkable coffee farmer, his meticulous attention to detail 
impressed me. His coffee fields were carefully contoured to prevent erosion, and he 
was committed to preserving large areas of old-growth, indigenous forests to protect 
various endangered species. Rob’s numerous innovations in producing exceptional 
coffee inspired me to learn more about coffee production, and I interrogated him the 
entire visit. 
The stream on John’s property.

26 ♦ Ildi Revi
He and his family also produced passion fruit and had a herd of dairy cows. His 
parents invited us for lunch at their house on the farm. Rob lived in a cottage, a 
couple of hundred meters away. I had met his parents before, as they were John’s 
closest neighbors, and they had shown me around their milk and cheese operation 
when it was just starting up in their garage. Now, they had an entire building with 
cold rooms, and it was a booming business with Vumba Cheese sold at supermarkets 
throughout Zimbabwe. 
I thanked them for lunch, and Rob took me back to John’s house, where I spent one 
more night before returning to Beira. The next morning, since he had to go to town 
anyway, Rob offered to take me back to the Mozambique border, where I could catch 
a minibus taxi on the other side for Beira. As we said goodbye, we planned to meet 
again the following month. 
We met that next month and then as often as we could over the next two years. When 
my contract ended, Rob asked me to stay with him in Crake Valley, and I did. Three 
months later, he asked me to marry him, with the understanding that although 
we could get married in the U.S. for tradition’s sake, we would live on the farm in 
Zimbabwe. I agreed.
We were married in Chicago in 1999 with 26 people in attendance, but when 
we returned to the Vumba, we celebrated with a much larger wedding ceremony. 
Hundreds of people came to join us. Afterward, I settled into work on the farm, 
helping out where I could, working on projects such as creating payroll spreadsheets 
and writing employee contracts. I also did short training courses independently for 
non-governmental organizations and companies. But my favorite moments were spent 
driving around the farm with Rob, constantly asking questions. He explained all his 
work with his coffee and taught me coffee seedling care, planting, nutrition, pruning, 
pests, diseases, harvesting and processing. Rob was a specialist in farm mechanization, 
using his training to design a large washing station that used gravity to move the coffee 
through the pulping, fermentation, washing, and drying processes. He had also built 
a massive mechanical dryer with conditioning bins and hullers next to it, and welded 
together a heavy stainless steel drum coffee roaster fueled by charcoal. This handmade 
roaster was the one on which I first learned to roast coffee. I spent hours at the coffee 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 27
factory and roastery, taking videos and photos, while being fascinated with every part 
of the process.
Everything was about to change, though. I was pregnant. The U.S. sent its first Peace 
Corps volunteers to Mozambique one month before the baby was due. They were 
being trained just across the border from the farm in Manica. I was asked to assist with 
the training, conduct teaching observations, and provide feedback to them. During 
school holidays, a few of them visited the farm, reminding me of my time with John.
In February 2000, our first daughter was born, and our second daughter was born three 
years later. Unfortunately, though, life was about to change drastically for us. After 
decades of fragile stability, the political situation in Zimbabwe rapidly deteriorated 
overnight. That part of my story is truly for another time or book. Crake Valley Farm’s 
advantage was its location on a quiet dirt road in a politically passive area. Yet, the 
Burma Valley below the Vumba was a different story, so we were continually on alert, 
with a duffel bag containing our passports and essentials packed and ready to grab if 
we needed to leave quickly.
In short, the circumstances led us to a difficult but necessary decision: I would take 
the girls to the U.S. and start a coffee business using beans from our farm. Rob and 
his parents would stay in Zimbabwe to weather the political and economic storm that 
ensued. We hoped that, eventually, things would either get so bad that Rob would have 
to leave and join us in the U.S. and his parents go to South Africa, or things would 
turn around and get better again, and it would be safe for me and the girls to move 
back to the farm once the dust had settled. 
One weekend in February 2004, we made a plan, researched the market, and decided 
to open a coffee roasting operation outside Greenville, South Carolina. In May 
2004, I packed up the girls and a few of our things, leaving the rest behind. We flew 
to Chicago, where my mother, a primary school music teacher, offered to take care 
of the girls as soon as summer vacation started. After settling the girls into a routine, 
I headed to Greenville to figure things out. A commercial real estate agent helped 
me find an ideal business location in Travelers Rest, in the Blue Ridge Mountains 
foothills, and a cedar log cabin with a stream for us to live in. I made down payments 
on both properties and purchased a pickup truck, using most of the inheritance my 

28 ♦ Ildi Revi
grandmother had left me after her passing. At this point, there was no turning back. 
I had put everything on the line. Failure was not an option. Too many people were 
counting on this to work.
Rob returned in August, carefully timing his trip to coincide with the arrival of 19 tons 
of coffee we had imported from the farm. Just before the coffee arrived, we bought a 
Probat L-12 drum roaster, loaded it onto the truck and drove it back from Memphis. 
Rob installed it, bashing a hole in the wall for an exhaust duct that went outside 
and up the side of the building. We paid the Travelers Rest High School football 
team to offload the 20-foot container of coffee, stacking the coffee sacks onto pallets 
throughout the roastery, and giving them coupons for pounds of coffee. 
We opened our wholesale and retail roasting business in January 2005 with a big 
coffee brewer and carafes to serve cups of coffee for takeaway only. This was to allow 
our customers to sample the roasts. Right away, people started coming in to buy our 
coffee. Many people came into the roastery disappointed to learn they could not get 
a cappuccino or caramel macchiato, only to walk out unhappy. 
Rob returned to Zimbabwe the day after we opened, but before he left, he told me I 
should not open a café for any reason. 
“Forget those people who are walking out without buying anything,” he said. I was to 
stick to the plan, focusing on coffee roasting and our growing wholesale business. He 
believed that selling large volumes of coffee wholesale was the fastest way to establish 
our company and that a coffee shop would be both a distraction and a money pit. If 
I were serving cappuccinos and muffins, I would not have time to market our coffee.
The local community had other ideas for me. The day after Rob left, a woman drove 
up with a table and four chairs she wanted to get rid of from her house. She thought 
I could use them for people to sit and enjoy a cup of coffee. That dining room set 
sparked a chain reaction. Soon, people began showing up with tables, sofas, armchairs, 
and equipment, all without me ever asking. When the local Winn-Dixie closed, their 
maintenance manager secured all sorts of stainless-steel tables, sinks and fridges for 
me at no charge and even installed them for free. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 29
I had a café except for a way to make cappuccinos. Modifying the plan slightly, I put 
the word out that I needed a functioning commercial espresso machine, and within 
the week, a young man walked through my door with a dark red Rancilio machine. 
He needed to sell it to buy a plane ticket to Thailand, where he would propose to 
his girlfriend, who was there on a mission trip. I paid him with the last cash from 
my grandmother’s money and called that machine the love brewer. I hired staff and 
asked an experienced barista to train my employees. Having worked in restaurants 
throughout my university days, I developed a simple menu and food program. By 
April 2004, the coffee shop was fully operational.
I faced a significant issue—despite the café’s successes, I hadn’t found the right moment 
to inform Rob about it. As his arrival approached, I decided to email him, revealing 
that we were now café owners. Initially, he was not pleased, but upon entering the 
café and seeing it busy with families and customers purchasing coffee and queuing at 
the cash register for a complimentary 12-ounce coffee with their purchase, he became 
more accepting of the idea.
Starting in 2005, we settled into an annual routine: Rob would arrive in May to pick 
up the girls when school let out and take them to Zimbabwe, and I’d join them in July, 
bringing them back to South Carolina in time for the new school year in August. Rob 
would come for a month at Christmas, or as the girls got older, I would send them as 
unaccompanied minors to the farm. 
As the business grew, I realized I needed to learn about importing, roasting and 
running a café. I turned to the Specialty Coffee Association of America (SCAA), as 
it was then called, to take the courses they offered. Although the subject experts were 
excellent in their coffee fields, I thought the SCAA could use my help creating an 
instructor training program with sound workplace learning principles. I volunteered 
for the organization in 2007, and by 2008, I was actively involved in developing coffee 
curricula and instructor learning programs for the SCAA. This is also where I met 
Trish Rothgeb, who became my mentor in coffee roasting and cupping. 

30 ♦ Ildi Revi
At the same time, several coffee 
roasters in South Carolina 
initiated a regional roaster 
gathering in Charleston 
in May 2008, known as 
the Southeastern Roasters 
Consortium (SERC). This 
gathering of roasters invited 
me and Rob to present on 
our farm and company. We 
met Steve Franklin during 
the event, whose story was so 
similar to ours that we became 
friends immediately. Steve 
imported coffee to Atlanta 
from his wife’s family farm 
in Nicaragua, Selva Negra, a 
remarkable, innovative organic 
operation. We decided to 
trade several sacks of coffee, 
allowing each of us to diversify 
our offerings to our respective 
customers, as we both only 
had coffee from our farms at 
the time. We ended up doing 
this for years afterward. As 
the SERC event ended, I was 
all in and offered to hold the 
next gathering at our café the 
following year.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 31
Our original café on Main Street, Travelers Rest, South Carolina.
Two weeks before the SERC event at our place the following year, Ricardo Pereira 
walked into my café, introduced himself, and told me he had started an import 
company for green coffee (the raw coffee before it is roasted) based in Greenville, 
which is close to Travelers Rest. He was trying to find roasting clients to buy the coffee 
from the Alta Mogiana region of Brazil near his hometown of Franca. He gave me a 
sample, which I roasted immediately, and we cupped it together the next day. It was 
lovely, and I bought ten sacks on the spot. 
I suggested that we do a roasting competition for SERC with his coffee. He happily 
sent five pounds (all from the same sack) to 19 roasters to introduce them to his 
company and see what they would do. For the event, I flew Trish in from San Francisco 
to do a coffee-cupping workshop, and afterward, we blind-tasted the 19 roasts made 
on each of our own roasting machines. They all tasted completely different. It was 
clear that the complexity of coffee, along with each roaster’s heat application and 
equipment choices, significantly altered the final product. As a huge win for Ricardo, 
he sold all his coffee and gained 18 new clients.
After that event, I became more involved with the SCAA, teaching instructor training 
courses and immersing myself in the world of coffee education. In 2010, I earned my 
Q Arabica Grader certification through the Coffee Quality Institute (CQI), a rigorous 
program used to calibrate sensory skills and standardize coffee quality evaluation. 

32 ♦ Ildi Revi
Although the Q exams played a major role in shaping the specialty coffee industry, 
the certification system evolved into a new model in 2025. At the time, the Q system 
was widely recognized for its cupping protocols and calibration process across multiple 
tests. 
Around this time, the SCAA invited me to join their team full-time. By then, I had a 
dependable staff at the café and roastery, including a few employees who had been with 
me from the beginning and knew the business inside and out. Stepping away made 
sense both operationally and financially. I became the SCAA’s Education Manager 
in early 2011, overseeing five committees: Barista, Roaster, Green Coffee, Brewing, 
and Coffee Taster. I led the development of classes and curricula, trained instructors 
worldwide and managed the volunteer instructor program for the organization’s three 
major annual events, which drew tens of thousands of participants. In 2012, the 
director and I traveled to Europe to train 30 of the top professionals in the Specialty 
Coffee Association of Europe, sharing the full set of course materials we had created. 
The program was inspired by the Highlander Folk School model, which prioritized 
collaboration and peer learning, and it evolved into a global network supported by 
hundreds of volunteer instructors. 
Our courses became a fast track for learning about the major fields in the coffee 
industry. Back then, coffee industry newcomers faced two paths to hone their craft: 
They could either take classes from seasoned experts (who had once been in their 
shoes), saving time and money, or they could learn through trial and error, risking costly 
mistakes and missing out on a supportive network of peers. Industry professionals 
could collaborate with peers to learn more advanced knowledge.
Meanwhile, our coffee company staff continued to run our business. We issued 49 
percent ownership to the roastery manager, an ingenious, diligent young man who 
had helped me tirelessly from our founding when he was still a university student. He 
became an active partner, and I stepped back further.
This coincided with ongoing political and economic problems in Zimbabwe, and by 
2015, the girls and I were no closer to moving back to Zimbabwe. We were getting 
more rooted in South Carolina. Rob and his family were constantly threatened with 
losing the farm. Yet, they would not leave because almost 1,000 people’s livelihoods 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 33
depended on the farm to stay in business, maintain their jobs, and keep their families 
fed and housed. Zimbabwe had just ditched its hyperinflated currency for the US 
dollar, which, although hopeful, did not make a difference to our situation.
Anyone who is or has been married can understand that ten years of trying to keep 
a marriage healthy under these circumstances was a lost cause. Rob and I decided to 
separate and eventually divorce, but we remained close friends because we respected 
and liked each other. In 2016, I sold the other 51 percent of our business to our 
roastery manager-partner and signed divorce papers. No more coffee company. No 
more husband. And soon no more SCAA. 
In 2016, the SCAA decided to unite with the SCAE to become the Specialty Coffee 
Association (SCA) in 2017. The organizations’ directors wanted to combine the two 
education programs, which had become quite different in their structures and delivery 
systems. Rather than focusing on what learners needed, negotiating what went in or 
what was thrown out of the blended program was more about politics and business 
negotiations. This was not something I was particularly good at. Moreover, it was not 
the kind of work I wanted to do at that point in my life. 
I sought advice from Ricardo, who had merged his company with the Brazilian 
importer Ally Coffee. Ally needed someone like Ricardo, with his skills, knowledge, 
talent and client base, to spearhead a specialty coffee division at its U.S. operation. 
Over the years, he built his division into a major influence, connecting with hundreds 
of people globally and establishing offices in several international locations.
Ricardo helped me take stock of my life while the girls were in high school. I trained 
over 1000 coffee professionals in instruction and instructional design, developed 
five certificate programs with over 50 classes and workshops, and collaborated with 
hundreds of the industry’s top performers. Tens of thousands of coffee industry 
employees and business owners attended SCAA courses, and thousands more were 
trained at their workplaces by instructors who developed programs based on the 
principles taught in SCAA classes. I decided that was a good record on which to end 
my tenure with the SCAA. I wanted to explore new opportunities in the industry and 
discover potential initiatives in the coffee sector. 

34 ♦ Ildi Revi
Ricardo had a solution. He had been helping a new client, Andrew Salisbury, who 
wanted to create a brand of coffee that would be the healthiest coffee on the market. 
He was doing it for his wife, Amber, who was having debilitating issues with her 
energy level. Andrew, an Englishman and exclusively a tea drinker, told Amber that 
she needed to quit coffee to get better. Amber refused, challenging him to find any 
science that indicated she should quit, and if it were compelling, she would. Although 
he set out to prove to her how unhealthy coffee was, he quickly realized he had lost 
the argument as he talked to experts and studied the literature. 
Nonetheless, when he asked professors at Vanderbilt University what the healthiest 
coffee on the market was, they were unable to tell him. The best they could come up 
with at the time was a brand of coffee free from mold and mycotoxins, which Andrew 
thought was a ridiculously low bar for any food company. 
“It’s not rotten or poisonous,” he would say. “That’s just table stakes. Why is that the 
main selling point? Isn’t that what we expect from any food product we buy? Why is 
coffee the exception?”
He posed the question rhetorically to anyone who would listen.
“It should be the minimum standard for every food on the market, especially coffee, 
which contains proven bioactive compounds and numerous health benefits.”
The Vanderbilt professors introduced Andrew to someone they thought could help 
him: Dr. Adriana Farah, from the Department of Nutrition at the Federal University of 
Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. Andrew went to visit her in her laboratory, where she facilitated 
the work of a dozen graduate students. 
Adriana had been at the forefront of coffee research on chlorogenic acids, their 
derivatives and isomers, and other compounds impacting human health for over 
two decades. She was part of the group responsible for reclassifying coffee from 
carcinogenic to anticarcinogenic with the World Health Organization (WHO), 
demonstrating that coffee not only does not cause cancer but can also lower the risk 
of developing it. Adriana agreed to help Andrew create a coffee produced with every 
decision based on health. Together, they discussed every step of coffee production 
and the supply chain, determining the highest level of healthiness at each point and 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 35
what would be needed to produce that high-level coffee. Andrew, an entrepreneur 
who had founded and sold various companies in Latin America, decided to search for 
the perfect coffee for Amber. 
To aid in his quest, he phoned Jon and Missy Butcher, friends who owned several 
businesses and were intrigued by his idea of creating a brand of healthy coffees. They 
agreed to become Andrew’s partners in what they thought was an important gap in the 
massive coffee market. Together they visited a biodynamic, certified organic farm in 
Brazil that seemed straight out of a postcard, with a windmill, waterfall and wildlife. 
It was a tropical paradise. 
Left to right: Missy and Jon Butcher, and Amber and Andrew Salisbury, Purity Coffee founders.
The coffee met all the criteria: it was clean, high-quality, certified organic and 
biodynamic, hand-picked, and carefully sorted. When roasted, it tasted excellent. 
Adriana tested both the green and roasted samples, measuring levels of caffeine, 
trigonelline, and various chlorogenic acids. The results showed strong antioxidant 
content, making it a healthy choice. Most importantly to Andrew, it was a coffee he 
felt confident giving to Amber every morning.

36 ♦ Ildi Revi
When he asked to buy it, the farmer introduced him to Ricardo, who agreed to help. 
Except, the entire lot had already been sold to a West Coast roaster, one of Ricardo’s 
long-time clients. Fortunately, that roaster had just received the shipment and agreed 
to sell Andrew ten sacks, trusting the producer’s assurance that it matched the sample.
Since Andrew didn’t yet have a roastery, Ricardo, who was in Brazil at the time, 
arranged for the coffee to be delivered to his facility. He texted roasting instructions to 
his in-house roaster, who was new to the job. Then he called me and asked if I could 
help. I agreed, not realizing how much that simple yes would shape the rest of my life.
Ricardo introduced me by email to Andrew, who phoned me immediately, and we 
got together the following day. At first, I did not understand what Andrew was trying 
to do. I asked him numerous questions because “mold in coffee” did not make sense. 
After all, wasn’t roasting a kill step? Why would there be mold in roasted coffee? At 
the time, I thought organic coffee seemed nice but unnecessary since it was being 
processed further. I was also certain that chemicals were either washed away, hulled 
away or burned off during roasting. Furthermore, I assumed that none of these 
concerns (mold, contaminants, or chemical residues) would be relevant if the coffee 
were high-quality and carefully processed. Surely, I thought, it had to be healthy if 
it met tasting standards for being clean, sweet, and free of defects, with identifiable 
notes of pleasant flavors and aromas. Andrew quickly challenged that assumption, 
answering every question and objection I raised with clarity and conviction.
He explained that what I had considered the endpoint (clean, well-processed, great-
tasting coffee) was really just the starting line for health. Drawing on a year of deep 
research, he distilled what he had learned into a few transformative hours.
He asked me, “What if we made every single decision about coffee based entirely on 
what’s best for human health? What if we followed the best scientific evidence and 
made every choice with that as our North Star? What if we could improve the world’s 
health through coffee?”
I went home, searched the internet, and started reading. The sky was in the jar! 
Throughout my time roasting, serving, and educating people about coffee, I had 
never considered its health benefits from a nutritional perspective; the idea had never 
occurred to me that coffee had any nutritional value.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 37
The next afternoon, I was scheduled to meet the roaster and assist him with the 
roasting process. We were to let the coffee rest and then pack and ship it to Andrew 
and Amber the following day. Unfortunately, the roaster had a completely different
