idea. He wanted to leave work early and had already roasted and shipped the coffee 
before I arrived. He shoved into my hands an extra bag of the coffee he had saved for 
me, telling me he had to leave within half an hour. I opened the coffee and sighed 
heavily at what I saw. Every roast defect we taught roasters to avoid in our SCAA 
classes. I took a deep breath as I embraced this teachable moment. 
“What is the capacity of your roaster?” 
I asked him.
“15 kilos,” he answered.
“So… how many pounds of green coffee 
did you put in the roaster to start?” I 
continued.
“I put in 31 pounds because they wanted 
25 pounds, and you needed a pound, 
too,” he replied, rolling his eyes, talking 
to me as if I were clueless and unaware 
that coffee loses roughly 16 percent to 
21 percent of its weight during roasting, 
depending on the roast level. 
I asked, “Did you know that with this 
particular machine, you should only 
use 80 percent of its capacity to give the 
coffee room to expand during the roast? 
Your charge weight of 12 kilos of green coffee would yield around 21 pounds of roasted 
coffee, which means you needed to do two batches.”
“Yeah, Ricardo told me that, but I wanted to get out of here, so I did it all in one 
roast,” he snapped. I pulled out a handful of the beans and laid them on the packing 
table next to us. 
Roasted coffee defects: Facing, 
tipping and scorching.

38 ♦ Ildi Revi
“These are all what we call ‘faced’; they are burnt on the flat side,” I said, pointing to 
several. “And these beans are scorched, tipped, and the whole roast is mottled, which 
means some beans got more heat than others in the drum.”
“These customers won’t notice. They don’t know anything about coffee,” he said 
dismissively as he walked out the door. 
It should not come as a surprise that he did not last long at that job. 
After the roaster left, I went to inspect the open sack of green coffee, since neither 
Ricardo nor Andrew had seen it firsthand. I was stunned by the number and variety 
of defects. It looked like a checklist of every flaw described in the green coffee grading 
guides. The beans were riddled with fungus damage, black and sour defects, and even 
patches of blue-green mold growing in insect-bitten crevices. Whatever this was, it 
wasn’t clean, and it certainly wasn’t healthy. Hoping it was an isolated issue, I opened 
a second sack, only to find more of the same. I grabbed my phone and began taking 
photos, even recording a video to document what we were dealing with. This began 
a long saga that ended with the producer air-freighting us ten sacks of high-quality 
coffee four months later and negotiating a settlement with the West Coast roaster.
The defective green coffee that was sent for Purity’s first roasting tests.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 39
I texted Andrew to call me when he received the box the roaster sent, but I chose to 
wait to tell him anything about the situation. He finally called me, but he hadn’t seen 
my text, so he and Amber opened the coffee immediately, excited to taste their first 
roast, the reward for months of research and effort.
But when they tasted it, they found it bitter and unpleasant. Despite several attempts, 
the coffee tasted bad, making them feel sick, disappointed and dejected. Andrew 
explained how he had meticulously brewed the coffee to emphasize that his brewing 
skills were not at fault. They questioned the viability of the project, if that is what 
healthy coffee tasted like. Andrew was ready to give up. 
Then I told him what had happened. I was sure we could find a different green coffee 
since the one in those ten sacks was all wrong. He was discouraged at the need to 
start over, but also driven by irritation that both the farmer and the roaster had taken 
advantage of his newcomer status in the coffee industry. 
He agreed to try again if I agreed to continue helping him. In January 2016, he and 
Jon Butcher offered me a full-time job. I submitted my notice to the SCAA, and in 
February, I delivered one final workshop to European coffee instructors in Budapest.
I got to work immediately, sourcing new coffees, sending samples to the lab for testing, 
collaborating with Jon Butcher on packaging design, and helping define standards for 
both the product and the brand. By July 2016, we produced our first batch using an 
organic, high-quality Costa Rican coffee that met our health-focused criteria. As the 
coffee was dwindling, I called Steve Franklin, who also had certified organic coffee 
from Selva Negra, which we still use today.
A few months later, I also went to work for Ricardo at Ally Coffee, where they needed 
an education director for staff and clients. Ally allowed me to do consulting work for 
Andrew while doing full-time performance and learning programs for Ally. By 2018, 
I was working full-time for both Ally and Purity Coffee, but it became too much, and 
in 2022, I resigned from Ally Coffee and worked exclusively for Purity. 
I have gone from science teacher to coffee professional to instructional designer to 
coffee science researcher. At this point in the story, my journey with coffee has led me 
to explore the new field of coffee for health, produced with environmentally sound 

40 ♦ Ildi Revi
practices. I breezed through this most recent part of my story because this book 
follows my journey to understanding coffee for health purposes, which is grown with 
environmental best practices. This is a category I would like to see as part of the rising 
food as medicine movement.
As we move from my personal story to the scientific exploration of coffee’s health 
impacts, the tone of this book naturally evolves to mirror the depth and complexity 
of the subject matter. This transition reflects my commitment to providing a well-
rounded understanding, drawing from experiential insights and strong scientific 
evidence. The following chapters explore how coffee affects our health, backed by 
solid research and evidence. 
I aim to present this information clearly and accurately, as it empowers us all to make 
informed choices about our health. While the language may become more formal, 
reflecting the nature of scientific inquiry, it serves as a bridge to understanding the 
fascinating ways coffee can influence our well-being. I invite you to join me as we 
uncover these insights, hoping they inspire and inform your relationship with coffee.

3
Research-Based Insights on 
Coffee and Health Conditions
At the beginning of each chapter, you’ll find key highlights that summarize the main 
points, providing a quick overview of what’s to come. These highlights serve as a 
roadmap, preparing you for the detailed scientific evidence and analysis that follow in 
the chapter. This is to give you a heads-up on what’s ahead, making the more detailed 
information easier to follow and understand. 
At the end of each section of the chapter, I put the key points of the section. You can 
gain an overview of coffee and health by reading the introductory highlights, and the 
section summaries provide details of specific sections. I hope this helps in absorbing 
the information.
This chapter aims to dive deep into major health concerns on which coffee has 
demonstrated an impact. I cover the major ones here, but there are dozens of other 
issues related to coffee and health that scientists are actively studying and have covered 
in several textbooks. I have listed them in the references. 

42 ♦ Ildi Revi
HIGHLIGHTS
 
Liver Health: Coffee consumption has been shown to mitigate liver fibrosis by inhibiting fibrotic 
cytokines and hepatic stellate cells. Higher coffee intake is linked to a lower prevalence of liver 
cirrhosis from various causes, with chlorogenic acids being a key focus. Additionally, research 
indicates an inverse relationship between coffee intake and hepatocellular carcinoma, especially 
in individuals with pre-existing liver conditions.
Type 2 Diabetes: Coffee consumption is associated with a lower risk of type 2 diabetes, partly 
due to its ability to increase insulin sensitivity alongside the protective properties of its bioactive 
compounds like caffeine, trigonelline, chlorogenic acids, and diterpenes.
Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases: Coffee’s antioxidative and anti-inflammatory properties 
may lower the risk of Alzheimer’s (AD) and Parkinson’s (PD). Polyphenols and caffeine contribute 
to this protective effect by reducing beta-amyloid deposition and tau aggregation.
Heart Health: Coffee has been shown to reduce the risk of cardiovascular diseases. Moderate 
coffee consumption has been linked to lower risks of stroke, coronary heart disease, and heart 
failure. However, individuals with a family history of heart disease should be cautious with high 
consumption levels.
Mast Cell Activation Syndrome (MCAS) While most histamine in low-quality coffee degrades 
during roasting, very poor-quality beans may still contain enough histamine to affect sensitive 
individuals. 
Gastrointestinal (GI) Issues: Because coffee stimulates gastric secretion, this may disturb those 
with gastric issues. Research on the cause of this is ongoing.
Cancer: Beyond liver cancer, coffee shows preventative benefits against aggressive prostate cancer, 
colorectal cancer, oral and oropharyngeal cancer, and breast cancer.
Longevity: Coffee consumption has been linked to longer telomeres, which protect DNA from 
age-related degradation. Studies suggest a positive correlation between coffee intake and a decrease 
in all-cause mortality.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 43
The following compounds are covered in more detail in the next chapter, 
but this reference chart is provided here for your convenience.
COFFEE HEALTH COMPOUND CORE INFORMATION
Compound
What it is
What it Does
Caffeine
Caffeine is a natural pharmacological 
compound in coffee and certain other 
plants. This crystalline xanthine alkaloid 
primarily works by blocking receptors 
of the neurotransmitter adenosine, 
which promotes sleep and relaxation. 
Caffeine is recognized as both a 
psychotropic, affecting the mind, mood, 
or behavior, and a nootropic due to 
its cognitive-enhancing properties.
Caffeine is widely consumed globally 
in various beverages, foods, and 
medications. It functions as a central 
nervous system stimulant, temporarily 
alleviating drowsiness and boosting 
alertness. It also promotes the release 
of dopamine and norepinephrine, 
which enhances its stimulating effects.
CGA
CGA are formed by trans-hydroxy-
cinnamic and quinic acids. They represent 
a family of phenolic compounds usually 
abundant in Arabica and Robusta 
coffees. CGA are bioactive with several 
significant physiological effects. 
They exhibit antioxidant activity, 
modulate glucose metabolism, and have 
anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective, 
antiviral, and neuroprotective effects, 
as well as vasodilatory properties. 
Melanoidins
Melanoidins are complex polymers 
formed during coffee roasting via the 
Maillard reaction. They contribute 
to coffee’s color, flavor, and body 
and exhibit potential health benefits 
due to antioxidant properties.
Melanoidins exhibit antioxidant, 
prebiotic, antimicrobial, anti-
inflammatory and potential 
anticarcinogenic properties. They 
act as soluble fiber, modulating gut 
microbiota and reducing inflammation. 
Diterpenes 
Diterpenes, mainly cafestol and 
kahweol (C&K), are bioactive lipids in 
the oil fraction of coffee. They exhibit 
diverse biological activities, including 
potential anticancer and cholesterol-
modulating effects, which contribute 
to coffee’s health implications.
Cafestol and kahweol stimulate 
antioxidant defenses, inhibit 
carcinogen-activating enzymes, and 
promote hepatic detoxification. 
These mechanisms contribute 
to their anticarcinogenic and 
hepatoprotective [liver protective] 
effects, particularly against aflatoxin-
induced carcinogenesis. Can raise 
serum total and LDL cholesterol levels.
Trigonelline
Trigonelline is a water-soluble alkaloid 
in coffee that contributes to its 
flavor and exhibits potential health 
benefits. During roasting, it partially 
degrades, forming niacin and aromatic 
compounds that influence the sensory 
characteristics of coffee. Trigonelline is 
also a product of niacin metabolism.
Trigonelline demonstrates antidiabetic, 
antimicrobial, and antioxidant 
activities. Its neuroprotective effects 
suggest potential applications in 
preventing neurodegenerative diseases 
and alleviating diabetic neuropathies.

44 ♦ Ildi Revi
Please keep these points in mind:
Observational studies often lack specific details about the coffee used. Since coffee is 
an agricultural product with numerous variables, any coffee used in a study represents 
only a snapshot in time and cannot be exactly replicated. By the time studies are 
published, the specific coffee used is no longer available. Still, these studies are reliable 
and meaningful because they provide valuable insights into general trends and effects of 
coffee consumption, which can be applied broadly despite the variability in individual 
coffee batches.
Scientific research uses two main methods: In vitro and in vivo studies. In vitro studies 
are conducted in laboratories with cells or biological molecules, whereas in vivo studies 
involve live organisms, such as mice and rats, to observe overall biological effects. In 
vitro studies provide valuable insights into mechanisms and potential benefits, but 
understanding true human health impacts requires more complex in vivo studies. 
Human physiology is complex, and effects seen in animal models may not always 
translate directly to humans.
Given these complexities, researchers tend to use cautious language, stating that a 
compound “has been shown to have an effect,” which typically references findings 
from in vitro or animal studies.

45
3.1 Liver
This chapter begins with a focus on the relationship between coffee and liver health 
conditions, as there is an extensive body of literature over the decades regarding coffee’s 
impact on the liver. This is also where I envision CHC having the biggest impact on 
the world’s health. 
Globally, liver diseases are a major health concern, with approximately two million 
deaths annually attributed to liver-related conditions, including cirrhosis and liver 
cancer. Chronic liver diseases affect hundreds of millions of people worldwide (He 
2013), with an estimated 1.5 billion cases of chronic liver disease, largely due to 
hepatitis B and C infections, alcohol abuse, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease 
(NAFLD). According to estimates from the World Health Organization (WHO), 
viral hepatitis (B and C) causes approximately 1.34 million deaths each year. This 
figure is comparable to the number of deaths caused by tuberculosis and exceeds the 
number of deaths caused by HIV. In the United States alone, liver-related healthcare 
expenditures totaled $32.5 billion in 2016 (Devarbhavi 2023, WHO 2024).
Coffee offers more than just caffeine. Its many components may help to reduce the 
risk of liver disease. Collaborating on coffee research can reveal how it supports liver 
health and explore ways to enhance its benefits naturally. 
Recent studies have deepened our understanding of the impact of coffee on liver 
health. They consider factors like lifestyle and smoking, which influence both coffee 
consumption and liver health. This leads to a clearer picture of how coffee reduces 
liver fibrosis, unlike earlier research that often overlooked these variables.
Newer studies include animal tests and observational research. The peer-review process 
adds credibility to these findings. A 2010 review of over 500 papers revealed strong 

46 ♦ Ildi Revi
links between coffee and various cancers. Researchers found a clear protective effect 
of coffee against liver and uterine cancers (Arab 2010). In 2013, Saab et al. reviewed 
12 key studies involving over 93,000 subjects from 1993 to 2010. They consistently 
demonstrated that coffee benefits liver health.
Coffee’s diverse bioactive compounds, each with its unique role, work in tandem to 
provide multifaceted hepatoprotective effects. This makes coffee a key player in the 
campaign against chronic liver diseases.
COFFEE’S IMPACT ON LIVER FIBROSIS: A 
REVIEW OF CURRENT LITERATURE
The expanding body of evidence suggests that coffee plays a crucial role in promoting 
liver health by reducing the risk and impact of liver fibrosis. Liver fibrosis, a condition 
characterized by the accumulation of scar tissue in the liver due to chronic injury or 
inflammation, poses significant health risks. 
First, let us discuss the extracellular matrix (ECM) in the human body. The ECM is a 
complex network of proteins, including collagen and elastin. It provides structural and 
biochemical support to surrounding cells. Maintaining tissue integrity and facilitating 
cellular functions are central roles of the ECM, especially in the liver. Excessive ECM 
production can lead to fibrosis, replacing normal tissue with scar tissue and impairing 
liver function. Chronic liver diseases, such as hepatitis B or C, alcohol abuse, and 
cholestasis can cause fibrosis. Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), linked to obesity 
and diabetes, can also lead to fibrosis.
Coffee acts as a strong protective agent against liver fibrosis through various 
mechanisms. It interferes with tissue repair processes and inhibits the overproduction 
of fibrotic cytokines and hepatic stellate cells (HSCs). Activated by liver injury or 
inflammation, HSCs produce extracellular matrix (ECM), contributing to the 
development of fibrosis. While caffeine is often associated with coffee’s effects, other 
components, such as CGA and polyphenols, are essential for its liver-protective 
properties.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 47
Research by Freedman et al. (2009) highlights the importance of coffee for patients 
with advanced fibrosis. The study showed that those drinking at least three cups 
daily had a much lower risk of liver complications or cancer when compared to non-
drinkers. Coffee’s protective effects stem from its antioxidant properties and its ability 
to suppress fibrogenic cytokines, such as TGF-β and PDGF-β. CGA and polyphenols 
are key compounds in these effects.
Studies by Shin et al. (2010), Modi et al. (2010), and Shi et al. (2009) demonstrate 
coffee’s potential in protecting liver health and limiting the progression of fibrosis. 
Before severe symptoms appear, coffee drinkers often show reduced liver stiffness, 
indicating the early stages of fibrosis. This suggests coffee may help prevent disease 
progression. These findings suggest that incorporating coffee into one’s diet may be 
a simple yet effective way to impact liver health positively.
In 2015, Shen et al. conducted a pivotal meta-analysis that included four cross-
sectional and two case-control studies, involving over 20,000 participants. This 
research demonstrates that regular coffee consumption significantly reduces the rates 
and severity of hepatic fibrosis in patients with liver disease. These findings suggest 
that coffee may help reduce liver fibrosis, regardless of its caffeine content. This 
groundbreaking research has significant implications for the treatment of chronic 
hepatitis C. 
The slow progression from liver injury to cirrhosis presents a major health challenge. 
With rising cases of fibrosis and cirrhosis from fatty liver disease and hepatitis C, 
effective interventions are urgently needed. Coffee offers a simple and cost-effective 
way to slow this process. Furthermore, the emphasis is on choosing high-quality, 
organic coffee that best supports liver health.
A COMPREHENSIVE LOOK AT MAFLD/MASLD (FORMERLY NAFLD) 
In late 2023, I was diagnosed with early-stage steatosis and decided I needed to get 
serious about my health. Steatosis is characterized by the excessive accumulation of 
fat in the liver. It might have started when I had COVID-19 in July 2020 and spent 
eight days in the hospital, complete with Remdesivir and steroids. Even worse was 
losing my sense of taste and smell for a month. I thought my career as a coffee taster 

48 ♦ Ildi Revi
was over, but thankfully, my organoleptics recovered. Life got a bit messy in the winter 
of 2021–22, when both of my parents passed away within a short time, following a 
period of being closely involved in their final chapter. It was hard to separate the weight 
of grief from the brain fog of long COVID. By 2023, I was gaining loads of weight 
and feeling progressively worse, but I resolved at New Year 2024 to sort myself out.
Fatefully, that was also when I began to write this book, sitting for long stretches of 
time at my computer. My dog became my unofficial wellness coach, clearly knowing 
that no one should be sitting still for that long and insisting we go out for walks every 
couple of hours. More to the point, I increased my coffee intake from one or two cups 
to four, sometimes six, as I pored over the research and wrote all this material. The 
coffees were from the stock I’m continually evaluating of our different roasts that pile 
up in my kitchen. Ironically, while I was researching all the ways coffee might support 
liver health, my own liver markers improved. In six months, my ALP dropped from 
62 to 54, and my ALT from 17 to 14. I’m not saying coffee was the magic fix, since I 
was also doing intermittent fasting, cutting carbs, and doing all the things, but I know 
the coffee helped. And my dog helped, too.
Interestingly, a customer review came in from a woman who shared that over the 
course of a year, her liver enzymes improved significantly (AST from 114 to 29, ALT 
from 62 to 14) while drinking coffee regularly. Of course, I’m not making claims, 
because many factors can influence liver enzyme changes, and individual experiences 
vary greatly. But she and I mirrored the research.
Back to the science.
For a long time, steatosis has been known as Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease 
(NAFLD), which is linked to various metabolic problems like obesity, type II diabetes, 
dyslipidemia, and metabolic syndrome. If left unchecked, NAFLD can lead to severe 
liver problems, including nonalcoholic steatohepatitis, advanced fibrosis, cirrhosis, 
and hepatocellular carcinoma. It’s the most common liver disease worldwide, affecting 
nearly a billion people.
In 2019, an international panel suggested new terms for this condition. They proposed 
Metabolic-Associated Fatty Liver Disease, or MAFLD. This change aims to state the 
underlying cause of fatty liver disease more clearly. The focus is on metabolic issues 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 49
central to the disease and its associated dysfunction. To diagnose MAFLD, you need 
to find evidence of hepatic steatosis along with at least one of the following: being 
overweight or obese, having type 2 diabetes, or experiencing metabolic dysregulation. 
In some cases, liver transplantation becomes the only treatment option (Chalasani 
2012).
MASLD, or Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease, is a specific 
type of MAFLD. It highlights fat accumulation in the liver, known as steatosis, 
alongside metabolic issues. The causes of MASLD are unknown, but obesity, diabetes, 
and metabolic syndrome significantly raise the risk. MASLD has two main forms:
1.	 Steatotic Liver Disease: This form involves fat storage without the 
enlargement of liver cells.
2.	 Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatohepatitis (MASH): Formerly 
known as nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), this occurs when liver cells 
enlarge.
Grasping the differences between MAFLD and MASLD helps deepen our 
understanding of liver health. Both focus on metabolic aspects of fatty liver disease, 
but MASLD emphasizes metabolic risk factors, potentially identifying more patients 
with the condition. This includes individuals who are lean but have metabolic issues. 
This new term is more effective in identifying patients at a higher risk of developing 
liver fibrosis and disease progression than the old NAFLD criteria. With that said, I 
will use the MASLED, MAFLD and NAFLD interchangeably because literature prior 
to 2020 refers to it as NAFLD. Apologies to hepatologists reading this.
As previously mentioned, the meta-analysis conducted by Shen highlights that regular 
coffee intake can reduce the severity of fibrosis and aid in liver tissue repair. Caffeine 
was particularly effective in reducing the risk of fibrosis in patients with NAFLD. 
Regular coffee drinkers are less likely to experience severe fibrosis, and researchers 
strongly recommend that NAFLD patients include coffee in their daily diet plan due 
to its potential benefits.
Because MAFLD is so prevalent, scientists have turned their attention to the 
epidemiological and clinical evidence of the past 20 years, which has indicated 

50 ♦ Ildi Revi
coffee’s potential hepatoprotective effects, including antioxidative, anti-inflammatory, 
and antifibrotic properties. A 2013 study conducted a systematic review of the 
epidemiology, magnitude, and mechanisms of possible beneficial effects of coffee 
consumption in patients with NAFLD using 12 detailed prior studies:
•	
The National Health and Nutrition Examination Surveys (NHANES) from 
2001 to 2008 were used in this study. It looked at how diet affects patients 
with NAFLD. The research considered diet, nutrition, demographics, and 
other factors. The results revealed a strong link between coffee consumption 
and a reduced risk of developing NAFLD (Birerdinc 2012).
•	
An Italian study with 137 NAFLD patients and 108 controls looked at 
coffee intake by counting the cups consumed. Researchers explored how 
coffee affects fatty liver disease severity. They found that coffee drinkers 
had less severe fatty liver compared to non-drinkers. This was assessed 
using ultrasound and a measure called the “Bright Liver Score.” The Bright 
Liver Score (BLS) detects a bright liver echo pattern on ultrasound, which 
indicates hepatic steatosis. It accurately identifies moderate to severe fatty 
liver disease.
•	
In a 2012 study,  Molloy et al.  looked at  liver biopsies and used 
questionnaires to measure caffeine intake. They found a strong link between 
higher caffeine consumption and lower liver fibrosis levels. This inverse 
relationship implies that caffeine might reduce the occurrence and severity 
of MAFLD.
•	
A 2024 study explored the relationship between coffee consumption and 
metabolic syndrome. Using data from the Korea National Health and 
Nutrition Examination Survey (2016-2021), which involved 14,631 adults 
aged 19-64 years, researchers employed a 24-hour dietary recall method 
to analyze coffee consumption patterns, distinguishing between black 
coffee and coffee with sugar and/or cream. The findings revealed that for 
women, consuming 2-3 cups of black coffee daily was inversely associated 
with the development of metabolic syndrome. This indicates that it may 
help reduce the risk or occurrence of metabolic syndrome by positively 
influencing factors such as HDL cholesterol levels. However, no significant 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 51
association was found for men who consume coffee with additives or in 
higher quantities (Choi 2024). 
In 2020, Alessio Calabrò and his team reviewed how coffee affects NAFLD and 
MAFLD. They found many studies, focusing on seven key ones since 2014. These 
studies demonstrate that coffee helps protect the liver in patients with NAFLD, thanks 
to its antioxidant and caffeine content. They also improve insulin resistance and reduce 
the production of inflammatory cytokines. There is some debate about the types and 
amounts of coffee, but researchers recommend consuming two to three cups per day 
for patients with MAFLD. Calabrò’s team synthesized insights from many papers. 
They highlighted coffee’s role in preventing liver steatosis, the progression of fibrosis, 
and overall liver damage. The antioxidant activity in coffee is quite potent. CGA also 
impacts insulin resistance positively and reduces inflammatory cytokines.
Additional research indicates that coffee significantly reduces the risk of fibrosis in 
MASH patients. MASH is the most severe form of MAFLD, linked to obesity and 
diabetes. Effective interventions are urgently needed (Kopzhassaruly 2020). The results 
revealed a clear pattern. Patients who drank two to four cups of coffee daily had better 
outcomes than those who drank only one cup.
Researchers noted inconsistencies in data about coffee types and dosages. Despite 
this, they strongly recommend two to three cups of coffee daily for MAFLD patients. 
Regular coffee consumption may support liver health in MASH individuals. This 
finding highlights the potential benefits of a simple daily cup of coffee for liver health.
CIRRHOSIS
Cirrhosis is a severe liver disease. Healthy liver tissue can transform into scar tissue, 
resulting in permanent damage and impaired liver function. Many liver diseases lead 
to cell death and inflammation. Conditions like alcohol abuse, NAFLD/MAFLD, 
and hepatitis B or C contribute to cirrhosis. Autoimmune diseases and other factors 
can also cause them. While cirrhosis is not curable, it is treatable. Doctors aim to stop 
further liver damage and prevent complications.
For decades, researchers have linked liver health to coffee consumption. Data from 
1978 to 1985 supported the idea that coffee protects the liver from cirrhosis (Klatsky 

52 ♦ Ildi Revi
2006). The research suggested that coffee contains ingredients that protect the liver 
against cirrhosis, especially alcoholic cirrhosis. A 10-year study of 128,934 patients 
hospitalized with cirrhosis, conducted by Klatsky et al. (1992), found that coffee intake 
was associated with a lower prevalence of cirrhosis. Furthermore, the study indicated 
that coffee consumption was associated with a reduced risk of death from cirrhosis.
A study conducted ten years later, involving over 700 people, discovered a strong 
link between daily coffee drinking and a lower risk of liver cirrhosis. The more coffee 
consumed, the lower the risk. Non-coffee drinkers had an odds ratio of 1.0. Those 
drinking one cup daily had an odds ratio of 0.47. For two cups, it was 0.23; for three 
cups, 0.21; and four cups, 0.16. In summary, coffee drinkers had a much lower risk: 
53 percent less for one cup, 77 percent less for two cups, 79 percent less for three cups, 
and 84 percent less for four cups (Kennedy 2016). This study built on earlier findings, 
reinforcing coffee’s protective effects against liver cirrhosis.
An odds ratio (OR) measures the link between an exposure and an outcome. It shows 
the odds of an outcome occurring with an exposure compared to without it (CDC 
2020). When OR = 1, exposure does not affect the odds. If OR>1, exposure increases 
the odds. If OR<1, exposure decreases the odds.
In a study on coffee and liver cirrhosis, researchers investigated whether caffeine alone 
was responsible for the protective effects (Corrao 2001). They found a clear link 
between coffee and a lower risk of liver cirrhosis, both alcoholic and nonalcoholic. 
By comparing other caffeinated drinks, they found caffeine alone didn’t significantly 
reduce the risk. This indicates that coffee has unique components that protect the 
liver, unlike energy drinks or synthetic caffeine. The presence of polyphenols and 
melanoidins in coffee contributes to liver protection and overall health.
In 2002, another study involving 101 patients with cirrhosis and over 1,500 controls 
supported these findings. Coffee drinkers had a much lower odds ratio for cirrhosis 
(0.54) compared to non-drinkers (Gallus 2002, Heath 2017). This trend was stronger 
for those drinking more than three cups daily, with an odds ratio of 0.29. The study 
agreed that other caffeinated drinks didn’t show the same protective effect. In later 
years, Tverdal (2003) found additional evidence that coffee consumption is beneficial, 
with lower death rates observed among individuals who drank three or more cups daily.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 53
The positive trend continued with a 2002 study in the Annals of Epidemiology. It 
showed a 71 percent lower risk for those drinking more than three cups daily. Focusing 
on chronic hepatitis C patients, Freedman’s 2009 study found that three or more cups 
daily reduced liver disease progression risk by up to 53 percent. This effect wasn’t seen 
with black or green tea. These findings strongly support coffee’s role in preventing and 
reducing the risk of liver cirrhosis.
Interestingly, this link was seen not just in alcoholic cirrhosis but also in cirrhosis from 
various causes, showing coffee’s broad protective effect. A threshold effect appeared, 
and no further drop in death rates occurred when coffee intake exceeded three to 
four cups. An increase of two cups daily was associated with a 44 percent reduction 
in the risk of cirrhosis, with a pooled relative risk of 0.56 (Kennedy 2016). Kennedy 
et al. (2016) found that drinking two additional cups daily nearly reduces the risk of 
cirrhosis by half. This threshold effect suggests that drinking coffee up to three to four 
cups daily may offer additional benefits; beyond that, there might not be any further 
protective effects against cirrhosis.
LIVER CANCER
For decades, scientists have explored the link between coffee consumption and liver 
cancer prevention, particularly hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). This severe liver 
cancer is often linked to cirrhosis or hepatitis infection and accounts for about 90 
percent of primary liver cancers. In the 1990s, Tanaka et al. conducted animal studies 
showing that CGA can inhibit chemical carcinogenesis in liver tissue.
As research progressed, studies found a clear inverse relationship between coffee 
consumption and liver cancer in people with a history of liver disease. The protective 
effect was stronger in those with existing liver conditions. Generally, coffee 
consumption was linked to a lower incidence of liver cancer (Shimazu 2005).
Large-scale epidemiologic studies have backed Shimazu’s findings. A ten-year study 
(Inoue 2005) involving 90,000 Japanese subjects, published in the Journal of the 
National Cancer Institute in 2005, showed that coffee drinkers had a 50 percent lower 
risk of developing liver cancer compared to non-drinkers. Moderate consumption of 
one to two cups daily made a difference, with greater risk reduction seen at three to 

54 ♦ Ildi Revi
four cups. Although due to the rarity of decaf coffee in Japan, the study struggled to 
separate the effects of caffeinated and decaffeinated coffee on HCC risk (Inoue 2005).
Exploring the relationship between coffee consumption and HCC has yielded 
consistent and compelling findings across various meta-analyses and large-scale studies.
•	
A landmark 2007 meta-analysis proved coffee’s protective effect against 
HCC. This study pooled data from thousands of cases across Europe and 
Japan, involving about 240,000 individuals. The conclusive result indicated 
a 41 percent reduction in the risk of HCC among regular coffee drinkers 
compared to non-drinkers. This reduction persisted across diverse study 
designs, geographical locations, and potential biases. Importantly, both 
men and women who regularly consume coffee exhibited a significantly 
diminished risk of developing liver cancer and related diseases (Bravi 2007).
•	
In the same year, another comprehensive review incorporating hundreds 
of thousands of cases corroborated the inverse relationship between coffee 
consumption and liver cancer risk. Across six studies, the association reached 
statistical significance. The results were impressive–an increase of two cups 
of coffee per day was linked to a 43 percent reduction in the risk of liver 
cancer, and each additional cup of coffee beyond that was linked to a further 
23 percent decrease in risk (Larsson 2007).
•	
Subsequent large-scale studies reinforced these observations, revealing 
important nuances. For instance, in patients with chronic hepatitis B, the 
protective effect of coffee was less pronounced due to the strong influence 
of viral replication on cancer development (Jang 2013).
•	
Similarly, Montella et al. (2007) found that abstaining from coffee was 
associated with a doubled risk for HCC compared to moderate drinkers 
(consuming less than 14 cups per week). It is worth noting that the protective 
effects were not observed with tea or decaffeinated coffee. 
These findings strongly suggest that coffee may lower the risk of liver cancer and are 
consistent across various chronic liver diseases. Lifetime coffee consumption acts as an 
independent protective factor against HCC. A 2020 meta-analysis by Bhurwal et al. 
supports this, concluding that drinking coffee is associated with a lower risk of liver 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 55
cancer. The analysis also found a dose-dependent relationship linking higher coffee 
intake to greater risk reduction benefits (Bhurwal 2020). 
The protective effect against HCC may be due to two diterpenes in coffee: cafestol 
and kahweol. These compounds reduce the harmful effects of carcinogens in liver 
cells through multiple mechanisms. For example, aflatoxin, a group of carcinogenic 
mycotoxins, can cause liver damage and increase cancer risk. One study used human 
liver cells engineered to mimic aflatoxin activation by P450 enzymes. Treatment with 
cafestol and kahweol reduced the binding of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) to DNA, potentially 
lowering the risk of DNA damage and cancer.
Cafestol and kahweol also increased levels of an enzyme called GST-mu, which 
detoxifies AFB1, making it less harmful. Additionally, they inhibited an enzyme that 
converts AFB1 into a more toxic form. These actions suggest that cafestol and kahweol 
play crucial roles in coffee’s cancer-preventive effects, particularly in the context of 
aflatoxin-induced liver cancer.
Liver Recap
Several large-scale studies point to coffee’s significant protective effects on liver health, highlighting 
its role in reducing the risks of liver fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. Research 
shows that coffee’s bioactive compounds, such as chlorogenic acids, contribute to these benefits, not 
just caffeine. Evidence supports habitual coffee consumption’s role in mitigating the progression of 
liver diseases like nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) and metabolic-associated fatty liver disease 
(MAFLD). Numerous studies and meta-analyses consistently confirm these hepatoprotective 
effects, suggesting coffee is a valuable ally in liver health.

56
3.2 Type 2 Diabetes
According to the WHO, approximately 537 million adults worldwide are living with 
diabetes, with type 2 diabetes accounting for about 90 percent of the population and is 
projected to rise to 783 million by 2045. In 2021 alone, diabetes caused approximately 
6.7 million deaths globally, equivalent to one death every five seconds. The economic 
burden is substantial, with global costs exceeding USD 966 billion annually, a 316 
percent increase over the last 15 years (International Diabetes Federation 2024). 
Southeast Asia, including countries like India and China, has the highest number of 
cases, with over 70 million people affected. (WHO 2024).
Type 2 diabetes is the most common diabetes type–a result of little insulin production 
and the body’s poor response to insulin. Many studies show that coffee drinkers 
are less likely to develop this disease. Insulin, produced by the pancreas’s beta cells, 
helps regulate blood glucose levels. In prediabetes, insulin’s effectiveness declines. It 
struggles to move glucose from the blood into cells for energy, a condition known as 
insulin resistance, which typically occurs in muscle tissue (Luc 2019). Insulin’s ability 
to suppress glucose production in the liver and signal fat breakdown (lipolysis) for 
energy is also diminished by insulin resistance (Scheithauer 2020). 
Early in prediabetes, the pancreas compensates by producing more insulin 
(hyperinsulinemia). This creates a chronic pro-inflammatory state (Scheithauer 
2020). If insulin sensitivity doesn’t improve, insulin resistance leads to beta-cell 
dysfunction and type 2 diabetes, along with severe complications and comorbidities 
(Luc 2019). Thus, type 2 diabetes can be seen as relative insulin insufficiency, even with 
hyperinsulinemia. Beta cells can’t meet the high glucose demand. Testing is essential, 
but addressing obesity, chronic inflammation, and other factors that contribute to 
insulin resistance is crucial in preventive healthcare. We must also consider daily food 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 57
and drink consumption. Food can be medicine, unless it isn’t. This has sparked interest 
in functional foods like coffee. Such foods may bridge the gap between nutritional 
science and pharmacology, offering everyday solutions to major lifestyle-related 
diseases.
COFFEE’S ROLE IN THE FIGHT AGAINST DIABETES
A dose-response meta-analysis and systematic review on coffee consumption and type 
2 diabetes was published in 2014. The study found that the risk of type 2 diabetes 
decreased as coffee consumption increased. This study analyzed data from 109,272 
participants, concluding that drinking six cups of coffee daily was associated with a 
33 percent reduction in the risk of type 2 diabetes (Nieber 2017). Studies have also 
identified a 9 percent lower risk of metabolic syndrome among high coffee drinkers 
compared to low coffee drinkers (Poole 2017).
Using three large prospective studies in the United States, researchers analyzed how 
changes in coffee and tea intake affect the risk of diabetes. Individuals who increased 
their coffee consumption by one cup per day (compared to those who made no 
change) lowered their relative risk of type 2 diabetes by 11 percent. Decreasing coffee 
consumption by one to two cups per day was associated with a 17 percent increased 
relative risk of type 2 diabetes. It is worth noting that no relationship was found 
between diabetes and changes in tea intake when analyzing individuals who reported 
changes in their tea intake (Kolb 2021).
No discussion of type 2 diabetes is complete without addressing its main risk factor: 
obesity. Animal studies and in vitro research show anti-obesity effects. These include 
inhibiting fat cell growth, reducing fat production, and promoting the development 
of healthy gut bacteria. Key coffee compounds for weight management are CGA, 
trigonelline, and magnesium (Lee 2019).
Some human studies suggest coffee can reduce fat stores in obese individuals, 
decrease body weight, and improve metabolic health (Sirotkin 2021). Additionally, 
epidemiological research shows an inverse relationship between coffee intake and 
several health markers. As coffee consumption increases, visceral obesity, insulin 

58 ♦ Ildi Revi
resistance, body fat percentage, metabolic syndrome, and BMI tend to decrease 
(Ohishi 2021).
Obesity is a loaded word for me, and not in a motivational way. In 2024, when I 
committed to taking better care of myself, four to six cups daily of our coffees (from 
my company quality-control stock—various roasts that I receive weekly for analysis) 
helped me get through intermittent fasting and carb-cutting. I’m convinced it was 
all of it working together: the fasting, the dietary changes, my four-legged coach who 
doggedly made sure I moved, and the coffee as a real part of my recovery. The shift 
was genuine, and I finally began losing weight. I’ve lost about 60 pounds, my energy 
has improved, the brain fog is gone, and my fasting glucose is now in a healthy range. 
I still have progress to make, but my mindset has changed. Instead of feeling like I’m 
battling myself or being mad at my body, I now have a sense of awe, gratitude for the 
way it keeps trying, recalibrating, healing, and doing everything it can to keep me alive.
Also, we had a customer review where a woman told us that while other black coffees 
caused her blood sugar to spike, our coffee didn’t. Each person’s body is unique, and 
these observations aren’t medical claims, but it’s encouraging to me and my coworkers 
when people improve their health and include our coffee as part of the journey.
Despite the overall positive link between coffee intake and diabetes prevention, not 
all coffee is alike, which is why CHC focuses on organic and regeneratively farmed 
coffee. Emerging research suggests chronic exposure to crop chemicals, including 
glyphosate and organophosphate insecticides such as chlorpyrifos, may disrupt 
metabolic function by impairing fat thermogenesis, promoting weight gain, and 
blunting glucose regulation (Otaru 2024, Pérez-Bermejo 2024, Wang 2021, Lam 
2023).  These findings imply that coffee grown without synthetic pesticide residues, 
such as certified organic, might offer advantages for metabolic health. While more 
clinical research is needed, choosing organically produced coffee could reduce one 
potential source of metabolic strain.
The growing conditions and preparation methods affect its chemical composition, 
too, resulting in varying health effects. Factors such as coffee bean variety, roasting, 
grind setting, and brewing method are essential for accurate health data analysis. While 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 59
these studies are promising, the strength of these relationships can vary. Other factors, 
such as overall diet and lifestyle, also influence metabolic health.
CAFFEINE’S COMPLEX IMPACT ON DIABETES
Both caffeinated and decaffeinated coffee may help prevent the development of 
prediabetes and type 2 diabetes. They improve insulin sensitivity and beta-cell function 
(Mirmiran 2018). Interestingly, coffee can promote weight loss regardless of its caffeine 
content (Sirotkin 2021).
Research on coffee and type 2 diabetes shows mixed results. Some studies find equal 
benefits from both types, while others favor decaf. The EPIC-Germany study linked 
drinking four or more cups of decaf coffee to a 30 percent lower risk of type 2 diabetes. 
For caffeinated coffee, the reduction was 23 percent (Nieber 2017), suggesting that 
other bioactive compounds in coffee may be more significant than caffeine.
Separating the effects of coffee from those of caffeine on glucose tolerance is a 
challenging task. Coffee improves glucose tolerance and insulin sensitivity. In contrast, 
pure caffeine, like in energy drinks, has the opposite effect. Interestingly, caffeine’s 
impact on glucose metabolism depends partly on skeletal muscle mass. This means 
body composition may influence individual responses to caffeine (Shearer 2014 and 
2020).
Caffeine with carbohydrates can reduce the body’s ability to handle glucose by 20 
to 30 percent in adults. In adolescents, this effect might be even greater (Shearer 
2020). Caffeine’s half-life is four to six hours, so its impact on insulin action can last 
for several hours after consumption. Regular caffeine intake doesn’t seem to lead to 
adaptation over time.
Notably, caffeine in coffee has a less severe effect on glucose tolerance than pure 
caffeine. Long-term coffee consumption appears to reduce the risk of type 2 diabetes 
despite its negative short-term effects. For those worried about these findings, 
decaffeinated coffee or a blend of regular and decaf might be suitable alternatives.

60 ♦ Ildi Revi
CGA AND OTHER MINOR POLYPHENOLS
CGA boost the production of GLP-1. An average coffee drinker consumes 500 to 
1000 mg of CGA daily (McCarty 2005). Studies in humans and animals show that 
GLP-1 protects against diabetes. It enhances beta-cell response to high blood sugar 
(McCarty 2005, Nieber 2017).
Coffee polyphenols, like 5-caffeoylquinic acid, help maintain beta-cell function. They 
do this by preventing misfolded proinsulin or amylin accumulation, which is a diabetes 
risk factor (Kolb 2021). Additionally, CGA and caffeic acid may help mitigate some 
of the damage caused by a high-fat diet to pancreatic beta cells. In a study, mice on a 
high-fat diet lost weight and visceral fat after taking CGA supplements (Lee 2019).
DITERPENES
Cafestol and kahweol may help manage diabetes by increasing insulin secretion, 
enhancing glucose uptake in muscles, and reducing inflammation (Ren 2019). In one 
study, researchers pre-treated rat pancreatic β-cells with kahweol. They then induced 
damage using streptozotocin (STZ). Kahweol increased insulin content and secretion, 
enhanced glucose uptake, and provided antioxidant protection. These results suggest 
kahweol can restore insulin-mediated glucose metabolism. Cafestol has shown similar 
effects in other studies (El-Huneidi 2021).
TRIGONELLINE
Trigonelline has been attributed to some of the anti-diabetic effects of decaffeinated 
coffee. During roasting, trigonelline produces two forms of vitamin B3: nicotinic acid 
and a small amount of nicotinamide. Vitamin B3 has a mixed impact on blood sugar 
metabolism in rodent and human studies (Vieira Porto and Farah 2019).
In several rodent studies, trigonelline shows protective effects, enhances insulin 
sensitivity, inhibits glucose uptake in the intestines, and regenerates pancreatic 
beta cells. Trigonelline also reduces serum tumor necrosis factor (TNF-α), a pro-
inflammatory cytokine that promotes insulin resistance (Vieira Porto and Farah 
2019). In non-diabetic rabbits, trigonelline reduces hyperglycemia when given with 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 61
cortisone but loses its effect if administered two hours later. Human studies reveal 
that combining trigonelline and CGA slows the glucose and insulin response in oral 
glucose tolerance tests (Vieira Porto and Farah 2019). 
Despite these promising early results, further research is needed to confirm whether 
trigonelline’s anti-diabetic effects are applicable to humans, as observed in animal 
studies.
MICRONUTRIENTS
Coffee provides about 92 milligrams of potassium and 8 milligrams of magnesium 
per 100 milliliters, but it is low in sodium (Institute for Scientific Information on 
Coffee). For comparison, one banana contains just over 400 milligrams of potassium. 
Therefore, consuming a few cups of coffee daily could boost daily potassium intake 
as part of a healthy diet.
People with type 2 diabetes face a higher risk of heart disease. They are strongly 
encouraged to follow the American Diabetes Association’s advice on consuming 
4680 milligrams of potassium daily and limiting sodium to 2300 milligrams or less. 
Unfortunately, many individuals do not adhere to these guidelines (Baqar, 2020).
Magnesium influences the risk of diabetes and heart health. Those with diabetes often 
lack magnesium due to increased urinary output. Diets low in magnesium are linked 
to high blood pressure and stroke. Lower serum magnesium levels also increase the 
risk of sudden cardiac death (NIH). Eating more magnesium can significantly reduce 
the risk of type 2 diabetes, as it aids in glucose metabolism (NIH). The efficacy of 
magnesium supplements in lowering the risk of heart disease and diabetes remains 
uncertain. Nonetheless, it is recommended that most adults aim to consume 300-
420 milligrams of magnesium per day. Nuts, seeds, vegetables, and grains are good 
sources. Also, three to four cups of coffee can add about 28 milligrams of magnesium 
to the daily intake. 

62 ♦ Ildi Revi
TYPE 2 DIABETES (T2D) RECAP
Research consistently shows that regular coffee consumption is linked to a reduced risk of developing 
Type 2 diabetes due to its array of bioactive compounds such as chlorogenic acids, diterpenes, 
and trigonelline. These compounds enhance insulin sensitivity, reduce inflammation, and help 
manage obesity, contributing to overall diabetes prevention. Caffeinated and decaffeinated coffee 
emerge as a functional food with significant health benefits, making it valuable in preventative 
healthcare strategies against lifestyle-related diseases.

63
3.3 Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and Parkinson’s disease (PD) are two of the most prevalent 
neurodegenerative disorders, affecting millions of people worldwide. Although their 
primary symptoms differ, both diseases involve progressive brain cell damage, oxidative 
stress, chronic inflammation, metabolic dysfunction, and toxic metal accumulation, 
making them key targets for dietary and lifestyle interventions.
In 2023, approximately 6.7 million Americans aged 65 and older were living with 
AD, a number projected to double by 2050. AD accounts for 60-80 percent of 
dementia cases, characterized by memory loss, cognitive decline, and ultimately 
loss of independent function. As the sixth leading cause of death in the U.S., AD 
has significant social and economic impacts, including nearly 11 million caregivers 
providing unpaid support and annual care costs exceeding $345 billion. Globally, over 
55 million people have dementia, most commonly linked to AD.
AD is a specific form of dementia, a broad term for the decline in mental abilities that 
affects memory, thinking, and behavior. The burdens of AD impact both patients and 
caregivers due to the gradual worsening of symptoms over time.
PD, while distinct from AD, has its own unique set of challenges. Unlike AD, PD 
is not classified as dementia, although some individuals with PD develop cognitive 
decline later in the disease. PD currently affects nearly one million people in the U.S., 
with that number expected to rise to 1.2 million by 2030, according to the Parkinson’s 
Foundation. Each year, approximately 90,000 Americans are newly diagnosed with 
PD, with over 10 million people living with PD worldwide. The economic burden 
is immense, with total costs in the U.S. reaching $52 billion annually. This includes 
an average of $2,500 per year in medications per patient and up to $100,000 for 
therapeutic surgeries.

64 ♦ Ildi Revi
The symptoms of each disease highlight their differences: AD primarily impacts 
memory and cognition, whereas PD affects motor skills, causing difficulties with 
movement and coordination. Despite these differences, both diseases share common 
underlying mechanisms:
•	
Oxidative stress (damage caused by unstable molecules called free radicals)
•	
Chronic neuroinflammation (long-term activation of the brain’s immune 
system)
•	
Metabolic dysfunction (problems with insulin and glucose processing in the 
brain)
•	
Heavy metal accumulation (which can damage neurons and contribute to AD 
and PD)
Both diseases place substantial physical, mental, social, and financial burdens on 
individuals and their caregivers. Given the limitations of current treatments, which 
primarily alleviate symptoms, research has increasingly shifted to prevention, with 
diet and lifestyle interventions playing a significant role. Studies suggest that coffee 
may help prevent or delay AD and PD due to its antioxidants, anti-inflammatory 
compounds, and metal-chelating properties, all of which combat oxidative stress, 
chronic inflammation, and heavy metal accumulation, three major drivers of 
neurodegeneration.
OXIDATIVE STRESS, INFLAMMATION, AND 
COFFEE’S PROTECTIVE ROLE
The brain is highly energy-demanding, requiring a large amount of oxygen to function. 
This makes it particularly vulnerable to oxidative stress, a process where unstable 
molecules (free radicals) damage brain cells. Over time, this contributes to the aging 
process, cognitive decline, and neurodegenerative diseases (Scarian 2024). 
Chronic neuroinflammation, which is another major contributor to brain aging, is 
regulated by immune signaling pathways, particularly the NF-κB pathway. Think 
of NF-κB as a master switch that regulates inflammation in healthy conditions; it 
helps the body fight infections and heal injuries. In PD and AD, NF-κB becomes 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 65
overactive, resulting in inflammation that can damage neurons rather than protect 
them (Mamun 2024).
Certain compounds in coffee, such as CGA, phenylindanes, and caffeic acid derivatives, 
are known to suppress NF-κB, acting as a “reset button” that tones down harmful 
inflammation and helps neurons survive (Gahtani 2024).
COFFEE AND ALZHEIMER’S: THE GLUCOSE CONNECTION
Emerging research suggests that brain insulin resistance is a major contributor to 
Alzheimer’s disease (AD), leading some researchers to describe AD as “type 3 diabetes” 
(de la Monte 2008). Insulin plays a crucial role in brain function by regulating energy 
metabolism. When insulin resistance develops in the brain, neurons struggle to process 
glucose efficiently, leading to cognitive decline.
The gut-brain connection may play a role in this process. Recent research shows that 
gut bacteria can influence brain function, with the gut’s nervous system (sometimes 
called the “second brain”) capable of acting independently of the central nervous 
system (Connell 2022). This highlights the importance of metabolic health in 
neurodegeneration, as dietary factors influencing gut bacteria and glucose metabolism 
may also impact AD risk.
Dr. Suzanne de la Monte and Dr. Jack Wands of Brown University first proposed 
the type 3 diabetes theory in 2008, based on the strong connection between AD and 
insulin resistance in the brain (de la Monte 2008). Studies have linked high sugar 
intake and impaired insulin function to increased AD risk, emphasizing the need for 
metabolic interventions in prevention strategies.
Long-term studies indicate that adults with type 2 diabetes have a significantly 
higher risk of developing AD, reinforcing the link between glucose dysregulation and 
neurodegeneration. Research suggests that coffee consumption is linked to improved 
insulin sensitivity and glucose regulation, with CGA and other polyphenols playing 
a vital role in supporting metabolic function and reducing neuroinflammation 
(McCarty 2005). These compounds may counteract insulin resistance, helping 
neurons efficiently utilize glucose, which may slow cognitive decline. CGA has been 

66 ♦ Ildi Revi
found to stimulate the production of glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1), a hormone 
that regulates blood sugar and supports pancreatic beta-cell function (McCarty 2005, 
Nieber 2017). This suggests that coffee may help mitigate the harmful effects of insulin 
resistance on the brain, further strengthening its potential role in preventing AD.
Additionally, AD is characterized by the buildup of beta-amyloid (Aβ) plaques and 
tau tangles, which disrupt neuron function and eventually cause cell death. While 
pharmaceutical trials targeting Aβ have largely failed, caffeine has proven to lower 
Aβ production and protect against cognitive decline (Arendash 2009). Higher coffee 
intake has also been linked to lower Aβ accumulation, further supporting coffee’s 
protective effects in AD (Cao 2009).
Notably, the 2021 Australian Imaging, Biomarkers, and Lifestyle Study found that 
regular coffee intake, not just caffeine, may protect against AD. The study reported 
that coffee consumption was associated with slower cognitive decline and reduced 
Aβ-amyloid accumulation, suggesting that coffee could help delay AD onset (Gardener 
2021). This reinforces the idea that coffee’s neuroprotective benefits extend beyond 
caffeine, likely due to the synergy of its polyphenols, antioxidants, and bioactive 
compounds.
Understanding CGA’s absorption and metabolism adds complexity to its role in brain 
health. While CGA is recognized for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and insulin-
regulating properties, its precise effects on cognition remain an active area of research. 
Interestingly, isolated CGA supplements have not shown the same mental benefits as 
CGA in coffee, suggesting that coffee’s full neuroprotective potential comes from the 
synergy between its many bioactive compounds (McCarty 2005).
In the progression of Alzheimer’s Disease (AD), it is advisable to be cautious with 
caffeinated coffee, as caffeine can increase anxiety and disrupt sleep, potentially 
exacerbating symptoms in AD patients (Baeta-Corral 2018). Decaffeinated coffee 
still offers neuroprotective benefits, making it a useful alternative for those sensitive 
to caffeine.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 67
COFFEE AND PARKINSON’S: GUT HEALTH 
AND INFLAMMATION (AGAIN)
Multiple studies have indicated that coffee drinkers have a lower PD risk, possibly 
due to coffee’s impact on the gut microbiome, its anti-inflammatory properties, and 
its ability to modulate disease pathways. 
Promising research suggests that Parkinson’s disease (PD) may start in the gut, where 
misfolded alpha-synuclein proteins, a hallmark of PD, may travel to the brain via the 
vagus nerve (Braak 2003). The gut-brain connection in PD has garnered significant 
attention, as gut inflammation may contribute to early neurodegenerative changes 
before symptoms emerge.
Inflammation plays a major role in PD’s onset and progression, as the disorder is 
characterized by the gradual degeneration of dopaminergic neurons–the brain cells 
responsible for producing dopamine. The loss of these neurons leads to the motor 
symptoms of PD, including tremors, stiffness, and impaired movement. Research by 
Marta Pajares and colleagues (2020) suggests that targeting disease pathways before 
major neuronal loss occurs may be more effective than attempting to reverse damage 
later.
As gut health becomes increasingly recognized as a key factor in PD, coffee’s effects 
on the gut microbiome may be a critical component of its protective role. Coffee 
polyphenols act as prebiotics, nourishing beneficial gut bacteria that may help reduce 
gut inflammation, support brain health, and prevent α-synuclein buildup (Riegelman 
2024).
A recent study published in Nature Microbiology found that coffee consumption 
is strongly linked to a specific gut bacterium, Lawsonibacter asaccharolyticus. This 
bacterium was found to be significantly more abundant, sometimes up to eight times 
more, in coffee drinkers compared to non-drinkers.
Why does this matter? Lawsonibacter asaccharolyticus is known to produce butyrate, 
a short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) essential for gut health. Butyrate helps maintain 
the integrity of the gut lining, supports digestion, enhances nutrient absorption, 

68 ♦ Ildi Revi
and has strong anti-inflammatory properties. Importantly, butyrate is also linked to 
brain health, as it can cross the blood-brain barrier, influencing neuroinflammation, 
neurotransmitter production, and cognitive function.
Researchers believe that compounds found in coffee, such as CGA, may stimulate the 
growth of L. asaccharolyticus, thereby increasing butyrate production and enhancing 
gut and brain health. The study also identified other beneficial microbes linked to 
coffee consumption, suggesting that coffee’s impact on the microbiome may be a key 
factor in its neuroprotective effects.
Additionally, strong evidence shows that caffeine has neuroprotective effects in PD. 
One study found that mid-life coffee consumption was associated with a significantly 
reduced risk of PD, suggesting that regular caffeine intake may lower the likelihood 
of developing PD later in life (Ko 2023).
Also, coffee’s protective effects may extend beyond caffeine. A 2025 study by Hong 
et al. found that coffee drinking and tobacco smoking were both associated with a 
lower PD risk, suggesting that certain molecules in coffee and tobacco may activate 
brain-protective mechanisms. While cigarette smoking poses obvious health risks, 
studies indicate that decaffeinated coffee and nicotine-free tobacco extracts can protect 
against PD in animal specimens by reducing brain inflammation and supporting gut 
microbiome balance.
COFFEE’S METAL CHELATING PROPERTIES
Environmentally persistent metals such as lead, cadmium, and manganese 
tend to accumulate in human tissues over time and are known to contribute to 
neurodegenerative diseases like AD and PD through oxidative stress, inflammation, 
and neuronal death (Bakulski 2020).
There is a widespread environmental exposure to these metals. For example, lead, 
commonly used in paint, gasoline, and industrial products, was banned in the 1970s 
but remains in older infrastructure. Cadmium is present in cigarette smoke, industrial 
pollution, and certain foods like shellfish and grains that absorb it from contaminated 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 69
soil and water. Manganese, although essential in small amounts, can accumulate in the 
brain with excessive exposure, especially in industrial settings or through contaminated 
drinking water.
Both cadmium and manganese have lengthy half-lives in the body, meaning that past 
exposure can contribute to ongoing brain toxicity in older adults. Animal studies have 
shown that these metals lead to AD-related brain changes, and human epidemiological 
studies link them to cognitive decline (Bakulski 2020).
Certain foods, including coffee, naturally help remove toxic metals from the body. 
This process, known as metal chelation, involves the binding of organic compounds to 
metals to form stable complexes that can reduce their toxicity and facilitate excretion 
(Bjørklund, 2017).
Coffee is particularly rich in metal-chelating compounds, including:
•	
CGA and caffeoyl tryptophan that may bind to iron and aluminum, 
reducing their potential toxicity (Takenaka 2005).
•	
Melanoidins bind to iron, copper, zinc, calcium, and aluminum, preventing 
them from accumulating in the brain (Muriel 2012).
•	
Phenylindanes, formed during coffee roasting, offer additional antioxidant 
protection against metal-induced oxidative damage (Mancini 2018).
Given the increasing exposure to environmental toxins, coffee’s ability to bind and 
neutralize neurotoxic metals makes it a valuable dietary defense against AD and PD. 
While no single food or drink can prevent neurodegeneration, coffee appears to have 
protective properties against it. Its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and metal-chelating 
compounds may help slow the progression of both AD and PD. With growing research 
on the gut-brain axis, neuroinflammation, and toxic metal accumulation, coffee is 
emerging as a simple and accessible tool for lifelong brain health. 

70 ♦ Ildi Revi
ALZHEIMER’S & PARKINSON’S Recap
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and Parkinson’s disease (PD) are major neurodegenerative disorders 
linked to oxidative stress, inflammation, metabolic dysfunction, and toxic metal accumulation. 
With current treatments focused on symptom management, dietary interventions, including 
coffee consumption, are gaining attention for their protective potential. Beyond caffeine, coffee’s 
CGA, phenylindanes, and melanoidins provide antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and metal-
chelating benefits.
AD: Coffee consumption is associated with improved insulin sensitivity, reduced β-amyloid (Aβ) 
accumulation, and lower cognitive decline risk. AD’s connection to insulin resistance (“Type 3 
Diabetes”) suggests coffee’s metabolic benefits may help prevent disease progression.
PD: Caffeine and CGA support dopamine function and reduce α-synuclein buildup. Given the 
gut-brain connection in neurodegeneration, coffee’s influence on gut bacteria may also contribute 
to PD prevention. Gut-Brain Support – Coffee promotes beneficial gut bacteria linked to 
neuroprotection. While not a cure, regular coffee consumption may help slow neurodegeneration, 
making it a promising addition to brain health strategies.

71
3.4 Heart Health
Cardiovascular diseases (CVD) are the leading cause of death globally, responsible 
for approximately 17.9 million deaths in 2019 alone. According to the WHO Fact 
Sheet on Cardiovascular Diseases 2021, heart attacks account for 32 percent of global 
deaths, with strokes causing 85 percent of these fatalities. The global economic burden 
of CVDs is expected to exceed USD one trillion annually by 2030 due to healthcare 
costs and lost productivity (World Economic Forum). Low- and middle-income 
countries are disproportionately affected, making up over three-quarters of CVD 
deaths. Major risk factors include unhealthy diet, physical inactivity, tobacco use, and 
harmful alcohol consumption, highlighting the need for comprehensive preventative 
measures (WHO 2021).
Historically, there has been a medical contention about the effects of coffee on the 
heart and cardiovascular system, with early medical opinions often viewing it as a 
potential risk factor for heart disease and high blood pressure. Public perception 
mirrored these concerns, leading to widespread caution against coffee consumption. 
However, research has evolved, revealing that moderate coffee consumption may 
actually offer cardiovascular benefits, such as reduced risks of heart failure, stroke, 
and even early death. Today, the consensus is that drinking one to three cups of coffee 
daily is considered safe and may be beneficial for heart health, although there is still 
caution against excessive consumption. 
Three investigations analyzed data from the UK Biobank, a comprehensive repository 
established in 2006 that contains health information on over 500,000 individuals, 
who have been tracked for at least ten years (Ding 2014). The objective was to examine 
the correlation between various levels of coffee consumption and the incidence of 
arrhythmias (irregular heartbeats), cardiovascular disease, heart failure, stroke, and 
heart-related mortality.

72 ♦ Ildi Revi
The first study, with data from 382,535 participants with no known cardiovascular 
disease, revealed that consuming two to three cups of coffee daily was linked to a 10-15 
percent reduced risk of developing coronary heart disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, 
or all-cause mortality. The lowest risk of stroke or cardiovascular-related death was 
observed among those who consumed one cup of coffee per day (Chieng and Kistler 
2021).
In the second study of 34,279 individuals with pre-existing cardiovascular conditions, 
it was noted that coffee intake of two to three cups per day was associated with 
reduced mortality risk compared to non-coffee drinkers. Moreover, no level of coffee 
consumption was linked to an increased risk of arrhythmias, including atrial fibrillation 
(AFib) or atrial flutter. Among the 24,111 participants with baseline arrhythmias, 
those who drank coffee had a lower risk of death. Patients with Afib who consumed 
one cup daily were 20 percent less likely to die than non-coffee drinkers (Chieng and 
Kistler 2021).
The third study concluded that consuming two to three cups of coffee daily, whether 
instant or ground, caffeinated or decaffeinated, was associated with the lowest risk of 
arrhythmias, heart blockages, stroke, and heart failure. Decaf coffee also contributed 
positively by reducing cardiovascular disease risk, except for heart failure. The results 
indicated that caffeinated coffee offers more potential health benefits than decaf coffee, 
which has no cardiovascular advantages over normal coffee (Chieng 2022, also see 
Simon 2022, both of these are UK Biobank studies).
The researchers noted some limitations. They could not account for dietary variables 
that might influence cardiovascular health or adjust for the addition of creamers, 
milk, or sugar.
COFFEE CAN REDUCE HEART DISEASE RISK FACTORS
A study at the University of São Paulo’s School of Public Health showed that drinking 
three cups of coffee daily can reduce coronary calcification by 63 percent in non-
smokers. The study was conducted with a population of 4,426 individuals, with an 
average age of 50, living in São Paulo. Each person underwent a CT scan to monitor 
the state of their coronary blood vessels. The encouraging results for the coffee drinkers 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 73
came with a major caveat that had nothing to do with coffee. The data showed that 
if the subjects had ever been smokers, the protective ability of coffee did not benefit 
them like it did for never-smokers (Miranda 2018).
While smoking negatively impacts the potential benefits of coffee in combating heart 
disease, other significant health issues, such as type 2 diabetes and obesity, can be 
mitigated. These conditions often stem from metabolic syndrome, which hampers 
the body’s ability to convert calories into energy efficiently. Individuals with metabolic 
syndrome have an increased risk of developing heart disease compared to those without 
it. Numerous authoritative studies demonstrate a substantial inverse relationship 
between coffee intake and metabolic disease, highlighting coffee’s role in reducing 
systemic inflammation and enhancing the body’s insulin production.
The key lies in CGA, which fortifies endothelial cells, the cells constituting the 
walls of blood vessels. CGA exhibits anti-hypertensive properties, thereby protecting 
against high blood pressure and optimizing glucose and insulin metabolism. Improved 
metabolism contributes to weight loss, reducing excess body fat and lowering 
triglycerides and LDL cholesterol levels, which are strong defenses against heart 
disease (Lukitasari 2020).
Furthermore, research has directly linked coffee consumption to a decreased risk of 
CVD. A pivotal study published by the American Heart Association, led by Ding 
et al. in 2015, monitored the relationship between coffee consumption and causes 
of death among 30,000 individuals aged 30 years or older. The analysis revealed an 
inverse relationship between coffee consumption and CVD. The protective effects 
are attributed to reduced oxidative stress and enhanced vein protection due to the 
high concentration of CGA compounds in coffee. Notably, the lowest CVD risk was 
observed among individuals who consumed three to five cups of coffee daily. There 
was no significant additional risk for those consuming more.
The American Heart Association recommends drinking coffee in moderation, up 
to four to five cups daily. How coffee is prepared matters too: boiled coffee can 
raise cholesterol levels, while filtered coffee can help lower cholesterol levels. 
Moderate coffee consumption is associated with lower mortality rates and 
improved heart health, but its impact on heart disease remains unsettled in 

74 ♦ Ildi Revi
scientific research (Mendoza 2023). Certain individuals must monitor their coffee 
consumption, especially those with a family history of myocardial infarction (heart 
attack) and high blood pressure (Mendoza 2023).
A 2006 study by Azevedo et al. involving Portuguese men aged 40 and older highlighted 
this. Participants had varying family histories of heart disease, and around 25 percent 
consumed approximately four cups of coffee daily. This is considered a moderate 
amount by coffee experts. The study revealed divergent effects of coffee consumption:
•
For men with a family history of myocardial infarction, coffee intake was
positively correlated with heart attacks.
•
For men who do not have a family history of heart disease, higher coffee
consumption was associated with a lower risk of heart-related issues. In other 
words, drinking coffee appeared to benefit their heart health, potentially 
reducing their risk of heart disease.
These findings suggest that individuals with a family history of heart disease should 
be cautious about coffee consumption. In contrast, those without such a history may 
experience significant heart health benefits from moderate coffee consumption.
Heart Health Recap
Multiple large-scale studies, including the UK BioBank and American Heart Association analyses, 
consistently link moderate coffee consumption—typically two to five cups per day—with a 
lower risk of cardiovascular disease, heart failure, stroke, and arrhythmias. This protective effect 
extends to individuals both with and without preexisting heart conditions and appears consistent 
across coffee types, including instant, ground, caffeinated, and decaffeinated. Mechanistically, 
compounds such as chlorogenic acids may support vascular health by improving endothelial 
function, blood pressure, lipid profiles, and glucose metabolism. However, coffee’s cardioprotective 
effects may be diminished in smokers, and individuals with a strong family history of myocardial 
infarction should approach intake more cautiously.

75
3.5 Mast Cell Activation Syndrome 
(MCAS)
Mast Cell Activation Syndrome (MCAS) is a complex and often underdiagnosed 
condition in which mast cells, immune cells that normally protect the body, become 
hyperresponsive to certain triggers. In affected individuals, these cells release excessive 
amounts of chemical mediators, especially histamine, in response to stimuli that would 
not usually trigger such a reaction. These triggers can include toxins, pathogens, 
environmental changes, and even stress. The result is a broad range of symptoms that 
can affect multiple systems, including the skin, gastrointestinal tract, cardiovascular 
system, and nervous system (Afrin 2015). The estimated prevalence of MCAS is 
about 5 to 17 percent of the population and is frequently misdiagnosed because its 
symptoms mimic other conditions, including allergies, autoimmune disorders, and 
gastrointestinal issues. 
HISTAMINE: AT THE CORE OF MCAS
Histamine is a biogenic amine with various physiological functions. Biogenic amines 
can be psychoactive, neuroactive, or vasoactive (Bomtempo 2016). Some Well-known 
amines include dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and tyramine. While many 
of these amines support health, at high concentrations, they may pose toxicological 
risks (Taban 2014).
Histamine plays essential roles in immune response, gastric acid secretion, vasodilation, 
and neurotransmission. It helps the body fight allergens, sends chemical messages 
between the brain and body, and maintains digestive function. Histamine receptors are 
ubiquitous in the body, and the molecules are involved in the inflammatory process. 

76 ♦ Ildi Revi
The problem arises when histamine accumulates beyond what the body can safely 
metabolize.
Histamine is primarily produced by mast cells and released when these cells detect 
a threat, whether real or perceived. Eosinophils and basophils also contribute by 
releasing pro-inflammatory mediators. Importantly, mast cells are often located near 
blood vessels, and histamine’s vascular effects suggest it may influence blood-brain 
flow. High concentrations of histamine and histamine decarboxylase have been found 
in neurons in the hypothalamus, where they modulate wakefulness, alertness, and 
attention. These effects are similar to norepinephrine and partly explain the sedative 
effects of antihistamines that cross the blood-brain barrier (Deuchars 1998).
HISTAMINE INTOLERANCE VS. ALLERGY
Histamine intolerance is not an allergy but the body’s inability to break down and 
eliminate excess histamine. Symptoms can include headaches, digestive issues, fatigue, 
anxiety, asthma, hives, flushing, dizziness, arrhythmias, menstrual irregularities, and 
more (Luby-Phelps 1999). The severity of symptoms may vary and often overlap with 
those of other conditions, making diagnosis challenging. Under normal conditions, 
enzymes known as amine oxidases, particularly diamine oxidase (DAO), detoxify 
histamine. But in individuals with low DAO activity, histamine can accumulate to 
problematic levels (Cesura 2007).
Histamine overload is often rooted in gut dysfunction. Conditions like small intestine 
bacterial overgrowth (SIBO), leaky gut, Candida overgrowth, and parasitic infections 
can reduce DAO production (Rodriguez 2014). Other contributing factors include:
•	
Nutrient deficiencies (vitamin C, B vitamins, zinc, copper)
•	
Genetic mutations
•	
Gluten intolerance
•
