Certain medications (NSAIDs, antidepressants, immunosuppressants, 
antihistamines)
•	
Hormonal imbalances like hyperthyroidism, which increases histamine 
receptors (Maintz 2007)

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 77
Interestingly, while antihistamines may offer temporary symptom relief, they may also 
suppress DAO production over time. In many cases, symptoms remain unnoticed 
until a triggering event, such as consuming a histamine-rich food, like avocado, tips 
the balance.
HISTAMINE’S CONNECTION TO COFFEE
Although research on coffee and histamine remains limited, existing studies suggest 
that green and roasted coffee beans, as well as brewed coffee, contain trace amounts 
of histamine. Moreover, most histamine appears to be degraded during the roasting 
process. In 2005, Oliveira et al. measured several amines in coffee and found histamine 
in low-quality beans. During roasting, histamine degraded to negligible levels (0.05 
mg per 100 g) within the first eight minutes. So why do we worry about histamine 
in coffee? Even though these concentrations are low, the presence of histamine 
could still be enough to cause adverse effects in histamine-sensitive individuals with 
gastrointestinal, neurological, hemodynamic, or cutaneous symptoms. Other amines 
in coffee, such as putrescine and cadaverine, can potentiate histamine toxicity.
Research has shown that histamine appears almost exclusively in low-quality coffee, 
indicating a strong association between this biogenic amine and defective beans (Dias 
2012, Lee 2021). Like all agricultural products, coffee spans a broad quality spectrum, 
from extremely low-grade lots to exceptional, clean-tasting selections. Based on 
industry patterns and labeling trends, a rough estimate suggests that only about 20–30 
percent of global coffee production meets the standards typically associated with 
premium or high-integrity coffees. (As noted earlier, this is a personal estimate, since 
precise figures are difficult to verify due to the lack of a consistent global definition.) 
This means that the vast majority of coffee roasted and consumed worldwide likely 
comes from lower-grade sources, many of which include a significant proportion of 
defective beans.
Numerous factors can cause coffee defects during farming, harvesting, processing, 
or drying. During farming, defects come primarily from pests and diseases. Since 
unhealthy plants may produce unhealthy coffee, soil quality and hydration are 
important for producing high-quality beans. 

78 ♦ Ildi Revi
                 
Coffee fruits can ripen at different rates. Picking by hand and not gathering fallen 
fruit from the ground are still the best methods for avoiding underripe, overripe, 
or unhealthy fruit. Fruit that has fallen to the ground raises concerns regarding 
its nutritional and organoleptic properties (characteristics that the human senses, 
including taste, smell, sight, and touch, can detect). Hand-selecting is labor-intensive 
and involves re-visiting the same plants throughout the season several times. The 
alternative to hand-picking is mechanical harvesting, followed by optical sorting, to 
maintain the highest quality coffee. Though this method is feasible only for large-scale 
operations with suitable topographic conditions. If coffee is not harvested properly, 
defective beans can result, which is linked to histamine. 
Post-harvest grading before processing and drying is important: Separating the unripe 
fruit is critical to avoid defective beans for high-quality coffee and potentially to 
prevent histamine since unripe beans demonstrate higher histamine levels (Dias 
2012). Once separated, these defective beans are often blended with other low-quality 
beans and sold, which makes financial sense for the producer and allows for a low-cost 
roasted coffee for consumers.
Once the coffee is harvested and sorted, it undergoes processing, which involves 
removing the coffee seeds (also known as coffee beans or green coffee) from the coffee 
fruit. Regardless of the method, some level of fermentation is involved, whether 
controlled by the farmer or occurring naturally, where microbes help break down 
the fruit layer that clings to the seeds. The processing method plays a crucial role 
in shaping the coffee’s final flavour profile, with each technique imparting distinct 
Unhealthy green coffee full of defects.
Healthy defect-free coffee

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 79
characteristics to the beans. Proper handling during this critical phase is essential to 
preserve the quality achieved through careful farming and harvesting, ensuring that 
the coffee’s full potential is realized from seed to cup.
Green coffee is typically roasted for at least eight minutes, reaching temperatures over 
400°F (204°C), often for longer and hotter periods. This is considered a kill step in food 
safety, which means microorganisms cannot survive the roasting process. This applies 
to any yeast or bacteria involved in the fermentation process. These microorganisms 
would be destroyed during this step; however, whether anything remains that can 
survive the heat and impact MCAS is currently unknown. It is essential to note that 
while this heat will eliminate microorganisms, it will not eliminate all mycotoxin and 
biotoxin levels. Another important step to take is to protect it from contamination 
during the packaging process after it has been removed from the roaster, which I discuss 
in more detail in the sections on contaminants, roasting and food safety.
For consumers dealing with MCAS or histamine intolerance, the key is not avoiding 
coffee altogether, but choosing well-sourced, properly processed coffee. When coffee 
is grown with care, harvested and sorted diligently, and roasted with food safety in 
mind, the risks are minimal, and the health benefits can be preserved.
High-quality, health-grade coffee should be tested for biogenic amines, and producers 
should prioritize post-harvest sorting and proper storage to prevent contamination. 
Just as important, consumers deserve transparency about what’s in their cup. Coffee 
is not inherently problematic for histamine-sensitive individuals, but not all coffee is 
processed the same way.

80 ♦ Ildi Revi
Mast Cell Activation Syndrome (MCAS) Recap
MCAS is a complex, underdiagnosed condition characterized by hypersensitive immune cells that 
release excessive histamine and other mediators, triggering symptoms across multiple systems. 
Histamine intolerance, often confused with allergies, arises when the body accumulates more 
histamine than it can break down, frequently due to gut dysfunction, nutrient deficiencies, 
certain medications, or genetic factors. While coffee generally contains only trace amounts of 
histamine—most of which degrade during roasting—low-quality, defective beans can retain 
enough to potentially provoke symptoms in sensitive individuals. Research suggests histamine 
presence is tied to poor post-harvest practices, pest-damaged or unripe beans, and inadequate 
sorting, reinforcing the importance of high-quality sourcing in health-grade coffee. For individuals 
affected by MCAS or histamine intolerance, coffee quality and processing matter deeply—not 
because coffee is inherently problematic, but because not all coffee is created equal.

81
3.6 Gastrointestinal (GI) Health
Globally, colorectal cancer is the third most commonly diagnosed cancer and the 
second leading cause of cancer death, with approximately 1.93 million new cases and 
903,000 deaths in 2022 (WHO 2025). Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) 
affects up to 20 percent of the population in Western countries, as reported by the 
National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases. Irritable Bowel 
Syndrome (IBS) impacts about 11 percent of the global population, with prevalence 
higher in industrialized nations, per the International Foundation for Gastrointestinal 
Disorders. These conditions represent significant public health challenges due to their 
high prevalence and impact on quality of life. Could coffee help?
Extensive research has examined the effects of coffee on gastrointestinal (GI) health, 
with many beneficial and adverse effects reported. Understanding the risks and 
benefits is central for individuals with gastrointestinal disorders and those seeking to 
maintain general health awareness. After drinking a cup of coffee, some individuals 
may experience indigestion or acid reflux afterward. Overarching scientific evidence 
suggests that coffee consumption has little to no significant relationship with major 
acid-related upper gastrointestinal disorders, including various types of ulcers and 
reflux. Of course, this is no consolation to people who experience issues after drinking 
coffee. A more complex narrative exists, and GI reactions to coffee can be highly 
individualized. To make matters more confusing for these individuals, there are 
contradictory studies or variable results in the research. I hope to use this section to 
clarify without diminishing anyone’s experience.

82 ♦ Ildi Revi
The most common complaint about coffee is that the acidity can irritate the stomach. 
When considering acidity and pH, recall that the pH scale measures acidity and 
alkalinity, ranging from 0 (extremely acidic) to 14 (extremely alkaline), with a neutral 
value of  7. As a solution’s pH value decreases from 7 to 0, it becomes more acidic, 
while values increasing from 7 to 14 indicate increasing alkalinity or causticity.
Average stomach acid levels are around 2.5 pH, and average roasted coffee levels are 
around 4.8 to 5.4 pH (less acidic than gastric acid). Coffee has approximately the 
same acidity as watermelon and a lower acid content on average than many common 
foods, like tomato (about 4.3) or orange juice (around 3.7). This means the acidity 
of coffee is unlikely to increase the stomach’s acidity pH level. This is not meant to 
downplay one’s experience, but rather to suggest looking for answers elsewhere, as we 
will examine other causes of stomach pain related to coffee.
When discussing coffee’s acidity, it’s essential to understand that the color of the roast 
is not always a reliable indicator of the acidity level. For example, light colour roasts 
are typically associated with higher acidity, but the roasting process determines the 
ultimate acidity of the coffee. Later in the roasting chapter, I discuss the changes in 
coffee’s organic acids during roasting. Yet, for now, it is worth noting that extending 
the roasting time at a lower temperature can result in a lighter color roast and reduced 
acidity. This method aligns with the ‘lower acid blonde roast’ coffee trend popularized 
in the early 2020s, but this approach comes with trade-offs. While the acidity may 
decrease, prolonged exposure to heat can reduce the volatile aromatic compounds and 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 83
antioxidants, potentially compromising both flavor and health benefits (Aytar 2024). 
Thus, these additional consequences must be taken into account even as a lower acid 
profile can be achieved without resorting to dark roasting.
Moreover, scientific evidence on the effects of coffee components on human gastric 
acid secretion remains scarce and contradictory. Caffeine is often blamed for causing 
stomach discomfort. Unfortunately, a literature survey indicates it is not the case. 
Studies have shown that regular and decaffeinated coffee can increase gastric acid 
secretion, leading to symptoms such as heartburn and discomfort in individuals with 
sensitive stomachs (Pike 2017).
Acid secretion is stimulated by both caffeinated and decaffeinated coffee (Verhoeven 
2017), but these studies do not identify the compound(s) responsible for this effect. 
Wendl et al. (1994) studied regular and decaffeinated coffee, tea, and water with 
laboratory-added caffeine. These results showed that coffee increased gastric reflux, 
and to a lesser extent, decaf coffee also had this effect; however, tea had no such effect. 
They concluded, “Caffeine does not seem to be responsible for gastro-oesophageal 
reflux, which must be attributed to other components of coffee.” This conclusion may 
be frustrating for those who experience stomach issues when drinking coffee.
Aside from acidity or caffeine (discussed later), there must be something else causing 
stomach discomfort. One coffee-sensitive study found that different coffees induced 
varying levels of reflux (Boekema 1999). In other words, certain coffee consumers 
may be more sensitive to stomach-related effects than others when they drink different 
types of coffee. An older study found that some individuals have an anatomical 
predisposition to coffee and heartburn, suggesting that “coffee may cause or aggravate 
heartburn by decreasing lower esophageal sphincter pressure” (Lang 2010). Although 
recent studies have not confirmed this, it is worth noting that decaffeinated coffee 
was not included in the analysis.
The real culprit of gastric acid production may not be PH or caffeine but other 
compounds entirely. Evidence suggests that different compounds are linked to a 
chemical signaling mechanism in the body that triggers the production of gastric acid 
in the stomach. These compounds include (β) N-alkanoyl-5-hydroxytryptamides 
(compounds with potential antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties) and CGA. 

84 ♦ Ildi Revi
Organic acids common to coffee, especially malic acids, have also been shown in other 
beverages, such as wine, to stimulate gastric acid production (Liszt 2012).
Another hypothesis states that our mouth signals to our stomach via our taste buds. A 
recent study demonstrated that caffeine ingestion stimulated bitter taste receptors in 
the mouth and stomach, triggering a signal to produce gastric acid. This is an example 
of the various methods our mouth uses to signal our stomach into action (Liszt 2017).
Emerging research shows that certain compounds naturally occurring in coffee 
can prevent gastric acid secretion in the stomach. One of the most significant of 
these is N-methylpyridinium (NMP), a compound formed during roasting from 
trigonelline as a non-volatile degradation product. Interestingly, while small amounts 
of trigonelline and nicotinic acid are present in many foods, N-methylpyridinium 
seems to be unique to roasted coffee (Viera 2019). Research by Rubach et al. (2010) 
demonstrated that higher levels of NMP in coffee, along with lower levels of serotonin 
amides (compounds with long fatty acid chains attached) and CGA, resulted in less 
gastric acid production in participants.
Further research supports these findings. A study by Rubach (2014) found that 
gastric acid secretion was less effectively stimulated after participants consumed a dark 
roast blend compared to a medium roast blend. The dark roast contained a higher 
concentration of N-methylpyridinium (87 mg/L) compared to the medium roast (29 
mg/L) but had lower levels of trigonelline and CGA. This suggests that the roasting 
process not only influences the flavor profile of coffee but also its impact on gastric 
acid regulation. Another study concluded that a darker roasted blend did not stimulate 
gastric acid secretion in healthy volunteers compared to a lighter roast market-blend 
beverage (Rubach 2014).
NMP’s influence on gastric acid regulation could be due to its unique chemical 
properties and interactions with other compounds present in coffee. While NMP 
appears to be a key compound in the search for a coffee that does not cause stomach 
discomfort, other compounds, such as CGA and serotonin amides, may also play a 
role. CGA is known for its antioxidant activity and has been identified, along with 
NMP, as an inducer of Nrf2, a master regulator of oxidative stress and cell survival 
(Boettler 2011). Interestingly, N-methylpyridinium was found to be an even more 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 85
potent extract than CGA in both in vitro and in vivo studies, further supporting its 
potential role in reducing gastric irritation.
Despite these promising findings, research on the complex interactions between coffee 
compounds and their effects on gastric acid regulation remains limited, signaling the 
need for further investigation. Research on the complex mixtures, relative abundances, 
and combinations of these and other potentially gastric-acid-regulating compounds 
is still in its early stages, with only a few studies having investigated this topic.
A 2022 pilot study by Polese et al. investigated the effects of dewaxed coffee on 
individuals with GERD and found promising results. Dewaxed coffee is a type of 
coffee that has undergone a process to remove its natural waxy compounds, which are 
found in the outer layer of the coffee bean. These waxes, known as Nβ-Alkanoyl-5-
hydroxytryptamines (C-5-HT), are not easily digestible and may cause mild irritation 
to the stomach lining, particularly in individuals with conditions such as acid reflux 
or GERD. The dewaxing process involves using an organic solvent to extract these 
waxes along with a small amount of caffeine (Polese 2022). Participants who consumed 
dewaxed coffee experienced fewer digestive symptoms, such as heartburn, bloating, 
and regurgitation, compared to those who drank standard coffee. Additionally, they 
reported more symptom-free days and overall improvement in the quality of life. 
Interestingly, the dewaxed coffee retained its beneficial polyphenols, particularly 
CGA (Polese 2022).
A review of 28 studies found mixed results on coffee’s link to GERD: 15 showed no 
link, 2 suggested protection, and 11 indicated aggravation (Gastrointestinal Society 
2022). Another review stated that reducing coffee might help some patients, but the 
evidence is not strong enough to recommend universally avoiding it (Mahadeva 2024).
Another recent meta-analysis also found that low-carbohydrate diets significantly 
reduced esophageal acid exposure time, while changes in eating speed showed no effect, 
suggesting a multifactorial approach to GERD symptom management may be best 
(Yin 2023). Updated guidelines now support a personalized approach to managing 
reflux-like symptoms, including coffee abstinence for individuals who experience 
symptoms after drinking it (Pace 2023).

86 ♦ Ildi Revi
Finally, a large study conducted in 2024 on coffee and functional dyspepsia found 
no association between coffee or caffeine consumption and the odds of dyspepsia or 
its core symptoms, including epigastric pain, early satiety, and fullness after meals 
(Pournaghi, 2024). These findings support the idea that coffee’s impact varies by 
individual and is not inherently problematic for the majority. That does not mean that 
we will ignore the problem for the minority. We have to figure out what compounds 
are involved, and they may be different compounds for different individuals.
HEARTBURN AND GERD
Heartburn is the most frequently reported gastric symptom after coffee consumption. 
Nevertheless, there is mixed scientific evidence on whether drinking coffee causes 
heartburn or irritates reflux disorders. One literature review concluded that coffee does 
not contribute to reflux (Boekema 1999). Although the effects of coffee on reflux are 
not profound or extensive, other evidence suggests that coffee could contribute to the 
condition. One study found that coffee precipitated heartburn in about 68 percent of 
patients with daily symptoms (Feldman 1995). The results may even be stronger in 
people who already identify as coffee-sensitive. One study found that different coffees 
induced varying levels of reflux in a coffee-sensitive study group and demonstrated 
that symptoms were more severe when consumed with a meal afterward (Brazer 1995). 
These studies, although limited, may suggest that certain coffee consumers are more 
susceptible to stomach-related issues than others, which partially explains why study 
results on this topic have been inconclusive.
Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is a specific condition characterized by the 
reflux of stomach acid into the esophagus. While heartburn is a symptom that can 
occur in the context of GERD, having heartburn does not necessarily mean one has 
GERD. GERD is a more severe and chronic form of acid reflux. If left untreated, 
GERD can lead to more serious complications, such as esophagitis (inflammation of 
the esophagus), Barrett’s esophagus, and an increased risk of esophageal cancer. GERD 
usually requires long-term management, including lifestyle changes, medications, and 
surgery in severe cases.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 87
Spicy or fatty foods and overeating are common causes of this condition. In some cases, 
coffee has been suggested as a cause. Despite this, there is no conclusive evidence that 
coffee consumption affects the symptoms of this disease (Kaltenbach 2006). Those 
who suffer from symptoms often self-regulate their diet according to their sensitivities, 
and some people may choose to limit their coffee consumption based on experience, 
especially if they are drinking poor-quality coffee (which might be the issue). Others 
may have misconceptions about coffee and its effect on the body.
A study by Nilsson and others (2004) found an inverse relationship between coffee 
consumption and the risk of reflux, indicating that individuals who drank coffee 
were less likely to experience gastric reflux. The same study found that other healthy 
lifestyle factors, such as a high-fiber diet and regular exercise, also led to fewer reflux 
issues. Some studies suggest that decaffeinated coffee may be less likely to cause these 
symptoms than regular coffee (Zhang 2018, Singh 2021). The dewaxing process is 
somewhat similar to the decaffeination process, which might explain why the results 
of decaf coffees caused less acid reflux. This could be a useful area for future research.
Although there is conflicting research on caffeine, a 1994 study examined the impact 
of decaffeination on gastroesophageal reflux, comparing the effects of regular and 
decaffeinated coffee. They found that decaffeinated coffee tended to cause less acid 
reflux or heartburn than regular coffee. One conclusion could be that caffeine is a 
key component in coffee, contributing to gastroesophageal reflux (Wendl 1994). A 
few years later, a review discussed various aspects of coffee’s impact on GI function, 
including its role in exacerbating GERD symptoms due to caffeine’s effect on the 
lower esophageal sphincter. Their research indicated that caffeine in coffee was shown 
to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, which can lead to increased acid reflux, 
heartburn, and potentially worsening symptoms of GERD (Boekema 1999). This was 
not entirely new information, as Fisher et al. had investigated the effects of caffeine 
and theophylline on lower esophageal sphincter pressure in 1986. They suggested 
that these substances could lower pressure, potentially leading to increased reflux and 
heartburn. When the lower esophageal sphincter pressure is reduced, stomach acid 
can escape into the esophagus, increasing the likelihood of experiencing acid reflux 
and indigestion.

88 ♦ Ildi Revi
Still, the problem remains unclear due to conflicting evidence and significant 
differences between coffees. No study, whether large or well-designed, shows that 
eliminating coffee or caffeine consistently improves reflux symptoms or outcomes. 
While scientific evidence on the relationship between coffee and heartburn and GERD 
remains mixed, individual experiences can vary significantly. 
For those experiencing symptoms after coffee consumption, consider the following 
strategies:
•	
Experiment with different roasts: Darker roasts may result in less gastric 
acid secretion compared to lighter roasts.
•	
Try high-quality naturally decaffeinated coffee, or blend some into regular 
coffee.
•	
Timing matters: Avoid drinking coffee immediately before or after meals, 
as this may exacerbate symptoms.
•	
Monitor your intake: Keep a journal to track how different types and 
amounts of coffee affect your symptoms.
•	
Consider brewing methods: Cold brew or low-acid coffee preparations might 
be gentler on your stomach. Using slightly alkaline water (or adding a small 
pinch of baking soda to the grounds before brewing) can help neutralize 
acidity and reduce irritation. Espresso-based drinks with milk may also 
be easier to tolerate, especially if dairy already agrees with you, though it’s 
worth noting that milk can reduce the bioavailability of chlorogenic acids, 
as discussed in the next chapter.
PEPTIC ULCERS, MYCOTOXINS AND MICROBIOTA
Peptic ulcers are lesions that develop in the mucosa of the stomach wall, causing pain 
and discomfort. A 2013 meta-analysis investigated whether various types of ulcers 
could detect any association of coffee consumption with either gastric or duodenal 
ulcers. Previously, coffee was linked with the development of peptic ulcers. The 
researchers found no significant link between coffee consumption and major acid-
related upper gastrointestinal disorders.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 89
Studies investigating the risk factors for the development of stomach ulcers conclude 
that coffee is no longer considered a risk factor (Boekema 2001). Although coffee may 
stimulate gastric acid secretion and is often blamed for gastric symptoms, including 
ulcers, there is no good scientific evidence linking coffee consumption to peptic ulcers. 
A cross-sectional study conducted in 2024, involving 3,362 Iranian adults, investigated 
the association between coffee and caffeine intake and functional dyspepsia. The study 
found no significant link between coffee or caffeine consumption and the odds of 
developing dyspepsia.
When individuals experience gastrointestinal discomfort after drinking coffee, 
contaminants such as mold and mycotoxins may be contributing factors, especially 
in lower-quality coffees. Molds, such as the Aspergillus species, are often found in 
defective or poorly processed coffee beans. These molds can produce mycotoxins, 
which are known to cause a range of adverse health effects, including nausea, bloating, 
stomach pain, acid reflux, and indigestion (FDA Mycotoxins 2024). The risk of 
mycotoxin exposure is significantly lower in high-quality coffees that have been 
rigorously sorted, properly dried, and stored under optimal conditions. Mycotoxins 
are discussed more in the chapters on compounds of concern, farming, and food safety.
The research conducted by González et al. (2020) examines the impact of long-term 
coffee consumption on the composition of gut microbiota in humans. Conducted 
with 147 healthy adults, the research categorized participants based on their daily 
coffee consumption into non-consumers, moderate consumers, and high consumers. 
They found that high coffee consumers exhibited a significantly higher abundance of 
the Bacteroides–Prevotella–Porphyromonas group compared to non-consumers. This 
group of bacteria is involved in carbohydrate metabolism and has been associated with 
various health outcomes. They also found a direct correlation between the levels of 
caffeine and certain coffee-derived polyphenols with the abundance of the Bacteroides 
group, suggesting that these compounds in coffee may influence the gut microbiota 
composition. Also, participants with higher coffee consumption demonstrated lower 
levels of lipid peroxidation, indicating a potential protective effect against oxidative 
stress. ​I discuss this more in other chapters.
These findings indicate that regular coffee consumption affects the gut microbiota 
by increasing the presence of bacteria linked to metabolic and anti-inflammatory 

90 ♦ Ildi Revi
functions. This change could contribute to a more balanced intestinal environment, 
potentially reducing susceptibility to dysbiosis and inflammation associated with 
gastrointestinal disorders. Dysbiosis is a disruption in the normal balance of the gut 
microbiota, the trillions of microorganisms, primarily bacteria, which live in the 
digestive tract. In a healthy state, this microbial community aids digestion, produces 
essential vitamins, supports immune function, and maintains the integrity of the 
intestinal barrier. When that balance is disturbed, due to factors like poor-quality 
food, stress, antibiotics, or environmental toxins, harmful bacteria may proliferate, 
beneficial microbes may decline, and microbial diversity may decrease. This imbalance 
can lead to inflammation, digestive discomfort, increased intestinal permeability 
(“leaky gut”), and heightened susceptibility to chronic diseases. Supporting microbial 
balance through a diet that includes moderate consumption of polyphenol-rich, high-
quality coffee may help prevent or correct dysbiosis (Saygili 2024, Iriondo-DeHond 
2021, Sales 2020).
While coffee is not considered a primary cause of peptic or gastric ulcers, the microbial 
and mucosal health it promotes may provide indirect protection against conditions 
related to ulcers. This is particularly relevant, given that microbial imbalance, low-
quality food products, or exposure to mycotoxins often cause gastrointestinal irritation. 
These risks are more likely to stem from contaminated coffee than from the coffee 
itself.
IRRITABLE BOWEL SYNDROME (IBS)
Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) is a common gastrointestinal disorder indicated 
by symptoms that typically include abdominal pain or discomfort, bloating, and 
changes in bowel habits such as diarrhea, constipation, or alternating between both. 
IBS is a chronic condition that requires long-term management. According to the 
International Foundation for Gastrointestinal Disorders, IBS affects between 25 and 
45 million people in the United States alone, with a higher prevalence in women.
The exact cause of IBS is poorly understood, but it is believed to involve a combination 
of factors, including gut-brain interactions, motility issues, and heightened sensitivity 
in the intestines (Chey 2015, Camilleri 2018).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 91
The relationship between coffee consumption and IBS symptoms is complex and 
varies among individuals. Studies have shown mixed results regarding the impact 
of coffee on IBS symptoms. Some research indicates that caffeine can exacerbate 
symptoms like abdominal pain and diarrhea in individuals with IBS-D (diarrhea-
predominant IBS) by increasing intestinal motility and sensitivity (Simrén 2001). 
Conversely, other studies suggest moderate coffee consumption may not significantly 
worsen symptoms for all IBS patients. They may even have beneficial effects due to 
coffee’s ability to stimulate bowel movements, which could be helpful for those with 
IBS-C (constipation-predominant IBS) (Tulk 2008). Given the variable responses to 
coffee, some individuals may need to reduce or eliminate coffee from their diet. In 
contrast, others may continue to drink high-quality coffee in moderation without 
adverse effects.
COLORECTAL CANCER (CRC)
Coffee consumption has been associated with a significant reduction in the risk 
of developing colorectal cancer. A study published in the National Center for 
Biotechnology Information found that coffee consumption was linked to a 26 
percent reduction in the odds of developing CRC after adjusting for various risk 
factors (Schmit 2016). This inverse relationship suggests that coffee has protective 
properties against the development of colorectal cancer. Research findings from the 
American Cancer Society indicated that components other than caffeine in coffee 
might contribute to the reduced risk. Using data from 1.2 million men and women, 
they examined the link between drinking caffeinated and decaffeinated coffee. They 
found that coffee drinkers who did not smoke had a lower risk of death from colorectal 
cancer, and their risk was slightly lower if they drank decaffeinated coffee. 
In a study by Vitaglione et al. (2010), researchers suggested that coffee’s antioxidant 
dietary fiber plays a key role in combating colorectal cancer and preventing related 
colon diseases. They noted that this fiber might reduce inflammation in the colon 
mucosa through various mechanisms, thereby lowering the risk of colorectal cancer. 
The study also found that individuals who consumed more coffee excreted higher 
levels of carcinogens through their digestive systems than those who drank less.

92 ♦ Ildi Revi
The relationship between coffee consumption and colorectal cancer risk appears to 
be dose-dependent. Research has shown that increasing coffee consumption (decaf 
or regular) lowers the odds of developing colorectal cancer (Schmit 2016). They also 
concluded that caffeine is not the key component driving this inverse association. 
The protective effect was not observed with filtered or instant coffee, suggesting 
that diterpenes may play a central role. The same compounds (cafestol and kahweol) 
that showed beneficial effects in liver cancer studies also appear active in colorectal 
cancer prevention. In the small intestine, cafestol and kahweol activate a pathway 
that involves a protein called Nrf2. Nrf2 enters the cell nucleus and turns on specific 
genes, initiating a protective cellular response (Boettler 2011).
Many studies show that damage to our genetic material is a key factor in developing 
cancer, in addition to other human diseases. Research involving humans, animals, 
and lab settings indicates that coffee and its specific components (like CGA, NMP, 
caffeine, and diterpenoids) can help prevent DNA damage. The main ways coffee 
protects our DNA include reducing inflammation, preventing oxidative damage, and 
shielding us from harmful chemicals found in food that can damage our genes. This 
protective effect is due to coffee’s ability to boost antioxidants and detoxifying enzymes 
in our bodies. Due to these properties, coffee may help protect against certain types 
of cancer, such as liver cancer (Al-Serori 2019).
GI Health Recap
Coffee is not a direct cause of peptic or gastric ulcers, but gastrointestinal discomfort may result 
from contaminants such as mold and mycotoxins, especially in poorly processed, low-quality 
beans. These heat-stable toxins can survive roasting and contribute to symptoms like indigestion 
or acid reflux. However, high-quality, well-sourced coffee carries a lower risk of contamination and 
may even support gastrointestinal health through its polyphenols and other bioactive compounds. 
Research shows that regular coffee consumption is associated with a reduced risk of colorectal 
cancer and beneficial shifts in gut microbiota, potentially reducing inflammation and supporting 
mucosal integrity. By promoting microbial balance and mitigating dysbiosis, coffee may indirectly 
protect against gastrointestinal disorders when quality and individual sensitivity are both taken 
into account.

93
3.7 VARIOUS CANCERS
We have already looked at colorectal cancer. As discussed in the liver section, coffee’s 
protective effect against liver cancer is among its most impressive health benefits. These 
are just two examples of how coffee may help fight cancer. Numerous studies have 
demonstrated its preventive potential across various forms of the disease. This section 
on cancer will be brief, despite the abundant studies on coffee and various cancers. 
I have listed many articles in the references at the end of the book. Search “cancer” on 
those pages, and you will find in-depth journal studies. For a comprehensive overview 
of coffee and cancer, three meta-analyses are cited in the references: Arab et al. (2010), 
Crippa et al. (2014), and Pauwels et al. (2021). Also, when we examine compounds 
in the next chapter, their effects on cancer will be discussed.
In 2017, Miranda’s systematic review and meta-analysis of oral and pharyngeal cancers 
combined indicated that high coffee consumption is associated with a lower risk of 
developing these cancers than low coffee consumption, showing moderate variability 
among the studies. The data did not show a statistically significant association with 
oral cavity cancers, but high coffee consumption was linked to a reduced risk of 
pharyngeal cancers. No significant publication bias was detected. The findings suggest 
that coffee may offer protective benefits against oral and pharyngeal cancers. Coffee 
has also shown potential preventive effects against other types of cancer. 
Regarding prostate cancer, the impact of coffee appears to be more nuanced. While 
its effect on overall prostate cancer risk is largely neutral, evidence suggests it may 
be particularly effective in preventing aggressive prostate cancer. A 2012 study by 
Shafique et al. found that men who consumed three or more cups per day had a 55 
percent lower chance of developing high-grade prostate cancer, as indicated by a high 
Gleason score.

94 ♦ Ildi Revi
Recent studies on the relationship between coffee consumption and ovarian cancer 
risk have shown mixed results. A systematic review and meta-analysis found no 
link between coffee or caffeine intake and ovarian cancer risk, but still suggested 
that decaffeinated coffee might further reduce the risk. Another meta-analysis of 
prospective studies concluded no substantial association between coffee consumption 
and ovarian cancer risk, but an additional cup of coffee daily was marginally associated 
with an increased risk. Additionally, a study from the Swedish Mammography Cohort 
found no significant link between long-term coffee consumption and the incidence 
of invasive epithelial ovarian cancer among women (Larsson and Wolk 2005).
Large cohort studies, such as the EPIC (European Prospective Investigation into 
Cancer and Nutrition) cohort, have also not found a significant link between coffee 
consumption and ovarian cancer risk. Meta-analyses combining data from multiple 
studies have similarly concluded that there is insufficient evidence to support a 
protective effect of coffee against ovarian cancer (Pauwels and Volterrani 2021).
While the exact mechanisms are not fully understood, some research suggests that 
coffee’s antioxidant properties and potential influence on cell cycle regulation 
may contribute to its possible protective effects against certain cancers, including 
ovarian cancer. Most studies analyzing coffee intake and ovarian cancer risk focus 
on moderate coffee consumption, as excessive intake may have potentially negative 
health consequences. Additionally, factors like genetics, lifestyle, and other dietary 
components could influence how coffee impacts an individual’s ovarian cancer risk.
COFFEE COMPOUNDS AND CANCER PREVENTION
Several compounds in coffee may be effective against cancer, either alone or in 
combination:
1.	 Diterpenes: These compounds have been implicated in anticarcinogenic 
activity, stimulating biochemical processes that specifically target and 
neutralize harmful carcinogens (Ren 2019).
2.	 Caffeine: Observed to have anti-cancer qualities, specifically contributing 
to DNA repair. It helps prevent cancerous effects by addressing damage to 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 95
telomeres, which shorten as they become damaged, potentially leading to 
cancer (Liu 2016).
3.	 CGA: Play a significant role in coffee’s potential cancer-fighting properties. 
CGA affects insulin sensitivity, glucose concentration, and cancer risk. It has 
demonstrated anti-inflammatory effects and may help combat metabolic 
syndrome (see section on CGA in next chapter).
Horrigan et al. found in their 2006 study that caffeine also has anti-inflammatory 
qualities. Their report cites studies on rats suffering from toxic shock and hepatitis, 
and found that caffeine had a visible anti-inflammatory effect. They note that normal 
“doses relevant to coffee drinkers” can create these positive health effects.
Cancer Recap
Coffee has shown protective effects against several types of cancer, particularly colorectal and liver 
cancer. Higher coffee consumption is also associated with a reduced risk of oral and pharyngeal 
cancers, with especially strong evidence for pharyngeal cancer. While overall risk reduction for 
prostate cancer appears neutral, some studies suggest coffee may help prevent its more aggressive 
forms. Findings on ovarian cancer remain mixed, though there is some indication that decaffeinated 
coffee might lower risk. Coffee’s antioxidant properties and its role in regulating the cell cycle 
likely contribute to these potential anticancer effects.

96
3.8 Longevity
Coffee has been linked to reduced risk of what scientists call “all-cause mortality.” This 
means there is solid scientific evidence that coffee is part of a healthy lifestyle that leads 
to prolonged life (Freedman 2012, Loftfield 2015, Park 2017). How do they come 
up with this kind of information?
When scientists study all-cause mortality in relation to coffee consumption through 
epidemiological research, they aim to determine if there is a significant correlation 
between coffee consumption and the overall risk of death from any cause. These 
studies often employ large-scale cohort designs, in which participants report their 
coffee intake and are followed over several years. Researchers collect detailed data on 
confounding factors like diet, lifestyle, and medical history. Using statistical models, 
they adjust for these variables to estimate the hazard ratios for death associated with 
different levels of coffee consumption. By comparing mortality rates among groups 
with varying coffee intake and controlling for these confounders, researchers can 
determine whether coffee consumption is associated with a reduced or increased risk 
of death. This research helps uncover potential mechanisms behind coffee’s health 
effects and guides public health recommendations.
This type of research tells us the following:
1.	 Association with Overall Mortality Rate: Studies on all-cause mortality aim to 
determine whether there is a statistically significant correlation between coffee 
consumption and reduced or increased mortality rates from any cause. For 
example, a study published in the New England Journal of Medicine found that 
individuals who drank three to five cups of coffee daily had a 10 percent lower 
risk of all-cause mortality than those who did not drink coffee (Freedman 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 97
2012). Causes ranged from cardiovascular, respiratory and infectious diseases 
to diabetes, injuries and accidents.
2.	 Risk Reduction or Increase: These studies may show that regular coffee 
consumption is associated with a lower risk of dying from various causes (such 
as cardiovascular disease, cancer, and others) compared to non-coffee drinkers. 
Research published in the American Journal of Epidemiology found that regular 
coffee drinkers had a significantly lower risk of death from cardiovascular 
disease. Those who consumed two to three cups per day had an 18% reduced 
risk compared to non-drinkers (Loftfield 2015).
3.	 Potential Mechanisms: A Journal of Clinical Nutrition study pointed to coffee’s 
CGA and other antioxidants as inflammation and oxidative stress reducers, 
possibly impacting telomere length. The study found that higher coffee 
consumption was associated with longer telomeres among the subjects (Liu 
2016).
Naturally, we would all like to know exactly how coffee contributes to longevity. Coffee 
is a complex beverage with beneficial bioactive compounds that may affect our health. 
Evidence also suggests coffee affects aging (Lopes and Cunha 2024).
TELOMERES… WONDROUS TO BEHOLD
The relationship between coffee and telomeres is, to me, one of the most fascinating 
and promising areas of research. Before exploring coffee’s potential role, we’ll take a 
deeper look at telomeres themselves, structures we briefly encountered in the caffeine 
section. These protective caps on our DNA hold a compelling biological story that’s 
well worth examining in greater detail.
In 2009, the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded to the team that 
discovered telomeres and their association with longevity. This team comprised 
Elizabeth Blackburn from the University of California, San Francisco, Carol Greider 
from Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine in Baltimore, and Jack Szostak 
from Massachusetts General Hospital in Boston. The three scientists originally met in 
1980 and subsequently began their collaboration, which ultimately led to their shared 

98 ♦ Ildi Revi
Nobel Prize. Their combined work has demonstrated how DNA would eventually 
shorten and degrade during the normal cell replication process if parts of telomeres 
were missing or shortened. Their early research found that this would lead to slower cell 
division and premature aging in yeast cells, a finding later confirmed in human cells.
Every cell in our human body has a nucleus filled with DNA. This DNA is bundled 
into shapes we call chromosomes. Humans normally have 23 pairs of chromosomes. 
Millions of our cells, including those in our skin, blood, and bones, divide as we 
grow and change. Each time cells divide, your DNA unwinds and is copied through 
a complex process. 
Understanding the structure and function of chromosomes and their protective 
telomeres provides a foundation for exploring how lifestyle factors, such as coffee 
consumption, may influence cellular aging and overall health. Discovered in the 1970s, 
telomeres are special DNA sequences at the end of each chromosome. Many people 
compare them to the plastic end of a shoelace. They are there to hold things together 
and keep the laces from unraveling. Telomeres protect the DNA in chromosomes. 
They are important because they prevent chromosomes from becoming mutated over 
time. Protecting chromosomes from damage is important because DNA damage can 
lead to many disease conditions.
When your cells divide, the unwrapped and copied DNA is protected by the 
chromosomal end cap, known as the telomere. During this process, the telomere 
naturally becomes shorter. Therefore, each time cells divide, their telomeres are 
shortened until they are completely degraded. When that occurs, the cell receives 
a signal that its DNA is no longer properly protected. Because the required DNA 
cannot be copied anymore, the cell self-destructs. This is known as cell senescence or 
cell death. Cells can normally only divide about 50-70 times before their telomeres 
are degraded. Telomere length generally decreases with age (Weischer 2012).
While damage to telomeres occurs naturally as part of cell division, it can also happen 
independently due to external forces. Notably, this is due to the body’s response 
to stress, including the accumulation of oxidative stress, as well as factors such as 
smoking and obesity (Muraki 2012). This process of general telomere shortening has 
been associated with aging, cancer, and the risk of death. It is essential to note that 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 99
in certain tissues, such as the heart muscle, where cells do not undergo continuous 
division, telomeres do not shorten. 
The body has evolved to counteract telomere degradation in certain cells through the 
use of an enzyme called telomerase. This enzyme acts naturally over time to replace 
telomere DNA as it is degraded. It works by adding DNA to the ends of telomeres, 
primarily in young cells, thereby preventing them from degrading excessively. Yet, as 
cells continue to divide over time, there is not enough telomerase to protect them all, 
and thus most telomeres get shorter with age. 
This enzyme remains active in sperm and egg cells, allowing us to pass on healthy 
DNA to future generations. If telomerase were not active in our reproductive cells, 
we would quickly go extinct. Studying telomerase, how to control it, and potential 
therapies utilizing it are all active research areas within the medical community. Some 
even believe telomerase could be harnessed to control cancer or slow or stop aging. 
There is evidence that we can influence the process of telomere degradation for better 
or worse, depending on our lifestyle choices.
EFFECT OF TELOMERE LENGTH AND LONGEVITY
Research has connected healthy lifestyle choices with longer telomere lengths and 
healthier aging (Balan 2018). A study on older people found that telomere length 
was positively associated with years of healthy life, inferring that they experienced a 
healthier aging process (Njajou 2009). The authors suggest that this research may 
indicate a relationship between telomere length and overall well-being in older 
individuals. Another study, specifically focusing on centenarians over the age of 97, 
found that healthier individuals had significantly longer telomeres than their less 
healthy counterparts, who had more instances of age-related diseases (Terry 2008).
Several studies demonstrate the positive effect of a healthy diet on telomeres. Studies 
have generally found that increasing consumption of antioxidant-rich plant foods 
and Mediterranean-style diets maintains telomere length (Freitas-Simoes 2016). 
Evidence also suggests that physical activity may be associated with longer telomere 
length (Denham 2016). Still, there is no consensus on the optimal exercise dose that 
yields the most benefits (Balan 2018). Running and endurance activities have shown 

100 ♦ Ildi Revi
promise in various studies (Denham 2016), but not all results have been conclusive. 
Research suggests that mind-body activities like yoga and meditation may preserve 
telomeres, slow cell senescence and death, and sustain health by mitigating life stressors 
(To-Miles and Backman, 2016). 
On the other hand, there is plenty of evidence that associates shortened, aged telomeres 
with various adverse health effects. Shorter telomeres have been linked to an increased 
risk of disease, all-cause mortality, cardiovascular diseases, and age-related metabolic 
disorders (Fasching 2018). In a compelling 19-year study following almost 20,000 
individuals, shorter telomeres were associated with people who experienced heart 
disease, heart attacks, and early death (Weischer 2012). Another study concluded 
that people over 60 years old with shorter telomeres had a higher mortality rate from 
age-related diseases (Cawthon 2003). Shorter telomeres have not only been associated 
with age but with chronic, preventable diseases, including hypertension (Tellechea 
and Pirola 2016), type 2 diabetes (Matboli 2018), and obesity (Mundstock 2015).
Research shows that stresses on the body brought on by negative lifestyle choices 
and experiences can influence and be influenced by telomere length. Certain dietary 
choices have been shown to adversely affect telomere length. One example is that 
sugar-sweetened beverages are associated with shorter telomeres (Freitas-Simoes 
2018). Other studies have shown that processed meat intake was associated with 
shorter telomere lengths (Nettleton 2008). A review found that in seven studies, a 
reverse association between telomere length and intake of grains, processed meat, fats 
and oils, and sugar-sweetened beverages was reported (Rafie 2016). Multiple studies 
have supported other choices, such as smoking (Astuti 2017), which results in shorter 
telomere lengths. In addition to choices, psychological stressors, such as Post Traumatic 
Stress Disorder (Li 2017), depression (Darrow 2016), and trauma (Jiménez 2019; Li 
2017), result in shorter telomere lengths.
WHAT DOES COFFEE HAVE TO DO WITH ALL OF THIS?
Coffee drinkers have been found to have longer telomeres. In a landmark 2016 study 
by Jason J. Liu and colleagues from the National Cancer Institute, Harvard Medical 
School, and Harvard School of Public Health, data from the comprehensive Nurses’ 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 101
Health Study, derived from nearly 5,000 subjects, was used to establish a link between 
coffee consumption and telomere length.
The Nurses’ Health Study was a prospective cohort study that began in 1976 when 
121,700 female registered nurses aged 30–55 completed an initial questionnaire. 
Personal information, such as lifestyle and dietary factors, was subsequently updated 
every 2–4 years through responses to questionnaires. From 1989 to 1990, blood 
samples were collected from 32,826 of these participants. For the Liu study, coffee 
and caffeine consumption levels were obtained from the survey administered in 1990. 
Participants could select categories of coffee consumption: Zero, less than one, one 
to two, two to three, and more than three cups daily. Ultimately, 4,780 of the 32,000 
nurses were identified as eligible for the current study on telomere length.
With this data, researchers examined the associations between total coffee consumption 
and telomere length after adjusting for covariates such as age, smoking, BMI, physical 
activity, alcohol consumption, and trans-fat consumption, among others. In this way, 
they could be sure that the observed effects were related to coffee consumption and 
not one of these potentially related lifestyle factors. They found that, compared to 
non-coffee drinkers, those who drank two to three cups of coffee per day and more 
than three cups of coffee per day had 1.29 and 1.36 times the odds of having longer 
telomere length, respectively. After adjusting for decaffeinated coffee consumption, 
they found a significant direct relationship with longer telomeres for higher caffeinated 
coffee consumption. 
Why is there a difference between decaf and caffeinated coffee? Fewer people drink 
decaf, so there’s less data on it. We’ll cover more about decaf in the farming and 
processing section. For now, know that decaf can also be high in antioxidants, though 
caffeine helps antioxidants function in the body.
Besides the different concentrations of chemicals in caffeinated and decaffeinated 
coffee, one study (conducted almost 20 years ago; society and processes have changed 
since then) indicated that caffeinated coffee drinkers had distinct personal traits 
compared to decaffeinated coffee drinkers (Shlonsky 2003). These traits could modify 
or be potential confounders of the associations between consumption levels and 

102 ♦ Ildi Revi
telomere length. It is essential to note that this is just one study; further research is 
necessary to clarify the role of coffee consumption in telomere biology.
The full mechanism by which coffee affects telomere length is yet to be completely 
elucidated, and it is an active area of research. Future studies will provide a more 
mechanistic understanding of how specific compounds in coffee are involved in 
telomere maintenance.
Although the Liu study is the only study specifically seeking to understand the 
association between coffee consumption and telomere length, other studies have 
indirectly supported these results. One study of 40 chronic hepatitis C patients found 
that telomere length was significantly longer (by as much as 40 percent in 89 percent 
of study participants) in patients who consumed four cups of coffee per day for 30 
days (Cardin 2013). This study was designed as an intervention study, in which some 
patients were asked to drink four cups of coffee daily for 30 days, while others were 
asked to abstain from coffee for the same period. These results are impressive not 
only because of the short study period (only one month) but also because the authors 
demonstrated in vivo that coffee consumption induced a reduction in oxidative DNA 
damage, which correlated with increased telomere length. It was noted that oxidative 
DNA damage leads to telomere shortening, an event linked to chromosomal instability 
(Rudolph 2009). 
These studies have shown a compelling relationship between coffee consumption and 
telomere length, suggesting that coffee may contribute to longevity and decrease the 
risk of age-related diseases. Coffee’s antioxidants and bioactive compounds promote 
cellular health by preserving telomere length. This preservation is important as it can 
help slow aging and enhance overall health. 
While the evidence is promising, research into the mechanisms by which coffee affects 
telomere biology is still in its early stages. I want to investigate whether holistic coffee has 
a greater impact on telomeres compared to conventional coffee. In 2023, a study linked 
soluble coffee with shortening telomeres, but the soluble coffee could have been of any 
quality and blended with sugars, powdered milk, and other ingredients. I did not reference 
it here because I didn’t think it was relevant without knowing the type of coffee it was. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 103
Longevity Recap
Regular coffee consumption has been associated with a lower risk of death from a wide range 
of causes, including cardiovascular disease, certain cancers, respiratory and infectious diseases, 
diabetes, and even accidents. Studies suggest that drinking two to five cups per day may reduce 
overall mortality by 10 to 18 percent, with particularly notable reductions in deaths from heart 
disease. These benefits are believed to stem from coffee’s rich profile of antioxidants and bioactive 
compounds, especially chlorogenic acids, which help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress.
Coffee may also play a role in healthy aging by supporting DNA protection and longevity. 
Compounds in coffee have been linked to longer telomeres—the protective caps on the ends of 
chromosomes that naturally shorten with age. Shorter telomeres are associated with age-related 
diseases, while longer ones are linked to slower cellular aging. Coffee’s potential to preserve 
telomere length and limit DNA damage may contribute to its protective effects against chronic 
diseases, though more research is needed to understand these mechanisms fully.

104
4
Coffee Compounds: 
Benefits and Concerns
This chapter is intended to provide an underpinning of knowledge and insight into 
the main compounds discussed throughout the rest of the book, from their roles in 
health, farming, and the environment to their effects during roasting and brewing. 
We will begin by examining the most studied compounds related to coffee’s health 
impacts and provide details on their effects.
I have streamlined information on each of these compounds and have cited key 
sources, focusing on details most relevant to those involved in the production chain of 
coffee. There is extensive information on caffeine worldwide, and I recommend going 
to well-cited books and scientific journal articles rather than blog posts or websites that 
do not cite sources. Interestingly, there is a lot of emotional and biased information 
about caffeine on many websites and social media posts, which often paints coffee 
with the same brush, ignoring that coffee can be decaffeinated and still provide many 
health benefits (discussed later in this book). To be clear, coffee is not caffeine; rather, 
caffeine is a compound found in coffee. You may grow tired of hearing me say this 
throughout the book… I suppose it is my proverbial “albatross.”

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 105
HIGHLIGHTS
 
This chapter explores the major bioactive compounds in coffee—caffeine, CGA, trigonelline, 
diterpenes, melanoidins, and several emerging compounds—and their roles in human health, 
disease prevention, and environmental considerations. Far more than just a caffeine vehicle, coffee 
is a chemically rich and complex beverage whose effects are driven by the synergistic actions of 
dozens of health-relevant constituents.
Caffeine, though the most well-known compound in coffee, is often misunderstood. It acts as 
a stimulant, anti-inflammatory agent, and central nervous system modulator, with potential 
protective effects against neurodegenerative diseases, depression, liver fibrosis, and some cancers. 
However, its effects vary widely depending on genetics, medication use, and metabolic differences. 
This section clarifies caffeine’s mechanisms, safety thresholds, and its role in the broader health 
matrix of coffee.
Chlorogenic acids (CGA) are potent polyphenols responsible for many of coffee’s antioxidant, 
anti-inflammatory, and metabolic benefits. They help regulate blood sugar, cholesterol, and blood 
pressure, and support gut, brain, liver, and cardiovascular health. CGA content is significantly 
influenced by cultivation practices, processing, roast level, and storage—making it a key compound 
in coffee graded for health rather than flavor alone.
Trigonelline is another remarkable compound with neuroprotective, antidiabetic, and 
antimicrobial properties. It serves as a natural precursor to vitamin B3 (niacin) and is converted 
into bioactive metabolites during roasting. Trigonelline works synergistically with CGA and 
caffeine, further supporting coffee’s functional potential in metabolic and cognitive health.
Diterpenes, specifically cafestol and kahweol, are lipid-based compounds found primarily in 
unfiltered coffee. They have shown hepatoprotective and anti-carcinogenic effects in lab studies, 
but may increase LDL cholesterol depending on the brewing method. Their dual nature—both 
beneficial and potentially problematic—makes them an important compound to monitor in 
coffee designed for health outcomes.
Melanoidins, formed during the Maillard reaction in roasting, contribute to coffee’s color, flavor, 
and aroma, but also offer significant health benefits. They act as dietary antioxidants, prebiotics, 
and metal chelators, helping to support gut health, reduce colon cancer risk, and inhibit oxidative 
stress and lipid peroxidation.

106 ♦ Ildi Revi
Other notable compounds include flavonoids, lignins, lignans, and beta-carbolines, each 
offering additional anti-inflammatory, hormone-modulating, or neuroprotective effects. 
N-methylpyridinium (NMP), formed during roasting, may help reduce oxidative stress and 
support liver detoxification. At the same time, phenylindanes—found mostly in dark roasts—may 
help prevent amyloid plaque accumulation in neurodegenerative diseases.
Throughout the chapter, special attention is given to compound variability—how agricultural 
practices, roast levels, brewing methods, and genetic differences in consumers influence the 
presence and bioavailability of coffee’s health-related molecules. Readers will learn why certain 
coffees are more protective than others, why some people feel worse after drinking coffee, and 
how health-focused production methods can preserve beneficial compounds while reducing 
harmful ones.
By understanding these compounds not just in isolation but in interaction, this chapter provides 
the foundation for evaluating coffee as a healthy beverage—one that can be grown, processed, 
roasted, and brewed with health as a measurable, science-based goal.

107
4.1 Caffeine
Coffee is not the same thing as caffeine. 
Caffeine is not the same thing as coffee.
Coffee is commonly treated in the media as caffeine only, and that is exhausting. Just 
recently, I read an article from a fairly reputable news company with “coffee” in the 
title, but the entire piece talked about recent caffeine research and did not mention 
coffee at all. 
This oversimplification likely persists because caffeine is the most well-known and 
easily understood compound in coffee. It’s a stimulant with clear, measurable effects on 
alertness and energy, making it convenient for headlines and soundbites. In contrast, 
the other thousands of compounds in coffee, like CGA, antioxidants, and melanoidins, 
are more complex to explain and less familiar to the general public.
Caffeine is just one of the many bioactive components found in coffee. Decaffeinated 
coffee is suitable for individuals who need to avoid caffeine, while still providing 
many of the health benefits of regular coffee, such as containing CGA, but without 
caffeine. Yet, because caffeine is a psychoactive compound with immediate, noticeable 
effects, it has dominated discussions about coffee’s health impacts. This has created a 
narrative where coffee is often reduced to a caffeine shot, overshadowing the complex 
interactions between caffeine and other beneficial compounds.
Historically, caffeine was singled out in scientific research due to its stimulating effects, 
linking it to studies on addiction, sleep disruption, and cardiovascular health. This 
focus set the tone for public discourse, reinforcing the misconception that coffee’s 
value lies solely in its caffeine content. Additionally, the caffeine industry, including 

108 ♦ Ildi Revi
energy drinks, supplements, and pharmaceuticals, has a vested interest in promoting 
caffeine’s stimulating effects, further perpetuating this narrative.
Because this is a book on coffee and health, this section on caffeine provides a 
comprehensive yet concise explanation of its effects on health while offering more 
in-depth information than typically found in popular literature. Coffee’s effects on 
alertness and focus have fascinated and sparked controversy since its earliest human 
consumption. Caffeine has been studied scientifically for the past two hundred years, 
beginning with German chemist Friedlieb Ferdinand Runge, the first to isolate caffeine 
from coffee beans in 1820. Since then, quite a lot of information, from scientific 
research to folklore and including accurate and inaccurate claims, has been produced 
on coffee primarily due to its caffeine content.
Focusing solely on caffeine misses the bigger picture. Coffee is a chemically rich 
beverage whose health effects result from the interactions of numerous bioactive 
compounds. These compounds can amplify, mitigate, or modify the effects of caffeine, 
leading to outcomes that are far more nuanced than those from caffeine alone.
To gain a more accurate understanding of how coffee influences health, we need 
to move beyond the caffeine-centric narrative. This requires exploring the intricate 
chemical composition of coffee as a whole. Only then can we appreciate coffee not 
just as a caffeine delivery beverage, but as a complex, functional food with diverse 
health impacts.
Caffeine is a bitter chemical compound classified as a central nervous system stimulant. 
According to Harvard’s School of Public Health and other sources, it is the most 
widely consumed psychoactive substance in the world (Harvard 2024). It is found 
in the leaves, seeds, and fruits of coffee, tea, cocoa, cola, and guarana plants. These 
plants and their derivatives are commonly used to reduce fatigue, increase alertness, 
and improve mood.
Caffeine enters the bloodstream quickly, passing through the lining of the mouth, 
throat, esophagus, and stomach. Within ten minutes of drinking coffee, the first 
part of the intestine absorbs the caffeine, reaching its maximum concentration in the 
bloodstream within an hour (Grzegorzewski 2022). 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 109
In the online community, there seems to be confusion about caffeine’s diuretic 
properties. Current research suggests that coffee’s reputation as a strong diuretic 
appears to be outdated. While caffeine does have mild diuretic properties, studies show 
this effect is much weaker than traditionally assumed. A 2014 study demonstrated 
that moderate daily coffee consumption provides similar hydrating qualities to water 
and does not cause dehydration (Killer 2014).
The evidence is clear: only exceptionally high caffeine doses (6 mg/kg) produce any 
meaningful diuretic effect, while normal consumption levels (3 mg/kg) don’t disturb 
the body’s fluid balance (Seal et al. 2017). Given that coffee is 98-99 percent water, it 
actually contributes to hydration. The widespread belief that coffee dehydrates appears 
to be inconsistent with modern scientific understanding.
RESEARCH ON CAFFEINE AND HEALTH
Caffeine is more than just a stimulant. While known primarily for boosting alertness 
and energy, it interacts with multiple body systems and influences various physiological 
processes. Recent studies show caffeine’s effects extend from brain function to 
metabolic balance, challenging the narrow view many hold. Its role in neural pathways, 
including mood and neurotransmitter modulation, demonstrates the need for a 
broader understanding of its effects.
Undeniably, many people drink coffee to ward off drowsiness and stay alert, but the 
list of positive effects of caffeine in coffee is long, and not just dealing with alertness 
and focus (Saraiva 2023; Institute of Medicine 2001). Thousands of studies with 
hundreds of thousands of subjects show that natural coffee with its caffeine intact 
(not just laboratory-created caffeine) is potentially beneficial in:
•	
Protecting against neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer’s and 
Parkinson’s by enhancing brain function, stimulating neuronal growth, 
and reducing inflammation.
•	
Reducing the likelihood of depression by modulating mood, 
neurotransmitters, and hormonal levels.

110 ♦ Ildi Revi
•	
Supporting weight management by increasing energy expenditure, fat 
oxidation, and appetite suppression.
•	
Lowering the risk of some types of cancer by inhibiting DNA damage and 
modulating immune responses.
Caffeine’s primary mechanism involves blocking adenosine, a neurotransmitter found 
in all body cells that plays a vital role in energy transfer. Adenosine interacts with 
specific cell receptors, typically having a calming effect by inducing sleepiness, slowing 
down nerve cell activity, and reducing dopamine levels. Caffeine acts as an adenosine 
receptor antagonist, competing for the same binding sites and preventing adenosine’s 
calming effects. This blocking action increases the release of dopamine, noradrenaline, 
and glutamate, promoting alertness (Carman 2011, Ferré 1997, Nehlig 1992).
Once absorbed, caffeine efficiently passes through cellular membranes and circulates 
to tissues. The liver metabolizes caffeine into three active compounds: paraxanthine 
(increases alertness), theobromine (contributes to mood elevation), and theophylline 
(relaxes the respiratory system). Theophylline’s ability to relax airway muscles and 
reduce lung response to irritants makes it valuable as a pharmaceutical treatment for 
asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) (Grzegorzewski 2022). 
Both my father and grandmother were right to give me coffee when my asthma flared 
up.
Current research reveals that adenosine’s effects are complex and sometimes 
paradoxical, particularly regarding inflammation and tissue healing. Adenosine has 
several receptor subtypes, each with unique roles:
•	
A1 receptors inhibit neurotransmitter release by reducing cyclic AMP 
levels, promoting sleep, reducing cardiac oxygen demand, and providing 
neuroprotection under stress (Fredholm 2001).
•	
A2A receptors have excitatory effects by increasing cyclic AMP levels, playing 
roles in cardiovascular function, vasodilation, and modulating dopaminergic 
activity relevant to Parkinson’s disease (Cunha 2005).
•	
A2B receptors also increase cyclic AMP levels but are less specific, involved 
in inflammatory responses and immune functions (Feoktistov 1997).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 111
•	
A3 receptors affect cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) and calcium 
levels, influencing cell growth and apoptosis (programmed cell death that 
eliminates damaged cells). These receptors are involved in anti-inflammatory 
and immune responses, with ongoing research exploring their potential in 
treating cancer and inflammatory diseases (Gessi 2008).
These receptors operate in complex networks, contributing to adenosine’s wide range 
of physiological and pharmacological effects.
In certain body parts, caffeine modulates intracellular levels of cAMP, playing an 
important role in metabolism and promoting insulin release from pancreatic beta 
cells, which are essential for regulating blood glucose levels and ensuring cellular 
energy supply. Increasing cAMP levels can prevent the activation of cells that cause 
inflammation, like T cells. In T cells, cAMP acts like a messenger that tells the cell 
to slow down or reduce certain activities. Specifically, it decreases the production 
of cytokines (proteins that T cells produce to communicate with other cells and 
coordinate the immune response). It reduces the T cell’s ability to kill infected or 
cancerous cells (cytotoxicity), and this helps regulate the immune response, preventing 
it from becoming too aggressive. (Sitkovsky 1988). 
Cytokines are signaling molecules that mediate and regulate immunity and 
inflammation. Cytokine production and cytotoxicity downregulation prevent 
excessive immune responses, which can lead to tissue damage, chronic inflammation, 
and autoimmune diseases. They also help maintain a delicate balance in the immune 
system, ensuring it remains effective against threats without turning against the body 
itself (Wu 2020). By reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines and increasing anti-
inflammatory cytokines, caffeine can help manage and reduce inflammation in the 
body.
The effects of caffeine on cytokines are the primary reason a significant amount of 
literature suggests that caffeine is considered to have anti-inflammatory properties. 
Inflammation is associated with oxidative stress (León 2019), an imbalance between 
the production and accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells and the 
body’s antioxidant system detoxifying these reactive products. ROS are free radicals, 
unstable molecules that can damage cells, proteins, and DNA by stealing electrons 
from them. They are produced naturally in your body, but can come from external 

112 ♦ Ildi Revi
sources like pollution, environmental contaminants, and certain foods. Antioxidants 
fight free radicals, preventing oxidative damage and supporting cellular health. 
Caffeine has been shown to effectively inhibit all three ROS species, supporting its 
role in reducing oxidative stress and promoting antioxidant activity (Scalbert 2000). 
In other words, caffeine helps reduce oxidative stress by enhancing the activity of 
antioxidant enzymes and reducing the production of ROS. So, while caffeine is not 
an antioxidant, it plays a supportive role in enhancing the body’s overall antioxidant 
defense system.
When the liver metabolizes caffeine, paraxanthine is produced as the main compound. 
Significant amounts of theobromine are also produced. Theobromine has exhibited 
antioxidant properties (Azam 2003), and paraxanthine has been shown to inhibit 
the production of the inflammatory marker Tumor Necrosis Factor-alpha (TNF-α), 
which is another cytokine (León 2019). Although TNF-α promotes inflammation 
to help fight infections and other threats, excessive TNF-α can lead to chronic 
inflammation associated with diseases like rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel 
disease, and psoriasis. Caffeine is anti-inflammatory by inhibiting TNF-α production 
(Rodas 2022). Caffeine’s anti-inflammatory effects, partly due to inhibiting TNF-α 
production, are also linked to CGA, which will be discussed in the next section.
Caffeine mitigates oxidative stress, a condition linked to anxiety, depression, and 
diseases related to aging, atherosclerosis, and neurodegenerative disorders. By reducing 
oxidative stress, caffeine shields cell membranes from damage by preventing lipid 
peroxidation. Lipid peroxidation is the oxidative degradation of lipids, especially 
polyunsaturated fatty acids, in cell membranes. This process is triggered by reactive 
oxygen species (ROS) and results in the formation of damaging byproducts that can 
trigger inflammation, damage DNA and proteins, and contribute to chronic diseases 
like atherosclerosis, neurodegeneration, and cancer. This protective role of caffeine 
seems paradoxical, given its common association with anxiety. This paradox may be 
attributed to individual differences in caffeine metabolism, which demands further 
research and exploration.
Additionally, caffeine has been shown to have multiple effects that can contribute 
to weight management. In addition to reducing oxidative stress, caffeine enhances 
fat oxidation, breaking down fat energy stores and potentially reducing body fat 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 113
over time. Caffeine increases metabolic rate as a stimulant to the central nervous 
system, allowing the body to burn more calories at rest and during physical activity. 
Furthermore, caffeine is an appetite suppressant, helping reduce overall calorie intake 
and also discourages overeating. These combined effects (increased fat breakdown, 
higher metabolic rate, and decreased appetite) make caffeine a practical and common 
tool in weight management programs.
As discussed in the previous chapter, coffee has been shown to reduce the risk of 
various diseases. When examining caffeine specifically, several studies using standard 
rodent models have demonstrated the beneficial effects of coffee and caffeine extract 
against liver fibrosis. In nearly every study, coffee effectively blocked toxin-induced 
liver fibrosis and cirrhosis.
One key study involving rats provides important insights into how different coffee 
components affect liver health. Scientists induced liver disease in the animals and 
tested regular coffee, decaffeinated coffee, and a water-caffeine solution. Regular 
coffee produced the most impactful positive results, showing that caffeine alone 
wasn’t responsible for the liver benefits. “A reduction in hepatic TGF-β1 expression 
in the group receiving decaffeinated coffee indicates that caffeine may not be the sole 
hepatoprotective compound in coffee beverages” (Furtado 2012). While caffeine 
may help prevent liver injury, with fibrosis and inflammation being lower in animals 
receiving regular coffee and caffeine, the study reveals that other compounds in coffee 
also contribute significantly to liver protection.
As we saw in the section on longevity, caffeine plays a role, promoting the expression 
of telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT), which is responsible for extending the 
telomere length, preventing cellular deterioration and loss of division/growth capacity. 
Additionally, caffeine has been proven to improve microscopic structural changes of 
the thymus, spleen and liver due to aging, which helps clarify different ways coffee 
benefits health regarding longevity (Tao 2021).

114 ♦ Ildi Revi
CAUTIONARY NOTES ON CAFFEINE
EVERYBODY METABOLIZES CAFFEINE DIFFERENTLY
Cells in our bodies contain numerous components, including cytochromes– 
enzymes essential for energy generation and the metabolism of drugs and toxins. The 
cytochrome P450 CYP1A2 enzyme is primarily responsible for metabolizing caffeine 
in the liver. Genetics significantly impacts how this enzyme functions and is a major 
reason people react to coffee and caffeine differently. 
Some people carry genetic variations that help them metabolize caffeine quickly and 
may experience a shorter-lived caffeine buzz. In contrast, slow metabolizers are often 
more sensitive to caffeine, experiencing heightened effects and an increased risk of 
adverse reactions like jitteriness and insomnia. The slow metabolizing of caffeine paired 
with excessive caffeine intake has also been associated with arrhythmias (abnormal 
heart rhythms), anxiety, headaches, moodiness, upset stomach and other issues.
One study followed 1,180 people with early-stage high blood pressure for 7.5 years 
and found that slow caffeine metabolizers were much more likely to develop kidney 
issues and high blood pressure if they drank more than 3 cups of coffee daily (Mahdavi
