2023). This was not the case for people who carried the genetic variation that processed 
caffeine quickly and were not observed to have an association between coffee intake 
and albuminuria, hyperfiltration, or hypertension. These findings suggest that caffeine 
may lead to the development of kidney disease in certain susceptible people.
Research conducted on those who take caffeine during pregnancy showed that it 
easily passes through the placenta to the fetus. Caffeine metabolism is slower, with a 
half-life of up to ten hours in pregnant women and even longer (up to 100 hours) in 
the fetus, premature infants and newborns (Aranda 2020). Caffeine metabolites are 
also present in breast milk and, combined with caffeine itself, can be 2.5 times greater 
than that of caffeine alone (McNamara 2004). While caffeine metabolism improves 
in infants after about a year, it remains slower in children than in adults, making them 
more vulnerable to the negative effects of caffeine.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 115
CAFFEINE AND MEDICATIONS
It is important to state that certain medications can impact how a person metabolizes 
caffeine, and caffeine may also affect those taking certain medications (Hladun 2021, 
Belayneh 2020, Zafar 2018). Because caffeine can interact with several medications, 
patients taking caffeine-containing drugs or coffee drinkers using medications 
metabolized by CYP1A2 may require dosage adjustments or need to avoid coffee 
altogether. 
Factors like age, pregnancy, liver health, diet, smoking habits, and certain medications 
such as omeprazole may increase the activity of CYP1A2. Many pharmaceutical drugs, 
including theophylline, fluvoxamine, quinolone antibiotics, verapamil, cimetidine, 
and oral contraceptives, can inhibit CYP1A2 activity. These interactions can affect 
caffeine metabolism and elimination, increasing the risk of side effects. In some 
cases, combining these drugs with caffeine can be lethal at high levels, especially for 
individuals with slow-metabolizing genetics, advanced age or other health conditions.
The metabolizing of caffeine is a highly personalized experience for each individual 
due to factors like genetics, age, and medication use. This variability may also explain 
why some people who normally have no problem with caffeine suddenly develop issues 
if they are taking any of the mentioned drugs without being informed of potential 
side effects. Another consideration is that regular consumption of caffeine can lead 
to tolerance, which might impact those who stop drinking coffee while on certain 
medications. The effects may change again once they finish the medication and return 
to their normal coffee drinking. 
There is a growing list of drugs that scientists are discovering that interact adversely 
with caffeine and can affect how our bodies metabolize caffeine. Check with reputable 
sources for the most up-to-date list if you are on medication. In addition to those 
mentioned earlier, caffeine has been shown to impact the following:
•	
Blood thinners like warfarin (Coumadin) can increase the risk of bleeding. 
Similarly, anticoagulant drugs have altered effects.
•	
Several oral contraceptives can slow the metabolism of caffeine, even in fast 
metabolizers of caffeine.

116 ♦ Ildi Revi
•	
Some medications for thyroid conditions are absorbed and metabolized 
differently.
•	
Quinolone antibiotics and osteoporosis drugs (Bisphosphonates) are not 
as effective.
•	
Mexiletine (Mexitil), which is prescribed to treat irregular heart rhythms, 
can interact with caffeine.
•	
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), a group of drugs used to 
treat depression, can have interactions with caffeine.
•	
Disulfiram (Antabuse), used to support the treatment of chronic alcoholism, 
is absorbed and metabolized differently.
Drug interactions between caffeine and other psychoactive drugs may lead to caffeine-
related or medication-related side effects. For example, using ketamine in combination 
with caffeine enhances its stimulant responses and lethal risk, which suggests a 
potentially toxic interaction between these two compounds. Another example is the 
interaction between caffeine and antiepileptic drugs, which can increase the frequency 
of seizures. Caffeine can also increase the effects of stimulants such as ephedrine or 
pseudoephedrine, which can cause nervousness, anxiety, or heart problems. It also 
decreases the effects of sedatives, such as diazepam or lorazepam, which can cause 
drowsiness, confusion, or impaired coordination.
Given the number and complexity of potential interactions with medications, 
individuals should check with their doctor or pharmacist for recommendations 
regarding the level of caffeine consumption when beginning or ending any medication.
CAFFEINE: POTENTIAL HEALTH IMPACTS 
OF STRESS AND POOR SLEEP
Recent research highlights that chronic stress can significantly impact physical health, 
contributing to conditions like high blood pressure, heart disease, and a weakened 
immune system. Effective stress management is important for overall well-being, and 
many wellness professionals recommend that patients monitor their caffeine intake 
during periods of significant stress. Glucocorticoids and catecholamines are hormones 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 117
that impact the body’s response to stress, and caffeine has been shown to elevate 
glucocorticoid and catecholamine output, increasing blood pressure (Lovallo 2005). 
This is why caffeine intake during stress can prolong and intensify blood pressure 
and stress endocrine responses. People who do not regularly consume caffeine may 
experience a particularly negative impact on cortisol, a steroid hormone secreted by 
the adrenal gland. In healthy young people who consume caffeine daily, these effects 
are reduced, though not eliminated. 
Cortisol, our primary stress hormone, is crucial in slowing down bodily functions that 
would be nonessential or harmful in a fight-or-flight situation (Lovallo 2005). As such, 
cortisol increases glucose in the bloodstream and the availability of substances in the 
body that repair tissues. Cortisol also helps regulate energy balance, so disturbances in 
secretion have harmful consequences if sustained for prolonged periods, but sustained 
disturbances in cortisol levels can have harmful consequences, particularly affecting 
sleep patterns.
The importance of good, undisturbed sleep cannot be overstated, as it directly impacts 
cortisol regulation (Liu PY 2024). It’s widely acknowledged, with frequent mentions 
on social media and in ads, that quality sleep is vital for overall health. Poor sleep 
has been linked to increased risks of heart disease, obesity, and mental health issues. 
Quality rest is necessary for cognitive function, emotional well-being, and physical 
health. To enhance sleep quality, managing factors like caffeine consumption is key. 
For optimal sleep quality, many wellness professionals advise people to avoid caffeine 
at least eight hours before bed, particularly for those who metabolize caffeine slowly.

118 ♦ Ildi Revi
Caffeine Recap
Caffeine, the most recognized compound in coffee, is a central nervous system stimulant with 
well-established effects on alertness, mood, and metabolism. Beyond its stimulating properties, 
caffeine exhibits anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, neuroprotective, and hepatoprotective actions. 
It enhances dopamine and noradrenaline activity by blocking adenosine receptors, influences 
glucose metabolism, and may support the prevention of neurodegenerative diseases, depression, 
and some cancers. Its impact on telomere preservation and cytokine regulation also links it 
to longevity and immune modulation. However, caffeine’s effects vary depending on genetic 
differences in metabolism, stress levels, medication use, and life stages such as pregnancy. While 
it plays an important role in coffee’s health effects, it is only one of many bioactive compounds 
whose interactions define coffee as a functional food.

119
4.2 Chlorogenic Acids (CGA1)
Of all the compounds found in coffee, CGA should be the headliner, not caffeine. As 
mentioned in the introduction, just as the quality of coffee varies, so do the compounds 
found within it. Coffee species, cultivars, environmental factors, agricultural practices, 
processing and other influences impact CGA levels in coffee. With focus and intention, 
it is possible to develop coffees with high levels of healthy compounds, like CGA. 
These powerful compounds are complex, and although they have been studied for 
almost two centuries, only during the past twenty years have analytical methodologies 
improved enough to measure their impact on humans accurately. Several detailed 
summaries of the bioactive properties of CGA are listed in the citations at the end of 
the book. One of the best is by Dr. Adriana Farah and Dr. Juliana de Paula Lima from 
Universidade Federal do Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, the Laboratory of Food Chemistry and 
Bioactivity and Coffee Research Center (NUPECAFÉ), Institute of Nutrition. It is 
open access at the time of this writing (August 2024): “Consumption of Chlorogenic 
Acids through Coffee and Health Implications,” in the journal Beverages, 2019. 
The overview of CGA that follows is intended to capture the essence of the literature 
without being overwhelming. Between Arabica and Robusta in the International 
Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) nomenclature system, there appear 
to be at least 69 named CGA (Jaiswal 2010). In the past few years, scientists have 
described more than 300 major and minor ones. 
1	  A reminder from earlier: The acronym for chlorogenic acid and chlorogenic acids, whether in singular or plural form, is 
frequently abbreviated as CGA in scientific literature. In this book, CGA is predominantly used in the plural form, and the 
corresponding verb agrees with this plural usage.

120 ♦ Ildi Revi
A grammatical reminder from the introduction: For clarification, when using the 
acronyms CGA, CQA, FQA, etc., in this book, we will consider these plurals because 
there are several forms of each. 
WHAT ARE CHLOROGENIC ACIDS (CGA)?
CGA are phenolic compounds known for their antioxidant properties and significant 
role in plant defense. They are abundant in various foods (fruits, vegetables, spices, teas, 
wine, and chocolate) and are associated with numerous health benefits. CGA exhibits 
multiple pharmacological properties, including antioxidant effects, anti-inflammatory 
characteristics, potential anticancer activity, and antiviral and antibacterial properties.
CGA are esters that are formed from quinic acid and one hydroxycinnamic acid 
(caffeic, ferulic, and p-coumaric acids). After esterification, the main classes of CGA 
found in both C. Arabica and C. canephora are caffeoylquinic acids (CQA) and 
dicaffeoylquinic acids (diCQA) (formed with caffeic acid), feruloylquinic acids (FQA) 
(formed with ferulic acid), and p-coumaroylquinic acids (formed with p-coumaric 
acid)(Farah 2008, 2019). 
Coffee contains especially high concentrations of CGA, the major phenolic compounds 
in green coffee. During roasting, some CGA transforms into chlorogenic acid lactones 
(CGL). Coffee is considered one of the most important sources of antioxidant intake 
in many countries because most people consume one to four cups daily.
CGA in coffee are only partially bioavailable, meaning that only a fraction of the 
ingested compound is absorbed and utilized by the body. This limited bioavailability 
is common among polyphenols and other plant-derived compounds due to factors 
like metabolism, solubility, and intestinal absorption. Like caffeine, people metabolize 
CGA at different rates based on individual genetics and physiology.
Yet, the human body processes CGA differently from caffeine, and CGA offers 
numerous potential health benefits with virtually no adverse effects when consumed 
naturally in coffee. Even people with various health conditions typically experience 
no problems with CGA from regular coffee consumption. The issue arises with CGA 
supplements, which provide unnaturally high concentrations that can cause side 
effects in some individuals.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 121
This highlights an important principle: coffee’s synergistic effects are greater than 
those of any individual compound. CGA consumed within coffee’s natural complexity, 
whether regular or decaf, produces different (and generally better) results than taking 
isolated CGA as a supplement. The whole is greater than the sum of its parts. 
SUMMARY OF CGA HEALTH BENEFITS
CGA are best known for their contribution to coffee’s health benefits. As potent 
antioxidants and anti-inflammatory agents, they help combat free radical damage 
and inhibit the production and release of inflammatory chemicals in the body. CGA’s 
extensive potential health benefits suggest it could offer a natural option for co-treating 
or preventing various chronic diseases. 
Research suggests that CGA may be important in regulating glucose and lipid 
metabolism. This regulation could potentially impact several chronic conditions, 
including:
•	
Diabetes
•	
Cardiovascular disease
•	
Obesity
•	
Cancer
•	
Fatty liver disease (hepatic steatosis)
The consumption of CGA have been linked to a lower incidence of various degenerative 
diseases and may even play a role in improving longevity. CGA have decreased immune 
and inflammatory markers in the liver (Farah 2008, 2012, 2018) and have antiviral 
and antimicrobial activities. CGA and their lactones (CGL) also have demonstrated 
anti-opioid activity and hypoglycemic properties. CGA also have demonstrated 
prebiotic activity because the unabsorbed portion feeds the beneficial microorganisms 
in the gut, including the probiotics (Sales 2020). 
CGA play multiple roles in gut health. Although they are relatively stable in saliva and 
in the acidic environment of the stomach, their absorption in the intestinal tract is 
limited. Most CGA absorption occurs in the small intestine, where uptake is influenced 
by compound solubility, intestinal pH, and other factors. The intestinal environment’s 

122 ♦ Ildi Revi
higher pH and the characteristics of different isomers of CGA, CQA, CQL and FQA 
reduce their solubility and permeability, leading to only partial absorption (Farah and 
de Paula Lima 2019).
On average, about one-third of consumed CGA are absorbed throughout the digestive 
tract (Farah 2008, Farah and de Paula Lima 2019, Fernández-Tomé 2024). The 
remaining unabsorbed CGA proceed to the colon, where they are metabolized by 
gut microbiota into smaller phenolic compounds. These microbial transformations 
are essential, as they contribute to CGA’s systemic bioactivity and provide prebiotic 
benefits, promoting the growth of beneficial bacteria, including probiotic strains 
(Sales 2020).
CGA and their roasting-derived counterparts, CGL, have also been shown to exhibit 
protective effects on the gut (León 2019). These compounds support the integrity of 
the intestinal barrier and help regulate inflammatory processes. Interestingly, during 
digestion, some CGL may revert to their parent CGA forms, thereby enhancing total 
CGA bioavailability in the body (Farah 2008, Farah and de Paula Lima 2019).
The extent and speed of CGA absorption can vary widely between individuals due to 
genetic, metabolic, and dietary differences. Factors such as coffee preparation, roast 
level, and food matrix interactions also influence how well CGA and CGL are utilized. 
Once absorbed, or metabolized and then absorbed, their isomers and derivatives may 
exert systemic protective effects across multiple organs, including the gut, liver, brain, 
and cardiovascular system. 
Lactones from coffee can enter brain tissue (de Paulis 2004), displaying both significant 
neuroprotective and antioxidant properties in tests of cellular degeneration and death 
of neurons subjected to oxidative stress. One study showed a notable correlation 
between the levels of CGL in coffee and neuron cell survival. Hydrogen peroxide 
(H₂O₂) is a reactive oxygen species that can induce oxidative stress, leading to neuronal 
cell death. This process involves lipid peroxidation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and 
DNA damage. Chlorogenic lactones have been shown to contribute to the increased 
protective effects against H₂O₂-induced death of neuron cells (Chu 2009). In the 
previous chapter on diseases impacted by coffee, we discussed how CGA is linked 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 123
to preventing AD, particularly reducing the toxic β-amyloid peptide deposits in the 
brain, a distinct characteristic of patients with AD.
Inflammation has been linked to both neurodegenerative diseases like Parkinson’s 
and depression. Dantzer (2011) discusses how inflammatory processes might play 
a role in the onset of depressive symptoms. According to Murtaza et al. (2015), 
levels of caffeic acid derivative (metabolite) may decrease when depressive symptoms 
appear. Research suggests a potential link between these metabolites and depression: 
Caffeic acid, the primary metabolite derived from CGA, is well-documented for its 
anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective properties. Numerous studies highlight its 
role in reducing inflammation and protecting neuronal integrity. This suggests that 
maintaining adequate levels of this metabolite could potentially help in managing 
inflammation-related depression and neurodegenerative conditions like Parkinson’s. 
CGA’s antioxidative and anti-inflammatory effects are well documented in helping 
people prevent cardiovascular disease and protect the heart from damage that can 
lead to disease. A 15-year study followed almost 42,000 postmenopausal women 
between the ages of 55 and 69 with coffee drinking and mortality from diseases with 
a major inflammatory component, including cardiovascular disease. They concluded 
that “Consumption of coffee–a major source of dietary antioxidants, may inhibit 
inflammation and thereby reduce the risk of cardiovascular and other inflammatory 
diseases in postmenopausal women” (Andersen 2006). 
CGA help improve vascular health and regulate blood pressure by releasing nitric 
oxide, a molecule that relaxes blood vessels, improving blood flow and reducing blood 
pressure. They have also been shown to lower plasma homocysteine levels, high levels 
of which can damage blood vessels and increase the risk of cardiovascular diseases. 
In addition to protecting the vascular system, CGA have been shown to suppress 
P-selectin expression on blood platelets. P-selectin is a molecule involved in platelet 
activation and aggregation, which can lead to blood clots. CGA reduces blood clot 
formation risk by suppressing P-selectin expression, further supporting cardiovascular 
health. These combined effects contribute to better vascular function and blood 
pressure control (Fuentes 2014).

124 ♦ Ildi Revi
CGA can also facilitate metabolizing lipids in the body, lowering the risk of developing 
cardiovascular diseases, such as hypertension and atherosclerosis, and conditions 
associated with cardiovascular risk, such as obesity and type 2 diabetes. In particular, 
the isomer 5-CQA influences important transcription factors and enzymes that 
regulate lipid metabolism, which helps with obesity and dyslipidemia. Transcription 
factors are proteins that play a key role in regulating the conversion of DNA into RNA, 
initiating gene expression. Precise regulation ensures genes are expressed appropriately, 
supporting normal development, cellular activities, and responses to environmental 
changes. 5-CQA can help regulate the genes responsible for these processes. In a trial 
with mice, 5-CQA reduced body weight by preventing the buildup of triglycerides in 
the liver and enhancing fat breakdown in adipose tissue. Another study showed that 
5-CQA helped decrease obesity in rats on a high-fat diet by reducing fat production 
and uptake in the liver, increasing fat breakdown, and boosting antioxidant activity. 
Again, this appeared to be linked to changes in the expression of specific genes involved 
in lipid metabolism (Murase 2011, Huang 2015, Lu 2020).
Linked to this, CGA also have been shown to affect various diabetes-related processes, 
including gluconeogenesis, glucose transport, and insulin secretion, and help to reduce 
the risk of developing type 2 diabetes, which can also lead to liver diseases. CGA have 
been shown to affect liver disease positively, and there is an extensive section on it in 
the previous chapter. Recall that CGA and other polyphenols exert protective effects 
against liver fibrosis through antioxidant action and the suppression of TGF-β and 
PDGF-β. 
Intestinal epithelial cells have a defense function against the pro-inflammatory 
cytokines and chemokines produced in response to external stimuli, such as the 
oxidative stress [Tumor Necrosis Factor-alpha (TNF-α)] discussed earlier with 
caffeine. Macrophages, lymphocytes, and neutrophils produce it, and it helps regulate 
these immune cells. Recall that overproduction can lead to excessive inflammation, 
inflammatory diseases and possibly autoimmune disorders. TNF-α signals the body 
that inflamed cells are problematic, which can cause the body to break down cells, 
dissolve and eliminate them. CGA have been found to have a considerable impact 
on TNF-α, blocking its synthesis and release by preventing the activation of both 
macrophages and monocytes, thus reducing inflammation (Huang 2023).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 125
Dietary polyphenols, including CGA, inhibit DNA damage and have been linked 
to a lower risk of certain cancers through epidemiological studies. In 2010, Lenore 
Arab reviewed substantial epidemiological evidence from over 500 papers connecting 
coffee consumption to various human cancers. She condensed the findings of the 
meta-analyses and more recent papers on site-specific human cancers. A strong and 
consistent protective association exists between hepatocellular and endometrial 
cancers (Arab 2010). Most of this credit goes to the CGA in coffee, although they 
harmoniously work with numerous other compounds to produce these positive 
health effects. CGA’s antioxidant properties help prevent DNA damage caused by free 
radicals, leading to cancer in general. CGA and its metabolites exhibit anti-mutagenic 
properties and support chemopreventive actions by modulating antioxidant defenses, 
DNA replication, cell differentiation, and other protective mechanisms, such as 
inactivating free radicals in the colon and inducing chemoprotective enzymes.
On the downside, CGA can have side effects if it is isolated and taken in large 
amounts or by individuals with specific health conditions (Tajik 2017). The potential 
detrimental effects associated with high doses of CGA may relate to the secretion of 
gastric fluids. These seem rare, and there is little literature on these issues or CGA 
interactions with common medications.
Although some researchers cite CGA as the culprit when people say that coffee upsets 
their stomachs, this is one reason to recommend a darker roast: to decrease certain acid 
levels (citric and malic, specifically) and also reduce the level of CGA significantly. 
Light roasts retain more CGA than dark roasts, with up to 45-54 percent of the 
original CGA in the raw green coffee lost in light roasts, and almost all of it lost in 
the darkest roasts. This reduction in CGA affects the coffee’s antioxidant properties 
and flavor profile (lactones are known to be bitter compounds) (León 2019). This 
finding is particularly relevant for those who prefer slightly darker roasted coffee, as 
the roasting process can convert some CGA to CGL. 

126 ♦ Ildi Revi
CGA CONTENT IMPACTED BY COFFEE 
PRODUCERS AND ROASTERS
A few months ago, I received a sample of green coffee of African origin (keeping it 
vague to prevent any misunderstandings about specific origins). It could have been 
a perfect coffee for Purity. I wanted to offer African coffee to our customers. It was 
certified organic and produced by a cooperative interested in health and environmental 
advancements. I green-graded it and cupped it. It had a creamy mouthfeel, clear 
apricot and roasted cashew-like notes, sweet acidity and an exceptional balance of all 
these strong characteristics. I sent it to the lab, and it came back free of contaminants, 
but it had very low, almost undetectable levels of CGA. I thought that must have 
been wrong, so I sent the sample to a different lab to double-check. They gave the 
same results. I still couldn’t understand how such a beautiful, complex coffee could be 
almost void of CGA, but the lab data was clear. I had to reject the sample for Purity. 
It would be an outstanding choice for flavor-focused markets, but it didn’t meet the 
standard for CHC or holistic coffee, where verified health compounds are essential.
Research on CGA in coffee production is fairly scarce, but one significant study 
was done in 2020 by Król et al. They demonstrated that organic farming practices 
positively impact the levels of CGA in coffee beans. Their testing showed that organic 
coffee beans generally contain a larger amount of bioactive compounds, including 
CGA, than conventionally grown beans. They attributed this increase to the absence 
of synthetic pesticides and fertilizers, which can affect the plant’s natural defense 
mechanisms and secondary metabolite production. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 127
Organic, regenerative coffee production at Hacienda La Pradera, Aratoca, Colombia.
Compared to industrial coffee production in Brazil.
The study offered us insight into how age and storage conditions affect coffee: Over 
a 12-month storage period, they observed a reduction in polyphenolic compounds 
due to the degradation of CGA, which affected overall bioactivity.

128 ♦ Ildi Revi
Additionally, organic farming often involves practices that enhance soil health and 
biodiversity, further supporting the synthesis of CGA. We will discuss this more in the 
farming chapter. To add depth here, organic farming practices significantly enhance 
soil health and biodiversity, supporting the synthesis of CGA in coffee plants. Organic 
fertilizers, crop rotations, and cover cropping, which improve soil structure, increase 
organic matter content, and promote a diverse microbial ecosystem, contribute to 
the overall synthesis of CGA because enhanced soil health leads to better nutrient 
availability and uptake by plants (Panday 2024). This is important for the biosynthesis 
of secondary metabolites like CGA. Also, the increased biodiversity in organic farming 
systems helps in pest and disease management, reducing the need for chemical inputs 
that can negatively impact plant metabolism and CGA levels (Shade and Tully 2020).
For coffee roasters, the browning of coffee beans involves more complex chemical 
transformations than once believed, including specific molecular changes that 
contribute to the development of their rich brown color. CGA and phenolic 
compounds also participate in the Maillard reaction, transforming coffee beans from 
raw, green agricultural commodities to brown beans that we brew. While the Maillard 
reaction primarily involves carbohydrates and amino compounds and has been widely 
studied in producing melanoidins, the involvement of phenolic compounds in these 
reactions has not received much attention. 
To explore this, Bork et al. (2024) studied caffeic acid and ferulic acid (hydroxycinnamic 
acids, phenolic compounds with strong antioxidant capacity) and their impact on the 
browning of certain sugars and amino acids (arabinose, galactose, alanine). Researchers 
found that hydroxycinnamic acids add health benefits and play an active role in 
the complex browning process of foods when heated, resulting in melanoidins and 
flavor compounds (Perrone 2012). This emerging understanding could reshape how 
coffee roasters approach their roasting process, allowing for more control over CGA 
degradation and melanoidin levels. More on this in the roasting chapter.
This body of research, and, frankly, what we may all know intuitively, clearly shows 
that coffee grown with environmentally conscious practices inherently enhances 
levels of beneficial compounds, such as CGA, and supports overall soil health and 
biodiversity, distinguishing it from conventional counterparts. These unique benefits 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 129
underscore that environmentally-conscious coffee can indeed be roasted for health 
and represents a fundamentally new and distinct category in the coffee industry.
Chlorogenic Acids (CGA) Recap
CGA are powerful polyphenolic compounds that contribute significantly to coffee’s antioxidant, 
anti-inflammatory, metabolic, and neuroprotective effects. Present in high concentrations in 
green coffee, CGA levels vary by species, processing, and roast level, with lighter roasts retaining 
more. These compounds support cardiovascular health by improving blood pressure and lipid 
metabolism, regulating glucose levels, promoting gut health through prebiotic activity, and may 
reduce the risk of chronic diseases, including obesity, diabetes, liver disease, and cancer. CGA also 
protects against oxidative stress and modulates immune responses by inhibiting pro-inflammatory 
cytokines like TNF-α. While available in supplement form, CGA is most effective and better 
tolerated when consumed as part of the synergistic matrix of whole coffee, particularly when 
health-focused sourcing and roasting methods preserve its integrity.

130
4.3 Other Important Compounds
DITERPENES: CAFESTOL AND KAHWEOL
Along with caffeine and CGA, the most cited biologically active compounds in coffee 
that impact the liver are the diterpenes cafestol and kahweol (C&K). These diterpenes 
are found exclusively in coffee’s lipids. During coffee plant growth, diterpenes form 
via a series of enzymatic reactions in the coffee seed endosperm, the part of the seed 
that feeds the plant embryo. 
C&K are usually studied and discussed in tandem since it is difficult to isolate 
them. Kahweol, in particular, is highly unstable when purified and is more sensitive 
than cafestol to heat, oxygen, light and acids. These bioactive compounds and their 
derivatives, which are mainly salts or esters of saturated fatty acids (predominant) and 
unsaturated fatty acids, represent approximately 20 percent of the lipid fraction of 
coffee, with cafestol accounting for about 0.2 to 0.6 percent of coffee weight (Farah 
2012).
The roasting process influences the levels of diterpenes, but the effect depends on 
the intensity and duration of the roasting, which we will discuss later. Diterpenes 
contribute to brewed coffee’s unique flavor, as well as other body and potential health 
benefits (Makiso 2024). It is important to note that the exact content of cafestol and 
kahweol in your cup of coffee will depend on how the coffee is prepared, as different 
brewing methods can affect the presence of these compounds in the final beverage 
(Bealu and Kasahun 2023).
Coffee diterpenes have exhibited anticarcinogenic and hepatoprotective properties in 
vitro. They stimulate intracellular antioxidant defense mechanisms and are a common 
agent for cancer chemopreventive blocking (Cavin 2002). The diterpenes help reduce 
the activation of mutation-causing agents and cancer (Guzzo 2012). By inhibiting 
specific enzymes, they reduce the likelihood of these substances causing harm to DNA 
that could potentially lead to cancer, as mutations can be related to carcinogenesis. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 131
These cancer-preventing effects primarily occur in the hepatocytes, which is why C&K 
are commonly discussed and associated with liver health (Ren 2019).
When studied in the lab and the body, C&K protect against several well-known 
carcinogens and have anti-inflammatory effects through several mechanisms:
•	
Liver Protection: Conjugating enzymes are critical to our detoxifying 
biological process by helping to make unwanted substances more water-
soluble, which allows them to be excreted from the body (Homan 2006). 
•	
Antioxidant Defense: C&K appear to help increase the expression of 
proteins that protect cells from oxidative damage caused by free radicals 
and other reactive molecules. This can strengthen the cellular defense against 
potential carcinogens by protecting cells from oxidative damage caused by 
free radicals and other reactive molecules. This is important when we discuss 
cancer in the next section (Cavin 2002).
•	
Carcinogen Inhibition: Cytochromes P450 are a family of detoxifying 
enzymes that C&K treatment appears to reduce carcinogen activation, 
lowering the risk of cancer development (Shimada 2006).
C&K treatment in human liver cells seems to protect DNA from the harmful effects 
of aflatoxin B1 by enhancing the detoxification process and inhibiting the activation 
of the toxin, thus exhibiting anticarcinogenic effects (Cavin 2002). These mechanisms 
demonstrate C&K’s potential in protecting against liver cancer caused by aflatoxin 
exposure.
The brewing method directly influences the amount of diterpenes we consume. French 
press, espresso, Turkish, and Scandinavian coffee maximize diterpene content, while 
paper filters trap most of these compounds. This difference is visually apparent: in 
unfiltered coffee, you can see oils floating on the surface when light reflects off it in a 
certain way. These oils are largely absent in paper-filtered coffee (Gross 1996). 
Although coffee contains no cholesterol, it can affect cholesterol levels starting in 
the gut. Diterpenes suppress the activity of certain enzymes and receptors involved 
in cholesterol regulation, such as the bile acid receptors in the intestine and enzymes 
in the liver responsible for cholesterol metabolism (Lanzotti 2013). The substances 
play a role in cholesterol breakdown; therefore, they allow for an increase in total 

132 ♦ Ildi Revi
cholesterol and LDL levels. Cholesterol absorption and breakdown vary from person 
to person, influenced by unique genetic makeup, gut bacteria and how their bodies 
interact with dietary fats and foods high in cholesterol. For those concerned about 
cholesterol, brewing the coffee with a paper filter using a pourover or drip method will 
eliminate the diterpenes while allowing the caffeine and CGA to remain bioavailable.
A final note about coffee lipids: Diterpenes are not the only lipids in coffee; they also 
contain some other lipids and phytosterols, natural compounds found in plants similar 
to cholesterol. Phytosterols have been shown to reduce human cholesterol levels and 
have demonstrated anti-carcinogenic properties (Moreau 2002). Coffee lipids also 
contain tocopherols, fat-soluble organic compounds, with around 6 to 15 mg per 
100 g in green coffee (Speer 2006). Vitamin E (specifically alpha-tocopherol) is a 
powerful lipid-soluble antioxidant that integrates into cellular membranes, effectively 
preventing lipid peroxidation. When combined with ferulic acid, α-tocopherol 
(vitamin E) creates a synergistic antioxidant system that protects liposomes from 
oxidation. Still, vitamin E levels in coffee vary due to cultivar, production factors, 
roasting, and even brewing. 
Diterpenes Recap
Cafestol and kahweol are lipid-based diterpenes found in coffee’s natural oils, particularly in 
unfiltered brews like French press or espresso. These compounds exhibit potent anticarcinogenic 
and hepatoprotective effects, supporting liver detoxification, reducing inflammation, and 
protecting against DNA damage from toxins like aflatoxins. They stimulate antioxidant defenses 
and inhibit enzymes that activate carcinogens, making them especially important for liver health. 
However, diterpenes also have a known effect on raising LDL cholesterol levels in some individuals, 
a risk largely mitigated by brewing coffee with paper filters. Their presence is influenced by roast 
level and brewing method, and they contribute to flavor and mouthfeel.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 133
MELANOIDINS
Another group of compounds related to the degree of roast is melanoidins. Melanoidins 
give coffee and other foods the brown colour when roasted, baked or toasted. However, 
coffee is considered the main source of melanoidins in the adult diet, with around 
100 to 400 mg per serving, depending on all the normal roasted and brewed coffee 
variables (roast level, grind particle size, brew time and temperature, ratio of coffee to 
water, volume of beverage, brew method, etc.) (Iriondo-DeHond 2021). 
Melanoidins are formed by the Maillard reaction during coffee roasting. We discuss 
specific times and temperatures of roasting in the chapter on roasting. During the 
Maillard reaction, polysaccharides (galactomannan, type II arabinogalactan, and 
small amounts of monosaccharides), amino acids (proteins), phenols, certain low 
molecular weight compounds, as well as sucrose, combine at high temperatures and 
low water activity. 
There are different sizes of melanoidin fractions, from low to high. Phenolic 
compounds are incorporated into high molecular weight (HMW) melanoidins, the 
end product of the Maillard reaction. Because of them and their properties, they 
are considered bioactive compounds. The extent of roasting-induced antioxidant 
formation is directly linked to the extent of melanoidin formation, and the longer 
we roast, the more molecular weight melanoidins we produce, from intermediate to 
high-molecular-weight. 
Coffee melanoidins have demonstrated antioxidant, antibacterial, and prebiotic 
properties. They act as soluble fiber, enhancing immune-stimulating properties and 
significantly reducing the risk of colon cancer (Vitaglione 2012; Moreira 2015; 
Fogliano 2011). This is likely due to decreasing colon inflammation through improved 
microbiota balance (prebiotic effect) and increasing the elimination rate of carcinogens 
(agents that can lead to cancer) through higher colon motility (the urge to use the 
toilet) a common experience of those who drink coffee. 
Coffee melanoidins, although not absorbed by the body, appear to function as an 
antioxidant dietary fiber with an overall antioxidant capacity via embedded phenolic 
compounds (Iriondo‐DeHond 2019). These compounds can act like a ‘sponge’ for 
free radicals moving through the gut. Research by Garsetti et al. in 2000 (and later 

134 ♦ Ildi Revi
supported by Folmer in 2017) noted, “The fact that coffee supplies antioxidants to 
the colon is of particular importance since high coffee consumption was repeatedly 
linked to a reduced risk of developing colon cancer in affluent countries.” 
One of the most useful functions of coffee melanoidins is their ability to chelate 
transition metal ions. This means they bind to metals like little magnets and hold 
onto certain metal ions such as iron, magnesium, and copper, all of which are found in 
roasted coffee. CGA facilitate this chelating action within the melanoidins’ structure. 
They carry a negative charge that attracts the positively charged metal ions. The 
chelating properties of melanoidins are important for health for a couple of reasons:
1.	 They can influence the availability of these metals in our bodies. By binding to 
iron, melanoidins can potentially reduce the amount of free iron in the body, 
which, in excess, can produce harmful free radicals. Although this chelating 
action might also interfere with the absorption of other minerals, its positive 
benefit depends on individuals and their health goals.
2.	 Spent coffee grounds can be used as compost, mulch and even biochar, as 
discussed in the production chapter later. This can prevent metals in the 
soil from becoming toxic to plants and may also help deliver essential 
micronutrients like zinc, iron, and manganese to the plants in a more 
bioavailable form. Additionally, metal-chelating ability is an important aspect 
of inhibiting lipid peroxidation. Additionally, SCG cellulose, hemicellulose, 
lignin, lipids, proteins and the peptides obtained through protein hydrolysis 
function as antioxidants, antimicrobials, and anti-mutagenic agents (Sarrouh 
2020). 
Earlier, we mentioned how lipid peroxidation plays a major role in developing 
certain diseases, particularly neurodegenerative, such as Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s 
diseases and even atherosclerosis. Research has shown that melanoidins inhibit lipid 
peroxidation, increasing the impact of caffeine and trigonelline in coffee (Tagliazucchi 
2010). These properties are not only interesting for their potential health implications 
but also for the preservation of coffee quality. Melanoidins can prevent the oxidation 
of fats in coffee by chelating metals, which helps to maintain its flavor and aroma over 
time (Portillo and Arévalo 2022).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 135
Coffee melanoidins may also promote the growth of beneficial colon microbiota, 
potentially affecting inflammatory pathways in the colon and the liver (Folmer, 2017). 
Interestingly, antioxidative structures formed in coffee melanoidins are similar to 
vitamin E. These appear to activate their scavenging properties gradually (Bekedam, 
2008). Research suggests that coffee melanoidins seem to have a protective effect on 
liver steatosis in obese rats, with glutathione playing a role in this process (Vitaglione 
2012). This suggests that melanoidins influence liver fat and functionality.
Coffee melanoidins remain an active area of scientific research, with ongoing studies 
revealing their complex roles in human health and environmental applications. As 
our understanding of these compounds deepens, we may uncover additional benefits 
that further underscore coffee’s potential as a functional food. The full scope of 
melanoidins’ impact on health and sustainability remains an exciting frontier in coffee 
science.
Melanoidins Recap
Melanoidins are large, complex compounds formed during the Maillard reaction in coffee roasting. 
They are responsible for the brown color of roasted coffee and contribute significantly to its 
antioxidant, antimicrobial, and prebiotic properties. Though not absorbed by the body, they 
function as antioxidant dietary fiber in the gut, helping to neutralize free radicals, support 
healthy microbiota, and reduce colon inflammation—thereby potentially lowering the risk of 
colorectal cancer. Melanoidins also bind to metal ions, helping regulate mineral bioavailability 
and reduce lipid peroxidation, which is linked to chronic diseases such as neurodegeneration and 
atherosclerosis. These compounds contribute not only to coffee’s health benefits but also to its 
flavor stability and shelf life, making them important both nutritionally and functionally. Their 
concentration increases with roast level, offering unique benefits in darker coffees often overlooked 
in caffeine-centric narratives.

136 ♦ Ildi Revi
TRIGONELLINE
Trigonelline is a water-soluble alkaloid found in coffee and a variety of other organisms, 
including seeds, legumes, jellyfish, and shellfish. It contributes to coffee’s distinctive 
flavor and has increasingly attracted scientific attention over the past four decades 
for its wide potential health benefits, including antimicrobial and antitumor activity, 
and protective effects against type 2 diabetes and neurological diseases (Vieira Porto 
and Farah 2019).
Fenugreek, a spice used in traditional Chinese medicine to support metabolic 
and nervous system health, has also been studied as a significant dietary source of 
trigonelline. In coffee, however, trigonelline’s importance is further underscored by its 
role as a major precursor to nicotinic acid, also known as niacin or vitamin B3. During 
the coffee roasting process, trigonelline degrades into nicotinic acid and nicotinamide, 
enhancing coffee’s nutritional profile and contributing to its health-promoting ability 
(Casal 2000).
In humans, nicotinic acid plays a central role in converting food into usable energy 
and supporting the health of organs such as the liver, skin, adrenal glands, and nervous 
system (Casal 2000). Together with nicotinamide (also called niacinamide), these 
forms of vitamin B3 help regulate cholesterol, improve circulation, support stress 
hormone production, and, like caffeine and chlorogenic acids, regulate inflammatory 
responses (Casal 2000).
Trigonelline also contributes to coffee’s synergistic metabolic benefits, especially when 
paired with CGA. It has been shown to inhibit intestinal glucose uptake, improve 
insulin sensitivity, and counteract cortisone-induced hyperglycemia, mechanisms that 
support its protective effect against type 2 diabetes (Vieira Porto and Farah 2019). 
It may also play a role in liver health by lowering triglyceride levels and modulating 
inflammatory markers such as TNF-α. For more on these mechanisms, see the sections 
on Diabetes and Liver Function.
Recent clinical evidence further supports trigonelline’s contribution to genomic 
stability. In a randomized controlled trial, daily consumption of a dark roast coffee for 
four weeks significantly reduced spontaneous DNA strand breaks in white blood cells 
by 27% compared to a water-only control group. The coffee blend used in the study 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 137
was rich in trigonelline, CGA, and N-methylpyridinium (NMP). It was associated 
with the upregulation of antioxidant defense enzymes such as NQO1 and activation 
of the Nrf2 pathway, both of which play a role in protecting cells from oxidative stress 
(Bakuradze 2015).
Protecting DNA from oxidative damage is crucial because accumulation of broken 
strands and base modifications can lead to mutations, impaired cell function, and the 
development of chronic diseases, including cancer, neurodegeneration, and metabolic 
disorders (Volz 2012).
One of the most significant roasting products derived from trigonelline is NMP, a 
compound with its own emerging body of health research, particularly in relation to 
antioxidant defense and DNA protection. We explore these effects in more detail in 
the next section.
Trigonelline Recap
Trigonelline is a nitrogen-containing alkaloid found abundantly in coffee that contributes both to 
its flavor and its functional health properties. It has demonstrated antidiabetic, antimicrobial, and 
neuroprotective effects, and serves as a natural precursor to nicotinic acid (vitamin B3). During 
roasting, trigonelline breaks down to form several beneficial compounds, including nicotinic acid 
and N-methylpyridinium (NMP), which carry their own distinct health benefits. Trigonelline has 
been shown to improve insulin sensitivity, inhibit glucose uptake, and help modulate inflammatory 
pathways relevant to type 2 diabetes and liver function. It also contributes to DNA protection by 
supporting antioxidant defense systems and has shown promise in preserving neuronal health. 
Though not as widely known as caffeine or CGA, trigonelline is a key player in coffee’s health-
promoting matrix.

138 ♦ Ildi Revi
PROMISING COMPOUNDS OF INTEREST 
N-METHYLPYRIDINIUM (NMP)
N-methylpyridinium (NMP) is a unique, non-volatile compound formed during 
coffee roasting as a degradation product of trigonelline (Bakuradze 2015). As discussed 
in the previous chapter, NMP formation increases with roast intensity. It is largely 
absent from foods outside roasted coffee, making it a distinct marker of dark-roasted 
brews (Vieira Porto and Farah 2019).
Once ingested, NMP is rapidly absorbed and detectable in blood and urine within 
hours, providing researchers with a reliable biomarker of recent coffee consumption 
(Brandl 2024). Its role, however, goes beyond traceability. Emerging research suggests 
that NMP contributes actively to coffee’s health-promoting effects, particularly in 
antioxidant defense and cellular protection.
Notably, NMP activates the Nrf2 pathway, often described as the master regulator 
of oxidative stress. This mechanism enhances the expression of phase II detoxifying 
enzymes, which neutralize reactive species and support long-term cellular resilience 
(Boettler 2011). These antioxidant responses have been associated with DNA 
protection, reduced inflammation, and possible anticarcinogenic effects.
While trigonelline may act upstream as a metabolic and microbial modulator, 
NMP appears to pick up where trigonelline leaves off, amplifying coffee’s health 
benefits through cellular defense and detoxification pathways. For example, regular 
consumption of a pure Arabica dark-roasted coffee blend significantly reduced 
spontaneous DNA strand breaks in blood cells of healthy volunteers, an effect 
correlated with high NMP content (Pahlke 2019; Schipp 2018). Lighter roasts 
contained, on average, three times less NMP.
This does not mean that dark roast is superior to medium or light roasts across the 
board. Rather, it highlights how roasting modulates the chemical profile of coffee 
and shapes its physiological effects. For instance, a study found that dark roasted 
coffee, which measured higher NMP levels, also led to lower gastric acid secretion 
than a medium roast. This reduction in stomach irritation was accompanied by 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 139
lower trigonelline and CGA levels (Rubach 2014), reinforcing the idea that complex 
interplays influence health effects among compounds.
Further studies have linked NMP-rich coffee to additional benefits, including mild 
weight loss in pre-obese subjects and improvements in markers of liver detoxification 
(Kotyczka 2011; Al-Serori 2019; Somoza 2005). In red blood cells, consumption of 
dark roast coffee enhanced antioxidant capacity, with elevated levels of tocopherol 
(vitamin E) and glutathione (Kotyczka 2011). These effects suggest that NMP 
contributes meaningfully to coffee’s systemic antioxidant network.
While the evidence is promising, research on NMP remains in its early stages. Further 
human trials are needed to understand its mechanisms and full health potential. Still, 
its role as a key post-roast compound underscores the importance of considering roast 
level and chemical composition when evaluating coffee’s functional benefits.
BETA-CARBOLINES: NORHARMAN AND HARMAN
The study of beta(β)-carbolines in coffee is a new area of active research, which is often 
controversial among coffee scientists. Since roasted coffee is the major known food 
source of norharman and harman β-carbolines, the possibilities of these compounds 
and their exclusivity to coffee make them worth discussing. 
β-carbolines are alkaloids found naturally in eight plant families. They are also formed 
during food processing and can be produced synthetically. These compounds have 
various health effects, diverse pharmacological activities in natural biological processes, 
and potential therapeutic treatments. The effects of β-carbolines depend on their 
specific chemical structures and substituents. At certain levels, β-carbolines can cause 
health problems, but they also have beneficial effects. This potential for therapeutic 
treatments makes the research on β-carbolines in coffee particularly exciting. The 
health effects are complex and can vary depending on individual factors such as 
genetics, overall health, and exposure levels (Casal 2019).
Roasting is the main way harman and norharman β-carbolines are produced in coffee 
(although amounts produced can be highly variable), whereas these β-carbolines are 
barely detectable in green coffee. Roasted Robusta coffee has shown higher values of 
norharman and harman compounds, although Arabica also has a notable amount of 

140 ♦ Ildi Revi
them. It has been indicated that a darker roasted coffee will have a higher amount 
of harman and norharman β-carbolines (Gomes, 2006), another note to keep in 
mind in the discussion of roasting for health purposes later in this book. In addition, 
β-carboline amounts in the final brew depend on the coffee brewing method and 
process, yet another way to focus on creating coffee for health.
β-carbolines are mostly considered neuroactive substances with a wide spectrum of 
reported pharmacological and neuroactive actions. These actions include binding 
to many receptors in the brain, such as opiate receptors, and acting as inhibitors to 
regulate neurotransmitters. So far, only norharman and harman β-carbolines have 
been reported in coffee, and any reference in this book to β-carbolines refers to only 
these two (Casal 2019). 
β-carbolines, like other coffee compounds, show antioxidant activity in addition 
to their possible anti-opioid and antidepressant activity. They also have a potential 
influence on the course of Parkinson’s disease. The high amounts of norharman and 
harman β-carbolines in roasted coffee place them at the top of the list of compounds 
suspected to influence the course of Parkinson’s disease (Casal 2015). This intriguing 
potential influence of β-carbolines on a significant neurological disease makes them 
a compelling subject of study in coffee research.
β-carboline alkaloids have been found to exhibit inhibitory properties on MAO 
enzymes, specifically MAO-A and MAO-B (Herraiz and Chaparro 2006 and Truman 
2017). These enzymes play an essential role in the metabolism of kynuramines (biogenic 
amines metabolites of melatonin) and other monoamines involved in various biological 
processes. They have been studied for their roles in scavenging reactive oxygen and 
nitrogen species, protecting tissues from damage, and modulating mitochondrial 
metabolism. They also appear to inhibit the breakdown of neurotransmitters such 
as serotonin and noradrenaline. This is why some β-carbolines have been studied for 
their potential antidepressant-like effects. This inhibiting effect of β-carboline on 
the levels of monoamine neurotransmitters makes it an especially interesting part of 
coffee’s already-known compounds, which impact neurological diseases. 
MAO enzymes are responsible for the oxidative deamination of monoamines–a process 
where amino acids are broken down or an amine group is removed from a molecule. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 141
This results in the formation of aldehydes, ketones, or acids and ammonia release. 
Interestingly, these compounds also make up various flavor and aroma notes in coffee, 
and β-carbolines are known to be aromatic compounds. 
The study of β-carbolines in coffee exemplifies the complexity of coffee’s potential 
health benefits. While these compounds may promise neuroprotection and mood 
regulation, their effects are nuanced and dose-dependent. As we continue to explore 
coffee’s role in promoting health, we must keep an eye on the interplay between 
various compounds, including β-carbolines, and how factors like roast level and 
brewing method can influence their presence and potency. By understanding these 
intricate relationships, coffee enthusiasts can make informed choices about their coffee 
consumption, potentially harnessing the beverage’s health-promoting properties 
while being mindful of individual sensitivities and health goals. As research in this 
field progresses, we may uncover even more ways to optimize coffee preparation for 
maximum health benefits, further cementing coffee’s status as a favorite beverage and 
a potential ally in our quest for better health. 
LIGNINS & LIGNANS. FLAVONOIDS, FLAVONOLS, 
FLAVANOLS & ISOFLAVONES
Lignins and lignans, flavonoids, flavonols, flavanols, and isoflavones may sound 
similar, but in chemistry, small structural differences lead to different functionality. 
These polyphenols play various roles, such as building plant cell walls and influencing 
human hormones. These compounds are present in coffee in minor quantities but 
still enhance its health benefits and quality. Even at low concentrations, their impact 
is significant due to daily multiple-cup consumption. Being part of the polyphenol 
family, these compounds have unique structures and biological functions (Bule 2024). 
Though seemingly negligible in roasted beans, they are found throughout the coffee 
plant, adding to the complexity of coffee’s health benefits.

142 ♦ Ildi Revi
LIGNINS VS. LIGNANS: DEFINITIONS, PROPERTIES, 
AND HEALTH IMPLICATIONS
Although their names sound similar, lignins and lignans are different compounds with 
distinct roles. The roasting chapter will explain how these compounds break down 
and convert to other substances.
LIGNINS: STRUCTURAL POLYPHENOLS WITH EMERGING HEALTH 
BENEFITS
Lignins are complex polyphenolic biopolymers found in plant cell walls, where they 
provide structural integrity, protection, and resistance to microbial attack (Katahira 
2018). Although lignins were traditionally thought to be inert structural compounds, 
recent research suggests that lignin-derived polyphenols may offer significant bioactive 
properties (Okonkwo 2023), particularly in its breakdown products that form during 
processing and roasting (Sarrouh 2020).
While lignins differ from low-molecular-weight polyphenols like flavonoids, they 
are still considered polyphenolic because their structure contains multiple groups of 
phenolic hydroxyl (Katahira 2018). During thermal or enzymatic degradation, lignins 
release smaller phenolic compounds, some of which exhibit antioxidant, antimicrobial, 
and anti-inflammatory properties (Okonkwo 2023). 
The study by Sarrouh et al. (2020) developed a fractional extraction method to isolate 
lignin-derived bioactive compounds from coffee husks, revealing strong antioxidant 
properties, demonstrated through high free radical scavenging activity, and anticancer 
potential, with extracted lignin inhibiting the growth of breast, liver, and colon cancer 
cells while maintaining low cytotoxicity (minimal or negligible ability to damage cells). 
These findings suggest that lignin degradation products in coffee may contribute to its 
functional health properties, supporting the idea that coffee’s benefits extend beyond 
caffeine and CGA (Sarrouh 2020).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 143
Beyond coffee, lignins are gaining interest in medical and food science research for 
their:
•	
Antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activities— Lignin breakdown 
products can neutralize free radicals, potentially reducing oxidative stress 
and supporting overall cellular health. Lignins exhibit natural antimicrobial 
properties, which may contribute to coffee’s bioactivity against harmful 
bacteria (Okonkwo 2023).
•	
Potential as a natural source of antioxidants, which may be useful in 
pharmaceutical and nutraceutical applications (Sarrouh 2020).
•	
Potential Hormonal Effects: Some lignin-derived compounds exhibit 
weak estrogenic or anti-estrogenic activity, similar to lignans, by binding 
to estrogen receptors or modulating estrogen metabolism (Wang 2019).
•	
Possible prebiotic effects, as lignins are resistant to digestion but can be 
fermented by gut bacteria, acting as prebiotic-like compounds that support 
microbiome diversity and digestive health (Okonkwo 2023).
These findings open up new frontiers in research for exploring lignins in coffee as 
bioactive compounds, and I hope the coffee industry collaborates on this. In his paper, 
Okonkwo has a suggestion that lignins might be used in biodegradable packaging, 
which is something the coffee industry could embrace, especially if derived from coffee 
husks and cascara (skins from the coffee fruit).
LIGNANS: BIOACTIVE POLYPHENOLS WITH HORMONAL AND 
ANTIOXIDANT ROLES
Lignans are low-molecular-weight polyphenols distinct from lignins. They are found 
in flaxseeds, sesame seeds, whole grains, and in small amounts in coffee. Lignans serve 
as precursors to enterolignans, which are produced through gut microbial metabolism 
(Katahira 2018). Once consumed, gut bacteria convert lignans into enterolignans, 
which have been linked to hormonal balance, cardiovascular benefits, and potential 
protective effects against hormone-related cancers (González-May 2024).
As lignans exhibit phytoestrogenic properties, they can weakly mimic estrogen 
or modulate estrogen levels in the body (Wang 2019). Lignans demonstrate both 

144 ♦ Ildi Revi
estrogenic and anti-estrogenic activity, a seemingly contradictory behavior that 
arises from their interaction with estrogen receptors and influence on hormonal 
signaling. Acting as selective estrogen receptor modulators, lignans can either activate 
or block estrogen receptors depending on the tissue type and hormone levels (Kiyama 
2019). In low-estrogen environments, such as in postmenopausal women, lignans 
weakly activate estrogen receptors, exerting an estrogen-like effect; however, in high-
estrogen environments, they compete with stronger estrogens for receptor binding, 
thereby blocking excess estrogenic activity, a mechanism that can protect against 
hormone-sensitive cancers (Kiyama 2019). Lignans preferentially activate estrogen 
receptor beta (ERβ), which is linked to anti-proliferative and protective effects. This 
is in contrast to estrogen receptor alpha (Erα) activation, which is associated with 
increased cell proliferation and growth signaling, particularly in hormone-sensitive 
tissues. This selective activation may explain how lignans demonstrate anti-estrogenic 
effects in some tissues while promoting estrogenic effects in others (Kiyama 2019). 
Their biphasic activity indicates that low doses may produce estrogenic effects. In 
contrast, higher doses might have anti-estrogenic effects, with overall effects varying 
based on the individual’s hormonal status, microbiome composition, and dietary 
intake of phytoestrogens (Kiyama 2019). Consequently, lignans’ dual ability to be 
both estrogenic and anti-estrogenic is not contradictory. Rather, it reflects their 
complex interactions with estrogen receptors, their role as selective estrogen receptor 
modulators, and dose-dependent effects. 
In 2022, Won Jang and colleagues released a study examining the antioxidant, anti-
inflammatory, anti-menopausal, and anti-carcinogenic properties of lignans and 
their metabolites. I highly recommend reading the whole paper, which provides clear 
explanations of numerous topics we are addressing in this book related to coffee. 
Following that study, Liu et al. (2024) showed lignans to be associated with reduced 
risks of chronic diseases, including cardiovascular diseases, type 2 diabetes, and certain 
cancers, due to their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. 
Additionally, the potential health benefits of lignans include cardiovascular protection 
and neuroprotective effects, and it does this by reducing oxidative stress (González-
May 2024).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 145
FLAVONOIDS, FLAVONOLS, FLAVANOLS, AND ISOFLAVONES
Understanding the differences between flavonoids, flavonols, flavanols, and isoflavones 
helps us appreciate the complexity of coffee even more fully. Flavonoids are a broad 
class of polyphenolic compounds that include several subgroups, such as flavonols, 
flavanols, and isoflavonoids. These compounds are widely distributed in the plant 
kingdom. They are recognized for their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and even 
neuroprotective properties, contributing to the prevention of chronic diseases such 
as cardiovascular conditions, certain cancers, and neurodegenerative disorders (Bravo 
1998; Mocanu 2015; Gonzalez-May 2024).
Flavonoids are found in both coffee beans and leaves, with the leaves having a higher 
concentration and variety of these compounds. Coffee leaves have been shown to 
contain quercetin, kaempferol, rutin, mangiferin, catechins, procyanidins, and 
other polyphenols, many of which degrade or diminish during the roasting of beans 
(Cangeloni 2022; Ruse 2025; Ngamsuk 2019). These compounds exhibit potent 
antioxidant activity and may support skin, cardiovascular, and cognitive health, as 
well as gut microbiota modulation (Kolb 2020; Gonzalez-May 2024).
Flavonols, including quercetin and kaempferol, are found in both green coffee beans 
and leaves, with higher concentrations in the latter (Mannino 2023; Cangeloni 
2022). These compounds are known for their ability to reduce oxidative stress and 
inflammation, modulate cellular signaling, and support vascular integrity.
Flavanols, such as catechin and epicatechin, are structurally distinct from flavonols 
and are more concentrated in tea, cocoa, and coffee leaves than in roasted beans. 
They are particularly valued for their cardiovascular benefits, including improved 
endothelial function and blood pressure regulation, and are known to accumulate in 
brain tissue, where they may exert neuroprotective effects (Batra and Sharma 2013; 
Gonzalez-May 2024).
Isoflavones are a class of phytoestrogens (plant-derived compounds with estrogenic 
activity) found in various plants, including coffee beans. In coffee, the primary 
isoflavones are formononetin, daidzein, and genistein.

146 ♦ Ildi Revi
From a farming and processing standpoint, cultivar choice, leaf maturity, drying 
methods, and even drying temperature can significantly influence the concentration 
of flavonoids and other polyphenols (Ngamsuk 2019). For example, formononetin 
is an isoflavone known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-carcinogenic 
benefits, and research indicates that Robusta coffee beans contain significantly 
higher levels of isoflavones, particularly formononetin, compared to Arabica beans. 
Specifically, the total isoflavone content is approximately six times higher in Robusta 
than in Arabica (Alves 2010). Formononetin has demonstrated a range of potential 
health benefits in experimental and emerging clinical research: It induces apoptosis in 
cancer cells, modulates signaling pathways involved in tumor suppression, supports 
vascular relaxation and reduces inflammatory markers associated with atherosclerosis 
(Ding 2024).
Understanding the complexity of coffee’s flavonoid and isoflavone profile opens up 
pathways not only for functional beverage innovation but also for more nuanced 
farming practices and supply chain design in alignment with CHC values. The 
chapters on roasting and brewing also have information on these compounds.
PHENYLINDANES
Phenylindanes are compounds found in coffee, especially in dark roast varieties. These 
compounds have attracted attention for their potential health benefits, including their 
ability to inhibit the aggregation of amyloid-beta and tau proteins, which are associated 
with neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s. Additionally, 
phenylindanes exhibit strong antioxidant properties, helping to protect cells from 
damage caused by free radicals (Mancini 2018).
Phenylindanes are formed during the roasting process when CGL breaks down and 
oligomerizes, meaning that small molecules join together to create a larger complex 
with a few repeating units. As a note of comparison, melanoidins are formed differently 
through polymerization during the Maillard reaction. Polymerization involves joining 
many small molecules to form large, complex structures with numerous repeating 
units. This process creates high-molecular-weight compounds contributing to roasted 
coffee’s brown color and rich flavor. This difference in formation processes highlights 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 147
the diverse chemical reactions during coffee roasting, each contributing to the final 
product’s complex flavor and health properties. 
Recap Of Compounds Of Interest
β-Carbolines
These are neuroactive compounds that form during coffee roasting and become more concentrated 
in darker roasts. Norharman and harman, the primary beta-carbolines in coffee, interact with brain 
receptors and may inhibit enzymes that break down neurotransmitters. This action is linked to 
potential antidepressant effects and support for neurological health. They also act as antioxidants, 
helping to neutralize harmful molecules and support mitochondrial function. While promising, 
their health effects are dose-dependent and can vary by individual, highlighting the need for 
further research and mindful consumption.
N-methylpyridinium (NMP)
NMP is created during roasting from the breakdown of trigonelline and is found primarily in 
dark-roasted coffee. It has been shown to activate the Nrf2 cellular defense system, helping to 
reduce oxidative stress and protect DNA. NMP may support liver function, reduce stomach acid, 
and increase levels of protective compounds like glutathione and vitamin E. It is quickly absorbed 
into the bloodstream and is being studied as a marker for recent coffee consumption.
Lignans, Flavonoids and Isoflavones
Although present in smaller amounts, these polyphenols add to coffee’s health-promoting 
properties. Flavonoids such as quercetin and kaempferol provide antioxidant and anti-
inflammatory benefits and may support brain and immune health. Isoflavones have been studied 
for their hormone-modulating effects, particularly in Robusta coffee. Lignans are metabolized by 
gut microbes and are associated with reduced risks of cardiovascular disease, metabolic disorders, 
and certain cancers.
Phenylindanes
These compounds are formed during the later stages of roasting through the transformation of 
chlorogenic acid derivatives. Phenylindanes are most abundant in darker roasts and are being 
studied for their ability to inhibit the buildup of proteins associated with Alzheimer’s and 
Parkinson’s disease. They also contribute to the depth of coffee’s flavor and add to its antioxidant 
potential.

148 ♦ Ildi Revi
BASIC COMPONENTS OF COFFEE
Carbohydrates: Carbohydrates in coffee seeds are primarily composed of 
polysaccharides and sucrose. Polysaccharides, including galactomannans, type II 
arabinogalactans, and cellulose, constitute about 59-61 percent of the bean’s dry 
weight and decrease by 12-40 percent during roasting. Sucrose comprises around 4-8 
percent of the bean’s composition and is almost entirely degraded during roasting, 
even in light-roasted coffee (Farah 2005). Among the polysaccharides, galactomannans 
show the greatest resistance to high temperatures. As a result, they are the main 
polysaccharides that survive the roasting process and are extracted when the coffee is 
brewed (Portillo and Arévalo 2022).
Proteins: Amino acids, peptides, and proteins are essential for the quality and aroma 
of coffee. They comprise about 13 percent of the seed and include amino acids that 
form typical aromas during roasting (Gloess 2013). Coffee beans contain a complex 
mixture of proteins, including enzymes that contribute to the biochemical processes 
within the bean. Despite ongoing research, our understanding of the full complexity of 
coffee proteins and their role in quality is still evolving (Toci and Farah 2008). Coffee’s 
protein quantity is insufficient for it to be considered a meaningful source of protein 
in the diet. Most of the protein content remains in the coffee grounds after brewing 
(ref: from the author’s conversation with Farah in December 2024).
Vitamins and Minerals: Coffee is a source of essential vitamins and minerals, including 
B vitamins and vitamin E, which are discussed in the section on trigonelline and 
diterpenes. Concerning minerals, potassium is usually the most prevalent, followed 
by magnesium and phosphorus, with other minerals like calcium, sodium, iron, zinc, 
and copper present in smaller quantities. The levels and types of minerals vary due 
to the complex factors mentioned in previous pages, from farming to roasting. It is 
worth noting that some minerals in coffee are metals. For people with certain health 
conditions, these metal contents may need to be measured to determine if the coffee 
is safe for consumption. This topic is explored further in the section on metals under 
“contaminants,” which also includes a discussion on how to read a COA.

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦149
Organic Acids: Organic acids such as citric, malic, acetic, and quinic acids are well-
known contributors to the acidity and brightness of roasted coffee’s flavor. Several 
other organic acids are found in coffee, including formic, glycolic, lactic, and succinic. 
Phosphoric acid, an inorganic acid, is also found in coffee in high quantities, especially 
in Robusta. These organic acids, especially quinic, can have significant health impacts. 
They have been shown to affect blood glucose and insulin responses, support the 
liver, aid digestion, improve antioxidants synergistically, and even show some anti-
inflammatory activity in the brain (citric acid, specifically). Citric acid in coffee 
acts as an antioxidant and can enhance mineral absorption, contributing to overall 
health (Sweiss 2018). Malic acid is known for boosting metabolism, improving 
digestion, and increasing energy levels (Chen 2023). Quinic acid has multiple health 
benefits, including antioxidant, antidiabetic, anticarcinogenic, antimicrobial, and 
antiviral effects. It influences cellular processes like insulin secretion, apoptosis, and 
bacterial ribosome function, making it a valuable compound for potential therapeutic 
applications (Benali 2022). It may also help lower blood pressure and blood sugar 
levels.
Excerpts of 3rd party COA. 
This is for one serving (15 grams) of the dry ground coffee, NOT brewed.

150 ♦ Ildi Revi
Most roasted coffee has a pH between 4.8 and 5.4, with a small buffering capacity 
or titratable acidity. This means that coffee has a lower acid content, on average, 
than tomato or orange juice. However, coffee’s pH is not so different from bananas, 
watermelon, or pumpkin. This makes the acidity of coffee very unlikely to affect 
stomach ailments or cause any significant difference in stomach acid, but personal 
experiences with coffee and stomach issues may vary. We discussed acids and gut 
health in the previous chapter, and more information on coffee acids and acidity is 
provided in the roasting chapter.
Testing the pH of brewed coffee can be tricky due to temperature 
fluctuations, which can affect the accuracy of pH measurements.

151
4.4 Compounds of Concern  
in Coffee
THE DOSE MAKES THE POISON
I have revised this introduction multiple times, losing sleep over it. I want to 
balance awareness without causing alarm, watchfulness with trust, culpability with 
responsibility, accountability with empathy and understanding that people doing 
the work in this category are striving to change a long-established industry. Concerns 
about contaminants in our food, including coffee, highlight potential hazards, but 
letting anxiety dominate us is also detrimental to our well-being.
Holistic coffee’s job is to do the work so that the consumer can verify via lab reports 
and other proof that we have met the standards we set. In this way, consumers, and in 
many cases, the wellness professionals advising them, can make informed, risk-based 
decisions and observe how their bodies react. Adjust as necessary and approach this 
balance calmly, confidently, and positively.
Concerning contaminants in food, remember the adage, “The dose makes the 
poison.” Almost any substance can be harmful in large quantities, but the 
small amounts typically found in coffee are not usually a reason to worry about. 
Contaminants in our food supply result from natural sources and human activities 
like environmental pollution and agricultural practices. Eliminating contaminants 
entirely from any food is nearly impossible, as virtually all foods naturally contain 
some amounts of something that, at some level, would be considered a contaminant. 
This is part of living.
At the same time, attention to food intake is critical for those whose bodies are unwell 
or out of balance. I deeply recognize the significance of my responsibility here in 

152 ♦ Ildi Revi
helping ensure the path to better health. I also respect those who are healthy and seek to 
maximize their potential by making wise choices. This is at the core of holistic coffee.
Knowledge is empowering. Understanding our bodies provides reassurance. For 
millennia, our bodies’ systems and processes have enabled our ancestors to survive 
numerous challenges, become resilient, and handle abundant environmental factors 
throughout the ages. Our bodies keep us alive through a complex interplay of 
biochemical processes, cellular functions, intricate chemistry, helpful microorganisms 
and regulatory systems that maintain homeostasis, protect against pathogens, and 
repair damage. Trust this to give you peace of mind, too. 
Learning about contaminants requires patience, as it can be profoundly personal, 
especially for those with conditions needing vigilance. I do not want to reduce concerns 
about toxins or other contaminants in coffee. They exist, and it is frustrating when 
people in the industry deny that there is anything to worry about. That obliviousness 
is a major part of why this category has emerged.
Also, please steer clear of fear-based marketing tactics regarding coffee. Exaggeration 
can create unnecessary confusion and anxiety among those who want to enjoy coffee 
and live a healthy life. While some compounds in coffee may sound alarming, they 
often result from the roasting process. The chemical names of natural compounds may 
sound intimidating, but they are simply scientific terms that describe their molecular 
structures and properties. Coffee is made up of hundreds of naturally occurring 
chemicals that also change during the roasting and brewing processes.
Coffee is no exception when it comes to containing contaminants. However, these 
contaminants are generally at such low levels that they are unlikely to pose a serious 
health risk for most healthy people. On the other hand, we do not want pollutants to 
accumulate in healthy bodies or cause sudden reactions, so we should not ignore their 
presence. The principle of holistic coffee is to identify, acknowledge, and reduce these 
contaminants to the lowest possible levels, ensuring a healthier product.
It is important to keep perspective: Healthy coffee typically has low levels of 
contaminants, which is just one aspect of its overall health benefits. While we 
strive to produce the healthiest coffees on the market, we must recognize that some 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 153
contaminants may still be present until we actively address them. Still, we aim to 
eliminate contaminants, just like the FDA’s “Closer to Zero” campaign. 
This is a process of continual improvement. Holistic coffee aims to demonstrate that 
coffee is produced with the highest health and environmental standards. It promises 
to improve and actively provide peace of mind. The purpose is to allow you to enjoy 
your coffee, start your day feeling positive, and live your life confidently. 
This category of coffee is setting standards, showing proof, being transparent, getting 
feedback from scientists and customers, listening and taking corrective actions. 
Information gives you the choice to drink or not to drink coffee. It is not intended 
for brand-bashing, which is counter-productive and unhelpful for real people with 
families striving to do honest work. We are doing the work, considering every point 
in the chain of custody of coffee. 
Coffee can be contaminated at various stages: farming, processing, drying, storage 
and transport. Mold and mycotoxins are frequently cited concerns, but others warrant 
attention. “Clean” coffee should be the most basic thing you expect from your coffee.
