Circular health coffee focuses on ways to encourage the best the coffee plant can 
provide, like antioxidants, bioactive compounds, minerals, and nutrients. Still, it also 
monitors the production cycle, reducing or eliminating “the bad stuff.” Providing 
laboratory results as proof, whenever possible, helps build trust and transparency 
to allow customers to make decisions for their wellness. In the next chapter, I will 
discuss laboratory testing, standards, limits, limitations and realistic expectations for 
consumers about the testing.
As you read about contaminants in this section, think about your intake from all 
sources (food, water, the environment around you) and decide which products to 
limit or ask for healthier versions. Knowing our health needs allows us to navigate 
food safety issues confidently without unnecessary fear. Knowing yourself and staying 
informed empowers you to make safer dietary choices as part of this movement toward 
food as medicine.
 

154 ♦ Ildi Revi
HIGHLIGHTS: Compounds of Concern
Mycotoxins such as ochratoxin A (OTA), deoxynivalenol (DON), and aflatoxin B1 are toxic 
byproducts of mold that may survive roasting. These compounds are colorless, odorless, and 
tasteless—only laboratory testing can detect them. While mold spores are often destroyed during 
roasting, post-roast contamination remains a risk. CHC standards require preventive measures 
and verified lab testing at origin to ensure consumer safety.
Heavy metals including lead, mercury, cadmium, and arsenic can enter coffee through soil, water, 
or processing surfaces. Even trace amounts matter due to bioaccumulation and health risks. Lead 
and mercury can affect neurological and kidney function; cadmium is linked to cancer and bone 
damage; arsenic has carcinogenic potential. Holistic coffee must undergo routine heavy metal 
testing, and coffees exceeding CHC limits are rejected—regardless of flavor or origin.
Acrylamide forms during roasting via the Maillard reaction and is typically higher in lighter 
roasts. Although partially degraded during roasting and extraction, it is considered a probable 
carcinogen. Health-focused roasters reduce exposure through optimized roast curves, strategic 
origin selection, and processing choices.
Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAH) form at very high roasting temperatures, especially with 
direct flame contact. Compounds like benzo[a]pyrene are carcinogenic in animal models. Roasters 
can reduce PAH by avoiding charring, using indirect heat methods, and carefully managing roast 
development.
2-Phenylphenol (2-PP) is not naturally present in green coffee but can form in trace amounts 
during roasting. It is also used in some industrial cleaning products and citrus preservation, though 
not approved for use in coffee farming. While the roasting-related amounts are minimal and 
pose little risk, contamination from facility disinfectants is a greater concern. Certified organic 
operations and well-managed facilities minimize this risk through proper protocols.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 155
MOLD AND MYCOTOXINS 
Mold and mycotoxins remain contentious topics within the coffee industry. Among 
professionals who learned to evaluate coffee through older sensory-based quality 
systems, it’s common to hear that “high-quality coffee doesn’t contain mold” or that 
mycotoxins are a non-issue. Many confidently claim that coffees scoring above certain 
thresholds, often associated with traditional quality classifications, could not possibly 
harbor these contaminants. There is some truth behind these assumptions, but it’s 
not the whole story.
One of the goals of this book is to unpack complex issues and resist the temptation 
to simplify them into blanket truths. When repeated often enough, oversimplified 
claims can harden into industry myth, unintentionally misleading producers, roasters, 
and consumers alike. Mold and mycotoxins are not just abstract fears or marketing 
buzzwords; they are well-documented food safety concerns in agricultural commodities 
around the world, and coffee is no exception.
The coffee industry is still muddled when it comes to clearly distinguishing between 
mold spores, visible mold growth, and the toxic byproducts known as mycotoxins. 
This is a “sky in the jar” moment, a chance to look closer. The immediate response 
from many professionals is, “Coffee doesn’t contain mold. That’s absurd.” But when 
we take the time to examine the scientific evidence, the regulatory frameworks, and the 
mechanisms of contamination, a more nuanced, and more accurate, picture emerges. 
I invite you to read this chapter with curiosity and a willingness to examine the details. 
Often, it’s in those details that clarity and insight are found.
While interrelated, each of these is distinct but often conflated with its properties 
and impacts:
•	
Molds are microscopic fungi that form visible colonies when they grow 
in large numbers. It is important to note that not all fungi are classified as 
molds.
•	
Mold spores are microscopic reproductive cells produced by molds for 
propagation. They are typically invisible to the naked eye and can sometimes 

156 ♦ Ildi Revi
appear as a dust-like powder. Essentially, they are the “seeds” of molds, but 
they could develop into molds under favorable conditions.
•	
Mycotoxins are naturally occurring toxic compounds produced by certain 
molds. Importantly, not all molds generate mycotoxins (Bennett and Klich 
2003).
Molds can be found anywhere that the conditions are right for them to live, grow 
and reproduce–whether indoors or outdoors. Molds and their spores are almost 
everywhere, and there are thousands of types of mold. Some are used to produce 
common things like medicine (i.e., penicillin) and foods (i.e., cheese). Others are 
harmful and can cause serious allergies and health problems, whether breathed in or 
ingested, and some, like household black mold, are poisonous. Not all individuals 
will process mold in the same way. Although most foodborne fungi are not infectious, 
some species can cause infection, especially in immunocompromised populations, such 
as those of advanced age, certain disabilities, HIV-infected individuals and persons 
receiving chemotherapy or antibiotic treatment (Chmel 1993, Kaur 2024). 
Mold can enter the coffee chain in a number of ways. Since molds commonly grow in 
warm and moist places such as soil, plants, decomposing matter and foods, farms in 
the tropical zones where coffee grows are perfect environments for molds to thrive. It is 
common for coffee fruits, if left unprocessed, to grow mold within a few hours of being 
picked. Some molds and fungi grow on the fruits while still on the plants, and some 
molds and fungi in the soils enter the plants at the roots. Mold can grow on harvested 
coffee when it is not dried quickly enough or properly, or when the dried coffee is 
stored improperly. When mold develops on coffee beans, it is sometimes visible and 
classified as “fungus damage”, a defect that disqualifies the coffee from certain historic 
quality grading systems. However, being excluded from one tier of classification does 
not mean the coffee is removed from the market. In practice, nearly every grade of 
coffee finds a buyer somewhere. This means that mold-damaged beans, though filtered 
out of premium categories, are still roasted, brewed, and consumed, raising legitimate 
food safety concerns. It would be misleading to suggest that mold in coffee is not an 
issue when, in reality, it enters the supply chain and ultimately someone’s cup. We will 
examine this more closely in the farming chapter, where post-harvest handling plays 
a critical role in determining microbial risk. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 157
Also, not all damage from molds is visible. You cannot see mycotoxins, so green grading 
for fungus damage is not the end of the story.
Molds reproduce with their powdery spores that are capable of widespread dispersion. 
One of the unfortunate realities of the coffee trade is that mold spores on green 
coffee are quite common. These spores may be inactive on the surface of processed 
and dried coffee. Laboratory tests on green coffee often reveal colony-forming units, 
indicating the presence of viable mold spores. During the busy seasons, dry mills 
typically do not clean equipment and screens between batches, potentially leading to 
cross-contamination where moldy or spore-covered coffees can affect clean coffees. 
HOW BIG A PROBLEM IS MOLD?
Most times, mold is inactive just like fungi or spores, due to the lack of proper 
conditions, which are not conducive for growth, and the coffee supply chain is rife 
with this kind of inactive mold. When discussing mold in coffee, we primarily discuss 
microscopic mold spores. A major industry concern is that many coffee roasters are 
unaware that their green coffee may be coated in mold spores. Roasters and employees 
in coffee roasting facilities frequently handle green coffee, inhale green coffee dust, 
and may carry it on their clothing. While mold spores do not produce mycotoxins, 
they may cause allergies or respiratory issues for workers exposed to them. Several 
people I know had to quit roasting because they had developed allergies, which I now 
hypothesize were mold-related. In the section on “good manufacturing practices and 
food safety plans for coffee importers and roasters,” we will discuss in-depth ways to 
remedy or lessen these risks.
As long as the moisture remains low, mold spores may only cause allergies or respiratory 
issues. Roasting coffee is an effective way to kill mold spores. Mold spores are destroyed 
in the coffee roasting process (which uses temperatures over 400°F [204°C]) and can 
be eliminated during the hot brewing of coffee. This is one of the many reasons we see 
those in the coffee industry cry “foul” when discussing mold in coffee. Discovering 
visible mold on roasted coffee is exceptionally rare and would indicate a severe 
breakdown in quality control. Properly roasted and stored coffee should be entirely 
free of mold. 

158 ♦ Ildi Revi
Yet mold spores can get onto roasted coffee through poor manufacturing practices, such 
as touching coffee in the cooling tray with unwashed hands or cross-contamination, 
such as using the same scoop or bucket for green and roasted coffee. Later in the 
production process, if contaminated coffee is ground, mold spores can transfer to the 
grinder, potentially affecting future batches. While it is true that hot brewing coffee 
typically kills mold spores, many people brew coffee cold, a process that involves 
steeping coffee at room temperature, which can create a conducive environment for 
mold growth if spores are present. We will explore these issues further in the upcoming 
“food safety and cold brew methods” sections.
These are the multitude of ways in which mold spores can enter the finished product. 
Many large roaster manufacturers use water quench cooling, rapidly cooling freshly 
roasted coffee beans by spraying a precise amount of water onto them to prevent 
over-roasting. It is not uncommon for their equipment to go out of calibration and 
over-quench, causing the moisture level of the roasted coffee to be conducive for 
mold growth. 
Luckily, coffee has antiseptic properties that usually do not allow mold to grow in the 
short term. It is rare to find visibly moldy roasted coffee when you open a bag you 
have just purchased. But it can happen. For commercial coffees that sit in warehouses 
for months or years before being put in big box store shelves, this is where molds can 
start to grow. These coffees that have become contaminated by spores may develop 
visible mold. It is not enough to use coffee’s antiseptic compounds as a food safety 
strategy; at best, it is a consideration when evaluating risk.
HOW BIG A PROBLEM ARE MYCOTOXINS?
Mycotoxins are an entirely different category of risk when roasting coffee for health. 
Mycotoxins are the metabolites (byproducts) of certain mold species metabolizing 
the host (coffee fruit, coffee beans, etc.). In other words, as the mold decomposes 
organic matter, it leaves behind the chemicals of its digestive enzymes, which can 
be toxic. Although roasting kills mold and its spores, this is not necessarily the case 
with mycotoxins. Some may be destroyed while some are not, and sometimes, there 
is only a reduction in the mycotoxin levels during roasting. Although mycotoxins are 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 159
the byproducts of living mold colonies, they are not mold. They are chemicals derived 
from mold that have integrated into the coffee seeds and, thus, are not living things 
that can be killed. Emphasis is placed on this because this still seems to be a point of 
confusion in the blogosphere.
One major issue for the coffee industry is that you cannot see mycotoxins; you must 
test for them. Multiple scientific studies have demonstrated that mycotoxins of various 
types can be found in coffee. Because these toxins are colorless, odorless and tasteless, 
the only way to know is by lab testing. Ochratoxin A and deoxynivalenol are two 
common mycotoxins in coffee, although aflatoxin B1 has also been known to occur 
(Pestka 2010). Visible mold on green coffee beans indicates a potential presence of 
mycotoxins, but the absence of visible mold does not guarantee that mycotoxins are 
not present. Many mycotoxins (including aflatoxins, fumonisins and trichothecenes) 
are immunosuppressive and pose serious implications for the ability of many people to 
resist disease, particularly those who are malnourished or who have mast cell disorders 
(covered in the previous chapter) (Merck Manual online 2024).
The prevalence of mycotoxin contamination in coffee is a complex issue that varies 
significantly based on several factors. While it is more common in low-quality 
coffees grown in hot, humid tropical environments, they provide ideal conditions for 
mold growth. Mold is not inevitable for all coffee production. High-quality coffees 
from well-managed farms and processing facilities are less likely to have significant 
mycotoxin contamination. 
It is important to note that while mold growth can occur on coffee beans, not all 
molds produce mycotoxins, and mold does not automatically indicate mycotoxin 
contamination. Some molds that do not produce toxins may be used to combat those 
that do.
Proper harvesting techniques, quick and effective drying processes, and appropriate 
storage conditions are vital in preventing mold growth and subsequent mycotoxin 
production. Regular testing and adherence to good agricultural and manufacturing 
practices are essential in mitigating the risk of mycotoxin contamination in coffee. 
While completely eliminating mycotoxins may be challenging, some in the coffee 

160 ♦ Ildi Revi
industry are implementing quality control measures to minimize their occurrence 
and ensure consumer safety. 
To control mycotoxin contamination in coffee for health, we want to avoid synthetic 
anti-fungal chemicals since their toxicity may be just as unhealthy as mycotoxins. 
Many researchers view using biological methods, such as yeasts, bacteria, and certain 
fungi, as a promising way to inhibit and reduce mold. Interestingly, different strains 
of the same fungal species can vary in their ability to produce mycotoxins within 
the fungal kingdom. Some strains can produce these toxic compounds, while others 
do not. For example, one such strain of mold that does not produce mycotoxins is 
Aspergillus niger strain C187 (GenBank accession no. KJ599619, ß-tubulin gene. 
This beneficial mold strain can aggressively outpace its toxigenic rival. C187 swiftly 
dominates the shared habitat, starving the harmful contaminant before it can establish 
a foothold or produce dangerous mycotoxins (De Almeida 2019). 
While using non-toxigenic molds to prevent mycotoxin-producing molds shows 
promising results, it still raises important concerns in coffee production. This 
approach, which uses one type of mold to control contamination by another, can be 
problematic when roasting coffee for health-conscious consumers, particularly those 
with extreme mold sensitivities. When employing this method, careful management 
of the biological control agent, the beneficial mold, is crucial to prevent it from 
becoming a contaminant. Food safety risks of mold spores and cross-contamination 
persist and must be mitigated. 
Evidence-based risk assessments are necessary for producers and roasters to develop 
sensible approaches to mold and mycotoxin mitigation. These efforts aim to ensure 
the safety of health-conscious, environmentally responsible coffee, providing peace 
of mind to consumers who prioritize these qualities in their coffee choices. By 
addressing these concerns, the coffee industry can work toward a balance between 
effective mycotoxin control and producing coffee that meets the highest health and 
environmental responsibility standards–an important step in developing the health 
coffee category. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 161
OCHRATOXIN A (OTA 
Ochratoxin A (OTA is a mycotoxin found in various agricultural products and is 
the main mycotoxin associated with coffee. Ochratoxin is a chemical contaminant 
produced by certain strains of Aspergillus ochraceus, Aspergillus westerdijkiae, 
Aspergillus carbonarius, and Aspergillus niger. Some ochratoxin can be degraded at 
high temperatures, but batches with significant contamination levels will still test 
positive after roasting (Castellanos-Onoria 2011).
The Joint Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA) of the United Nations Food 
and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and WHO reported that the major sources of 
human OTA exposure are cereals, wine, grape juice, coffee, spices, and pork. OTA 
can also accumulate in animal tissue via contaminated stock feed. Dietary exposure to 
ochratoxin can cause acute toxicity in mammalian kidneys and may be carcinogenic. 
It is also an immunosuppressant, inhibiting the body’s ability to heal or protect itself 
from disease. 
The FDA, which ensures the safety and quality of food, drugs, and other consumer 
products in the U.S., does not provide an action level for OTA. However, the European 
Union’s action levels differ according to food products. In 2022, the EU set new limits 
for OTA (Regulation (EU) 2022/1370). The maximum OTA level for roasted and 
ground coffee was lowered to 3 μg/kg (μg is the symbol for microgram). In coffee, it 
is 5 μg/kg (European Coffee Federation 2022).
Given the potential health risks associated with OTA and worldwide regulatory 
standards, coffee producers and roasters must implement rigorous quality control 
measures throughout the supply chain. By prioritizing the prevention and detection 
of OTA contamination, the coffee industry can work toward ensuring the safety of its 
products and meeting the highest global standards, ultimately protecting consumer 
health and maintaining trust in the market.

162 ♦ Ildi Revi
DEOXYNIVALENOL (DON), KNOWN 
COLLOQUIALLY AS VOMITOXIN
Deoxynivalenol (DON) is a type of mycotoxin produced by Fusarium species of 
fungi, particularly Fusarium graminearum, but in coffee, it is caused by F. xylarioides 
and is called “coffee wilt disease.” Species within the Fusarium genus are notorious 
for inhabiting soil and causing various crop diseases that have significant economic 
impacts, affecting many crops, particularly wheat, corn and oats (Shabeer 2021). 
These fungi harm plants, humans, and animals, leading to various illnesses. A 
particularly hazardous trait of these fungi is their production of toxic fumonisins and 
trichothecenes, which can lead to multiple symptoms, including nausea, vomiting, 
diarrhea, skin irritation, and, in severe cases, immunosuppression (Pinton 2012). 
I have a theory, which I hope scientists can test soon–that one potential cause of 
intestinal issues with coffee may be DON in the coffee from Fusarium, which has 
become a real problem for many farmers worldwide. It does not help that other foods 
which people may eat with a cup of coffee are even more contaminated with DON 
than most people realize. Wheat, barley, oats, rye, millet, corn, soy, rice, and seed oils 
are all sources of DON, nivalenol, T-2, HT-2 and others (Gonya 2023). DON is even 
found in dairy because it is in the grains that cows are fed.
Chronic low-dose exposure to DON upregulates cytokines, chemokines, and 
inflammatory genes. Recall that we noted earlier that their upregulation causes the 
body to produce more of these signals and proteins, which can lead to an enhanced 
inflammatory response. This can be a part of the body’s normal reaction to fight off 
infections. In some cases, excessive or chronic upregulation can lead to inflammatory 
diseases or exacerbation of conditions like Crohn’s disease or other gastrointestinal 
disorders. Higher doses exacerbate these effects, causing leukocyte apoptosis, significant 
immune suppression, and symptoms like nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, headache, 
diarrhea, hemorrhaging, gastroenteritis, dizziness, and fever (Gonya 2023).
DON exposure disrupts and compromises the intestinal mucus barrier. It also alters 
gut microbiota (Saint-Cyr 2013) and increases intestinal permeability, often called 
“leaky gut,” facilitating the uptake of DON and other luminal antigens (Pinton 
2012). This triggers an inflammatory response, activating antigen-presenting cells and 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 163
T-lymphocytes, causing intestinal inflammation (Park 2017). Further complications
include impaired glucose uptake, diarrhea, and water malabsorption (Gonya 2024).
Due to its potential health risks, there are regulatory limits for DON in food products 
to minimize exposure. Coffee roasting can reduce DON slightly, but not entirely 
(Gonya 2023). It is, therefore, vital when roasting ecological and health-conscious 
coffee, to add this to your testing protocol and find out the levels before you approve 
the coffee, as mitigating DON during roasting is not a good strategy for limiting levels.
There have been few studies on coffee and DON levels, although I have had 
conversations with one of the researchers cited here, who tested almost 200 coffees 
from all different levels of quality and found DON in nearly all of them. The data 
from this is unpublished, though. I tried to find a published study on coffee and 
DON. It was conducted on 169 ready-to-drink coffee samples from Valencia, Spain, 
which revealed multiple mycotoxins (García-Moraleja 2015). The study found that 
43 percent of the samples contained deoxynivalenol (DON) in concentrations ranging 
from 16.58 to 954 mg/kg. Additionally, 3-acetyldeoxynivalenol (3-ADON) and 
15-acetyldeoxynivalenol (15-ADON) were detected in 20 percent and 41 percent of
the samples, respectively, with levels ranging from 6.31 to 24.57 mg/kg for 3-ADON
and 61.04 to 884.25 mg/kg for 15-ADON.
Despite these findings, a risk assessment indicated that the combined levels of DON 
and its metabolites (3-ADON and 15-ADON) accounted for only 0.77 percent of the 
Provisional Maximum Tolerable Daily Intake (PMTDI). According to the conclusion 
of the study led by Garcia-Moraleja, the levels of DON in the coffee samples do not 
pose a significant risk to consumers. Because people drink multiple cups of coffee daily, 
it is something for people with gut issues to be aware of. I hope this area of study gets 
traction soon, because there is too much we do not know, yet data and indications 
from other crops with DON are alarming (Gonya 2023 and 2024).
There is hope on the horizon, although, again, further human research is needed. 
Researchers have found that targeted mycotoxin binders and probiotics, such as 
Lactobacillus rhamnosus RC007, can mitigate the adverse effects of biohazards like 
deoxynivalenol (DON) in animal gastrointestinal systems and animal feed (Garcia 
2017). While I could not find studies on humans, the study tested 12 strains of 

Lactobacillus bacteria and 6 strains of brewer’s yeast for their ability to detoxify 
mycotoxins. The scientists observed significant reductions in mycotoxin concentrations 
with up to 43 percent of DON. 
Another study showed that combining glycyrrhinic acid with brewer’s yeast and certain 
bacteria found in humans degrades DON, reducing its cytotoxicity and inflammation 
(Xu 2023). Finally, recent research highlighted the beneficial effects of probiotics, 
prebiotics, synbiotics (pre- and probiotics combined), and postbiotics (bioactive 
compounds produced when probiotic bacteria in your gut ferment prebiotic fibers) 
on gut microbiota, which detoxified and alleviated the harmful effects of mycotoxins. 
However, more in vivo and long-term studies are needed to fully understand these 
interactions and develop them as a preventive strategy against mycotoxin toxicity.
In mitigating risk, our primary focus is on coffee plants that bear fruit contaminated 
by Fusarium. If we can eradicate Fusarium in coffee soils, it should eliminate the 
deoxynivalenol from the coffee beans. Also, we need to get the word out to farmers to 
isolate any coffee they have collected from diseased plants. Sometimes, the coffee plant 
will kill off fruits when it gets infected, but some producers will continue to harvest 
the coffee fruit that has died. When a tree is infected with this species of fungi, it will 
ultimately die, resulting in years of lost labor and resources and significant investment 
in remedying the soil. Coffee farmers know there is a market for low-quality coffee 
and are simply trying to mitigate their losses. Many of them are unaware that the 
fruits contain deoxynivalenol. You will read about organic best practices for dealing 
with such issues at the farm level in the chapter on farming. The only way to be sure 
that a coffee does not contain this and other mycotoxins is through laboratory testing.
164 ♦ Ildi Revi

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 165
For Coffee Roasters: Food Safety Notes
As roasters, we know that heat is a powerful kill step—yes, roasting destroys most mold spores. 
But that doesn’t mean we’re off the hook when it comes to food safety. Mold spores and other 
contaminants can still re-enter the coffee after the roast, especially if post-roast handling isn’t 
clean.
You’ll find more detailed guidance in the food safety chapter, but here are a few key reminders 
we need to keep top of mind in the roastery:
•
Keep your hands off the coffee unless they’re clean and sanitized. No exceptions, even
during busy production days.
•
Use separate scoops for green and roasted coffee—and sanitize them between uses. Cross-
contamination can happen fast.
•
Never load green coffee over the cooling tray unless you’re absolutely sure there’s zero risk 
of dust or debris falling into the finished batch.
Also, let’s clear up a persistent myth: that mycotoxins don’t exist in high-quality or clean-looking 
coffees, or that roasting eliminates them entirely. That’s simply not true. Some mycotoxins 
degrade at high heat, but not all—and not completely. Lab testing has shown detectable levels 
of mycotoxins even in clean, defect-free, premium green coffees. The only way to know what’s 
in your coffee is to test it.
If we’re serious about coffee quality—and especially health-focused coffee—then food safety 
isn’t optional. It’s part of the craft. Keep reading for more on contamination risks and how we 
can do better.

166 ♦ Ildi Revi
Coffee fruits are still on plants that Fusarium stilboides have killed (top left and top right). 
A coffee plant was affected (above left) and killed (above) by Fusarium stilboides.

undetectable. The reporting limit is the sensitivity. These reports have been cropped and 
redacted due to copyright limitations, but none of the results have been altered.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 169
ACRYLAMIDE PRODUCED IN ROASTING
Acrylamide is a chemical compound found in baked goods, French fries, and roasted 
coffee. Since it is unavoidable in coffee roasting, our focus must shift toward reducing 
its levels rather than eliminating it entirely. As you read this section, consider your 
own health priorities and how important minimizing acrylamide exposure might be 
for you personally.
Acrylamide wasn’t even discovered in food until 2002, and since then, scientists have 
been building databases to track acrylamide levels in different foods and working 
to understand how it forms. We now know that acrylamide is a byproduct of the 
Maillard reaction, that complex series of small chemical reactions that also gives roasted 
coffee its flavor and aroma (Endeshaw and Belay 2020). I cover the Maillard reaction 
in more detail in the roasting chapter, but for now the point is that acrylamide is 
formed when free asparagine reacts with reducing sugars during heating, especially at 
temperatures above 120°C. This means acrylamide is not found in raw, green coffee, 
but after roasting.
Roast degree plays a major role in acrylamide concentration. Lighter roasts tend to 
have higher levels, while darker roasts, exposed to longer roasting times and higher 
temperatures, show reduced acrylamide due to thermal degradation (Fernandes JO 
2019). Interestingly, some of this reduction may also be due to interactions with 
melanoidins, which may help bind or chemically react with acrylamide, reducing its 
availability in the final roasted coffee (Pastoriza 2012).
Although coffee roasting produces acrylamide, it’s worth noting that brewing coffee 
significantly reduces acrylamide exposure. Acrylamide is water-soluble, but only a 
fraction is extracted into the brew. Studies have found that while roasted coffee may 
contain 346–906 μg/kg, the actual brewed beverage contains much lower amounts, 
typically 25–89 μg/L, depending on roast level and brewing conditions (Endeshaw and 
Belay 2020). Espresso may yield slightly more due to finer grind and high pressure, but 
overall, brewing acts as a natural limiter. This is important for consumers concerned 
about acrylamide risk: you’re consuming far less than the raw numbers on roasted 
beans suggest.

170 ♦ Ildi Revi
From a health perspective, acrylamide can be metabolized into glycidamide, a 
compound shown to cause DNA mutations in laboratory animals. Rodent studies have 
linked acrylamide to several types of cancer, as well as to neurotoxicity, reproductive 
toxicity, and even osteoarthritis (Zhao 2023). Human studies, however, have yielded 
less consistent results. While no firm conclusions can be drawn about dietary 
acrylamide and cancer risk in humans, many regulatory agencies recommend limiting 
exposure as a precautionary measure.
In fact, the European Commission has established benchmark levels for acrylamide in 
foods. For roasted coffee, the reference value is 400 μg/kg; for instant coffee, it’s 850 
μg/kg. These are not legally binding limits, but serve as important reference points for 
health-focused coffee producers. Unfortunately, some commercially roasted coffees 
still exceed these levels (Endeshaw and Belay 2020).
When producing coffee intentionally for health, roasters must navigate the tradeoff 
between flavor development and acrylamide formation. The good news is that roasting 
can be strategically optimized. The Endeshaw and Belay (2020) study on Sidama coffee 
found that roasting at 190°C for 6 minutes significantly reduced acrylamide content 
to 336 μg/kg, while also improving antioxidant capacity and nutritional quality. This 
kind of intentional, data-informed roasting is essential in the context of health-grade 
coffee, where quality can include both sensory and health considerations.
Lightish-medium 
roast will usually 
have higher 
acrylamide than 
darker roasts.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 171
2-PHENYLPHENOL PRODUCED IN ROASTING
2-Phenylphenol is a regulated chemical used in certain fungicides and is commonly
applied to orange and lime skins to prevent mold during shipping and storage. It is
also found in cleaners and deodorizers due to its antibacterial properties. Because
of residue levels, especially on citrus and pears, regulations around this compound
developed. It is highly soluble in water and does not persist in the environment. While
there is a consensus that 2-phenylphenol is unlikely to cause cancer, it is listed under
California Proposition 65 based on rodent studies. The WHO’s International Agency
for Research on Cancer (IARC) has found limited evidence of its carcinogenicity,
noting that high doses have caused kidney and urinary tumors in male rats.
There is no justification for using this type of fungicide on coffee. While well-regulated 
pesticides or fungicides can occasionally be the most effective tool for coffee farmers, 
this particular fungicide is not listed among those regulated for use on coffee by the 
EU Commission. Organic coffee farmers would not use this chemical regardless.
During the coffee roasting process, 2-phenylphenol is produced in small quantities. 
Due to chemical reactions during the heating process, the amount of this compound 
increases progressively with the intensity of the roast. Research has shown Arabica 
coffee beans are slightly more prone to producing 2-phenylphenol during roasting 
than Robusta beans (Theurillat 2022). 
In 2000, the WHO conducted a comprehensive investigation to determine the 
safe consumption level of 2-phenylphenol. Their rigorous research established the 
Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) at 0.4 milligrams per kilogram of body weight. 
This means that for an individual weighing 80 kilograms, a daily intake of up to 32 
milligrams of 2-phenylphenol is deemed safe without health concerns during the coffee 
brewing process, where less than 20 percent of the original amount of 2-phenylphenol 
transfers to the beverage, the amounts are barely trace levels.
To put it into perspective, if you drink five cups of coffee daily, you consume less than 
0.0008 milligrams of 2-phenylphenol, a mere fraction (0.004 percent) of the WHO’s 
established safe limit. This amount is so small that health experts consider it negligible. 
In other words, for coffee enthusiasts, there is no need to worry about 2-phenylphenol 
from the roasting process. Even in extreme cases, the amount in your cup is minuscule.

172 ♦ Ildi Revi
Research in 2022 by Theurillat et al. of approximately 1,500 samples of green coffee 
tested negative for 2-phenylphenol, while levels of this compound increased with 
the degree of roasting. Therefore, finding 2-phenylphenol in roasted coffee does 
not automatically mean that the conventional or organic green coffee was treated 
with fungicide or insecticide, as some suspect. The study also looked at the five most 
common materials used in coffee packaging and found no 2-phenylphenol, noting 
that before approval for use, packaging materials are subjected to detailed assessments 
on composition to comply with regulations.
Proper manufacturing practices should be the real focus. While the roasting process 
produces a minimal amount of 2-phenylphenol, improper use of this compound as a 
cleaning agent in coffee production facilities could be a greater concern. 
The major concern of 2-phenylphenol as a contaminant is when it is used as a cleaner 
in the coffee manufacturing facility. It would not and should not be used in certified 
organic facilities, including certified organic coffee roasteries. In other food areas, it is 
mainly used to preserve citrus fruits. 2-Phenylphenol is mainly used as a disinfectant, 
bactericide, and virucide. In agriculture, it disinfects fruits, vegetables, and eggs. It 
is also used as a general surface disinfectant in many venues: hospitals, farms, food 
processing plants, etc. 
According to the WHO’s chemical fact sheets, 2-phenylphenol is readily degraded in 
surface waters and has a half-life of about one week in river water. 
POLYCYCLIC AROMATIC HYDROCARBONS:
Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH) are a group of toxic and potentially 
carcinogenic compounds that can form during the roasting process of coffee. Recent 
research has highlighted the presence of various PAH in coffee, with chrysene and 
pyrene being the most common PAH detected. Studies have found that levels of PAH 
in coffee can vary significantly, ranging from undetectable to 95.6 ng/g for chrysene 
and up to 404.7 ng/g for pyrene. Benzo[a]pyrene, a particularly harmful PAH, has 
been detected in some very dark-roasted coffee samples. These findings highlight the 
need for careful monitoring and controlling PAH levels in coffee to ensure consumer 
safety (Vegas 2019).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 173
Charring coffee by roasting it excessively, typically at temperatures above 446°F 
(230°C), can lead to the formation of PAH, which have also proven carcinogenic to 
laboratory animals. PAH form when smoke or flames react with the surface of the food 
being cooked or coffee beans being roasted. The correlation between PAH exposure 
and cancer in humans has been heavily documented, particularly in relation to grilled 
and smoked meats. When roasting coffee with health in mind, it is vital that coffee 
roasters optimize roasting temperatures and times, avoid direct flame contact with 
the beans, and monitor for noticeable charring in the coffee.
Several strategies can be employed to mitigate the formation of PAH in coffee 
production. By optimizing roasting conditions, such as temperature and time, coffee 
roasters can minimize PAH formation. Using indirect roasting methods and avoiding 
direct contact with flames can also help reduce PAH levels. This is further explained 
in the chapter on roasting. 
HEAVY METALS IN COFFEE
In May 2024, we were offered a new green coffee to consider for Purity, one that 
checked all our initial boxes for quality and sustainability. It came from a respected, 
certified organic producer with a strong track record of supplying well-known roasters 
focused on high-end coffees. When we cupped it, we were impressed. The balance, 
sweet acidity, creamy mouthfeel, and clear notes of chocolate, red apple, and roasted 
cashews made it a standout. It was free of visible defects and scored highly on every 
sensory metric.
But when we submitted a sample for laboratory testing, the results told a different 
story. Although the coffee was free of mycotoxins, it contained elevated levels of lead. 
To confirm, we sent it to a second lab, and received the same result. Despite the coffee’s 
exceptional flavor and overall quality, we could not accept it for use at Purity. Our 
health-grade standards require mercury to be undetectable and lead to be as close to 
zero as possible, significantly below international regulatory thresholds. This policy 
is non-negotiable because even trace exposure to these heavy metals can pose serious 
health risks. Lead disrupts enzyme activity, contributes to cancer risk, and is associated 
with anemia, kidney damage, and nervous system disorders. Mercury affects the 

174 ♦ Ildi Revi
brain and internal organs, leading to tremors, memory loss, mood swings, and other 
neurological symptoms.
This experience underscores a key distinction between holistic coffee and traditional 
quality systems. The coffee we tasted met all the usual expectations for premium-grade 
coffee, but for a holistic coffee framework, that isn’t enough. Flavor alone can’t override 
the need for verified safety. There will be beautiful coffees that must be rejected when 
they pose health risks. That’s the difference when quality is defined not just by taste, 
but by integrity.
Finding heavy metals in coffee is not uncommon, but their levels are typically low 
enough to fall within international safety limits set by agencies like the WHO and the 
FDA. Like many crops, coffee plants absorb minerals and trace elements from their 
environment. The soil, whether naturally mineral-rich or affected by pollution, along 
with irrigation water and post-harvest processing, can all contribute to the presence 
of heavy metals in coffee beans.
Before any coffee is contracted for purchase, it must undergo lab testing. This isn’t 
just about taste or moisture levels. It’s about verifying that the coffee meets clear, pre-
established health and quality requirements. These requirements should be explicitly 
outlined in the purchase agreement so there are no surprises at pre-shipment. For 
Circular Health Coffee, this includes a firm requirement: all pre-shipment lots must be 
tested for mycotoxins and heavy metals before the coffee leaves the country of origin. 
These tests are mandatory and central to our sourcing process.
Heavy metals deserve special attention. Elements like lead, cadmium, and arsenic 
are naturally present in soil and water, and coffee plants, like many crops, can absorb 
them from their environment. Post-harvest practices, such as drying coffee on concrete 
patios, for example, can also contribute to contamination. Even certified organic 
coffees from pristine farms can sometimes show unexpectedly high levels of heavy 
metals, as we’ve experienced firsthand. These metals accumulate in the body over time, 
and even low levels can contribute to long-term health risks. Lead is associated with 
nervous system damage, kidney dysfunction, and cognitive decline. Mercury exposure 
can affect the brain and internal organs, even at low doses.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 175
What makes this especially important is that most of the coffee industry doesn’t test 
for heavy metals. Regulatory agencies like the FDA, the European Commission, or 
Japan’s Ministry of Health have established safety limits for heavy metals in food, but 
not specifically for coffee. Unless required by an importer or certification program, 
most producers and roasters don’t test for contaminants. The focus remains on cup 
quality, not chemical composition, leaving significant blind spots when it comes to 
consumer health.
CHC draws a hard line here. Every pre-shipment lot should be tested for heavy metals 
in an accredited lab. If results exceed the thresholds of our current CHC standards, 
guided by toxicological data, international guidelines, and our own internal policy, 
the lot is rejected, regardless of its taste. That is what it means to produce coffee for 
health. Every accepted lot is backed by a COA so that ultimately, consumers can make 
decisions based on their own health priorities and risk tolerance.
People with certain health conditions may need to monitor their ingestion of specific 
levels of other metals, such as copper, chromium, cobalt, nickel and zinc. For example, 
people with Wilson’s disease must be careful about copper consumption. Although 
these metals are micronutrients in small amounts for various physiological functions, 
they can be toxic in high amounts. Too much zinc can cause digestive and metabolic 
disorders. Chromium can cause allergic reactions and anemia. Cobalt and nickel can 
cause asthma and other respiratory disorders. If you want to learn more about these 
metals and their effect on human health, a study by Scutarașu and Trincă (2023) is 
worth reading. This open-access paper provides an excellent review of heavy metals 
in food and beverages.
Cadmium exposure, primarily from food, poses health risks, including kidney failure 
and increased cancer risk. Regulatory bodies like the Joint FAO/WHO Expert 
Committee and EFSA have set intake limits to protect consumers. High cadmium 
levels were found in foods with algae as a base, cocoa products, and seafood. Other 
significant dietary sources of cadmium include grains, vegetables, and starchy roots. 
Cadmium is commonly found in soils worldwide (U.S. Environmental Protection 
Agency [EPA] 2015).

176 ♦ Ildi Revi
For most consumers, cadmium levels in coffee are negligible and pose no health 
concern. The purpose of providing this information is to enable informed decision-
making for those who wish to understand the complete nutritional and safety profile 
of their coffee.
Coffee producers can reduce cadmium and other metal levels through organic 
methods, including applying spent coffee grounds directly to the soil. This allows 
coffee’s metal-chelating properties to absorb the cadmium before the plant can (Sari 
and Taufik 2019).
Arsenic is a heavy metal that should be tested for in the production of health-focused 
coffee, since it is associated with an increased risk of cancer, skin conditions, and 
cardiovascular disease. Historically known as the “king of poisons” because of its high 
potency and ability to be administered discreetly, arsenic was used to murder members 
of the ruling class in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance, and Napoleon Bonaparte 
was said to have died from being poisoned by it. At the same time, it has also been 
used as a medicine throughout history, an example of “the dose makes the poison.” 
Arsenic naturally occurs in soil and water, both of which are essential to coffee 
cultivation. Coffee plants may absorb arsenic from contaminated soil or irrigation 
water, leading to its presence in coffee beans. This is particularly concerning in areas 
with high levels of naturally occurring arsenic in groundwater. The FDA classifies 
arsenic into two categories: inorganic arsenic, which is found in industrial products 
and is more toxic, and organic arsenic, which is found in nature, particularly in 
groundwater. During testing, coffee beans that initially have no arsenic may become 
contaminated with it if they are soaked in water that contains arsenic, highlighting 
the importance of water quality throughout the entire production chain.
For accurate assessment of metals, it’s better to look at lab tests of roasted coffee 
instead of brewed coffee because brewing methods can differ greatly and introduce 
variables that affect results. The grind size affects extraction rates, water temperature, 
and contact time, and some amount of metals remain in the discarded grounds rather 
than transferring to the final beverage.
There is limited research on heavy metal transfer from coffee grounds to brewed 
coffee. However, one study by Winiarska-Mieczan in 2021 examined how cadmium 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 177
and lead transfer from coffee grounds to brewed coffee by analyzing 23 types of 
coffee. The researchers found that approximately 95.5% of the cadmium present in 
the ground coffee transferred into the brewed coffee infusion, with extraction rates 
ranging from 79% to 100%. Similarly, about 94% of the lead was transferred to the 
brew. Despite this high extraction efficiency, the researchers found very low absolute 
levels of cadmium and lead in the final coffee, suggesting that drinking coffee poses 
little risk regarding these metals. Winiarska-Mieczan stated, “The content of Cd 
and Pb in the analysed coffee infusions was very low, so drinking coffee does not 
pose a risk for consumers regarding the content of these metals. However, it must be 
remembered that no threshold limits for toxic metal consumption exist because these 
metals accumulate in the body for a long time.”
Natural methods can help reduce cadmium levels in the body through dietary 
adjustments and nutrient intake. Increasing calcium and iron intake through foods like 
dairy products, leafy greens, and lean meats can reduce cadmium absorption because 
calcium and cadmium compete for absorption in the intestines, and iron deficiency 
increases cadmium absorption (Kippler 2020). Eating foods rich in antioxidants, 
such as citrus fruits, bell peppers, almonds, and sunflower seeds, which are high in 
vitamins C and E, can also reduce oxidative stress caused by cadmium (Manca 2021). 
Additionally, selenium has been shown to reduce mercury in the body (Bjørklund 
2017). Dietary fiber from whole grains, legumes, and vegetables can bind to cadmium 
in the digestive tract, preventing its absorption (Kippler 2021). Regular exercise that 
promotes sweating can also help eliminate cadmium from the body, supporting overall 
detoxification pathways (Cowan 2022).
These examples illustrate that our bodies have natural ways to mitigate toxins. By 
adopting a balanced approach that includes lab testing results and awareness of our 
genetics, reactions, and health goals, we can make informed decisions about coffee 
consumption while reducing anxiety about heavy metal exposure.

178 ♦ Ildi Revi

METALS IN A CUP OF COFFEE

cadmium.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 179
The FDA has adopted a toxicological reference value (TRV) range for cadmium of 
0.21-0.36 micrograms (µg) per kilogram of body weight per day. To calculate the TRV 
for a person weighing 70 kg, you would multiply the person’s weight by the TRV range.
1. Lower Limit: 0.21 µg/kg/day × 70 kg = 14.7 µg/day
2. Upper Limit: 0.36 µg/kg/day × 70 kg = 25.2 µg/day
Therefore, for a person weighing 70 kg, the TRV for cadmium ranges from 14.7 to 
25.2 micrograms per day.
To determine how much cadmium might be in a cup of coffee made from 15 grams 
of ground coffee with a concentration of 6.75 parts per billion (ppb), we can use the 
following calculation:
Step-by-Step Calculation:
1. Convert ppb to micrograms per gram:
•
1 ppb = 1 microgram per kilogram (µg/kg)
•
6.75 ppb = 6.75 µg/kg of coffee
•
Per gram: 6.75 ÷ 1000 = 0.00675 µg/g
2. Calculate total cadmium in 15 grams:
•
0.00675 µg/g × 15 g = 0.10125 µg
3. Account for extraction efficiency:
•
Using Winiarska-Mieczan’s approximately 95.5% cadmium extraction
maximum
•
Actual cadmium in cup: 0.10125 × 0.955 = ~0.097 µg
Safety Assessment
Daily Consumption Analysis:
•
1 cup: ~0.097 µg (0.4-0.7% of daily limit)
•
4 cups: ~0.39 µg (1.5-2.6% of daily limit)
Even consuming 4 cups of this coffee daily results in less than 3% of the safe daily 
intake limit.

We’ve previously discussed how certain compounds in coffee, such as chlorogenic acids 
and melanoidins, may help reduce the body’s burden of cadmium through antioxidant 
and metal-chelating actions. For most people, cadmium levels in coffee are negligible 
and not a cause for concern. Still, the purpose of this category of coffee is to empower 
consumers and professionals with credible information about compound levels when 
they are relevant.
Most lab testing is conducted on whole bean or ground coffee, not the brewed cup. 
The Australian Food Composition Database includes some brewed examples. While 
no cadmium was detected in those samples, other metals were. It’s unclear whether 
this reflects a lack of cadmium in the beans or a failure of extraction during brewing. 
In truth, the brewing method (grind size, water temperature, water quality, contact 
time, filter type, and pressure) can all influence chemical extraction. Without mass 
spectrometers in our kitchens, it’s impossible to know precisely what we’re consuming 
or how bioavailable each compound may be.
Laboratory testing should be scientifically valid, practical, and targeted. It doesn’t 
require testing every single roast batch, but rather includes strategic points for quality 
control—such as during sourcing, product development, roasting curve design, or 
shelf-life analysis. What matters most is that protocols are clear, consistent, validated, 
and fit the company’s goals for both quality and consumer safety.
Regulatory frameworks exist to ensure that these heavy metals in coffee and other 
foods are kept as low as reasonably achievable to protect consumer health. It is 
hard to find one place where all the information is consolidated. I actually have not 
been able to find one anywhere. Here is a simple chart of the limits from three 
standard-setting agencies:

182 ♦ Ildi Revi
TRV (Toxicological Reference Value): The TRV, such as the FDA’s cadmium range, 
can be ingested daily over a lifetime without appreciable health risks. It is a guideline 
for assessing the risk of exposure to cadmium from all sources, including food, water, 
and air.
Limits or Levels (e.g., 3 ppm): Limits like 3 ppm (parts per million) refer to the 
maximum allowable concentration in specific food products or additives. These limits 
ensure that the compound content in individual food items does not exceed safe levels, 
helping to keep the overall intake within the TRV.
How They Relate: Risk Assessment: The TRV is used in risk assessments to evaluate 
whether the compound levels in food products, when consumed as part of a typical 
diet, are likely to exceed safe intake levels. Regulatory Standards: Limits are regulatory 
standards that help control the amount of compounds in specific foods to ensure that 
the total dietary exposure remains within the TRV. The TRV provides a benchmark 
for safe exposure, while the ppm limits are practical measures to control cadmium 
levels in individual food items to protect public health.
High lead levels in quality coffee show why strict quality control and health standards 
are vital. This and other issues of metals highlight the need for testing and holistic 
coffee as a new category. Transparency is essential, empowering consumers who may 
be vulnerable to health risks related to heavy metals so that people can take control 
of decisions for their health. 
Now that we have surveyed a few compounds that influence coffee and health, 
let’s look at coffee farming practices. Circular agriculture offers a powerful way to 
reduce environmental impact and enhance coffee quality by using organic farming, 
composting, and repurposing spent coffee grounds, allowing producers to lead the 
way in fostering a healthier coffee industry. This approach helps the environment and 
protects our coffee from harmful contaminants, supporting our holistic standards.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 183
Organic regenerative coffee in Colombia.

5
Circular Agriculture: 
Organic Regenerative Cool Farms
HIGHLIGHTS
According to sources cited in this chapter, we may only have 50 harvests left before our soil 
is irreparably degraded. Factors contributing to this crisis include soil erosion, loss of organic 
matter, and heavy reliance on chemical fertilizers and pesticides, which deplete soil health. Experts 
warn that urgently adopting sustainable, regenerative farming practices is essential to restore soil 
health and ensure long-term agricultural productivity, thereby preventing devastating impacts 
on food security and ecosystems. Organic and regenerative farming for holistic coffee may be 
the answer to preserving Coffea cultivars for future generations. 
The chapter starts with a discussion of the background of coffee and details of Arabica and 
Robusta. Despite there being over 131 recognized species of coffee (Lachenmeier 2023) the 
industry is primarily dominated by two species: Coffea arabica (Arabica) and Coffea canephora 
(Robusta). Within these species are numerous varieties and cultivars, each contributing unique 
health-related factors to consider in addition to taste. 
Although coffee, in general, is considered a healthy beverage, innumerable factors impact the 
quality of coffee. For those who produce, manufacture or drink coffee for health benefits, this 
chapter shows how coffee is grown, giving a general picture of aspects we can focus on in the future 
to optimize health factors in the plant naturally and in harmony with the environment. As the 
chapter will discuss, these factors influence the healthiness of the coffee:
Nursery and Land Preparation: Integrating compost, mulch, and green manure 
to enhance soil fertility and moisture conservation; controlling erosion and planting 
strategic shade trees. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 185
Plant and Soil Maintenance: Crop rotation, organic fertilization, and biological pest 
control to maintain soil structure and prevent infestations.
Harvesting and Processing: Manual picking ensures optimal ripeness but is labor-
intensive; mechanical harvesting is efficient but less selective. Washed, natural, and honey 
processes are detailed, with information on fermentation and health. This is an area where 
strict hygiene standards are crucial in preventing molds and mycotoxins.nd mycotoxins.
Putting it all together for CHC: Sustainable, organic, regenerative circular agriculture, various 
methods produce nutrient-rich, chemical-free coffee, improve soil health, sequester carbon, and 
conserve water. Certifications like USDA, Utz, and Rainforest Alliance verify organic status 
throughout the supply chain, though some farmers face barriers to certification. Organic coffee 
cultivation can gain more traction with increasing consumer demand and advances in farming 
research supporting high-quality production. This will also ensure progressive pricing models 
for producer stability, growth and development while fostering community and trust among all 
stakeholders in the supply chain. 
The previous chapters examined coffee’s health-supporting and harmful compounds. 
Now we’ll explore how these compounds enter the coffee in the first place, starting 
with production.
At Purity Coffee, once our lab results consistently showed high levels of beneficial 
compounds and low contaminants, we became curious: what was driving these 
differences? Lab testing revealed only part of the answer. We needed to trace back to 
the source. What we found was that coffee’s health profile begins long before roasting, 
in the soil and practices of the farm itself.
I thought I had a pretty good understanding of farming, but spending time with 
Purity’s producing partners changed everything. I gained a much deeper appreciation 
for what it actually takes to grow coffee with health as the priority. I hope to do justice 
to what they taught me and communicate it clearly in this chapter.

In late 2024, the U.S. Food and 
Drug Administration updated 
its definition of “healthy” foods, 
formally recognizing coffee as a 
nutrient-dense beverage. But not 
all coffees offer the same benefits. 
Like any crop, coffee varies in 
nutrient density and contaminant 
risk depending on how it is 
produced.
Coffee production spans a 
wide spectrum, from chemical-
intensive, high-yield systems to 
ecologically rich, health-focused 
models. These differences shape 
not just the nutritional quality of 
your cup, but also the well-being 
of farmers and the long-term 
health of the land. Lower-quality 
coffees may still offer some health 
benefits, but they carry a greater 
risk of contamination and harmful 
compounds. Higher-quality 
coffees require greater knowledge, 
effort, and investment, and they 
should be valued accordingly.
This chapter compares coffee 
systems with low and high health 
and environmental standards, not 
to pass judgment, but to reveal 
how growing practices lead to 
real and measurable outcomes. By 
Circular Health Goes WAY Back
“We are the responsible actors in the circle of life, 
guardians of our planet and defenders of its health,” 
Dr. Ilaria Capua wrote in her book Circular Health, 
published in 2020. She asked, “Can our species 
(since it looks like we’re in charge) implement a 
new type of responsible innovation in health that 
is respectful of all stakeholders and sustainable at 
the same time?” 
The cornerstone of circular health coffee (CHC) 
involves organic and regenerative farming 
practices.  Coffee grown consciously for the 
environment is a critical aspect of CHC.
The Circular Health movement highlights the 
connection between personal health and 
environmental well-being. This holistic idea dates 
back to ancient Greek medical literature, which 
suggested that our environment is integral to us. 
The food and water we consume are essential for 
building our body’s cells.
Regenerative farming practices lead to the 
return of wildlife, as healthier ecosystems 
and restored habitats provide a favorable 
environment for diverse species.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 187
examining both ends of the spectrum, we can better understand what defines truly 
healthy coffee and how intentional practices support both personal and planetary 
health.
We’ll also take a brief look at coffee’s cultivation history before diving into the biology 
of the coffee plant and modern growing methods. We’ll continue to explore these 
topics in this and the following chapters.

5.1 We do not want the  
Story to End.
“While the true origin of coffee drinking may be forever hidden among the 
mysteries of the purple East, shrouded as it is in legend and fable, scholars 
have marshaled sufficient facts to prove that the beverage was known in 
Ethiopia “from time immemorial.” 
 
-William Harrison Ukers 
All About Coffee, 1922
Before we dive into the story of dancing goats, there’s another, lesser-known tale 
found in William Ukers’ seminal 1922 book on coffee. It is about coffee as medicine, 
featuring Sheikh Hadji Omar, a dervish (a Sufi ascetic) once exiled from Mocha, 
Yemen, in the 13th century for doing something naughty, which had to do with the 
King of Mocha’s daughter, allegedly. Struggling to survive in the wilderness, Omar 
discovered coffee trees and chewed their buds and fruits. Because it was bitter, to 
improve the taste, he tried roasting it and eventually boiling it as a broth, which 
provided him with miraculous mental and physical relief from pain. He shared 
this remedy, and soon, the sick and weak sought him out, rejuvenated by his coffee 
concoctions. 
Omar’s growing reputation led to his pardon and return to Mocha, where he continued 
to treat people with coffee. In gratitude, the people built him a monastery, allowing 
him to live out his days there while his disciples continued his legacy of using coffee 
as a medicinal remedy (Wellman 1961). This story highlights coffee’s early recognized 
impact on human culture and health.
As an initiation into the world of coffee, newcomers to the industry are often told the 
tale of Kaldi, an Ethiopian goat herder who supposedly discovered coffee’s stimulating 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 189
effects (Ukers 1922). According to the story, Kaldi noticed that his goats became 
unusually energetic after eating red berries from a particular tree (coffee). Kaldi’s 
Dancing Goats legend symbolically marks the beginning of coffee’s journey into 
human culture worldwide, even if the specific details are not historically verified.
Historians believe coffee traveled from Ethiopia to Yemen as early as approximately 
575 AD, becoming well-established as a drink by the 14th century. Roasted beans were 
first brewed around 1000 AD. By the 13th century, Muslims drank coffee religiously, 
using the bean broth to invigorate Sufi mystics during long nights of devotion. Coffee’s 
spread closely followed trade routes, often coinciding with the expansion of Islamic 
influence. From Yemen, coffee journeyed to North Africa, the Eastern Mediterranean, 
and India, echoing the cultural and commercial exchanges of the time.
The Dutch played a pivotal role in spreading coffee globally. In the late 1600s, they 
transported coffee seeds from Yemen to Java. Then, in 1706, they took a plant from 
Java to the Amsterdam Botanic Gardens, where it flourished and multiplied. Seedlings 
from this plant became the foundation for coffee’s introduction to the Americas. In 
the early 18th century, Dutch colonies and other European powers began cultivating 
coffee throughout the Western Hemisphere, marking the beginning of large-scale 
coffee production in North and South America.
Coffee’s adaptability has been astonishing. It flourishes from the volcanic slopes of 
Guatemala to the plains of Brazil and the forests of South Sudan and Ethiopia. Each 
region’s microclimate shapes the coffee fruit’s composition, influencing plant growth 
and cup flavor. Climate conditions, altitude, soil, seed, and cultivation practices 
contribute to coffee’s unique regional characteristics. 
Despite its status as a globally influential commodity with a rich history and robust 
supply chain, coffee faces formidable challenges, with climate change posing the 
most significant threat. As mentioned earlier, global coffee yields are expected to 
decline significantly, and land suitable for coffee cultivation is projected to shrink 
dramatically. Like other agricultural systems, coffee is vulnerable to climate-induced 
risks affecting soil, water, crop, and nutrient management. These include drought, 
salinity, biodiversity loss, reduced suitability, changes in seed availability, and resistance 
to both abiotic and biotic stressors. These global challenges, but their impacts may 

190 ♦ Ildi Revi
vary by region, emphasizing the need for localized adaptation strategies in coffee 
production.
Again, just as with talking about contaminants, I do not want to cause anxiety or 
fear. Despite environmental challenges, many sustainable coffee systems incorporate 
essential ecological practices. These practices help maintain soil fertility, preserve 
biodiversity, sequester carbon, and manage pests and diseases, but they still face 
significant issues like soil health deterioration, biodiversity loss, and pollution, 
which threaten the overall health of ecosystems. The journey of coffee, from plant to 
processing, is a testament to resilience and adaptation. Yet, in the face of climate change 
and mounting environmental pressures, proactive measures are crucial to safeguard 
coffee’s future and preserve its enduring legacy (Bilen 2023).
I encourage readers interested in learning more about specific coffee climate-related 
challenges to visit the World Coffee Research (WCR) website. Their purpose is 
to “unite the global coffee industry to drive science-based agricultural solutions 
to urgently secure a diverse and sustainable supply of quality coffee today and for 
generations to come” (WCR accessed December 2024). I believe that CHC could be 
one of the solutions.
The study Climate Change and Land (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change 
[IPCC], 2019, available free online) is a report authored by dozens of scientists. 
It focuses on the interconnections between climate change, desertification, land 
degradation, sustainable land management, food security, and greenhouse gas 
fluctuations in land ecosystems. 
The report set off a slew of follow-up studies in the coffee industry because it 
highlighted several key points relevant to coffee production, especially: 
•
Climate change is expected to significantly reduce coffee-suitable land by
2050, necessitating urgent agronomic adjustments.
•
Land clearing for coffee plantations leads to deforestation, exacerbating soil
degradation and erosion. This process diminishes soil fertility and increases
vulnerability to pests and diseases.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 191
•
Sustainable land management practices are essential to mitigate these
impacts, as they help maintain soil health, preserve biodiversity, and enhance
carbon sequestration.
The report states the importance of incorporating sustainable practices to ensure the 
long-term viability of coffee farming.
As participants in the ongoing journey of coffee throughout history, especially for 
health, allowing coffee to become extinct due to climate change would be a great loss 
for future generations. No matter how many coffee substitutes pop up, they are not 
coffee. All the compounds and health outcomes we have discussed will not apply. The 
story of coffee will no longer be told. 
Let’s start where the story of coffee often begins… with the basics. From there, we 
can build an understanding of the efforts and skills needed to develop coffee with a 
focus on health.

192
5.2 The Genus Coffea and 
its Main species
Coffee belongs to the plant genus Coffea. While experts estimate there are over 130 
different species of coffee plants, only two are widely commercially available currently 
– Coffea arabica (C. arabica, commonly referred to as “Arabica”) and Coffea canephora
(C. canephora, widely referred to as “Robusta”). In Brazil a distinct variety of C.
canephora is Conilon. While both Conilon and Robusta fall under the C. canephora
species, Conilon is typically traded regionally within Brazil and Latin America under
its local name as “Conilon” but is traded internationally as Robusta on the London
International Financial Futures and Options Exchange (LIFFE). C. Arabica coffee is
primarily traded on the ICE Futures U.S. Coffee “C” contract, the global benchmark
for Arabica coffee pricing.
Coffee is grown in approximately 70 countries, with Brazil, Vietnam, Colombia, 
Indonesia, and Honduras leading global production, accounting for about 75 percent 
of global production. Arabica coffee makes up nearly 60 percent of the world’s coffee 
(ITC 2021). Brazil is its top producer, followed by Colombia, Ethiopia, and Honduras. 
On the other hand, Vietnam, Brazil, Indonesia, India, Ivory Coast and Uganda are 
the main producers of Robusta coffee, which accounts for about 40 percent of the 
world’s coffee (ITC 2021). More recently, unofficial statistics suggest that Arabica 
now represents closer to 53% of production, with Robusta nearing 47% (Statista.
com). However, as of this writing, these figures have not yet been confirmed by official 
sources such as the ITC, International Coffee Organization (ICO), or the FAO.
Arabica coffee, believed to be the first cultivated coffee species, is a descendant of C. 
canephora and C. eugenioides. It thrives in regions close to the equator, between the 
tropics of Capricorn and Cancer, generally above 3,000 feet, but can also be grown 
commercially from 1,500 to 6,000 feet above sea level. Arabica plants require sufficient 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 193
moisture, nutrient-rich soil, and a balance of sun and shade to flourish. Shade-grown 
Arabica is particularly valued as it can produce more complex flavors due to the longer 
development time for the beans.
Arabica coffee generally fetches higher prices than Robusta. Panama, Costa Rica, 
Kenya, Ethiopia, and Guatemala command high unit value prices, especially in the 
premium coffee segments. Coffee exports significantly contribute to the gross domestic 
product of many countries, providing foreign exchange earnings and tax income. Some 
countries that traditionally produce Arabica, like Mexico, Nicaragua, and Guatemala, 
also cultivate Robusta.
Some arabica varieties contain canephora genes, enhancing their resilience. C. 
canephora hybrids and cultivars present challenges due to open fertilization, making 
genetic tracing difficult. 
Robusta, broadly recognized as SG2, originates from Central Africa, while conilon, 
known as SG1, comes from the Kouilou River region in the Democratic Republic of 
Congo. Conilon plants are generally shorter, feature a larger canopy, mature earlier, 
and exhibit greater drought resistance than typical robusta plants. Despite these 
differences, they share many traits and are often farmed together for staggered harvests. 
In this book, the term “robusta” will be used to include “conilon,” acknowledging 
that while Brazil differentiates between the two, the rest of the world generally does 
not. This choice is meant to streamline the discussion, though I offer my apologies to 
devoted conilon advocates and enthusiasts for the simplification.
Robusta plants are typically grown at lower elevations and are naturally more resistant 
to pests and diseases than Arabica. In recent years, high-quality robusta has begun to 
gain recognition, thanks to improved farming and processing techniques that have 
elevated its cup potential. Carefully cultivated and processed robusta can now offer 
distinct flavor attributes, including lower bitterness and notes of fruit, spice, and cocoa.
Programs like the Coffee Quality Institute’s Q Robusta certification and initiatives 
led by Brazil’s Associação Brasileira de Cafés Especiais (BSCA) have helped raise 
awareness of robusta’s quality potential. Coffees from Rondônia, Brazil, in particular, 
have drawn praise for their clean profiles and reduced astringency. Research efforts by 

194 ♦ Ildi Revi
World Coffee Research and the International Coffee Organization (ICO) continue 
to challenge long-held assumptions about robusta’s limitations.
As climate change threatens Arabica’s viability in many growing regions, robusta’s 
resilience is gaining strategic importance. Beyond the beverage sector, robusta is also 
being explored for use in functional foods, cosmetics, and energy products, opening 
new pathways for value creation across the supply chain.
Sugars in coffee play a fundamental role in the final flavor profile during roasting. 
Higher sugar content typically results in more flavorful coffee, with caramelization 
producing notes resembling maple or honey, depending on other compounds. Lipids 
also significantly influence the sensory experience, contributing to a richer mouthfeel 
and a longer-lasting aftertaste. During roasting, lipids undergo reactions that create 
volatile compounds like aldehydes and ketones, which enhance the aroma and taste 
of the coffee.
Arabica and Robusta differ not only in taste but also in their distinct chemical 
compositions. Arabica is generally sweeter and more aromatic, while Robusta is more 
bitter and contains higher caffeine levels. High-quality Arabica is often preferred for 
its rich flavors, though some high-quality Robustas can be quite enjoyable. Given the 
health benefits of both types, people naturally gravitate toward the one that best suits 
their taste preferences. A coffee that goes undrunk will not benefit anyone’s health.
My biggest challenge with Robusta regarding health has been sourcing organic, 
regeneratively farmed Robusta at the pureness levels and in the quantities my company 
needs for production. I have a theory (I hope to test soon) that, along with the taste 
and quality improvements, have come health and food safety improvements, too. I 
hope this is an area where a community of CHC professionals can collaborate.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 195

OTHER SPECIES
Emerging coffee species such as Coffea liberica, Coffea stenophylla, Coffea eugenioides 
and Coffea racemosa are gaining attention for their potential in commercial cultivation. 
These species offer unique flavor profiles and increased resilience to harsher climates 
and diseases compared to Arabica and Robusta. Racemosa also stands out for its low 
caffeine content; naturally caffeine-free coffee and its health aspects are promising 
research areas.
According to a study presented at the International Coffee Convention 2023, Coffea 
liberica includes two recognized botanical varieties: C. liberica var. liberica and C. 
liberica var. dewevrei, the latter commonly known as Excelsa (Farah 2019; Ablan 
Lagman 2023). Both Liberica and Excelsa are resilient species capable of thriving in 
challenging growing conditions. Liberica is often noted for its distinctive fruity and 
smoky flavor profile. C. stenophylla is known for its unique flavors and heat tolerance, 
while C. eugenioides offers low bitterness and could enhance breeding programs. 
C. Liberica (above) with huge leaves, C. Arabica (bottom right),
and the Robusta-Arabica hybrid Catimor (top right).

Washed or natural processing, Semi-washed, pulped natural or honey 
process are terms for similar processing that is not washed or natural. These 
processes expose different amounts of pulp to enzymatic browning, yellow,

for fermentation, which, although intended to impact flavor primarily,
