Shipping and warehousing can impact mold development, off-flavors if the 
coffee gets wet, or loss of organic matter if exposed to heat from sitting in 
a sealed metal container in the tropical sun on a dock waiting to be loaded 
onto a ship.
COFFEE BOTANY
Depending on the cultivar, coffee plants produce fruits of varying colors, though red 
is the most common. Many people compare them at first glance to cherries (which is, 
of course, why they are commonly called “coffee cherries”) or gooseberries (sometimes 
they are called “coffee berries”). Botanically speaking, coffee fruits are classified as 
drupes, with an outer skin, a pulpy and juicy middle layer, and a hard and woody 
inner shell that typically contains a single seed (Ferreira 2019).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 199
Coffeea arabica var. Catimor 129 growing in Zimbabwe
Coffee blossoms

200 ♦ Ildi Revi
Unripe coffee fruit
Red coffee “cherries”
Purple coffee
Yellow coffee “berries”
(Photo credit Purity Coffee)
Orange or Pink coffee

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦201
One, two (most common), or sometimes three seeds develop inside the fruit: 
The seeds have a very thin membrane surrounding them called silverskin. There is an 
additional protective layer, called parchment, which must be removed before roasting. 
Coating the parchment is a layer of pectin, referred to as “mucilage,” the slippery (but 
turns sticky as it dries), sugary layer surrounding the coffee bean, located between 
the outer skin and the parchment layer of the coffee fruit. The mucilage is covered in 
pulp within the coffee skin.

202 ♦ Ildi Revi

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 203
Regarding food safety and U.S. import laws, green coffee is a dry, raw agricultural 
product normally roasted before customers can consume it. Health labs extract 
compounds from green coffee as an ingredient in drinks, supplements and beauty 
products, but I do not know the processes for that.
Coffee can also be processed differently, affecting its roasting, taste, and health 
compounds. Before explaining processing in more detail, let us examine the journey 
of the coffee seed from nursery to harvest through the lens of holistic coffee.

204
5.3 Organic and Regenerative 
production: Nursery to Harvest
CHC depends on organic farming and regenerative agriculture, not just to reduce 
harm, but to actively restore ecosystems. Protecting biodiversity, enriching soil, and 
preserving water quality aren’t optional, but are foundational to holistic coffee.
While organic, regenerative, and permaculture farming all aim for sustainability, 
each has a distinct approach. Organic agriculture avoids synthetic chemicals and 
GMOs, relying instead on natural inputs and practices like composting and crop 
diversification. It follows strict certification standards and often overlaps with both 
regenerative and permaculture methods. Regenerative agriculture focuses on healing 
the land, rebuilding soil health, increasing biodiversity, and drawing down carbon 
through practices like cover cropping, composting, and rotational grazing. 
Though permaculture shares some of these tools, it operates on a systems-thinking 
model, emphasizing closed-loop cycles, waste reduction, and ecological harmony. 
Rooted in indigenous knowledge and coined as a term in the 1970s, permaculture is 
often practiced through techniques like no-till farming, crop rotation, and agroforestry, 
helping farmers adapt to climate shifts while conserving resources.
Together, these methods form the foundation of holistic coffee farming. They support 
a more sustainable, ethical coffee supply chain by promoting ecological resilience, 
reducing reliance on chemicals, and strengthening local communities. As consumer 
demand for environmentally conscious coffee rises, and as research continues to affirm 
the benefits of organic methods, the future of CHC looks not only viable but essential.
Not all organic coffee is healthy, and not all of it is high quality. Some organic lots 
suffer from poor management, resulting in defects caused by insect damage, inadequate 
processing, or other conditions that can lead to the formation of undesirable or 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 205
unhealthy compounds. In some cases, coffees are “organic by default,” grown without 
synthetic inputs, not by design, but due to lack of access to agrochemicals. This does 
not necessarily reflect intentional organic practices or ecosystem stewardship.
Farmers may face limitations in tools, labor, compost materials, nutrient solutions, 
or natural pest deterrents, all of which are critical for cultivating truly healthy plants. 
These coffees may still qualify for organic certification, but that alone doesn’t ensure 
they meet higher standards for health, integrity or flavor. What ultimately matters most 
is the producer’s knowledge, care, and commitment to growing resilient plants in living 
systems. Organic farming sets important baseline standards by restricting synthetic 
chemicals, but true sustainability requires going further, adopting regenerative 
practices that actively restore soil health, support biodiversity, and strengthen ecological 
resilience.
Intentional organic farming (as opposed to organic by default) is defined by its 
commitment to enhancing soil health, biodiversity, and ecological balance. This 
approach ensures that coffee plants receive a necessary supply of nutrients, leading to 
the development of essential compounds like polyphenols and antioxidants.
This section will guide you through the coffee nursery to harvest, highlighting the 
intentional organic practices and the significant efforts made by producers. Despite 
the challenges and costs, such as land, labor, fuel, infrastructure investments, and 
time, these practices support high-quality, health-beneficial coffee while protecting 
the environment.

206 ♦ Ildi Revi
CULTIVAR SELECTION AND THE NURSERY
A coffee nursery.
In coffee production, selecting the right cultivar and implementing effective nursery 
practices are critical for achieving high-quality coffee with the potential for health and 
sustainable outcomes. We have already explored Arabica and Robusta, and mentioned 
emerging species like C. liberica, C. eugenioides, and others gaining traction due to 
their enhanced resilience to climate stressors and diseases (Davis 2019).
Understanding the distinctions between coffee species like Arabica and Robusta 
informs our health choices, and each species encompasses a variety of cultivars. These 
cultivars, or cultivated varieties, exhibit unique characteristics influenced by their 
genetic makeup and growing conditions.
A word about the terms: variety, cultivar, and varietal. A variety refers to a naturally 
occurring subspecies of a plant that has distinct characteristics but occurs through a 
mutation in nature. In contrast, a cultivar is a cultivated variety, a plant produced by 
selective breeding. Since almost all coffee is now cultivated, the term “cultivar” is more 
accurate. Some people use the term “varietal,” but since that is based on “variety” and 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 207
is used by the wine industry, it is 
less precise in this context.
Arabica cultivars offer a range of 
flavor profiles and have different 
propensities to develop nutrient 
compositions, but this is difficult 
to determine due to the many 
variables involved in cultivation. 
The primary cultivars of Arabica 
coffee are Bourbon and Typica, 
with dozens of others like 
Caturra and Catuai common in 
many producing countries. New 
cultivars are also being developed 
and tested at coffee research 
stations around the world. I focus 
on Arabica cultivars in this book 
because they’re the ones I know 
best, but I appreciate excellent 
Robustas.
World Coffee Research has put together a Cultivar Catalog, accessible online, 
that consolidates information on over 100 Arabica and Robusta varieties globally. 
This catalog has extensive agronomic data, including expected yield, nutritional 
requirements, optimal altitude, and pest/disease resistance. Coffee farmers can make 
strategic decisions to match cultivars to local conditions and enhance disease resistance, 
yield, and quality, making it a vital tool amid climate-related threats. The catalog 
addresses the urgent need for replanting with resilient and improved varieties, and 
as a result, promoting resilience, sustainability, and productivity in coffee farming.
While the chart on the next page provides some tasting notes, these flavors reflect 
common perceptions, whether accurate or not. Variations in taste are influenced by 
the geographical, agricultural, and processing factors listed above. Variations in color 
are also influenced by processing, as the chart on the next page shows.
Healthy roots of a seedling cultivated 
in soil with activated biochar.

208 ♦ Ildi Revi

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 209
Effective nursery practices produce healthy seedlings, which grow into healthy, hardy 
coffee plants. Using seeds from disease-free, high-yield parent plants and treating them 
with organic fungicides or beneficial microorganism-infused biochar can prevent the 
spread of diseases and ensure robust seedlings. In cooler climates, pre-germination 
techniques can also speed up seedling development. Using soil with the right pH 
(4.5-5.5), which is also rich in organic matter content, is key. Adding compost and 
lime enhances soil fertility and structure, supporting healthy root development and 
preventing issues like nematode infestations. Consistent and appropriate watering 
schedules and nitrogen-rich organic amendments support vigorous seedling growth. 
Overwatering, however, should be avoided to prevent root diseases, as coffee seedlings 
are especially vulnerable to root rot and damping off. Containers with good drainage, 
such as pots or sleeves, prevent waterlogging and root diseases. While smaller containers 
may require earlier transplanting, they also make seedlings easier to transport and 
handle. Care must be taken during handling to avoid damaging the roots.
Selecting disease-resistant cultivars reduces the need for chemical pesticides and 
fungicides, lowering health risks for farmworkers and consumers while minimizing 
environmental contamination. Organic soil amendments, like biochar, integrated 
pest management, and efficient water use, promote environmental sustainability 
and enhance soil health. These practices strengthen the overall resilience of coffee 
production systems. By choosing the right cultivars and adopting the best nursery 
practices, coffee producers can improve the quality and sustainability of their coffee 
while aligning with broader health and environmental goals. 
LAND PREPARATION
Proper land preparation is one of the most important steps in organic regenerative 
coffee farming. It sets the stage for everything that follows, from soil health to plant 
resilience and yield consistency. Regenerative agriculture goes beyond simply avoiding 
synthetic inputs; it involves actively building healthy, living soil and protecting the 
surrounding ecosystem.
Minimizing soil disturbance is a key principle of regenerative farming. Conventional 
tilling disrupts soil structure, releases stored carbon, and damages microbial 

210 ♦ Ildi Revi
communities. No-till or low-till methods are preferred in regenerative systems 
because they preserve organic matter, improve water retention, and allow beneficial 
microorganisms to thrive (Leng 2024). Compost, green manure, and high-quality 
organic matter are worked into the surface or applied as mulch rather than plowed 
deep. This feeds the soil while preserving its delicate ecosystem.
Erosion control
Erosion control is essential, especially in sloped terrain common to coffee-growing 
regions. Contour planting, where rows follow the natural curves of the land, slows 
water runoff and helps retain topsoil. It’s often combined with living ground covers 
or grass strips to stabilize the soil between rows. These techniques prevent degradation 
and gradually build soil carbon over time (Gomes 2025).
While organic amendments like compost and manure improve fertility, regenerative 
farmers also use materials like biochar to enhance structure and long-term nutrient 
retention. Biochar is a stable form of carbon produced by pyrolyzing organic waste. 
Incorporating it into the soil increases cation exchange capacity (a measure of the 
soil’s ability to hold and exchange essential nutrients), improves microbial habitat, 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 211
and reduces nutrient leaching (Jayasinghe 2023). Later in this chapter, we’ll explore 
in more detail how and when to apply activated biochar.
Cover crops are plants grown not to harvest, but to heal and prepare the soil. Farmers 
plant them between crop cycles or during the off-season to protect and improve the 
land. Often fast-growing legumes like clover or beans, cover crops help in several 
ways: they hold moisture in the soil, prevent erosion, and add organic matter. Many 
also “fix” nitrogen, pulling it from the air and storing it in the soil, where future 
crops can use it. Some farmers also allow naturally soft, non-invasive weeds to act 
as cover crops. Once grown, these plants are cut or gently worked back into the 
earth, becoming “green manure” that feeds the soil and boosts its fertility. This helps 
jumpstart microbial activity and improve structure, especially in soils that have been 
overworked or depleted (Khangura 2023).
A well-prepared regenerative plot supports more than coffee plants. It supports an 
entire system. It holds water, resists erosion, feeds the microbiome, and builds organic 
matter year after year. These early decisions, made before a single coffee tree is planted, 
are what make long-term health and productivity possible.
SHADE-GROWN COFFEE: ENVIRONMENTAL 
AND HEALTH BENEFITS
Choosing which shade trees to plant is a strategic decision considering factors such 
as growth rate (the tree’s capacity to fix atmospheric nitrogen into a usable form) and 
compatibility with coffee plants, and some trees even offer additional nutrients. These 
trees can also be economically valuable to the farm with proper management. Many 
farmers choose hardwoods as shade trees, which, when mature, can be sold as valuable 
lumber when harvested.
One of the most comprehensive environmental standards in coffee today that 
incorporates shade-grown principles is the Smithsonian’s Bird Friendly certification. 
Developed by the Smithsonian Migratory Bird Center, it sets strict requirements for 
canopy cover, native tree height, and on-farm biodiversity. Farms must also meet 
USDA organic standards, making Bird Friendly coffee a model for systems that protect 
ecosystems while avoiding synthetic inputs. 

212 ♦ Ildi Revi
Shade grown coffee at Hacienda La Pradera, Aratoca, 
Colombia, an organic, regenerative, SBFC farm.
In 1996, Perfecto et al. underscored the ecological importance of shade coffee 
plantations in their study, “Shade Coffee: A Disappearing Refuge for Biodiversity.” 
They found that shaded coffee plantations support a high level of biodiversity, 
comparable to natural forests, by providing habitats for various species, including 
birds, insects, and plants. This study underscored the critical role of shade coffee in 
maintaining biodiversity, especially in regions heavily impacted by deforestation, 
where these plantations serve as vital refuges for species otherwise losing their habitats 
to agricultural expansion.
Building on this foundation, Philpott et al. (2007) investigated the ecological and 
economic benefits of coffee certification programs in Chiapas, Mexico. Their research 
revealed that vegetation variables important for shade certification were significantly 
correlated with ant and bird diversity. None of the cooperatives met the standards for 
shade-coffee certification due to a lack of vertical stratification in their plantations. 
Philpott et al. suggested that while organic and fair-trade certifications provide some 
economic benefits, they protect less biodiversity than shade-certified farms. They 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 213
recommended pursuing triple certification (organic, fair trade, and shade) to enhance 
biodiversity protection and farmers’ economic benefits, highlighting a gap in current 
certification schemes.
A 2021 study on shade management in coffee farming analyzed 179 farms in 
Costa Rica and Guatemala. It found that medium shade levels optimize both high 
coffee productivity and the delivery of broader ecosystem services. It concluded 
that agronomic intensification is compatible with medium shade levels, enhancing 
sustainability without significantly compromising yields (Haggar 2021). 
The health benefits of shade-grown coffee are considerable, as discussed in a study 
highlighting the higher concentrations of beneficial compounds such as antioxidants 
in such coffee (Campos-Vega 2021). Additionally, research focusing on the ecological 
and health benefits of shade-grown coffee reveals that such plantations support higher 
biodiversity and produce coffee beans with better health properties, including reduced 
pesticide residues and increased anti-inflammatory properties(Deines and Rosson 
2022). Furthermore, a study examining the economic and health impacts of shade 
coffee certification on smallholder farmers in Latin America proved that accreditation 
leads to improved health outcomes for farmers. This improvement is attributed to 
safer farming practices and better economic stability, enabling access to healthcare 
and nutrition (Perez and Gonzalez 2023). 
In 2023, another study emphasized the health benefits and sustainability potential 
of coffee by-products, noting the importance of biodiversity in coffee production as 
a response to climate change. They revealed several health advantages, particularly 
their potential as prebiotic ingredients. Coffee by-products, such as coffee pulp and 
husk, are rich in dietary fiber and other compounds that can improve gut health by 
stimulating beneficial bacteria in the colon. Additionally, the study underscored the 
economic and environmental benefits of converting these by-products into valuable 
resources, such as biofuels and functional food ingredients, which support sustainable 
health. This evolution of thought shows the growing recognition of the multifaceted 
benefits of shade-grown coffee, from biodiversity conservation to economic and health 
advantages (Smith 2023).

214 ♦ Ildi Revi
The Smithsonian’s Bird Friendly certification puts these principles into practice by 
requiring coffee farms to maintain shade-grown environments rich in species diversity 
and ecological function. As the only certification that combines organic standards 
with habitat protection, it ensures that coffee production enhances biodiversity while 
offering farmers tangible economic value. This approach reflects the findings of all four 
studies, showing how focused certification programs can deliver both environmental 
and financial benefits.
Agroforestry, which involves intentionally integrating trees and crops on the same 
land, is central to this model. Coffee plants grow beneath a canopy of shade trees that 
improve soil fertility, moderate temperature, and sequester carbon. One of the most 
widely used species is the nitrogen-fixing Inga edulis, a fast-growing, broad-canopied 
tree native to South America and valued for its ability to withstand regular pruning. 
In southern Africa, farmers might border their lands with msasa trees (Brachystegia 
spiciformis), which also contribute nitrogen to the soil and offer practical on-farm 
resources. They become materials for fence posts, inputs for biochar, and fuel for 
drying coffee. And most coffee pickers, especially in hot climates, prefer to spend a 
day picking coffee the shade.

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦215
Continuous monitoring and adaptation of these practices, informed by ongoing 
research and environmental changes, ensure the long-term sustainability and resilience 
of coffee farming. These organic and regenerative methods enhance plant health, yield, 
and environmental sustainability. And in the process, create robust and productive 
coffee farms.
Hacienda La Pradera, Aratoca, Colombia.

216 ♦ Ildi Revi
EUROPEAN REGULATIONS AND 
DEFORESTATION CONCERNS
The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) will take effect on December 30, 2024, 
for large operators and traders, and on June 30, 2026, for small and micro entities. 
The regulation mandates that coffee and other commodities like cocoa, soy, and palm 
oil must be proven deforestation-free to be sold in the EU. This requires companies 
to provide detailed evidence that their products are not linked to deforestation, 
significantly impacting supply chains.
Coffee companies and trade associations are actively working to comply with the 
EUDR. One way they are doing this is by engaging with suppliers to ensure they 
understand and meet the EUDR’s deforestation-free standards. This involves training, 
resources, and support to help suppliers implement sustainable practices. Additionally, 
companies are investing in technology to track and verify the origins of their coffee, 
including using geolocation data to ensure that coffee farms have not contributed to 
deforestation.
The European Coffee Federation (ECF) has been involved in discussions with EU 
legislators to address implementation challenges and advocate for clear guidelines. 
The ECF emphasizes the need for clear guidelines and a streamlined process to avoid 
duplicative efforts and ensure effective compliance. As the January 2025 enforcement 
date approaches, the coffee sector must unite to overcome obstacles and ensure 
a deforestation-free supply chain, with the EU providing the necessary tools and 
guidance for successful implementation.
Some companies also participate in pilot projects with the EU Agency for the Space 
Programme (EUSPA) to use satellite data to monitor deforestation and ensure 
compliance. These efforts aim to create a more transparent and sustainable coffee 
supply chain, aligning with the EUDR’s objectives.
The EUDR supports the principles of holistic coffee by promoting sustainable 
and ethical practices throughout the coffee supply chain. Circular health coffee 
focuses on reducing waste, improving resource efficiency, and enhancing the overall 
sustainability of coffee production because these things also impact health. This 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 217
legislation encourages the coffee sector to adopt practices that protect forests and 
promote the well-being of coffee farming communities and the environment at large.
SOIL HEALTH AND PLANT MAINTENANCE
During our walks around his farm, Rob would often share his approach to maintaining 
healthy coffee plants by repeating a simple principle: “What you take off, you need to 
put back on.” Each harvest depletes soil nutrients, making it crucial to replenish them 
using organic matter, compost, and other natural inputs to maintain system balance 
and ensure plant health.
Keeping both the soil and plants in good condition takes planning, labor, and 
resources, even though the work might look simple. Core practices include:
•
Mulching to protect soil and conserve moisture
•
Cover cropping to build fertility and improve structure
•
Crop rotation to break pest and disease cycles
•
Fertilization using balanced, organic inputs
•
Pruning and plant upkeep
•
Biological pest control that supports natural resistance
•
Efficient irrigation like drip systems to reduce waste
•
Rainwater harvesting to make the most of seasonal rains
•
Proper drainage to prevent waterlogging and root stress

218 ♦ Ildi Revi
These methods synergize to maintain 
fertility and keep pests in check 
without relying on chemicals. Crop 
rotation and intercropping add 
further resilience by improving soil 
health, disrupting pest cycles, and 
making better use of land. Over time, 
this kind of integrated management 
supports the growth of stronger plants 
and leads to higher concentrations of 
important nutrients like magnesium 
and potassium, both essential for 
human health.
Maintaining mature coffee plants in 
a regenerative system means taking 
a whole-farm approach. Everything 
is connected: soil biology, plant 
nutrition, and water management all 
matter. Balanced organic fertilization 
improves structure, holds moisture, and feeds the plants with key nutrients like 
nitrogen, phosphorus, calcium, and magnesium. Later in this chapter, we’ll also look 
at how biochar infused with “nutrient teas” can support this process. All of it starts 
with regular soil testing to guide what the land actually needs.
Just as soil and nutrient management are essential, plant health in a regenerative 
system also depends on labor-intensive upkeep that supports plant productivity and 
health. Pruning improves structure, air circulation, and light penetration—especially 
important in shade-grown environments. Beyond removing diseased branches, 
pruning is a strategic tool to guide growth. It allows producers to direct the plant’s 
energy toward fruit production or stimulate new shoots for future harvests, aligning 
short-term productivity with long-term resilience.
Of course, pest and disease management is critical, too. Organic producers use a 
range of organic remedies that are effective. These are natural pesticides and repellents 
Testing soil for pH. Photo by Purity Coffee.

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦219
like neem oil that disrupts pest growth and reproduction, pyrethrin extracted from 
chrysanthemum flowers controls various insects, and diatomaceous earth dehydrates 
and kills pests. 
Biological controls in organic pest management include beneficial insects like ladybugs, 
predatory mites that naturally manage pest populations, and microbial solutions like 
Bacillus thuringiensis, which targets specific pest larvae. Fungal biopesticides such 
as Beauveria bassiana, which infects and kills numerous insect pests, and Trichoderma 
species, which suppress plant pathogens and promote plant health, are integral to the 
biological control toolkit (Harman 2004). 
The coffee berry borer beetle (Hypothenemus hampei) (CBB), coffee leaf miner 
(Leucoptera caffeine), and coffee white stem borer (Xylotrechus quadripes) are major 
pests, while diseases such as coffee leaf rust (Hemileia vastatrix) and coffee berry disease 
(Colletotrichum kahawae) pose significant threats. Organic management practices 
effectively mitigate these challenges using traps, parasitic wasps, resistant coffee 
varieties, and organic soil amendments.  
Coffee berry borer damage above left.

Beetles seen emerging from coffee cherries above left. Processed and dried green coffee with 
borer damage above right. Dead beetles in the center (each small graph square is 1/8 inch).
CBB is a small beetle native to Africa and is considered the most harmful pest to 
coffee crops worldwide. It bores into coffee fruits to lay eggs, and the larvae feed on 
the coffee beans, significantly reducing yield and quality. It is frightening to open a 
coffee fruit and have the seeds crumble into black, gritty bits. Infestations can lead to 
severe economic losses for coffee producers for years.
A study conducted over three years (2019-2021) in Aceh, Indonesia, compared the 
impact of organic and conventional cultivation systems on CBB infestation in Arabica 
coffee plantations. The findings revealed no significant difference in CBB attack levels 
between organic and conventional methods. This indicated a couple of things: 1. That 
the natural enemies in organic systems effectively controlled CBB populations, and 2. 
That the synthetic pesticides were a waste of money (and actually might have reduced 
the population of natural enemies, causing an imbalance). This case shows an example 
of how organic coffee cultivation is more cost-effective and safer for farmers, workers, 
and consumers (Husni 2022).
Instead, an effective organic remedy for the coffee berry borer is using fungal 
biopesticides containing Beauveria bassiana or Metarhizium anisopliae. These fungi 
infect and kill the beetles, but are considered generally safe to humans when used 
properly (although those with compromised immune systems are advised to avoid 
them).

222 ♦ Ildi Revi
   
    
Trichogramma pretiosum is a tiny parasitic wasp, less than 1mm in length, 
used as a biological control agent against various pests. Eggs can be purchased 
commercially, and drones can spread them across coffee lands (right).
Just because something is organic does not mean it is automatically safe or free from 
potential risks. Diatomaceous earth is a naturally occurring, siliceous sedimentary 
rock that can be ground into a fine powder to enhance soils. Particularly in its food-
grade form, it is generally safe for human consumption and is used for detoxification, 
digestion improvement, and skin and hair health. But it can also cause problems, since 
it consists of fossilized remains of diatoms, a type of hard-shelled microalgae, which can 
cause allergic reactions in some people, leading to symptoms like skin rashes, itching, 
or respiratory issues. We once had a customer ask our support team whether our farms 
use diatomaceous earth, as they had experienced a bad reaction to produce grown 
with it. Because we have a direct relationship with our producers, we were able to get 
an answer fairly quickly (only one had used it a few years earlier, but not currently).
Nevertheless, while not all organic farming practices are completely free of health 
risks, they offer a significantly safer alternative than synthetic pesticides, herbicides, 
and fertilizers, which pose greater health concerns.
By detailing all these practices, I hope to encourage an appreciation of the time, labor, 
and costs involved in proper organic farming. Although some may think organic 
farming costs less than the use of conventional synthetic pesticides, fungicides and 
fertilizers, that’s not always the case. The organic and regenerative work necessary for 
this category actually has different costs, but they produce value in their ingenuity, 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 223
health and environmental outcomes. Regeneration and preservation of ecosystems 
are priceless.
Leaf miner damage (left), antestia beetle (right). Natural predators like parasitic wasps 
can help control these pests. Neem oil and hot pepper spray mixtures with other plants 
have been effective; however, biological controls, such as the fungi isolate Metarhizium 
ansopliae (S-15HU in particular), are effective when used for ten days (Kidanu 2024).
Beneficial microorganisms play a critical role in sustainable agricultural practices by 
offering eco-friendly alternatives to chemical fungicides and reducing harmful fungal 
diseases in crops. These microorganisms work by competing with pathogens, producing 
antimicrobial compounds, or inducing plant resistance. Although a bacterium, Bacillus 
subtilis is often included in microbial solutions for its ability to compete with harmful 
fungi for space and nutrients, and its production of antibiotics that inhibit fungal 
growth. This bacterium is effective against soil-borne pathogens such as Fusarium and 
Verticillium. Similarly, Pseudomonas fluorescens produces siderophores and antibiotics 
that suppress pathogenic fungi and is widely used against Fusarium and other soil-
borne fungal pathogens.
Mycorrhizal fungi form symbiotic relationships with plant roots, enhancing nutrient 
uptake and protecting against soil-borne pathogens through competition and induced 
resistance (stimulating the plant’s defense mechanisms). These fungi improve plant 
health and reduce disease susceptibility (Prates 2019). Gliocladium species are known 
for producing antifungal metabolites and parasitizing harmful fungi, effectively 
controlling soil-borne pathogens in horticulture and agriculture (Hassine 2022). 
Saccharomyces cerevisiae is used in biocontrol formulations and competes with 

224 ♦ Ildi Revi
pathogenic fungi on plant surfaces. It is primarily employed in post-harvest disease 
management (Freimoser 2019).
A range of healthy species of fungi can thrive on organic regenerative farms.
These beneficial microbial species support sustainable agricultural practices by offering 
an eco-friendly alternative to chemical fungicides, thus reducing the prevalence 
of harmful fungal diseases in crops. However, the widespread use of glyphosate, a 
common weed killer, introduces complications. Over the past decade, glyphosate has 
been used extensively worldwide, with its application increasing significantly since 
the introduction of glyphosate-tolerant crops in 1996. Although initially considered 
environmentally safe due to rapid soil adsorption and microbial degradation, concerns 

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦225
have arisen about its ecological impact and potential carcinogenicity. Studies have 
shown that glyphosate remains in the environment longer than previously assumed, 
particularly when crop residues are left in the field. This extended presence can disrupt 
soil microorganisms, and both aquatic and land invertebrates, such as earthworms. 
This raises big concerns about its impact on long-term soil health and ecosystem 
resilience.
Despite some contrasting findings, there is a growing call for more research into 
glyphosate’s long-term effects on soil health and its broader ecological implications 
(Soil Association 2016). By altering microbial communities it has been shown to 
increase crop disease, fueling the growth of harmful fungi like Fusarium (Castro 
2007). Some scientists believe this contributes to the rapid spread of fungal infections 
globally, exacerbated by climate change. Spraying crops with glyphosate provides an 
ideal food source for Fusarium species by altering soil chemistry and weakening plant 
defenses, making these fungi more challenging to control (Castro 2007). In light of 
these issues, farmers can use other methods for controlling unwanted weeds (not all 
weeds are bad) that are likely less expensive and less harmful, like allelopathic herbs.
Marigolds are allelopathic and especially effective against nematodes.

226 ♦ Ildi Revi
Allelopathy refers to the biological phenomenon where one plant releases biochemicals 
(known as allelochemicals) into the environment, which can have beneficial or 
harmful effects on the growth and development of neighboring plants. For example, 
intercropping sage, spearmint, basil, and oregano withCoffea arabica stimulates 
the plants’ growth. It induces stomata opening in coffee leaves through essential oil 
volatiles. Sage and oregano, in particular, absorb caffeine, potentially impacting soil 
microbial communities (Pohlan 2008). Herbal and plant-based solutions such as 
garlic extract, chrysanthemum extract, and capsicum sprays serve as natural insect 
repellents and fungicides.
Allelopathic lavender
Nutrient management is bolstered by using “teas,” or liquid solutions made 
from compost, manure, and herbs that provide essential nutrients and beneficial 
microorganisms. These teas enhance plant growth, soil health, and plant immune 
systems. This organic approach helps maintain soil fertility and microbial diversity, 
leading to higher quality and more resilient coffee crops. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 227
To prepare these teas, appropriate materials like compost, manure, herbs, and 
water are used, along with aerated and nonaerated brewing methods, which help to 
optimize nutrient extraction. Regular application, guided by frequency and dosage 
recommendations, maintains plant health by promoting robust root development 
and enhancing resistance to pests and diseases (Ingram 2000).
Organic nutrient “teas” made on the farm. Each mineral is highlighted, 
so the producer can customize a formula to target different nutritional 
needs. These teas can be used to “activate” biochar.
Composting techniques further support soil health by transforming organic waste 
into valuable fertilizer, enhancing soil structure, aeration, and biodiversity. Various 
types of composting include aerobic composting, which uses oxygen to produce rich, 
crumbly compost; vermicomposting, which employs worms to create nutrient-dense 
castings; and Bokashi composting, an anaerobic process that ferments organic waste. 
Best practices in composting involve using a mix of green (nitrogen-rich) and brown 

228 ♦ Ildi Revi
materials (carbon-rich), maintaining a balanced carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, and ensuring 
proper moisture and aeration.
An organic regenerative coffee farm in Colombia 
(above). Key aspects: Soil health, biodiversity, 
minimal tillage, cover crops, livestock integration, 
water management, carbon sequestration (right).
Industrial farming requires growing coffee trees in rows exposed to sunshine, like a 
winery vineyard. It is a monocrop, and the soil has no diversity. When pests arrive, 
industrial farmers spray and kill everything, including harmless or potentially beneficial 
pests. This impacts birds that rely on those insects for food, so they abandon the area. 
Over time, industrial soil is degraded due to a lack of microorganisms (eliminated by 
herbicides, insecticides and limited compost). It then requires more fertilizer and, in 
some cases, irrigation. This has led to the use of both methods increasing dramatically 
in the last 15 years. These farms continue to require even more inputs of dangerous 
chemicals as the soil degrades. The UN-FAO report (2015) predicted that we only have 

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦229
60 harvests left before we run out of soil. When land is not diverse, the soil does not 
have the chance to develop the important microbes needed to feed the growing plants.
Ducks are helpful animals as part of regenerative agriculture (above). 
(Below) On a farm in Brazil beginning the regenerative process, the 
producer has ceased coffee production to allow the soil to recover its fertility 
and organic matter to improve soil health and productivity.

230 ♦ Ildi Revi
In 2020, Dr. Mark Hyman wrote a book titled Food Fix: How to Save Our Health, 
Our Economy, Our Communities, and Our Planet--One Bite at a Time, illuminating 
how organic regenerative farming is critical for the future of the earth…and food 
supply. Among the abundance of evidence and statistics, Hyman highlighted that 
the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization calculates that 12 million 
hectares of arable land are lost annually, and experts say we have lost 50-70 percent of 
our topsoil from industrialized agriculture, deforestation, over-fertilizing and other 
poor agricultural practices. Industrial farming also releases carbon into the air and 
contributes to climate change.
“Soil is everywhere, but our agriculture practices increasingly 
turn our soil into dirt. Dirt is dead. Soil is alive. Plants thrive 
in soil and not dirt.” 
– Dr. Mark Hyman, Food Fix, 2020
Healthy soil rich in organic matter retains water, and as a result reduces the effects 
of floods and drought. It also stores carbon, feeds all microbial life, makes nutrients 
available to plants (and humans that eat them), and detoxifies pollutants, amongst 
other things. Soil is rich in fertility, microbes, fungi and nutrients.
Purity’s research farm in Colombia, Montebonito. Photo credit:  Amanda Perry, Purity Coffee.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 231
Microbes play a vital role in maintaining healthy soil in coffee agriculture by enhancing 
nutrient availability, promoting plant growth, and suppressing soil-borne diseases. 
Beneficial microorganisms such as bacteria, fungi, and actinomycetes decompose 
organic matter, improving soil structure and nutrient cycling (de Bruyn 2017). 
Additionally, microbial interactions support plant health by inducing systemic 
resistance against pathogens and facilitating nutrient uptake (Harman 2004). Effective 
microbial management through composting and organic amendments can significantly 
enhance coffee crop productivity and sustainability (Ingham 2000).
Dirt needs fertilizer, pesticides, herbicides, nutrients and water to grow food, while 
soil does not. Dirt causes climate change, while soil reverses it. The top meter of soil 
contains three times as much carbon as the entire atmosphere. Building healthy soil 
allows plants to put down deeper roots, pulling carbon from the air deep into the soil. 
The rich microbial life in healthy soil also helps keep the carbon in the soil, creating 
a virtuous cycle (Hyman 2020).
BIOCHAR 
Since the dawn of civilization, the evolution of agriculture has profoundly influenced 
human societies. The shift from nomadic lifestyles to settled farming necessitated 
deliberate soil management practices and impacted the prosperity of early civilizations. 
One such practice was the application of biochar, dating between 450 BC and 8000 
BC. Ancient Amazonians created fertile terra preta by adding charcoal, bones, broken 
pottery, manure, and compost to the soil. This early form of biochar significantly 
enriched the otherwise nutrient-poor Amazonian basin soils. Similar techniques were 
observed in Japan with haigoe, where farmers combined human waste with rice husk 
biochar, and among the Mayans, who used charcoal mixed with waste to improve 
soil fertility.
Interest in biochar for coffee production took hold in the early 2000s. Although 
extensive research is still desired, coffee farms worldwide have reported positive 
outcomes from biochar usage over the past two decades. Biochar is acclaimed for its 
potential to mitigate the adverse effects of global warming on agriculture. It enhances 
soil nutrients, moisture, and biodiversity, thus boosting crop yields and immunity. 

232 ♦ Ildi Revi
Its stable structure allows long-term carbon sequestration, which can last centuries or 
even millennia, offering a sustainable alternative to synthetic fertilizers.
Biochar is extensively studied for its agricultural benefits, carbon credits, and soil 
remediation. Its characteristics depend on the temperature, feedstock (the material 
that it is made out of, like wood, coffee husks, etc.), and pyrolysis duration (explained 
shortly). Coffee is primarily grown in tropical environments with low fertilizer 
efficiency due to evaporation and leaching. Biochar contributes to soil health by 
increasing the cation exchange capacity (CEC), which is important for nutrient 
absorption and retention. Studies have shown that biochar can improve fertilizer 
efficiency and naturally improve soil fertility. It helps the soil retain essential nutrients 
like ammonium, calcium, magnesium, and potassium, acting as a reservoir for plant 
growth. Additionally, biochar’s porous nature promotes microbial activity, vital for 
soil biodiversity and health, facilitating processes like nitrification.
Biochar production at Hacienda La Pradera, Aratoca, Colombia.
Synthetic fertilizers reduce soil microbial relationships, while biochar’s pores and 
moisture content support microbial life, enhancing soil diversity and nutrition. 
Biochar also improves soil structure, porosity, and aeration, promoting nitrification 
and steady nitrogen release. With a pH of 6 to 9, biochar neutralizes soil acidity, 
making it suitable for acidic soils. Coffee biochar, in particular, has a higher surface 
area and more developed porous structure, making it ideal for CO2 capture and soil 
amendment applications.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 233
One of biochar’s most compelling benefits is its role in carbon sequestration. 
Annually, over 3.5 billion tons of agricultural waste are dumped or burnt, releasing 
significant quantities of greenhouse gases. Biochar addresses this by converting 
unstable CO2 in biomass into a stable material resistant to decomposition 
for over 150 years. It also reduces the production of methane and nitrous oxide 
in soils, further mitigating climate change impacts. One hope in this area is 
that producers who sequester carbon will find ways to obtain carbon credits. 
The feedstock and pyrolysis parameters significantly influence biochar’s properties. 
Coffee farms generate various byproducts like pulp, husks, and spent grounds, all 
of which can be converted into biochar (and other helpful inputs). Each feedstock 
imparts different properties to the biochar; for instance, coffee husks and parchment 
have high lignin content, contributing to the thermal stability and carbon content of 
the biochar. Also, spent coffee grounds yield biochar with a high specific surface area, 
enhancing its pollutant absorption and extraction capabilities. 
BIOCHAR PRODUCTION, ACTIVATION AND APPLICATION
Biochar is created through pyrolysis, which heats the feedstock in a low-oxygen (or 
no-oxygen) environment. This is not combustion. The absence of oxygen prevents 
the carbon in the biomass from burning off into the atmosphere as CO2. Instead, it 
stabilizes into a solid form. This process transforms carbon into chemically stable 
structures that resist microbial breakdown and oxidation, allowing it to remain in the 
soil rather than returning to the atmosphere as CO2 (Shrestha 2023, Tomczyk 2020). 
Pyrolysis typically takes place at temperatures between 500 and 700 °C and results in 
three outputs: biochar (the solid), bio-oil (liquid), and volatile gases. Both the gases 
and bio-oil can be captured and used as renewable energy sources (Thomsen 2022). 

234 ♦ Ildi Revi
Feedstock for an earth mound kiln for biochar production at Hacienda La Pradera
It is important not to confuse biochar with ash. Ash is the product of complete 
combustion, where biomass is burned in the presence of oxygen. This process oxidizes 
the carbon, releasing it as CO2 and leaving behind mineral residues. Ash has virtually 
no carbon left and lacks the porous structure and microbial benefits of biochar. It 
may temporarily raise soil pH or provide minerals like potassium or calcium, but 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 235
these nutrients leach quickly, and ash does little to improve soil function (Smołka-
Danielowska and Jabłońska 2021). Biochar, on the other hand, is made through 
pyrolysis, and this preserves the carbon, builds a porous structure, and supports 
beneficial soil microbes. 
Traditional methods of pyrolysis include earth mound kilns, while modern systems 
use rotatable, enclosed kilns that optimize consistency and carbon retention. Because 
biochar is beginning to be used more in agriculture, new and advanced systems have 
been created.  I won’t go into those here, but you can easily find them online.
Biochar in process at Hacienda La Pradera
One of the most remarkable features of biochar is its pore structure. During pyrolysis, 
moisture is driven off, and the vascular tissue of the biomass is restructured, leaving 
behind a matrix of microscopic pores. This results in an incredibly high surface 
area, and these pores function like a sponge. For coffee farms, this makes biochar 
incredibly useful. Coffee is a thirsty crop, requiring anywhere from 1 to 20 cubic 
meters of water per ton of green coffee (Tomczyk 2020). Biochar helps retain that 
moisture in the soil, reducing irrigation needs and buffering plants during drought. 
Just as importantly, it also prevents waterlogging by wicking excess water away from 
the roots—something coffee plants are notoriously sensitive to (Tomczyk 2020). In 

236 ♦ Ildi Revi
trials, biochar use has been linked to longer roots and greater access to nutrients in the 
rhizosphere, improving plant resilience and health overall. The pores of the biochar 
can be seen in the photos below.
Hacienda La Pradera’s finished biochar

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 237
Backscattered electron (BSE) images taken using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) 
by Chiza Mwinde, University of Chicago. Images show the structure of the biochar 
from Hacienda La Pradera, Colombia. Biochar (pyrolysis) has this porous structure seen 
in the photos, but ash (combustion) does not. [Photos property of Purity Coffee]
Biochar must be activated before soil application. Due to its porous structure, raw 
biochar will absorb nutrients from soil if not pre-loaded, potentially depriving plants 
of available nutrition initially. Activation saturates the cation and anion exchange 
sites on biochar surfaces, enabling nutrient exchange with soil rather than nutrient 

238 ♦ Ildi Revi
depletion. Co-composting is 
the most effective activation 
method, combining biochar 
with compost to create 
co-composted biochar. This 
process loads biochar with 
nutrients and beneficial 
microbes while improving 
composting efficiency. 
Activation also allows 
customization. Producers can 
tailor activation to specific 
soil needs by incorporating 
targeted organic nutrients 
or specialized composts 
to address deficiencies. 
Activated biochar becomes a 
biologically active, nutrient-
rich soil amendment ready 
for soil regeneration.
Biochar application ratios 
vary, but ten to twenty tonnes 
per hectare is recommended 
for significant benefits. 
Biochar applications can 
be tailored to specific crop 
needs, improving immediate 
and long-term soil health. 
Regular applications can 
accelerate soil structure 
changes, boosting production 
yields. Producers should take 
Purity Coffee’s Biochar Trials: 
Field Evidence
To explore biochar’s impact under real-world conditions, we 
conducted multi-year field trials to evaluate biochar types, 
microbial inoculants, composted amendments, and various 
application methods. We conducted two on-farm trials at La 
Primavera in Cundinamarca, Colombia to assess biochar’s effects 
on growth, development, and yield. 
The first trial tested a range of biochar formulations, including 
fertilized and co-composted biochars. Fertilized biochar, enriched 
with goat and chicken manure and conditioned with coffee honey, 
produced the most robust results, increasing productivity by 1.5 
to 3 times over control groups. Combinations of biochar with 
biostimulants (from local agricultural companies) or with pig 
manure also improved plant height, stem diameter, branch length, 
and node development. Notably, treatments that combined 
biochar with microbial amendments yielded both physiological 
growth and strong flowering-to-fruit conversion, indicating 
higher long-term yield potential.
A parallel trial focused solely on microorganism applications, 
using foliar and soil-drench methods. Results showed that foliar 
applications at 25% concentration applied twice were the most 
effective, significantly improving plant growth metrics and harvest 
yield. While composts inoculated with microbes did not show 
increased nutrient content, they did exhibit greater microbial 
density, suggesting potential as a vector for delivering beneficial 
soil organisms. Together, these trials confirmed that biochar—
particularly when paired with living microbial systems—can play 
a vital role in regenerative coffee farming. These findings represent 
the early stages of a longer-term vision to build high-functioning 
soils through circular inputs and low-cost innovations.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 239
soil samples and customize the biochar to determine the exact amounts to apply. It 
can also be added to the soil in the nursery to produce seedlings with robust roots.
Getting ready to plant seedlings and amending soil with nutrient-
infused biochar at Hacienda La Pradera, Aratoca, Colombia.

240 ♦ Ildi Revi
BIOCHAR AND CIRCULAR AGRICULTURE

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 241
CHROMATOGRAPHY: HEALTHY AND UNHEALTH SOILS
This section highlights the work of Oscar Daza, owner of La Pradera Coffee Farm in 
Santander, Colombia, and Tulio Lozano, a soil scientist whom Oscar commissioned 
to study the effects of biochar, composts, and microorganisms on soil health, coffee 
plant vitality, and the final coffee product. Their research uses visual tools like 
chromatography to track soil recovery, microbial life, and nutrient balance. They 
will be publishing their complete findings, but have permitted me to give a preview 
of their work here.
Soil chromatography is a simple yet powerful method for evaluating soil health 
through color and structure. It involves placing a small soil sample on filter paper and 
applying a solvent that draws out different soil compounds. These separate into colored 
rings and patterns, creating a unique chromatogram. Each layer represents different 
components of the soil, from mineral content to organic matter to microbial activity.

242 ♦ Ildi Revi
Photo credit: Tulio Lozano, 2024
The soil system’s chromatogram shows it is biologically active and well-structured. 
Its balanced zones and integration of organic matter, minerals, and microbes point to 
organic regenerative practices and strong overall soil health.
The next diagram shows the five zones typically visible in a soil chromatogram. Each 
ring tells part of the soil’s story.

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦243
Central Zone – Oxygenization - The pale, creamy center of this chromatogram 
indicates balanced oxygenation and good soil porosity. Its smooth transition into the 
next layer suggests there is no compaction and no excess of nitrogen-based fertilizers.
Internal Zone – Minerals - The golden-brownish ring in the mineral zone shows that 
mineral content is present and biologically active. The absence of sharp boundaries 
implies that minerals are integrated into the broader soil system rather than isolated 
or inert.
Intermediate Zone – Organic Materials - This zone, which is lightish-brown and 
is evenly distributed, suggests the presence of decomposed organic matter forming 
humic substances. It reflects good interaction between minerals and microbiology.
External Zone – Enzymatic Nutrition - The outer band has wave-like patterns 
that indicate active enzymatic processes. These forms reflect nutrient movement and 
microbial activity within the soil, demonstrating ongoing biological exchange.
Radial Zone – Microorganisms - At the perimeter, fine radial striations extend 
outward. These are visual markers of microbial diversity and vitality. Their clarity and 
extension suggest a thriving microbial population actively supporting soil function.

244 ♦ Ildi Revi
Healthy chromatograms show smooth transitions between these zones, rich colors, 
and fine radial lines that reflect biological complexity and structural integrity. In 
contrast, degraded soils show breaks between zones, faded or muddy colors, and little 
to no radial pattern—indicators of nutrient isolation, compaction, or microbial 
absence. 
Chromatogram 1: Degraded Industrial Sugar 
Cane Crop Soil
In this soil chromatogram there is a complete 
lack of integration and harmony. The central, 
internal, intermediate, and external zones are 
absent, showing undesirable colors such as black 
and bluish tones with a small strip of lilac. The 
ends are flat and circular, lacking any radial 
system, indicating no microbiological presence. 
This soil, from an industrial sugar cane crop, is 
completely degraded due to constant 
mechanization and applying pesticides and highly soluble fertilizers.
Chromatogram 2: Poorly Oxygenated Soil 
from Transitional Bean Crops 
This soil in the central area indicates a lack 
of oxygenation. The internal or mineral 
zone extends to the external zone, and the 
intermediate zone, which would reveal organic 
matter, is completely absent. The edges are 
pointed and teeth-like, with no radial system, 
indicating no microbiological presence. This 
compacted and mineralized soil, lacking natural 
cover, is typical of transitional bean crops where 
crop rotation wasn’t practiced.
