HEALTHY VERSUS UNHEALTHY COFFEE: 
THE SOILS AND THE BEVERAGE
The following chromatograms provide side-by-side comparisons of soils and brewed 
coffee taken from organically managed systems and chemically treated ones. These are 
the exact soils in which the final coffee beverage chromatograms were grown. Together, 
they visualize what’s happening beneath the surface and within the final cup. This 
work by Daza and Lozano demonstrates clearly that we are onto something, when 
we say that coffee grown organically and regeneratively produces a healthier coffee 
for us to drink.
  
Photo credit: Tulio Lozano, 2024
The chromatogram above of the brewed coffee mirrors the same structural integrity 
seen in its soil chromatogram. The central oxygenation zone is compact yet open, with 
a smooth transition into the mineral and organic matter zones, which are warm-toned 
and well-integrated. Rich radial striations extend outward, showing that the coffee 
retains the imprint of a vibrant microbial system and balanced nutrient environment. 
The outer enzymatic and microbial zones display complex, expressive edges, indicating 
biochemical diversity and energetic movement—traits commonly associated with high 
antioxidant activity and functional quality in the final cup. 

246 ♦ Ildi Revi
This image presents a clear contrast between soil and coffee chromatograms 
from chemically treated, unhealthy agricultural systems. The top row shows soil 
chromatograms with pale centers, sharp mineral bands, and little to no organic or 
enzymatic zones—classic signs of compaction, isolated mineral residues, and microbial 
absence. The lack of integration between zones suggests disrupted biological cycles 
and a breakdown in nutrient exchange. In the second row, the chromatograms of 
the brewed coffees from these same soils mirror the damage: each sample displays 
rigid, enclosed forms with limited radial expression, dark toxic-like centers, and little 
evidence of biochemical vitality. Together, these images visually confirm how degraded, 
chemically dependent soils yield coffees lacking complexity, balance, and biological 
resonance. They offer a striking representation of the downstream effects of industrial 
inputs on the final product.
These chromatograms reveal a powerful truth: the health of the soil shapes the health 
of the coffee. From microbial life to nutrient flow, what happens in the ground carries 
forward into the cup. This is what the entire chapter has been about—how growing 
practices influence not just plant health and soil structure, but the final beverage 
itself. These images bring that message into focus, offering a visual demonstration of 
everything we’ve explored. By capturing this relationship so clearly, Daza and Lozano 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 247
give us a new way of seeing what lab data alone cannot fully express. Soil structure, 
microbial diversity, and biochemical vitality are not abstract concepts. They are 
visible, tangible, and deeply connected to the quality and health impact of the coffee 
we drink. Chromatography reminds us that truly healthy coffee begins below the 
surface, in living soil.
HARVESTING FOR HEALTH
Coffee harvesting is a critical phase in coffee production, directly influencing the final 
product’s quality, flavor, and health benefits. The methods used to harvest coffee fruits 
can vary widely, from labor-intensive manual picking to more automated mechanical 
harvesting and stripping. Each method has distinct implications, not only for the 
flavor and nutritional content of the coffee but also for the health of workers and the 
environmental sustainability of the coffee farms. 
When it comes to harvesting coffee for health benefits, the ripeness of the coffee fruits 
play a role in determining their CGA content. Research indicates that immature or 
slightly underripe coffee beans contain significantly higher levels of CGA than fully 
ripe beans. One study found that CGA levels in ripe beans were 6.5 times lower than 
in immature beans (Bastian 2021). This suggests that harvesting coffee slightly earlier, 
before peak ripeness, could help preserve more of this health-supportive compound.
This presents a clear example of how CHC prioritizes health outcomes over 
conventional sensory evaluation. While traditional quality systems may favor fully 
ripe cherries for optimal flavor, CHC considers the functional properties of earlier-
stage fruit as well. Coffee that is clean, free of defects, and harvested just before full 
ripeness can still meet health-grade standards, even if it doesn’t align with the flavor 
preferences that drive other grading systems. In the CHC model, the value of the coffee 
lies not only in how it tastes, but also in what it contributes to wellness.

248 ♦ Ildi Revi
Multiple factors, including plant genetics, environmental stress, and post-harvest 
practices influence CGA content in coffee. The Farah et al. (2005b) data, cited in 
the review by Farah and Donangelo (2006), confirm that immature coffee seeds can 
contain CGA levels up to 8.7% dry matter, compared to just 1.3% in overripe seeds. 
This suggests that earlier harvests may preserve more of these bioactive compounds.
CGA levels also shift in response to oxidative stress, polyphenol oxidase activity, and 
other physiological variables during maturation and processing. As a result, optimizing 
CGA content is not simply a matter of choosing ripeness, but requires an integrated 
understanding of how maturation, climate, cultivar, and processing each influence 
CGA stability and transformation. Future research should continue exploring how 
these variables shape both the concentration and isomeric profile of CGA to align 
coffee composition with health-promoting potential better.
MANuAL HARvESTING involves hand-picking 
ripe coffee fruits, focusing on the fruit’s pre-
defined quality (or target quality) to harvest. 
Workers selectively pick fruits at their 
optimum ripeness (determined by health 
or taste), often going through the same land 
or lot multiple times. This method allows 
for selecting diseased or dead fruits, which 
can be isolated immediately, ensuring only 
healthy fruits are processed. This method 
results in higher-quality coffee for consumers, 
potentially leading to higher antioxidant 
levels and better nutritional value in the final 
coffee product. Environmentally, minimal 
Hand-selected coffee: Some cherries have 
already been picked in the lower part of the 
photo. In the upper left, see that some cherries 
are not even ripe. They may need to revisit 
this land four or five times in a season.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 249
soil disturbance promotes sustainability and can help reduce the need for chemical 
interventions, indirectly benefiting consumer health. Pickers must be trained not to 
damage the coffee plants because some damage will impact the plant’s health and fruit 
development the following season. 
Coffee pickers play an important role in ensuring the health quality of coffee. By carefully 
selecting beans based on ripeness, avoiding those that are not ready to be picked, and 
isolating beans compromised by pests or diseases, they significantly influence the overall 
health benefits of the final product. Some producers have optical sorting machines 
that remove defective fruits, but coffee pickers serve as the first line of defense.

250 ♦ Ildi Revi
Mechanical harvesting uses machines to shake coffee trees, causing fruits to fall onto 
collecting mechanisms. This method is less selective than manual picking, making it 
harder to sort out diseased or dead fruits during the initial harvest. While it reduces 
physical strain on workers, it introduces other health risks, such as noise and machinery 
vibration exposure. Unless the cherries are sorted by color after harvesting, the mixed 
fruit quality due to non-selective harvesting can result in uneven flavor and potentially 
lower nutritional quality if overripe or diseased fruits are included. 
Environmentally, soil compaction from machinery can lead to long-term soil health 
issues, affecting water retention and nutrient availability. Just as manual picking must 
be done while being conscientious of damaging the coffee plants, machine calibration 
must be maintained to do the same.
A machine-picked lot of coffee at an industrial coffee farm in Brazil.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 251
Overripe cherry from a machine-picked lot
Machine harvesting coffee (above). Soft picking brushes inside 
the machine jostle the cherries off the plants (right).

252 ♦ Ildi Revi
Machine harvesting coffee cannot happen in most coffee-growing regions due to topography.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 253
Stripping, or Strip Picking, involves manually or mechanically stripping all ripe 
and unripe fruits from the branch simultaneously, usually done closer to the end of 
the harvest season. This method also makes it difficult to sort out diseased or dead 
fruits during the initial harvest. In some cases, plants are stripped when diseased, as 
with fusarium-killed plants, or plants affected by other diseases or pests. Picking these 
types of fruits is the most concerning for health and should be avoided entirely for 
health-focused coffees. The flavor and nutritional content can be impacted, and there 
is also a risk of higher levels of undesirable compounds from diseased beans, such as 
mycotoxins. 
There is still a market for this quality of coffee, and it is one of the areas where 
consumers need to be careful. Generally speaking, the lowest cost of coffee may 
indicate this type of harvest. Environmentally, non-selective harvesting can lead to 
higher waste and increased usage of processing resources, potentially leading to more 
environmental contaminants indirectly affecting worker and consumer health.
These activities highlight the balance between efficiency and quality. Manual 
harvesting is generally considered higher quality due to the careful selection of ripe 
coffee cherries, provided the pickers are well-trained and do not damage the plants. 
However, it is usually more costly and less efficient. Mechanical methods, on the other 
hand, offer greater efficiency but can compromise coffee quality and health outcomes. 
This is not always the case, as untrained workers can pick coffee poorly, while machines 
in a field of uniformly ripe coffee can perform well.
Either way, CHC relies on trusted partnerships with producers who monitor their 
harvests for accuracy and conduct laboratory testing on pre-shipment samples to 
ensure health standards are met.

254
5.4 What does it mean to “process” 
coffee?
Once coffee is harvested, it must be processed as soon as possible, within about 12-24 
hours, depending on the temperature and weather. Unlike tomatoes, coffee cherries do 
not continue to ripen after picking. Instead, they start to rot in a few hours, especially 
in hot and moist climates. The processing method used is another aspect of protecting 
green coffee quality. It is not the processing typical of “processed foods” which is high 
in added sugars, unhealthy fats, sodium, artificial additives, and little nutritional 
value. It is a different definition of processing. When we take the harvested coffee 
fruit, remove the parts we do not want and end up with just the coffee beans without 
parchment, we can say we have processed the coffee.
There are two key processing stages: fruit removal and parchment removal. Sometimes, 
this happens simultaneously; sometimes, in two different steps. Along with the fruit 
and parchment removal, at some point, is the critical step of drying the coffee, which 
many consider the most important and impactful aspect of processing. The 
coffee must be dried to about 12 percent moisture content (below 0.70aw water 
activity) before being dry milled, bagged and shipped. This moisture level is for 
food safety reasons, and we can be confident in the science of this moisture level, as 
we discussed with the approach to lab testing earlier.
The core processing types range from fully washed to pulped natural (and semi-
washed) to full naturals. There are dozens of variations between them, all impacting 
the taste and health aspects of the finished green coffee, which will be discussed later 
in the chapter. Most conventional producers choose a certain processing method for 
practical reasons rather than for sensory enrichment. For example, producers in Yemen 
do not have the water needed for the washed coffee method. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 255
 Coffee cherries just harvested are being sorted and pulped.

256 ♦ Ildi Revi
Washing coffee after fermentation and before drying (left).  Natural processed coffee (right).
THE MAIN METHODS OF PROCESSING COFFEE
Coffee processing is one of the most critical factors influencing coffee’s health and 
environmental impact. Processing also affects taste, which matters for holistic coffee, 
not for competition scores, and for meeting health-grade green grading standards 
and avoiding defects. The two most common processing methods are washed and 
natural (also called dry-processed or unwashed), though naming conventions can vary 
depending on the region. A third broad category, often called “honey,” pulp natural or 
semi-washed, falls somewhere between washed and natural, with dozens of nuanced 
techniques and local variations, sometimes with unique names, that influence the 
final chemistry of the bean.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 257
The same lot of Coffea arabica ‘Caturra’ processed two ways: Washed (left) and natural (right).
A SHORT DISCUSSION ABOUT FERMENTATION GENERALLY
All coffee processing begins with the breakdown of sugars in the coffee fruit by natural 
yeasts and microbes. Fermentation has been part of the human diet throughout history. 
We use fermentation to preserve food, alter its sensory attributes, and enhance its 
nutritional value. Fermentation involves microbes that consume the sugars and break 
down the mucilage, coating the parchment. 
Before I begin, though, I need to take a short sidetrack on fermentation: At Purity 
Coffee, we get some customer support questions every year about whether or not 
coffee is a fermented food. Some people consume fermented foods to improve their 
health, but others avoid them for specific health reasons. 
While coffee undergoes fermentation during its processing, it is generally not classified 
as a fermented food in the traditional sense, like pickles, kimchi, or yogurt. Fermented 
foods typically refer to those preserved and altered by microorganisms well into their 
final form when consumed, through procedures such as lactic acid fermentation. In 
contrast, fermentation in coffee processing is a transitional step aimed at enhancing 
flavor and quality, rather than preserving or creating a probiotic-rich food. 

258 ♦ Ildi Revi
The fermentation process in coffee is primarily used to break down the mucilage 
surrounding the coffee bean, thereby impacting the bean’s flavor profile. This 
fermentation usually lasts between, on average, 12 and 48 hours, followed by washing 
and drying (De Bruyn 2017). Conversely, the fermentation in foods like pickles and 
kimchi serves to preserve the food, develop complex flavors, and produce beneficial 
probiotics. This type of fermentation is typically done through lactic acid bacteria 
and can last several weeks (Lee 2004).
Vinegar pickles undergo a form of preservation rather than true fermentation. While 
traditional pickling methods rely on fermentation through the natural production 
of lactic acid bacteria, the process involving vinegar (acetic acid) differs. In vinegar 
pickling, the cucumbers are submerged in an acetic acid solution that preserves them 
and imparts a sour flavor. This process does not rely on the live microbial activity 
characteristic of true fermentation.
The key difference between vinegar pickling and traditional fermentation is the 
presence of live microorganisms. In fermentation, beneficial bacteria (such as 
Lactobacillus) consume sugars in the food to produce lactic acid, which acts as a 
preservative and creates a distinctive tangy flavor. This method preserves the food and 
introduces probiotics that can benefit gut health (Jung 2014).
In contrast, vinegar pickling involves directly adding acetic acid, which creates an 
environment that inhibits microbial growth, thereby preventing fermentation. Acetic 
acid, typically derived from vinegar, acidifies the cucumbers and preserves them 
without the activity of live bacteria (Lee 2004). Therefore, while both methods result in 
tangy and preserved foods, only traditional lactic acid fermentation can be classified as 
fermented, because it involves active microbial fermentation and produces probiotics. 
Coffee also undergoes microbial fermentation post-harvest.
After the fermentation stage in coffee, the beans are dried, roasted, and brewed before 
consumption. The final product, coffee, is not consumed for its live microbial content, 
but for its sensory qualities, such as aroma, flavor, and body (Jesus 2020). In contrast, 
pickles and kimchi are consumed in their fermented state and are rich in live cultures 
of beneficial bacteria, which are considered healthy. They are valued not only for their 
taste but also for their probiotic content (Jung 2014). 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 259
Additionally, the microbial activity during coffee fermentation is carefully controlled 
to prevent the development of off-flavors and unwanted microbes, and to enhance 
the bean’s natural flavors without leaving live cultures in the final product (Saia 2020). 
Meanwhile, the fermentation process in pickles and kimchi encourages the growth of 
beneficial bacteria that continue to live in the food even after packaging, providing 
probiotic benefits (Lee 2004). 
Thus, while coffee does undergo fermentation, it differs substantially from traditional 
fermented foods in purpose, end-product characteristics, and microbial activity.
Outdoor fermentation tank (left). Indoor stainless steel and 
black ceramic fermentation tanks (right)

260 ♦ Ildi Revi
WASHED COFFEE: (also referred to as: washed Arabica, 
fully washed, wet method, wet-milled; also “washed 
milds” for Central and South American Arabicas)
Washed coffees are pulped and then fermented at a wet mill to remove the fruit 
from the coffee seeds. The washed coffee process (the “wet” method) involves several 
meticulous steps to ensure a clean coffee profile. Coffee cherries are sent through large, 
texturized metal discs, tubes or cones, and pulped to remove the outer skin, leaving the 
beans in a mucilage layer. The beans are sent to tanks to undergo fermentation for 12 
to 48 hours (as low as 8 hours in hot climates and up to 72 hours in cold locations), 
during which naturally occurring enzymes break down the mucilage. This stage must 
be carefully monitored to avoid contamination with toxic compounds and to prevent 
off-flavors. After fermentation, the beans are thoroughly washed with clean water to 
remove any remaining mucilage, in preparation for drying them.
A drum-shaped coffee pulper (above, 
middle) and close up (above). 

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦261
After fermentation, the coffee is released from the tanks into washing channels that 
help rinse any remaining mucilage, and mechanical demucilaging machines are often 
used. The washed beans are then sent to dry on raised beds, patios, or mechanical 
dryers until they reach a moisture content of around 11 percent to 12 percent. They 
must be turned to dry evenly. Proper drying is critical to prevent mold growth and 
preserve bean quality. Once dried, the beans usually have a resting period, where the 
water moisture evens out in the beans, after which they undergo hulling to remove 
the parchment. Then, they are sorted, graded and bagged.
The washed method has several health and environmental implications. For workers, 
the process involves exposure to waterborne pathogens and cleaning chemicals. The 
technique generally poses minimal risks for consumers if processing is well-controlled, 
although poorly managed drying can lead to the growth of mold and mycotoxins. 
Environmentally, the high water consumption required for pulping and washing can 
strain local water resources, especially in drought-prone areas. If not treated, effluent 
from washing and fermentation can cause water pollution. Also, mechanical dryers 
increase energy consumption, contributing to a higher carbon footprint. In some 
countries where the electricity grid is challenged, it can cause other issues, like the 
need to use diesel generators, which has environmental consequences and adds costs.
A disk-shaped coffee pulper.

262 ♦ Ildi Revi
Financially, the washed method requires significant investment in pulping machines, 
fermentation tanks, and drying facilities. Operational costs may be higher due to 
whatever method may be used for wastewater treatment. Also, using metered water, 
as opposed to natural water reservoirs on the farm that supply the washing station, 
for example, from mountain streams originating on the farm, can lead to higher costs. 
Degradation of the coffee’s quality can also lead to economic loss when the processing 
process is not carefully managed. 
Despite these challenges, the washed method’s precise nature ensures that only top-
quality coffee gets to consumers. It delivers a cup characterized by its clean profile 
and possibly bright acidity, a hallmark of this processing method. Still, it is not 
always the case if the coffee lacks organic acids. Additionally, selecting diseased or 
dead fruits during the manual sorting helps maintain the coffee’s overall quality and 
health benefits. 
ANAEROBIC FERMENTATION
Anaerobic fermentation, a process that has captivated specialty coffee enthusiasts 
and engaged producers worldwide, is gaining traction for its ability to produce coffee 
with exceptional flavor profiles. Innovation in coffee processing driven by anaerobic 
fermentation is a testament to the industry’s evolving nature. It is so new in the public 
eye that I could not find any scientific literature on its health and food safety aspects. 
When examining it for health, I will cross-reference with whatever literature seems 
relevant.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 263
The same lot of Coffea arabica ‘Gesha’ with two different anaerobic 
processing times (above). A washed Coffea arabica ‘Gesha’ is below.

This fermentation method, which is carried out in oxygen-free environments, enables 
coffee beans to develop distinct and complex tastes, often fetching higher prices 
in the market. Regardless, this sophisticated process also comes with substantial 
risks and requires a significant investment of resources from producers in time and 
expertise, amongst other things. Anaerobic conditions can foster the growth of harmful 
microbes if not properly controlled. For instance, Clostridium spp. and other spoilage 
microorganisms can proliferate, potentially producing off-flavors and even toxins.
First, let us look at fermentation’s fundamental role in coffee processing. Traditionally, 
this process occurs in the presence of oxygen (“aerobic fermentation,” explained 
in a bit), but anaerobic fermentation deviates by using sealed environments rich 
in CO2. Similar to carbonic maceration (more on this shortly), this method 
prolongs fermentation periods and produces chemical compounds that result 
in bold, occasionally unpredictable, and potentially exciting, unique flavors 
exclusive to anaerobic coffees.
Anaerobic fermentation, aerobic fermentation, and carbonic maceration each offer 
distinct approaches to fermenting the fruit of the coffee seeds in their parchment. 
Anaerobic fermentation occurs in sealed environments devoid of oxygen, where 
specific microbial communities thrive, producing unique and complex flavor profiles 
(Daglia 2014). In contrast, aerobic fermentation happens in the presence of oxygen, 
through a process where natural yeasts and bacteria break down sugars more rapidly, 
typically resulting in brighter, cleaner flavors (Flament 2002). Inspired by winemaking, 
carbonic maceration creates an anaerobic environment to ferment whole coffee 
cherries from the inside out, enhancing fruity and vibrant notes (Jesus 2020).
CARBONIC MACERATION PROCESS AND BENEFITS
Carbonic maceration involves placing whole coffee fruits in sealed tanks filled with 
carbon dioxide, creating an oxygen-free environment (Jesus 2020). This method 
allows intracellular fermentation within the fruits, breaking down sugars and other 
compounds (Miyatake 2006). The process typically leads to complex and often fruity 
flavor profiles with enhanced acidity, body, and aromatic characteristics. 
264 ♦ Ildi Revi

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 265
Coffees processed this way can be highly prized in the specialty coffee market for their 
distinctive and memorable flavors, often commanding premium prices due to their 
unique sensory attributes (Jesus 2020). In my experience, producers need to have buyers 
lined up, agreeing to buy this type of coffee before they invest in the equipment and 
supplies they need to produce it. I have seen some cases where importers have unsold 
stock of these coffees in the warehouses. I cannot find any literature on the health 
implications of this type of coffee aging versus other types, but I wonder about it.
CONSIDERATIONS AND CHALLENGES
Carbonic maceration enhances coffee’s flavor and market appeal, but it presents 
challenges. This process requires expert control of CO2 levels, temperature, and 
fermentation time (Saia 2020). Any mistake can lead to off-flavors or spoilage 
(Miyatake 2006). It also demands significant investment in specialized equipment 
like sealed tanks and CO2 supplies. Consistency across batches necessitates careful 
monitoring and adjustments (Weller 2007). Safety protocols are also essential to 
manage risks in sealed environments and with carbon dioxide. Despite these hurdles, 
carbonic maceration offers exciting new flavors in coffee (Jesus 2020).
A 2024 study by Grzelczyk et al. examined how different maceration methods and food 
flavor additions affect coffee’s antioxidant activity, and also their caffeine, phenolic, and 
organic acid content. Coffee beans macerated in melon and apple pulp saw significant 
increases in polyphenols and antioxidant activity. This suggests potential in inhibiting 
α-amylase, which could help prevent type II diabetes. The study also revealed that 
fruit pulp maceration boosted hydroxycinnamic acids (5-CQA and 3,5-diCQA) and 
their esters, leading to high α-amylase inhibition and antioxidant potential. 
In contrast, adding food flavors reduced beneficial CGA and increased harmful 
compounds like acrylamide and 5-HMF, lowering antioxidant potential. These flavors 
also showed low enzyme inhibition activity, correlating with reduced CGA. Artificial 
flavors contain toxic substances, further diminishing antioxidant activity. Coffee 
macerated in fruit pulp displayed the highest health benefits, including antioxidant 
activity and potential diabetes prevention. Maceration in wine also increased 
polyphenols, but was less effective than in fruit. Roasted coffee beans macerated 

266 ♦ Ildi Revi
in fruit had higher organic acid content, except for apple pulp, which decreased it 
(Grzelczyk 2024).
While this is promising for holistic coffee, allergen and intolerance issues must be 
considered. People can be allergic to fruits and pollen, leading to symptoms like 
itching, swelling, or digestive issues. Fruit intolerance differs from an allergy; it involves 
the digestive system’s inability to process certain fruit components, causing nausea, 
bloating, and diarrhea. Grzelczyk’s study suggests a beneficial effect of fruit pulps on 
antioxidants and α-amylase-inhibiting activity in green beans. Future research should 
focus on optimizing maceration conditions using food industry waste to enhance 
coffee’s taste and healthfulness. Future research should also include in vivo studies 
about allergens. Do people involved in coffee production with allergies to those 
fruits have reactions to handling green coffee? What about workers at the wet mill or 
dry mill? Does roasting eliminate the allergen? These and other questions need to be 
answered before they are used for circular health coffees.
IMPLEMENTING ANAEROBIC FERMENTATION
At its core, anaerobic fermentation requires an airtight container with a mechanism 
to exclude oxygen. Not only is it important to select appropriate container materials 
that prevent oxygen ingress and allow CO2 release, but food safety and the breakdown 
of materials must be considered since the process is acidic. Stainless steel and food-
grade plastic are the best materials for fermentation tanks. Stainless steel is preferred 
due to its durability, non-reactive nature, and ease of cleaning, which minimizes the 
risk of contamination. Food-grade plastic is also viable, provided it is BPA-free and 
specifically designed for food contact to ensure no harmful chemicals leach into the 
product. Materials that must not be used include galvanized steel and low-quality 
plastics, which can corrode or release contaminants into the system, compromising 
food safety. 
The infrastructure required for efficient anaerobic processes can vary significantly, 
from basic manual methods to more advanced, automated systems that minimize 
labor. Monitoring and controlling variables like temperature, pH, and sugar content 
are essential for ensuring high-quality and consistent results. Such requirements mean 
considerable investment, which can be a barrier for many producers. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 267
Tanks that can be used for anaerobic fermentation.
RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES ASSOCIATED 
WITH ANAEROBIC FERMENTATION
While the potential rewards of anaerobic fermentation are high, so are the risks. 
The process is time-intensive, and the extended timeframe can increase operational 
expenses, requiring producers to secure robust financial support. The complexity 
of anaerobic processes has an associated risk of defects, particularly for those still 
mastering the technique. Producers may ruin any number of batches of green coffee 
before getting to the point of consistency and accuracy.
On the other hand, we can develop innovation for health using specific microorganisms 
that can impact health. As we learn how certain bacteria, for example, are healthy for 
our bodies and neutralize mycotoxins in our guts, it might be possible to ferment coffee 
with similar bacteria to reduce any mycotoxin load in green coffee before it is dried. 
At Purity, we have begun a project with a university and one of our producers to 
explore this possibility. These bacteria are food-safe for people, thus safer for employees 
at the farm. 
While innovative, anaerobic fermentation and carbonic maceration in coffee 
processing pose environmental sustainability challenges primarily due to high water 
usage and wastewater production. Anaerobic fermentation can strain local water 
resources, especially in drought-prone regions, and generate wastewater that, if 
untreated, can pollute water bodies with organic materials (De Bruyn 2017). Similarly, 

268 ♦ Ildi Revi
carbonic maceration involves fermenting coffee fruits in an anaerobic environment. It 
requires careful management of CO2 emissions and energy consumption to maintain 
optimal fermentation conditions, which can amplify the carbon footprint if not 
managed sustainably (Saia 2020). Both processes necessitate efficient water and waste 
management practices to mitigate environmental impacts.
The cost and sustainability issues of anaerobic fermentation and carbonic maceration 
in coffee still face growing questions, particularly concerning market demand and 
economic viability for producers amid rising production costs and climatic challenges. 
As more farmers adopt this method, its impact on the coffee industry’s supply-demand 
dynamics remains uncertain.
AEROBIC FERMENTATION
While anaerobic fermentation and carbonic maceration have drawn recent attention 
for their novelty and flavor impact, aerobic fermentation remains one of the most 
widely used and historically important processing methods in coffee. It plays a central 
role in traditional washed coffee processing, where the mucilage layer is broken down 
by enzymatic and microbial activity in the presence of oxygen.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 269
In aerobic fermentation, coffee beans (in parchment) are submerged in tanks or 
exposed to air in shallow troughs, where naturally occurring bacteria and yeasts break 
down mucilage over 12–72 hours, depending on temperature and altitude. The process 
is highly sensitive to microbial balance, pH, and cleanliness. When well-managed, 
aerobic fermentation yields coffees with bright acidity, clean cup profiles, and strong 
clarity of origin.
New research supports the notion that specific microbial populations during 
aerobic fermentation can positively influence flavor and safety. A 2023 study using 
metagenomic sequencing identified key microbial players, such as Leuconostoc, 
Lactobacillus, and Saccharomyces, that contribute to desirable flavor precursors, while 
also highlighting how excessive fermentation can lead to spoilage or increased biogenic 
amines (Zhao 2023). Additionally, aerobic fermentation can reduce bitterness by 
breaking down glycosides and increasing certain volatile aromatic compounds (Bengoa 
2023).
That said, aerobic fermentation carries food safety risks if mismanaged, particularly 
in warm climates. Fermentation tanks must be cleaned between batches, and water 
used in the process should be free from contamination. However, the potential for 
mycotoxin contamination during subsequent drying still exists if the coffee is not 
evenly and promptly dried afterward.
Aerobic fermentation remains a foundational method for producers aiming to create 
clean, health-forward coffee, especially when combined with good agricultural 
practices, thorough washing, and controlled drying environments.
NATURAL PROCESSING: (also called Dry Processing, Unwashed, 
Dry Method, Dried-in-Cherry, Sun-dried, Cherry-dried)
The natural coffee processing method, known as the dry process, involves drying whole 
coffee cherries with intact fruit. This method produces coffee with a heavier body, 
more fruit-forward flavors, and greater perceived sweetness than washed coffee. The 
process begins with meticulously harvesting and sorting the coffee fruit to eliminate 
any defective or unripe fruit. Sometimes, a small amount of water is used for flotation 
to sort the denser cherries, which are likely higher quality. The harvested cherries 

270 ♦ Ildi Revi
are then thinly spread under the sun on drying beds, patios, or tarps, where they are 
consistently turned to ensure even drying and mitigate fermentation risks.
Sun-drying on expansive patios or drying racks is the drying method that requires 
proper techniques of turning the coffee to avoid mold growth and contamination, 
targeting a moisture content of about 11-12 percent. As the coffee dries, it looks like 
big raisins, but unlike raisins, the outside skin of the coffee becomes a hard shell. When 
it comes time to get the green coffee out, the skin and parchment are stuck together 
and come off during hulling. 
Mechanical hulling removes the stiff, tough dried fruit and parchment, revealing green 
coffee beans. These beans are then further sorted and graded based on size, weight, 
and quality, before being bagged for shipping, where 10-12 percent moisture is the 
industry standard (water activity is discussed in the roasting chapter).
Freshly harvested coffee is graded, cleaned, and ready for natural (dry) processing.
The natural process stands out for its minimal water usage, a major advantage in 
regions with water scarcity. Unlike the washed method, which requires large amounts 
of water for pulping and washing, the dry process relies solely on sunlight for drying, 
thus conserving water and reducing environmental impact. This method is labor-
intensive, requiring considerable manual labor for harvesting, sorting, and turning 
the cherries during drying. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 271
From a food safety perspective, the natural process poses risks if not managed correctly. 
Large-scale natural processed industrial coffee is being dried on concrete patios.
Improper drying practices can lead to mycotoxin formation, and poor hygiene during 
drying and handling can cause contamination. Consumers usually face minimal risks 
if processing is well-controlled, but the presence of mycotoxins due to inadequate 
drying remains a concern.
The natural process’s environmental impact is relatively low due to its minimal water 
usage and the natural energy input from sun drying. Yet, waste management of 
discarded pulp and husks is necessary to prevent environmental contamination. The 
waste makes excellent feedstock for biochar, as we will discuss in a moment. Sun drying 
reduces energy consumption compared to mechanical drying methods, lowering the 
carbon footprint.
Financially, the natural process is attractive due to its lower initial investment than wet 
processing methods. It also requires less complex infrastructure. On the other hand, 
the high labor costs associated with the process’s manual nature must be considered. 

272 ♦ Ildi Revi
Additionally, there is a great risk of financial losses if the process is not carefully 
managed, as improper drying or storage can lead to quality loss and economic setbacks.
Despite the challenges, the natural process sometimes delivers fruity coffee profiles or 
mellow, sweet acidity. Its suitability for water-scarce regions and lower environmental 
impact make it a sustainable choice. At the same time, the labor-intensive process 
demands a commitment to quality control to avoid health and safety risks.
Healthy, clean, well-sorted natural processed coffee being 
dried hygienically with no mold growth.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 273
Coffee cherries are non-climacteric fruits, meaning they do not ripen after being 
picked and must be processed quickly to avoid spoilage. If left too long, they 
begin to ferment and rot, affecting the quality of the coffee beans inside.
Coffee cherries that are not picked well, not dried properly, and 
become moldy can lead to mycotoxin contamination. 

274 ♦ Ildi Revi
SEMI-WASHED, PULPED NATURAL, HONEY COFFEE: 
There are numerous other ways of referring to this type of 
processing that is neither washed nor dry-process.
The pulped natural or semi-washed coffee processing method often called the honey 
process (no actual honey is used, rather the sticky, pectin-coated parchment resembles 
and even smells like honey), balances the attributes of washed and natural processes, 
resulting in a coffee profile intended to be a harmonious blend of body, perceived 
sweetness, and clarity. The fruits are pulped, which involves removing their outer skin 
while leaving varying amounts of mucilage, giving the process its name. Depending on 
the amount of mucilage left on the beans, the coffee is categorized into white honey 
(minimal mucilage), yellow honey, red honey, or black honey (maximum mucilage). 
The method requires specialized equipment because, without water, the sugars and 
mucilage can get extremely sticky and make the coffee clump. 
(Left to right) Black honey, red honey, yellow honey.
After pulping, the beans are spread out to dry under the sun on raised beds, patios, 
or tarps. Regular turning of the beans is critical during this drying phase to prevent 
uneven drying, unwanted fermentation, and mold growth, again targeting a moisture 
content of about 11-12 percent. Once dried, the beans undergo milling to remove 
any remaining parchment and mucilage residues. They are then sorted and graded 
based on size, weight, and quality.
Food safety and health considerations are paramount in the honey process. Given 
the extended drying time, there is an increased risk of fermentation and microbial 
contamination if drying is not managed correctly. This makes diligent drying practices 
and strict hygiene standards essential to prevent food safety issues, such as mycotoxin 

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦275
formation. Health risks are minimal for consumers if the drying process is well-
controlled, but improper drying could lead to harmful mycotoxins.
Environmentally, the honey process is favorable due to its reduced water usage 
compared to fully washed processes. This makes it particularly suitable for regions 
where water scarcity is a concern. However, the process still generates pulp and 
mucilage waste, which requires proper disposal to avoid environmental contamination. 
Energy consumption is minimized as sun-drying is utilized instead of mechanical 
drying methods, reducing the carbon footprint. It may also use a small amount of 
water. 
 Just pulped “red honey” coffee. The red color is from the enzymatic 
browning of mucilage coating the parchment surrounding the seed.
The financial implications of the honey process are a mix of advantages and challenges. 
It involves a lower initial infrastructure investment than fully washed processing 
due to reduced water and simpler infrastructure requirements. Despite this, the 
manual nature of the drying process results in high labor costs. Additionally, the risk 
of financial loss is present if quality control measures during drying and storage are 

276 ♦ Ildi Revi
not rigorously maintained. Poor management can lead to quality degradation and 
economic setbacks.
Honey-processed coffee is intended to yield a rich, sweet coffee with varied flavor 
profiles based on the amount of mucilage left on the beans. This reflects both the 
method’s complex heritage and the meticulous care given by its producers. The process, 
while demanding, offers a unique product that can command higher market prices.
This photo shows hygienic, well-processed honey coffee that uses food safety 
precautions. The employee wears sterilized boots as he turns the coffee, and the 
delivery belt is sanitized between lots. The cover allows for good air circulation with 
additional warmth to speed the process while keeping it dry.
Honey-processed coffee is not easy for producers. It takes time and requires the use 
of special equipment to become a sustainable, replicable product for the market. 
Harvesting can be more complicated when targeting a certain ripeness. Recall that 
slightly less ripe fruits tend to be higher in CGA, so if you are creating a coffee 
for health purposes, this may challenge using the honey-processed method due to 
equipment calibration. Also, if the honey process is carefully controlled, consumers 
face minimal health issues, although improper drying could result in mold and 
mycotoxins, of course.
CGA undergoes changes during coffee-fruit processing, whether washed, pulped 
natural or dry processed. The phenolic compounds, primarily tannins, are in the coffee 
fruit’s skin and pulp, but these compounds decrease during washing, fermentation 
and drying (Barcelos 2001). Dried coffee fruit skin, cascara, and even coffee pulp are 
increasingly used as novelty food products and ingredients. The coffee plant’s leaves 
also have CGA, although in lesser amounts, but enough to consider popularizing 
coffee leaf tea. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 277
Honey-processed coffee is dried on a covered screened patio with good airflow. 
The coffee must be turned continually to prevent mold growth.
OTHER APPROACHES TO PROCESSING CLASSIFICATION 
The influence of processing methods on nutrients in coffee is an active area of research. 
Coffees can also be categorized based on how drying methods impact their flavor. 
These categories include fruit-dried, pulp-dried, parchment-dried, and seed-dried 
coffees, each offering distinct sensory experiences. This approach to classifying coffee 
processing aligns flavor differences with drying practices rather than fruit removal 
techniques.
FRUIT-DRIED COFFEES
Fruit-dried coffees are left to dry with the skin intact. This category includes various 
methods such as coffees dried partially or entirely on the coffee plant, or those picked 
ripe and dried on patios or screens, discussed earlier as dry process or natural. 

278 ♦ Ildi Revi
Fruit drying in a covered area on screens in African beds at Hacienda La Pradera, Colombia.
PULP-DRIED COFFEES
In pulp-dried coffees, the beans are dried with their skin removed but with the pulp 
remaining, discussed earlier as honey process or pulped natural. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 279
PARCHMENT-DRIED COFFEES
Parchment-dried coffees have their skin and pulp removed, leaving only the parchment 
intact during the drying process, discussed earlier as washed or wet process coffees. 
Elevated, uncovered/open-air African drying beds in Zimbabwe, optimizing airflow. Coffee still 
needs to be turned and raked on the beds for drying uniformly, which helps prevent mold growth.
Parchment-dried coffee.

280 ♦ Ildi Revi
SEED-DRIED COFFEES
In terms of processing, this is called “wet-hulled” and 
is primarily associated with Indonesia, particularly 
in regions like Sumatra (photo, right) and Sulawesi. 
Seed-dried coffees experience complete removal of 
skin, pulp, and parchment before the final drying 
phase, where only the silverskin (perisperm) remains. 
Known in Indonesia as giling basah or ‘wet-hulled’ 
coffees, these beans are commonly said to have 
muted acidity and earthy or woody flavors.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 281
BIODIGESTERS: COFFEE 
INDUSTRY ADVANCES
Biodigesters are innovative systems that convert 
organic waste into valuable resources through 
anaerobic digestion. These systems can play 
a pivotal role in enhancing sustainability 
and efficiency on coffee farms. A biodigester 
breaks down organic materials such as coffee 
pulp, husks, and other farm waste, producing 
biogas (a renewable energy source) and 
nutrient-rich slurry, often called digestate. The 
generated biogas can power farm machinery 
and heat facilities or even generate electricity, 
significantly reducing reliance on external 
energy sources and lowering operational costs.
The use of a biodigester on a coffee farm 
provides multiple benefits, both economically 
and environmentally. By converting waste into 
biogas, farmers can save money on energy bills 
and reduce the need for fossil fuels, lowering 
their carbon footprint. The digestate produced 
as a byproduct of digesters is a highly effective 
organic fertilizer rich in nutrients essential 
for plant growth. They reduce the need for 
chemical fertilizers, promote healthier soil and 
plants, and contribute to sustainable farming 
practices. Furthermore, biodigesters help in 
waste management, minimizing the risk of 
environmental pollution from decomposing 
organic waste. By integrating biodigesters into 
their operations, coffee farms can achieve greater 
sustainability, improve resource efficiency, and 
reduce waste while promoting a more eco-
friendly approach to coffee production.
 Biodigester on Selva Negra (La 
Hammonia) coffee farm in 
Nicaragua above and page 308.

282
5.5 The Role of Coffee Dry Mills, 
Exporters and Importers
The global coffee industry is a tightly woven network, with each stage playing a critical 
role in shaping the quality and safety of the final cup. To visualize these connections, 
the Specialty Coffee Association (SCA) released the Coffee Systems Map in June 2020 
as part of its Price Crisis Response Initiative. Originally designed to map relationships 
among key players in the specialty coffee sector, the map also highlights opportunities 
to improve health, safety, and environmental standards across the supply chain.
By showing how “actors” like dry mills, importers, and roasters interact, the map 
underscores the impact of their decisions. Dry mills, for example, can reduce health 
risks by improving ventilation and providing PPE. Importers can prioritize farms 
with clean, sustainable practices, protecting both workers and consumers. Roasters 
can invest in low-emission technologies to minimize harm and enhance product 
integrity. With tools like this, the industry has a clear blueprint for promoting food 
safety, sustainability, and human well-being, one decision at a time.
Similarly, the article “Sustainable Coffee: A Review of the Diverse Initiatives and 
Governance Dimensions of Global Coffee Supply Chains,” published by Dale R. 
Wright et al. in 2024, provides a comprehensive review of sustainability initiatives 
and governance mechanisms within the coffee sector. This review highlights how these 
initiatives impact various stages of the coffee supply chain, including farming, dry 
milling, importing, and roasting, with a particular focus on health and environmental 
sustainability. Combining both of those resources, along with other research, let’s look 
at actors in the industry that are often overlooked. 
Coffee dry mills, exporters, and importers usually operate behind the scenes and don’t 
receive as much attention in coffee company marketing. While the spotlight typically 
shines on farmers and roasters, these key players still make significant contributions 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 283
to the industry. They are indispensable in delivering our coffee by confirming quality, 
facilitating logistics, and bridging the gap between producers and markets. Their 
expertise and efforts are vital in maintaining coffee’s seamless movement and quality 
from farm to cup, underscoring their essential, albeit less visible, presence in the coffee 
supply chain.
DRY MILLS, EXPORTERS AND CHC
In many cases, coffee exporters combine with dry mills, which are facilities where coffee 
cherries are processed to remove the outer fruit and dry the beans. This integration 
allows for better control over the quality of the beans, as the exporter can oversee 
the entire process from drying to export, ensuring that the coffee meets the desired 
standards before it reaches the global market. Coffee exporters play a critical role by 
purchasing coffee beans from farmers and cooperatives, processing them to meet 
international standards, and then selling them to importers and roasters worldwide. 
They ensure the quality and consistency of the beans, handle logistics, and navigate 
the complex regulations of international trade. 
Dry milling is part of the post-harvest processing of coffee beans. Sometimes, coffee 
is received in parchment and hulled at a coffee dry mill if the producer does not have 
a huller on the farm. Green beans first go through sorting and grading by size and 
density. 
The beans are then evaluated for quality through cupping, or liquoring. The process 
includes blending different lots, bagging the beans, and determining pricing. 
Negotiations occur based on quality and quantity, while meticulous record-keeping 
logs the received coffee versus the milled output into various grades. 
GRADING FOR BEAN SIZE 
Green coffee is usually sorted by size and density using sorting screens with holes or 
slats as part of the dry milling process. Large machines with long, wide, perforated 
metal panels stacked on a slight downward slope vibrate as coffee enters from a large 
hopper above, and gravity does the rest of the work. The holes retain beans over a 

284 ♦ Ildi Revi
certain size, allowing smaller beans to pass. Nearly all coffee for export is graded to 
exclude the largest and smallest beans, broken beans and other particles. Strictly 
speaking, grade indicators are used to describe the size of the bean and are commonly 
expressed in 1/64 of an inch. For example, beans of grade 18 will pass through a 
screen with hole diameters of 18/64 but are retained by screen 16/17 by International 
Coffee Organization and FAO standards. In Colombia, the Federación Nacional de 
Cafeteros uses the screen number on which the coffee is retained for the size. Screen 
sizes are expressed as numbers (e.g., screen 16), by letters (like AA), or by colloquial 
names (supremo).
The International Coffee Organization uses this:

Workers at a dry mill hand sorting green coffee.

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦285
To complicate things, each country or region has colloquial grading terms:
 Metal panels from a large dry mill. Some of these may be several meters long.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 287
Density
At the coffee dry mill, after sorting 
by bean size, coffees of the same 
size are density-graded. The 
machine responsible for this, 
called an Oliver Table, is an 
inclined, finely corrugated, and 
perforated metal table (photo on 
the following page). Air is blown 
through the perforations to 
“fluidize” the bean mass. It seems 
counterintuitive, but the heavier 
beans stay at the top of the slightly 
tilted plane. Lighter beans and 
most foreign matter fall/blow to 
the lower edge of the table. The 
different densities are then 
sectioned off into containers designated for their grade.
Some origins include density in their 
classifications as “hard beans” (HB), “soft 
beans” (SB), with “strictly hard beans” (SHB) 
and “strictly soft beans” (SSB) as opposite ends 
of the density scale, which can vary from country 
to country. These densities are associated with 
the elevations at which coffee is grown, with 
lower-grown coffees being less dense and higher-
grown coffees being denser. Several other factors 
influence density, such as nutrition, shade, 
climate, sunlight, latitude, variety, etc. Note 
this density in your data logging system. If you 
keep a large amount of one type of green coffee 
in storage, consider checking the density every 
(Below) Close-up of airflow holes on an Oliver 
table with some green coffee and dust left behind. 
This is an example of how dry mills can impact 
the health of a coffee by cleaning equipment 
before running batches of circular health coffee 
through potential contaminants that have been 
left behind. (Above) A dry mill employee showed 
where to adjust the airflow on the Oliver table.

288 ♦ Ildi Revi
three to four months or if the coffee behaves differently than your roast curve says it 
should.
A dry mill’s Oliver tables. Note the wooden guides that channel different 
densities to the chute that leads to the tubes that send the coffees of similar 
densities to holding containers in another part of the facility.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 289
 
 
 
(Top) Green coffee in 
parchment before hulling.

a farm. The huller removes 
the parchment (and

green coffee. Dry milling

and contaminants, an 
area that millers and 
exporters can impact.

on large farms or as regional shared 
facilities or co-ops due to the high cost and
