290 ♦ Ildi Revi
The beans are then stored in a warehouse until they are loaded into containers for 
transport and shipping. These processes ensure that the beans meet quality standards 
and are prepared for roasting, guaranteeing a premium product for consumers. 
Collaboration with coffee cooperatives enables dry mills to support small-scale farmers 
in pooling resources and accessing larger markets. They employ mechanical screens 
for impurity removal and optical sorters for detecting and separating defective beans.
IMPROVING HEALTH, ENVIRONMENTAL, 
AND FOOD SAFETY ASPECTS
Dry mills present several health and environmental challenges that should be addressed 
to ensure sustainable coffee production. Worker exposure to coffee dust and crop 
chemicals can lead to respiratory issues, highlighting the need for proper ventilation, 
protective equipment, and adherence to organic certification standards. Improper 
storage conditions (too much moisture, not enough airflow, etc.) may also promote 
mold growth on beans, negatively impacting both flavor and health.
The milling process generates significant waste, including husks and dust, which can 
contribute to environmental pollution if not properly managed. Outdated machinery 
in dry mills can increase energy consumption and emissions, which holistic coffee 
production seeks to avoid.
Implementing advanced ventilation systems and protective gear can reduce worker 
exposure to coffee dust. Adopting waste management strategies that utilize by-products 
like coffee husks for composting or energy generation can enhance environmental 
management. Regular audits and inspections ensure compliance with safety standards. 
Training employees on hygiene practices can mitigate contamination risks, and 
blockchain technology can enhance accountability in the supply chain. 
Multi-stakeholder collaborations and public-private partnerships are essential to scale 
sustainability across the supply chain. Monitoring and reporting mechanisms ensure 
compliance with sustainability standards and foster continuous improvement. By 
aligning the goals and actions of farmers, mills, importers, and roasters with sustainable 
practices, the industry can mitigate its environmental impact, improve social equity, 
and ensure economic viability for all stakeholders.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 291
Moreover, the global transportation of coffee contributes to carbon emissions, which 
is a consideration and point of attention for CHC. Addressing these issues requires a 
concerted effort to implement better practices and technologies throughout the coffee 
supply chain, ensuring health and environmental sustainability.
Mechanical bean sorting at the dry mill.
ACTIONS OF COFFEE IMPORTERS FOR CHC
Importers act as intermediaries between producers and roasters, ensuring bean quality, 
handling logistics, and providing financing and market access for growers. They advise 
producers about market demands and quality specifications, strengthening overall 
coffee quality. Exporters bridge the gap between producers and international buyers, 
supporting farmers in navigating export regulations and meeting global standards.
Importers and exporters can also collaborate with the principles of CHC, supporting 
and observing organic farming practices and maintaining their own certifications. 
They can establish and communicate the sourcing policies around environmental 
sustainability as part of the circular system.

292 ♦ Ildi Revi
INDUSTRY FOOD SAFETY AND CHC STANDARDS
Ensuring food safety in coffee production involves adhering to Good Agricultural 
Practices (GAP) and Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) to prevent contamination 
from biological, chemical, and physical hazards. Mycotoxins can contaminate beans 
if stored improperly. Extensive cleaning and proper ventilation are essential during 
processing. Importers must be vigilant about chemical contamination due to pesticide 
residues, requiring rigorous testing to ensure compliance with food safety standards. 
Exporters must ensure that coffee adheres to international food safety standards.
In the U.S. the Foreign Supplier Verification Program (FSVP) mandates that coffee 
importers ensure coffee farms, cooperatives, and mills produce and process coffee 
safely, providing public health protection equivalent to U.S. standards. 
Importers must comply with the FSVP for green coffee by adhering to supply-chain 
program requirements and implementing preventive controls for identified hazards. 
Additionally, they must confirm that their customers know it is their responsibility to 
apply appropriate preventive controls since green coffee is a raw agricultural product. 
This communication maintains the safety and quality of the coffee throughout the 
supply chain.
Importer responsibility also includes preventing allergen cross-contact and ensuring 
the coffee is not adulterated or misbranded.
Examples of Risks
•	
Allergen Cross-Contact: Farms producing both coffee and allergens (e.g., 
peanuts, tree nuts) pose a risk.
•	
Equipment Maintenance: Poorly maintained mills may introduce physical 
contaminants (e.g., metal parts, oil).
•	
Chemical Storage: Improper storage of chemicals at mills can lead to 
contamination.
As part of their role in the safety and quality of coffee, importers are required by the 
U.S. food safety laws to conduct a thorough hazard analysis across their entire supply 
chain. This involves identifying potential hazards at various stages, including farms, 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 293
mills, warehouses, and transportation. Importers can better understand vulnerabilities 
by pinpointing these risks, such as chemical contamination or poor employee hygiene.
Once hazards are identified, a detailed risk evaluation must be performed. This 
assessment considers both the nature of the hazards and the performance of suppliers, 
including factors like water quality and chemical storage practices. Based on this 
evaluation, importers can approve suppliers and establish appropriate verification 
activities to monitor and mitigate risks. 
Additionally, it is essential to develop corrective action plans to address any issues, 
such as discovering allergens in coffee samples. These written food safety plans ensure 
that any problems are swiftly and effectively managed, maintaining the integrity and 
safety of the coffee supply.
These procedures ensure they import coffee only from approved suppliers, and CHC 
coffee standards can be integrated to monitor simultaneously. Procedures must also 
cover temporary purchases from unapproved suppliers and outline verification 
activities for new or replacement suppliers. Importers must be able to show written 
documentation that good agricultural practices (covered later in this book) were used 
on each farm that produced the coffee they imported. 
Although the activities described above are required by law, they support CHC. 
Importers have additional ways to contribute meaningfully to CHC, and there are vast 
opportunities for business in this category of coffee, such as vetting CHC farms for 
roasting clients (even doing green coffee laboratory tests, so a COA is readily available) 
and facilitating communication between roasters and producers regarding CHC 
standards. This communication is vital because the roasters interact with consumers, 
who may have health needs and questions. The more engaged an importer is with the 
others in the circle, the stronger the category will become.

294
5.6 Coffee Farming:  
Global Concerns
Coffee growers are facing several climate-related challenges that can significantly 
impact the quality and quantity of coffee production. Global coffee production is 
increasingly vulnerable to challenges and difficulties due to weather-related hazards 
linked to large-scale climate patterns like the El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO). 
Research shows a rise in climate risks and compound events in major coffee-producing 
regions from 1980 to 2020, shifting from cold to hot conditions. 
The past decade has seen a spike in these threats, with El Niño conditions causing 
drier, hotter weather affecting coffee yields. Another tropical disturbance pattern, the 
Madden-Julian Oscillation (MJO), also influences coffee-related climate areas. Its 
activity correlates with an increase in cold/wet or a decrease in hot/dry hazards. The 
MJO influences the ENSO cycle but does not cause it. Rather, it can contribute to 
the speed of development and intensity of El Niño and La Niña events. With ongoing 
climate change, coffee production is expected to face continuous systemic challenges 
from these compounded climate events.
Climate change may impact the healthiness of coffee produced in certain regions. Rising 
temperatures may produce mycotoxins, like aflatoxins, especially in mycotoxigenic 
fungi such as Aspergillus flavus, which thrive in warmer conditions. Arabica and 
Robusta coffee plants have limited capacity to adapt to new locations. As a result, they 
may be cultivated in less ideal climates, heightening plant stress and making them 
more vulnerable to fungal infections and mycotoxin contamination.
The science behind changing climates is strong. World Coffee Research was founded 
to examine this and has made strides in studying cultivars in particular. Scientists 
have considered these issues, which together paint a picture indicating we need to 
take action to secure Arabica coffee, particularly for the future. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 295
•
Temperature Increases: Optimal conditions for growing coffee, especially
Arabica coffee, are within a temperature range of 64°-70°F (18°C-21°C).
With global temperatures rising, these conditions are becoming harder to
maintain, leading to reduced quality and yields. Increased temperatures can
accelerate the ripening process of coffee fruits, often resulting in smaller, less
desirable beans and reduced cup quality (Barahona 2017). Regarding health
impacts, within this range, coffee plants can synthesize ample amounts of
CGA and other beneficial compounds:
■
Elevated temperatures beyond the optimal range can lead to
metabolic stress in coffee plants. Studies suggest that as temperatures
rise, the activity of enzymes critical for CGA synthesis may be
impaired, leading to lower concentrations of CGA in coffee beans
(DaMatta 2004). Higher temperatures can also accelerate ripening,
resulting in premature bean development, which might adversely
affect levels of CGA and other compounds.
■
Temperatures below the optimal range can slow down metabolic
processes, thereby reducing the synthesis of CGA. Prolonged
exposure to low temperatures can also damage the tissues of coffee
plants, negatively impacting the quality and concentration of these
acids.
•
Water Stress: Changes in rainfall patterns and increased droughts can lead
to water stress, which can negatively affect the taste, aroma, and even dietary
quality of coffee. Prolonged drought periods can reduce bean size and lead to
undesirable characteristics in the final product, such as increased bitterness
(DaMatta 2004).
•
Pests and Diseases: Warmer climates can bolster coffee plant pests and
diseases previously controlled naturally by cooler temperatures. For
example, the coffee berry borer (Hypothenemus hampei) thrives in warmer
conditions, leading to frequent infestations. Diseases such as coffee leaf rust
(Hemileia vastatrix) have also become more prevalent, reducing yields and
increasing management costs (Jaramillo 2011). Regarding CGA specifically:

296 ♦ Ildi Revi
■
CGA exhibit antimicrobial properties that help coffee plants
resist fungal and bacterial infections (Mendonça 2018). Higher
concentrations of CGA in coffee leaves and beans can inhibit the
growth of pathogens like coffee leaf rust (Hemileia vastatrix), so if
CGA levels are compromised, it adversely affects the plant’s ability
to defend itself.
■
CGA can deter pests such as the coffee borer beetle (Hypothenemus
hampei). These phenolic compounds can reduce the palatability of
coffee plants to pests, consequently protecting them from damage
(Jaramillo 2011).
•
Reduced Growing Areas: As discussed in the introduction, international
research suggests that the areas suitable for coffee cultivation could be
reduced by up to 50 percent due to climate change, which poses risks to the
quality of coffee and to farmers’ livelihoods. This is particularly concerning
for countries that rely heavily on coffee exports for their economies (Bunn
2015).
•
Impact on Quality and Health: Factors such as increased temperatures,
carbon dioxide levels, and water stress can reduce the overall quality of
coffee, affecting its flavor and aroma. High temperatures can cause
physiological stress to coffee plants, leading to suboptimal development of
flavor compounds in the beans (Läderach 2010). Some flavor compounds,
like diterpenes, affect the healthfulness of coffee, along with reduced taste
aspects, although this is another area we need more research. More targeted
research is also needed regarding the effects of mycotoxins on coffee under
various climate change scenarios. Currently, we can only generalize, look
at trends and pay attention to anecdotes from those in vulnerable zones.
Nevertheless, all indications point to climate change having a profoundly
negative impact on global coffee production, resulting in both a reduction
of suitable growing areas and an increase in mycotoxin contamination.
•
Extreme Weather Events: Increasing frequency and strength of storms
can damage coffee trees and disrupt production cycles. Coffee plants may
alter CGA synthesis as a protective mechanism under environmental stress

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 297
conditions, such as drought or high temperatures. This modulation of CGA 
levels can help acclimate the plant to stressors, albeit at the cost of reduced 
overall CGA content (Barahona 2017).
Farmers and the rest of the coffee industry are working on strategies to combat these 
effects, such as shifting cultivation to higher altitudes where temperatures are cooler 
and implementing sustainable farming practices to improve resilience against climate 
change. These efforts support the sustainability of the coffee industry and the millions 
of people involved worldwide. 
HEALTH AND SUSTAINABILITY IMPLICATIONS OF 
ORGANIC REGENERATIVE COFFEE FARMING 
The push toward organic farming practices has gained momentous traction, driven 
by a growing force of consumers seeking healthier, more sustainable options. 
Unfortunately, I have been able to find little research that directly addresses the 
health benefits of organic coffee farming specifically. This point is exactly why I have 
called for collaboration with others, because the more we can get together and research 
these things, the more we can change the world’s health through coffee and support 
thriving ecosystems. 
When I started in the coffee industry in 1998, organic coffee did not make sense since 
chemicals were washed or hulled off, and any residue was destroyed during roasting. 
Whether these chemicals end up in your morning cup of coffee is debatable. The 
general industry belief is still that the roasting process, which reaches temperatures 
above 450 degrees Fahrenheit (232ºC), likely burns off or neutralizes any residual 
chemicals. While it is improbable that these chemicals result in toxic levels in the 
coffee, comprehensive research to confirm this for each specific chemical is lacking. 
Few studies have documented pesticide residues in green coffee, and even fewer have 
examined residues post-roasting or in the brewed beverage. Regardless of whether 
these pesticides remain in coffee, their use has significant human health implications. 
Here is some of the current research I have found, though:
A study in 2022 by Merhi and others reviewed the health effects, detection, and 
mitigation of pesticides in green, roasted, and brewed coffee. They investigated the 

298 ♦ Ildi Revi
transfer of the pesticide dinotefuran through various coffee processing steps: washing, 
roasting, and brewing. Key findings showed:
• Washing: Reduced dinotefuran concentrations by 44.4-86.7 percent.
• Roasting: Further decreased concentrations by 62.2-100 percent.
• Brewing: Most effective, reducing residues by 92.9-100 percent.
The study confirms that following proper processing protocols can significantly reduce 
pesticide levels, ensuring safer coffee consumption. Still, it appears that roasting did 
not ALWAYS destroy 100 percent of this one pesticide.
Based on a review of the available information on organics and pesticides in coffee, 
there is significant uncertainty surrounding this topic, prompting consumers to be 
concerned about the chemicals used in coffee production. The US government does 
not set maximum residue limits (MRLs) for all pesticides used on coffee, leaving 
consumers vulnerable unless they opt for organic options.
The use of chemicals in farming continues to grow worldwide. The United Nations’ 
FAO monitors these statistics globally. According to the latest FAO data, in 2022, 
total pesticide use in agriculture reached 3.70 million tonnes, marking a 4 percent 
increase from 2021 and a doubling since 1990. China, Brazil, and the United States 
remain the top three countries for total pesticide use. While the US has maintained 
relatively stable pesticide use over the past twenty years, China has increased its use 
by over 200 percent since 1991, and Brazil has increased it by nearly 800 percent 
since 1990. Notably, Brazil has applied this amount of pesticides on roughly the same 
agricultural land area, whereas China has expanded its permanent cropland area by 
over 200 percent in that period. This means Brazil has intensified pesticide use per 
acre, with crops, including coffee, now grown with almost eight times more pesticides 
than a few decades ago.
Regarding chemical fertilizers, the most recent FAO data from 2022 lists China, the 
United States, India, and Indonesia as the top users by total weight of NPK-type 
fertilizers. Organic certification helps protect against chemical fertilizers, as synthetic 
fertilizers are not allowed in organic agriculture (FAO Pesticides Use and Trade 1990-
2022, and FAO Inorganic Fertilizers 2002-2022).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 299
Coffee-growing countries have varying laws and monitoring systems for pesticide use, 
which may differ significantly from those in importing countries. Local regulators 
typically address this gap by setting and monitoring maximum residue tolerances 
upon import. Many coffee-growing countries use pesticides banned in the US and 
Europe, regardless of whether individual farmers comply with local laws. There is 
ample evidence that coffee farmworkers use various pesticides and herbicides that 
harm the environment and the health of farmworkers and communities within the 
watershed and local ecosystem. 
A recent search of the Global MRL database identified 42 pesticides used in coffee 
(BCGobal 2024). But this list does not account for all globally banned chemicals that 
may still be present in the environment or used illegally. Several harmful pesticides lack 
established MRLs for coffee, raising concerns about whether governments are aware 
of their use in coffee production. Without MRLs, there are no set limits for pesticide 
residues, meaning they are not monitored, and the population are left unprotected.
Among the highly hazardous chemicals listed, Abamectin, Cypermethrin, Dichlorvos, 
Disulfoton, Endosulfan, Haloxyfop, Parathion-methyl, Permethrin, and Propiconazole 
do not have coffee MRLs, despite being documented in coffee production. This 
represents a significant safety gap.
Some of these chemicals are organophosphates, among the most widely used 
insecticides globally. They are also among the most acutely toxic, acting by deactivating 
an enzyme called cholinesterase, which is essential for healthy nerve function in 
humans, vertebrate animals, and insects. Others on this list are toxic to bees, possibly 
carcinogenic, or suspected endocrine disruptors. Despite the endocrine system’s 
critical role in metabolism, development, and hormonal balance, scientists and 
regulators still lack consensus on what qualifies as an endocrine-disrupting chemical 
(Koutouleas 2023). As a result, many of these substances remain “suspected,” with 
minimal regulatory oversight and limited consumer protection.
Research on chlorpyrifos, one of the organophosphates commonly used on coffee, has 
revealed more serious health risks. While chlorpyrifos is already known to be toxic to 
the nervous system, the study by Wang et al. (2021), mentioned in the diabetes section 
of this book, showed that it can also interfere with how the body burns calories, even 

300 ♦ Ildi Revi
at extremely low exposure levels. The researchers found that chlorpyrifos disrupts 
the function of brown adipose tissue, which is a type of fat that helps regulate body 
temperature by burning energy to produce heat. This process, called diet-induced 
thermogenesis, is especially important after eating and is controlled by a protein called 
UCP1 (uncoupling protein 1). UCP1 works like a switch inside the mitochondria 
of fat cells, allowing them to convert stored energy into heat instead of storing it as 
fat. Chlorpyrifos was shown to suppress UCP1, which effectively shut off this heat-
generating, calorie-burning mechanism. As a result, mice exposed to chlorpyrifos 
accumulated more fat, developed insulin resistance, and showed signs of fatty liver 
disease, even though they did not consume more calories than the control group. 
These findings suggest that chlorpyrifos may contribute to obesity by impairing the 
body’s natural ability to burn energy, which can lead to weight gain, insulin resistance, 
and fatty liver disease. 
Environmental data from Vietnam underscore the scale of the problem. Lam et al. 
(2023) reported chlorpyrifos levels as high as 954 µg/kg in coffee-growing soils, despite 
official bans. Their ecological risk assessment flagged several regions as high-risk, with 
contamination likely to persist for years, posing threats to both environmental and 
human health.
Similar concerns apply to glyphosate, a widely used herbicide in coffee production. In 
2024 Otaru et al. demonstrated a significant association between urinary glyphosate 
levels and metabolic syndrome, with the strongest effects seen at mid-level exposures 
in older adults and racial minorities. These findings raise alarms not only for coffee 
consumers but also for the farmworkers and rural communities who are chronically 
exposed to these chemicals.
The increased use of both chlorpyrifos and glyphosate over the past few decades seems 
to mirror the global rise in obesity and metabolic disease. Chlorpyrifos is not used 
only on coffee, but on a wide variety of crops, including grains, vegetables, fruits, 
legumes, and nuts. Although the U.S. banned its use on food crops in 2021, it is still 
widely used in many other countries. This raises the possibility that chronic exposure 
through multiple foods may be compounding these effects. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 301
Given these mounting concerns, there is an urgent need to critically assess and 
reform how chemicals are managed in global agriculture, particularly in crops like 
coffee, where regulatory oversight often lags behind scientific understanding. Many 
consumers remain unaware of the hidden risks embedded in their daily cup, even 
as research continues to link agricultural chemicals to adverse health and ecological 
outcomes. The proliferation of chemicals banned in one country but permitted or 
unregulated in another points to significant gaps in international governance, further 
complicated by differences in enforcement capacity and transparency across coffee-
producing regions.
This complex landscape has prompted renewed interest in alternative approaches that 
can protect farmworkers, consumers, and ecosystems alike. Organic and regenerative 
agricultural methods, which prohibit the use of most synthetic pesticides and fertilizers, 
are gaining traction as both a market-driven solution and a public health imperative. 
By emphasizing soil health, crop diversity, and integrated pest management, these 
systems aim to break the cycle of chemical dependence while supporting biodiversity 
and long-term farm viability. The studies below look at other foods, which can fuel 
the discussion on the value of organic regenerative coffee:
•
Worthington (2001) found that organic produce consistently contains
higher essential nutrients than conventionally grown counterparts, such
as vitamin C, and three minerals found in coffee: iron, magnesium, and
phosphorus. This research also highlights reduced levels of nitrates and
pesticide residues in organic crops.
•
Lagier et al. (2020) reviewed the correlation between organic food
consumption and health outcomes, revealing a lower incidence of allergies
and obesity in individuals consuming organic foods. We’ve already seen
evidence in this book about histamine in the MCAS section and above
about obesity.
•
Bradbury et al. (2018) primarily focused on cancer risk, but also mentioned
a correlation between organic food consumption and lower incidences of
chronic diseases, which often impact heart health. This doubles down on
the earlier discussion on coffee and its potential role in protecting against
cardiovascular diseases,

302 ♦ Ildi Revi
•	
Edwards et al. (2019) found that children who consume primarily organic 
foods show better cognitive development and fewer metabolic syndrome 
indicators. While this study focuses on children, its findings suggest that 
organic foods support better overall health, making a strong case for 
considering organic coffee as part of an overall lifestyle that promotes 
cognitive health across all ages.
All these studies (and more are coming out all the time) collectively highlight the 
health benefits of organic farming practices, particularly in nutrient content, pesticide 
reduction, chronic disease prevention, and overall better health outcomes. This is why 
CHC is rooted in organic and regenerative farming. It’s not just better for the planet, 
it’s better for our health too.
Organic certification, along with programs that require it, such as Smithsonian Bird 
Friendly and Demeter, remains the most reliable way to know that a coffee farm is 
being monitored and verified, and that no synthetic pesticides are being used, whether 
legal or illegal. Other certifications, including UTZ and Rainforest Alliance, also 
enforce strong standards around chemical use and environmental practices, and can 
be a solid alternative where organic certification is not feasible.
That said, there are a few special cases where farms are remote and follow strict 
organic practices based on cultural values or long-standing traditional methods. These 
producers may not have formal certification, but they grow with deep integrity. We 
visit and vet these farms ourselves. This is where CHC’s lab testing plays a critical 
role. It allows us to trust, but verify. It ensures we can include this small but powerful 
group of growers who meet the spirit and standards of CHC, even if they do not carry 
an official seal.
For holistic coffee principles, though, two main issues support organic production: 
(1) The health and safety of everyone involved in coffee production is important, 
regardless of their country of origin; and 
(2) Using crop chemicals over time destroys the microorganisms necessary for optimum 
plant nutrient uptake, negatively impacting the coffee’s health. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 303
Retaining shade trees and biodiversity helps in the management 
of pests and diseases (Van Emden 2022).
WHY “CERTIFIED ORGANIC” IS VALUABLE
Third-party verification, such as USDA Organic certification audited annually, is 
currently the most reliable way to confirm that coffee is truly organic throughout the 
supply chain. Simply choosing not to use pesticides or herbicides is not enough to 
qualify as certified organic, though it may reflect good intent. Certification requires 
full adherence to established organic standards and ensures that organic integrity is 
maintained from farm to final product, including storage, roasting, and packaging. 
Every step is verified by accredited agencies, ensuring transparency and accountability.

304 ♦ Ildi Revi
In the United States, all certified entities are listed in the USDA Organic Integrity 
Database, a public tool that allows consumers to confirm whether a farm, importer, 
roaster, or supplier is officially certified. Certification is required not just for farming, 
but for every step of the supply chain. Exporters, importers, storage facilities, roasters, 
and even retailers handling unpackaged organic products must be certified under the 
USDA’s Strengthening Organic Enforcement (SOE) rule, which took effect in March 
2024. Even if a retailer is exempt, they must still maintain records to demonstrate the 
organic integrity of the products they sell. Each year, inspectors verify that certified 
entities preserve the organic chain of custody through paperwork, site reviews, and 
physical checks.
Warehouse operators must show how they segregate and label organic versus 
conventional lots, maintain cleaning protocols, and document their procedures. 
Roasters must demonstrate practices such as purging machines before organic batches, 
using approved cleaning products, and scheduling separate roasting times or lines 
for conventional beans. All of these steps are audited and confirmed during annual 
inspections to uphold certified integrity. If any actor uses the USDA seal without 
certification or falsely labels products, they risk serious penalties and legal action.
Like any system, organic certification has its limitations. With CHC we also support a 
small but important group of producers who meet the spirit and standards of organic 
agriculture, but for various structural reasons, formal certification does not make sense 
for their operations. Certification can be bureaucratically demanding, especially for 
smallholder farmers in remote areas, where the administrative requirements are often 
out of scale with the size and nature of the farm. These are not organic by default. 
On the contrary, they follow truly intentional organic practices rooted in cultural 
values, deep generational knowledge, and systems of land stewardship that align 
closely with organic standards. They would never consider undermining that work by 
turning to synthetic inputs. We visit these farms directly, and this is where CHC’s lab 
testing comes in. It allows us to trust, but verify, and to include these growers without 
compromising on integrity.
It’s important to distinguish this group from others who intentionally sidestep 
certification simply because they do not want regulatory oversight. Some frame this 
decision as resistance to bureaucracy or corporate influence, but in many cases, they 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 305
may be unwilling to invest the time, money, or effort required for transparency. While 
these actors often present themselves as independent or principled, the result can be 
consumer confusion and a lack of accountability.
The system of monitoring the entire supply chain for traceability and accountability 
is the reason to buy certified organic. It goes far beyond being free of crop chemicals. 
Some people actively disparage the entire organic certification system, citing its flaws 
as proof that the whole model is broken. But the only alternative is to take someone’s 
word for it, which carries its own risks. Certification, while imperfect, was created 
to protect both farmers and consumers. It gives the public a way to verify claims, 
and it gives producers a way to prove they are doing the right thing. It is unfair, and 
sometimes implicitly biased, to assume that farmers in developing countries are 
less informed or less principled in their choices or behaviors. Many of the organic 
producers for CHC have chosen this path not for marketing purposes, but because it 
reflects their cultural values, their practical experience, and their hopes for the health 
of their families and communities.
Despite these complexities, certified organic remains a valuable and trustworthy 
standard. It gives consumers confidence that a product has met rigorous, internationally 
recognized criteria and has been verified at every link in the supply chain. While 
individual exceptions exist, the certified system remains the clearest way to ensure 
consistency, protect buyers from greenwashing, and honor producers who have taken 
the steps, often at great effort, to achieve full traceability and compliance.

306 ♦ Ildi Revi
 A chameleon walking through coffee plants. Organic and 
regenerative farming encourages wildlife to return.
The future of organic coffee cultivation is promising. It is driven by increasing 
consumer demand for sustainably grown coffee, as well as the clear connection between 
organic systems, environmental protection, and human health. As research and 
practices continue to evolve, farmers will need ongoing support to implement best 
practices and meet evidence-based guidelines. By investing in education, science, and 
integrity, we can ensure that organic coffee continues to support the health of the crop, 
the environment, and the people who drink it.
COMBINING SUSTAINABILITY AND ECONOMICS 
IN REGENERATIVE COFFEE FARMING 
Organic regenerative farming solves soil issues and the impending crisis of agricultural 
lands encroaching on natural ecosystems. The regenerative movement is holistic, 
involving soil health, biodiversity, animal welfare, and fair wages and working 
conditions, among many other things. We translate this to coffee, ensuring holistic 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 307
coffee is grown in its natural environment with plenty of natural shade for the slow 
development of the coffee seeds within their fruit.
The Regenerative Organic Certification (ROC) was established in 2017 by the 
Organic Regenerative Alliance (ROA), a coalition of organizations and leaders in the 
organic and regenerative farming movements. The most recent update shows that over 
100 farms and businesses worldwide have achieved this certification. This certification 
aims to create robust standards that improve agricultural resilience and sustainability, 
promoting healthier ecosystems and communities. 
The ROC’s three core pillars are soil health, which focuses on practices like crop 
rotation and reduced tillage to improve soil biodiversity and carbon sequestration; 
animal welfare, ensuring the humane treatment of livestock through appropriate 
living conditions and natural behaviors; and social fairness, advocating for equitable 
treatment, wages, and working conditions for farm workers. The ROC requires at least 
three certifications for each of its principles, each needing to be audited by agencies 
specific to those certifications, and the ROC compliance, auditing, and administration 
make it thorough but administratively heavy. The aspirations of this certification align 
with CHC, and I look forward to seeing how it advances over the coming years.
Sustainable farming practices are the bedrock of a healthy coffee supply chain. Organic 
and agroforestry methods enhance biodiversity, soil health, and farmer livelihoods. 
Certifications such as Fair Trade and Rainforest Alliance are pivotal in promoting 
practices that reduce harmful chemical use, conserve water, and mitigate climate 
change impacts by sequestering carbon through agroforestry systems. These initiatives 
benefit smallholder farmers economically and ensure that the coffee produced is 
environmentally friendly and socially responsible (Wright 2024).
This relationship between health and sustainability is underscored by the work of 
Castillo and Iriondo-DeHond (2023), who argue that the coffee value chain, when 
approached through the lens of circular economy and nutritional innovation, has 
the potential to contribute meaningfully to both human and planetary health. They 
emphasize that up to 90 percent of the coffee cherry is discarded during conventional 
processing. Yet, these by-products (such as cascara, mucilage, parchment, silverskin, 
and spent grounds) contain fibers, polyphenols, and other compounds that could 

308 ♦ Ildi Revi
support gut health if safely and thoughtfully incorporated into the diet. Integrating 
these underused materials into food systems could reduce agricultural waste, enhance 
nutrient density, and provide functional benefits relevant to microbiome stability.
Efforts to align sustainability with economic viability in coffee farming are increasingly 
supported by emerging tools and methodologies designed to measure environmental 
impact. These tools, developed by nonprofit and science-based organizations, help 
producers calculate and manage their greenhouse gas emissions, carbon sequestration, 
water usage, and biodiversity performance. When integrated into farming operations, 
they support the transition to regenerative practices by making environmental impacts 
visible, measurable, and manageable. Tools like these are already being used across the 
coffee sector to inform better land management decisions, optimize soil health, and 
support long-term climate resilience.
The idea of a “climate-smart” farm refers to an agricultural system that reduces 
greenhouse gas emissions and actively enhances soil carbon sequestration. Rather than 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 309
relying on synthetic inputs and extractive practices, these farms prioritize ecological 
regeneration through organic matter retention, biodiversity enhancement, minimal 
soil disturbance, and integrated nutrient cycling. By focusing on long-term soil health 
and ecosystem resilience, regenerative systems offer a scalable, evidence-based model 
for addressing climate impact and food system sustainability challenges.
Regenerative coffee farming aims to restore degraded lands and foster ecological 
balance while producing a high-quality crop. As discussed throughout this chapter, 
cover-cropping, composting, polyculture systems, and minimal soil disturbance build 
soil health and enhance biodiversity. These systems can potentially increase carbon 
sequestration, improve water retention, and stabilize microclimates, all of which 
support environmental and human health. When applied to coffee production, these 
principles help create nutrient-dense, resilient, and transparently produced coffees 
that serve wellness-driven consumers and farming communities’ long-term viability.
Measuring and managing carbon footprints at the farm level has become increasingly 
important. Many producers now use open-access or low-cost digital platforms to 
track emissions and carbon storage. These tools can inform decision-making, support 
data-driven storytelling for consumers, and, eventually, facilitate entry into voluntary 
carbon markets. In these markets, verified carbon credits from regenerative practices 
may offer producers an additional income stream. When structured equitably, such 
systems can direct revenues back to the farms based on their sequestration performance, 
reinforcing environmental efforts with real economic value.
However, producing coffee to serve the “food as medicine” movement places significant 
demands on farmers. It requires extensive labor, expertise, and infrastructure to 
meet expectations for purity, sustainability, and bioactive compound preservation. 
This includes everything from soil testing and post-harvest handling to food safety, 
traceability, and ongoing quality assurance. When sustainability is layered onto 
these health-driven demands, the investment of time, equipment, inputs, and risk 
management increases. Supporting this movement means acknowledging the real 
costs borne by producers and ensuring that pricing systems reflect not just commodity 
value, but the public health and ecological value of the food produced.

310 ♦ Ildi Revi
Transitioning to an outright pricing system for Circular Health Coffee is necessary. 
It compensates producers based on the total value they provide, not just the cup 
quality, but the carbon they store, the toxins they avoid, the biodiversity they restore, 
and the human health they support. Pricing models built around this deeper value 
proposition foster transparency and shared purpose among producers and consumers 
alike, ensuring that ecological and wellness goals are aligned across the entire supply 
chain. The discussion will continue after the presentation of Health-Grade Coffee 
standards to clarify what we are pricing.
The following chart outlines proposed minimum standards for farming and post-
harvest practices necessary to qualify a coffee for CHC consideration. These criteria 
emphasize organic-regenerative production, careful processing, and contamination 
risk mitigation, laying the foundation for health-grade coffee.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 311

systems, etc.

320 ♦ Ildi Revi
5.7 Health-Grade Green Arabica 
Coffee Standards
This section proposes a new set of standards for CHC, specifically for green coffee; I 
use the term “health-grade coffee,” which builds upon the foundations of other grades 
of coffee but integrates additional requirements aligned with the “food as medicine” 
movement. While taste is important, health-grade coffee standards support CHC’s 
documented health benefits, regenerative farming, and food safety practices. It’s not a 
rejection of past frameworks, but an evolution that reflects where consumer priorities 
and scientific understanding are going.
CHC reframes coffee evaluation around health outcomes. In this model, health-grade 
coffee is measurable, traceable, and has the standards for excellent taste potential. 
Health Grade Coffee is designed to go further by:
•
Uniting grading, food safety, and compound preservation in a single standard
•
Requiring verified lab testing (not just visual grading)
•
Enforcing hygiene and occupational safety
•
Elevating contract standards to reflect nutritional value and regenerative
integrity, not just defect count
Moving toward this new standard, we support a future where coffee contributes to 
pleasure and performance, universal wellness, and agricultural resilience. 
THE HISTORICAL CONTEXT OF GREEN COFFEE GRADING
Green coffee grading systems were originally developed to support consistent 
commercial coffee sector trade, pricing, and quality expectations. The most 
established frameworks are those maintained by the International Organization for 
Standardization (ISO), the International Coffee Organization (ICO), and various 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 321
national export regulatory bodies. For example, the ISO 10470:1993 Green Coffee 
Defect Reference Chart outlines a standard set of visual and physical defects, providing 
internationally accepted defect definitions and relative severity ratings.
Similarly, the Green Coffee Association (GCA) of the United States, established 
in 1923, formalized grading to support commercial uniformity, reduce disputes, 
and establish enforceable contract norms. Their published Contract Terms and 
Conditions and associated arbitration mechanisms created a foundational commercial 
infrastructure for importers and traders (Green Coffee Association 2021).
Grading systems such as those used in Brazil (MAPA), Colombia (FNC), and Costa 
Rica (INTECO), as documented by the ICO, tend to focus on exportable physical 
characteristics like bean size (screen), number and type of defects, moisture content, 
and cup clarity (ICO 2013). These systems categorize coffee based on measurable, but 
primarily aesthetic or cupping-quality metrics, useful for pricing and trade contracts, 
but not for assessing coffee’s safety or functional benefits.
In Europe, ISO 10470:1993 remains the principal reference standard for green 
coffee defects. At the same time, food safety oversight is handled by the European 
Food Safety Authority (EFSA), including ochratoxin A and other contaminants. The 
European Coffee Federation (ECF) influences trade policy, residue limits, and import 
quality standards. Most European contracts use GAFTA models for arbitration, but 
no Europe-specific equivalent to GCA exists.
Moving across hemispheres, the Australian Coffee Traders Association (ACTA) 
provides some trade guidance, while Food Standards Australia New Zealand (FSANZ) 
enforces safety regulations in line with Codex Alimentarius.
In Asia, systems vary by country. In Indonesia, grading and export requirements are 
governed by SNI (Indonesian National Standards) and the National Standardization 
Agency of Indonesia (Badan Standardisasi Nasional, or BSN). India uses standards 
managed by the Coffee Board of India, which also licenses wet mills and oversees post-
harvest processes. Vietnam regulates quality through its TCVN system, while Japan 
enforces strict import standards and ochratoxin A limits through its JAS (Japanese 
Agricultural Standards).

322 ♦ Ildi Revi
Comparative Example: Vietnam Arabica Grade 1 vs CHC
How is health grade coffee 
different? Let’s take, for 
example, Vietnam’s Arabica 
Grade 1 system (S14–S18 
WASH), which represents 
how many traditional 
green grading systems work 
globally. The standards 
state that moisture cannot 
exceed 12.5 percent, 
foreign matter has to stay 
under 0.1 percent, and no 
more than 0.4 percent of 
the beans can be black or 
broken. At least 90 percent 
have to hit a minimum 
screen size, whether 14, 
16, or 18, depending on 
the grade. This works well 
for trade. It keeps things 
visually clean, physically 
uniform, and consistent for 
buyers. But it doesn’t tell 
you whether those beans 
meet food safety rules or 
are good for your health. By 
comparison, CHC takes an 
entirely different approach. We tighten the moisture range to 9.0–11.5 percent to 
reduce mycotoxin risk, completely reject OTA-associated defects, and require testing 
for things like Bacillus cereus enterotoxins and heavy metals. We look at bioactive 
compounds like chlorogenic acids and others. Vietnam’s system, like many others, is 
focused on what you can see. CHC is focused on what you can’t. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 323
Importantly, none of these commercial grading systems were designed with human 
health, toxicology, or compound retention in mind. The health-grade green arabica 
standard aims to enhance existing trade infrastructures by establishing guidelines for 
food safety, preserving bioactive compounds, preventing harmful substances, and 
ensuring workplace hygiene.
MOISTURE AND WATER ACTIVITY STANDARDS
Moisture is not only an aspect of food safety, but also a key metric for calculating the 
dry weight of coffee and estimating the total yield of a roasted batch. This information 
has long been a standard used to evaluate green coffee for purchasing decisions, and it 
is a useful data point for roasters determining how to approach a coffee’s roast profile. 
After harvest, coffee must be dried for transport. Most coffee typically ranges between 
9 percent and 12 percent moisture content, and many exporters aim for 10–11 percent 
during shipping (ITC 2011). CHC adopts the range of 9.0 percent to 11.5 percent as 
a health safeguard. Excessive moisture (above 12.5 percent) increases the risk of fungal 
contamination, germination, and nutrient depletion, while under-dried coffee (below 
9 percent) may indicate staling or loss of volatile compounds. Excessive moisture can 
also cause the beans to continue germinating, depleting their store of sugars and other 
vital nutrients. The moisture content of beans changes dramatically during roasting, 
dropping to 2–3 percent in medium roasts (Eggers and Pietsch 2001) and as low as 1 
percent in dark roasts (Bonnlander 2005).
Water activity (aw) is a critical measure that complements moisture content by 
indicating how much water is actually available for microbial growth, not just how 
much is present. It ranges from 0 (no available water) to 1.0 (pure water), and each 
microorganism has a minimum aw to survive. Molds that produce mycotoxins, such 
as Aspergillus ochraceus, are inhibited below 0.75 aw; general molds and yeasts may 
persist at 0.61 aw, while 0.60 aw is widely recognized as the critical threshold below 
which microbial growth is not supported (Graintec 2006). CHC sets a stricter water 
activity range of 0.50–0.60 aw to reduce the risk of OTA formation and other microbial 
hazards. This range also aligns with optimal Maillard reaction performance during 

324 ♦ Ildi Revi
roasting, protecting food safety and flavor chemistry. While some recommend <0.70 
aw, CHC’s tighter specification reflects its health-driven priorities.
Ambient humidity affects water activity over time. Foods with higher aw tend to lose 
water to dry environments, while those with lower aw will gain moisture from humid 
air. Average humidity levels in coffee warehouses are 50–70 percent, corresponding 
to approximately 0.65–0.70 aw, which is right on the microbial growth threshold. 
CHC’s lower target ensures more stable storage, especially in fluctuating climates 
(Taniwaki 2001).
The science of water activity also helps us understand how coffee behaves in the 
warehouse and the roaster:
•
In storage, water that is not tightly bound can evaporate, degrading volatile 
aroma compounds and aging the coffee. Coffees stored in liners like 
GrainPro® or Ecotact® experience less oxidative damage.
•
In roasting, free water is released up to and through first crack. Water activity 
directly affects the Maillard reaction, the chemical browning that develops 
flavor. The highest rate of browning generally occurs between 0.65 and 
0.75 aw, while the reaction stops below 0.30. Roasting is most efficient and 
flavor-rich when green coffee begins with water activity around 0.50–0.60 
(Wong 2015). Recent research by Wong et al. (2015) demonstrates that 
Maillard browning reactions can still occur in environments with very low 
moisture and aw, so long as molecular mobility is maintained. Their findings 
suggest that even when water activity is below 0.50 in coffee, browning may 
continue if alternative reactants (e.g., sugars, glycerol-like intermediates) 
are present. This reinforces the importance of CHC’s aw target, not only 
for microbial control, but to preserve the delicate balance between desired 
flavor development and the prevention of over-browning or acrylamide 
accumulation during roasting.
By setting parameters for both moisture and water activity, CHC protects quality, 
safety, and functionality in one integrated standard, but also serves as a key metric 
for calculating the dry weight of coffee and estimating the total yield of a roasted 
batch. Moisture affects coffee’s behavior during roasting, and beans with higher 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 325
moisture content require more energy and airflow to maintain roast momentum. 
Beans with lower moisture can present different challenges, as their behavior under 
heat is influenced by the bean’s internal structure and not as straightforward as simple 
evaporation (Fadai 2018).
Proper storage is essential. Green coffee loses volatile compounds in warehouses, 
especially if the water inside the beans isn’t tightly bound. Beans stored in plastic-
lined jute sacks (e.g., GrainPro®, Ecotact®) preserve their chemistry better, slowing 
oxidation and degradation. 
CIRCULAR HEALTH CONSIDERATIONS: WET 
MILL MICROBIOLOGICAL HAZARDS, GRADING 
HYGIENE, AND WORKER SAFETY
While most green coffee grading systems focus on beans’ visual or structural integrity, 
microbiological risks, especially those arising in wet mills, are an overlooked but critical 
component in health-oriented assessments. During fermentation and washing stages, 
conditions of high moisture and moderate warmth provide an ideal environment for 
microbial growth (Oldenburg 2009).
Wet mill operations may harbor:
•
Gram-negative bacteria, such as Enterobacter and Klebsiella, release harmful
endotoxins to workers and are potentially retained in residual water films
on green beans.
•
Fungi, such as Aspergillus and Cladosporium, some of which can produce
allergens or toxins.
•
Lactic acid and acetic acid bacteria are often beneficial to flavor in
moderation, but they can be potential sources of spoilage and off-odors if
uncontrolled.
These organisms may pose:
•
Occupational health risks to wet mill workers through respiratory exposure
to aerosolized endotoxins and spores (Oldenburg 2009).

326 ♦ Ildi Revi
• 
Cross-contamination risks at the roastery, especially in facilities that handle 
green and roasted coffee in proximity or without physical and airflow 
separation.
Although roasting effectively reduces 
microbial loads, any contaminated green 
dust can linger on equipment, packaging 
zones, or be re-aerosolized, creating an 
indirect route for food safety compromise 
or quality degradation (Oldenburg 2009).
Green coffee grading involves close 
handling of raw coffee beans and exposure 
to dust particles that may contain mold 
spores, bacteria, and microbial byproducts 
from fermentation. To protect their health, 
graders should work in well-ventilated 
areas, wear dust masks or respirators if 
airborne particles are present, and practice 
good hygiene after handling green coffee. Avoiding the consumption of food or drink 
in grading zones, washing hands, and regular cleaning of sieves and tables reduces 
cross-contamination risk. Facilities should monitor humidity and storage conditions 
to prevent microbial growth in stored green coffee.
Grading typically takes place on large rubber or silicone mats. These surfaces should 
be cleaned daily using an organic-approved sanitizer such as a 3 percent hydrogen 
peroxide solution, a hot water–vinegar mixture, or a certified biodegradable plant-
based surfactant. Harsh chemicals like bleach or quaternary ammonium compounds 
should be avoided. Mats should be fully dried before reuse and stored off the ground 
to prevent recontamination. Weekly sun exposure or UV treatment can help reduce 
microbial load naturally and maintain hygiene.
Toward a Health-Based Framework: Scientific Rationale for Defect Assessment
Unlike traditional grading systems, Health-Grade Green Arabica Coffee Standards 
emphasize food safety, bioactive compound integrity, and chemical hazard reduction. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 327
Defects are not just cosmetic, but they are often the first indicators of microbial risk, 
loss of functional compounds, or processing failures that may compromise consumer 
safety.
This chapter outlines key defect types prioritized under the Health Grade model. For 
each, we present:
•
Scientific definition and visual characteristics
•
Biochemical or microbial causes
•
Impacts on health, food safety, and nutritional integrity
•
Effect on roasting and final sensory quality
SAMPLE SIZE, HANDLING AND SENSORY EVALUATION
CHC requires two separate 250-gram samples from each lot: one for physical grading 
and cupping, and one for laboratory testing. The laboratory sample remains sealed 
in its original packaging, as received from the importer or producer. This ensures an 
uncontaminated and representative analysis of the green coffee as received. Most labs 
require at least 250 grams to conduct all necessary tests (moisture, water activity, CGA, 
organic acids, mycotoxins, heavy metals, etc.). However, this typically allows for only 
a single round of testing per parameter. If isolated results appear questionable, there 
is usually enough volume to retest one or two parameters, but if broader re-analysis 
is needed, additional material will likely need to be sent.
The physical evaluation is also based on 250 grams. This aligns with historical green 
coffee grading practices outlined under the ICE Futures U.S. Coffee “C”® rules. 
Although ICE uses a volume-based standard, a 5 x 8 inch aluminum grading tray 
filled level to the rim (approx. 1.2 inches deep). This typically corresponds to 250-300 
grams, depending on bean size and density. For CHC, a standard 250-gram sample 
provides consistency, practicality, and sufficient volume for accurate visual inspection 
and defect classification.

Defect assessment in health-grade green coffee goes beyond visual appearance or taste 
to include food safety and compound preservation. The CHC model reclassifies long-
accepted commercial imperfections based on current microbiological and toxicological 
science. Physical grading focuses on visible defects that signal potential health risks, 
while laboratory testing verifies the presence or absence of contaminants and confirms 
the coffee’s nutritional integrity. Together, they form a more complete standard that 
evaluates coffee not just for how it looks or tastes, but for how it supports human 
health.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 329
Screen size variation is not a core criterion of CHC, but it remains a practical 
consideration. As a general guideline, CHC recommends limiting screen size variation 
to within 5% of the dominant size to support even roasting. However, this is not a 
health-based standard, and coffees with greater size diversity, especially those from 
certain high-altitude or heirloom cultivars, may still fully qualify as health-grade. 
Screen size should be interpreted in context, and this is an area where flexibility and 
future refinement are expected.
Cupping for Health-Grade Arabica Coffee
Cupping is a vital step before sending a sample to the lab. If the coffee has unpleasant 
or inconsistent flavors, that alone may justify holding off on testing. Lab panels take 
1–3 weeks to return, an eternity in coffee buying timelines. A clean, well-balanced 
cup with no strange aromas or sourness is your best cue that the coffee is worth the 
lab investment. Trust your palate, then verify with the data.
Cupping is not the focus of CHC, but it plays an important supporting role. A 
coffee that goes undrunk cannot support anyone’s health. We use cupping to verify 
that a coffee is clean, pleasant, and free of off-notes, especially signs of microbial 
spoilage, age, or storage-related degradation. We aim to find well-balanced, sweet, 
and consistent coffees rather than exotic flavors. The health benefits of coffee are often 
dose-dependent, with recommendations suggesting the consumption of three to five 
cups per day. Naturally, individual preferences and needs should take precedence.
While CHC does not prescribe a fixed cupping protocol, we recommend using the 
same general parameters as other standardized systems: consistent sample roasting, 
water quality, coffee-to-water ratio, grind size, and steep time. The number of cups per 
sample is flexible. Three to four cups may be sufficient for coffees you know and buy 
regularly. For new or unfamiliar coffees, a six-cup evaluation helps ensure uniformity 
and flag inconsistencies.
Cupping should always be performed before lab testing to screen out coffees with 
obvious defects, saving time and cost. A clear, sweet, and stable cup indicates proper 
processing, storage, and suitability for CHC lab evaluation.

330 ♦ Ildi Revi
On the CHC evaluation form, we suggest simple sensory notetaking to verify key 
attributes, match them from pre-shipment to arrival samples, and monitor them over 
the years with normal variations that we know happen from year to year:
•
Clean Cup
Free from off-notes, taints, fermentation, or storage-related flavors.
•
Balanced and Uniform
All cups of the sample taste similar; no cup stands out for a negative reason.
•
Fragrance and Aroma
The dry and wet smell of the coffee should be pleasant, sweet, or neutral,
with no chemical, dusty, or moldy notes.
•
Flavor and Aftertaste
General flavor during sipping and the taste that lingers should be clear, free
of sharp or sour defects, and leave a clean finish.
•
Acidity
Brightness or sparkle in the cup; should feel natural and integrated, not
aggressive, sour, or flat. While “low acid” is a selling point for some, CHC
values balanced, pleasant acidity and leaves this to individual companies to
define within their broader health profile.
•
Body
The weight or texture of the coffee in the mouth should be appropriate for
the origin and sample roast, without being thin or oily.
•
Sweetness
A natural impression of sugar-like roundness, especially evident in the finish
or when the coffee cools.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 331
The section on the Health Grade Arabica Coffee Evaluation Sample form is open-
ended without point scores:

taste standards?
If YES, sample passes.
Cupping is inherently complex and subjective. While some may utilize it to pursue 
novelty or adhere to scoring systems, CHC employs it to uphold health integrity and 
consumer trust.
CHARACTERIZATION OF DEFECTS
LEVEL 1 DEFECTS
(Sample may contain no Level 1 defects. Partial Black and Partial Sour become Level 
1 if thresholds are exceeded.)
1. Full Black
Beans that are entirely blackened are typically the result of over-fermentation, 
prolonged moisture exposure, or fungal degradation. They have reduced sucrose and 
protein, higher ash content, and are strongly associated with ochratoxin A (OTA) 
contamination (Vasconcelos 2007; Mazzafera 1999).

332 ♦ Ildi Revi
2. Full Sour
Sour beans are discolored (typically yellow-brown) and often emit a vinegary odor. 
They are associated with uncontrolled fermentation and show lowered pH, reduced 
solids, and elevated biogenic amines like histamine and putrescine (Belay 2014).
3. Mold or Fungus
Visible mold, or hidden fungal growth due to poor storage, presents serious food safety 
risks. Common species such as Aspergillus ochraceus and Penicillium verrucosum can 
produce OTA, which is nephrotoxic and immunosuppressive (Pardo 2004; Bucheli 
2002).
4. Dried Cherry Pod
Beans that remain encased in dried husk or mucilage, often due to incomplete pulping 
or drying, create microenvironments for mold growth. They are especially risky in 
semi-dry or honey-processed coffees (Bucheli 2002).
5. Foreign Matter
Non-coffee material, like stones, metal, parchment, or sticks, may introduce physical 
hazards and contamination. These fragments are often vehicles for bacteria or fungal 
spores (FDA 1984).
6. Severe Insect Damage
Extensive boring from insects such as Hypothenemus hampei or Araecerus fasciculatus 
compromises bean integrity. These insects can vector fungi and bacteria, contributing 
to OTA formation and elevated volatile compounds like hexanal (Walker 2023).
7. Partial Black
If more than three partial black beans are present, this defect is escalated to Level 1. 
Partial blacks are early-stage fermentation or mold-affected beans that may carry OTA 
risks and degrade roast consistency (Ramalakshmi 2007).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 333
8. Partial Sour / Amber / Buttery
When more than three are found, these are considered Level 1. Sourness may result 
from overfermentation or physical stress; amber and buttery discoloration suggests 
age, heat stress, or storage damage. These defects are often associated with acetic acid 
elevation or partial microbial degradation.
LEVEL 2 DEFECTS
(No more than three full Level 2 defects are allowed per 250g sample. Defect count 
is based on whole multiples of defined thresholds.)
9. Shell
Thin-walled, hollow beans with a cup-like shape. Their fragile structure results in 
uneven roasting, increased acrylamide risk, and the potential to collect dust or residues 
during storage (Mazzafera 1999).
10. Hull, Husk, or Parchment
Pieces of the dried pulp or the translucent yellowish layer that have become separated 
from pods (FDA 1984). Residual outer layers signal incomplete hulling. These can 
trap moisture and lead to mold growth, particularly under humid storage conditions. 
Parchment has been linked to OTA in adulterated soluble coffee (Bucheli 2002).
11. Withered / Shrunk
Small, collapsed, or irregular beans caused by drought, stress, or premature drying. 
They have lower density and roast unevenly, often producing a thin or dry cup 
(Ramalakshmi 2007).
12. Broken / Chipped / Cut / Nipped / Pressed
Beans damaged mechanically during hulling or handling, including pressed or 
compressed beans. Fractures expose interior tissues to oxidation and can impair even 
roasting. These beans are structurally weak and may absorb off-flavors.

334 ♦ Ildi Revi
13. Faded (Old, Overdried, Uneven) / Crystallized
Visual signs of age or poor processing. Crystallized beans are heat-damaged during 
drying. Faded beans may be brittle, oxidized, or unevenly dried, impacting flavor 
stability and structural integrity.
14. Floater
Low-density beans that float in water. Floaters roast unevenly and contribute little 
to cup quality.
15. Immature / Unripe
Picked too early, these beans are dense, green, and often grassy in flavor. They have 
incomplete development of sugars and acids and may contribute to sourness or 
imbalance in the cup.
16. Minor Insect Damage
Small, mold-free boreholes caused by early insect activity. Common in organic farming 
systems. Acceptable up to threshold, but counts must be monitored, as they can signal 
underlying microbial or storage risks (Chen 2020).
RATIONALE FOR COMPOUND TESTING STANDARDS 
(CAFFEINE, CGA, DECAF AND TRIGONELLINE THRESHOLDS)
In addition to physical grading, health-grade green coffee standards establish specific 
chemical thresholds for key bioactive compounds. These thresholds are grounded in 
nutritional biochemistry and supported by decades of coffee research.
CGA, as we have discussed before, are the primary class of phenolic antioxidants 
in green coffee, contributing to blood glucose modulation, liver protection, gut 
health, and DNA protection (Makiso 2024; Saud 2021). Arabica coffee typically 
contains between 4–8 g/100 g CGA by dry weight (Farah 2012), with levels dropping 
significantly during roasting. CHC sets a minimum green coffee CGA content of 4000 
mg/100 g to ensure meaningful retention after light-to-medium roasting, preserving 
coffee’s therapeutic potential, but >6000 mg/100 g would be exceptional. This is one 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 335
of the standards we want to look at with others. Is 4000 mg practical? Should it be 
lower? Or should we seek higher levels as the category develops?
As discussed earlier in this book, caffeine is well-documented in supporting mental 
focus, metabolic regulation, and inflammatory balance. In green Arabica beans, 
caffeine typically ranges from 0.9 to 1.6 percent (Makiso 2024), with Robusta varieties 
often significantly higher. CHC prioritizes transparency to accommodate various 
individual needs, whether seeking caffeine, avoiding it, or aiming for moderation. 
Rather than prescribing intake, we provide clear content disclosure. CHC sets a 
minimum of ≥0.9 g/100 g caffeine for health-grade classification to ensure functional 
benefits in the brewed product. For decaffeinated coffee, though, we follow a threshold 
of <0.1 percent DWB (dry weight basis) to safeguard caffeine-sensitive populations, 
including those with neurological or cardiovascular considerations.
Trigonelline is not a compound commonly tested in commercial laboratories, but 
it is more frequently studied in university laboratories with food science programs. 
Therefore, it is listed as “optional,” although trigonelline plays a role in the healthiness 
of coffees. The target amount should be ≥0.6 g/100g, with ≥1.1 g/100g considered 
an exceptional sample.
RATIONALE FOR OPTIONAL ORGANIC ACID 
THRESHOLDS (CITRIC, MALIC, QUINIC)
In addition to CGA and caffeine, certain organic acids in green coffee contribute 
to health outcomes and roasting functionality, and therefore merit threshold 
consideration in CHC’s standards. 
Citric acid is a tricarboxylic acid naturally present in green coffee as a product of 
cellular respiration. It supports antioxidant defenses and is linked to improved mineral 
absorption and chelation of free radicals. In green coffee, levels typically range from 
0.2–0.6 g/100 g, decreasing with roast degree (Yeager 2021). CHC sets a minimum 
citric acid threshold of 200 mg/100 g in green coffee to ensure antioxidant and 
buffering potential remain after roasting.

336 ♦ Ildi Revi
Malic acid, also present in apples and other fruits, plays a key role in energy metabolism 
and exhibits mild antibacterial properties. Green Arabica beans often contain 0.15–0.4 
g/100 g malic acid, which diminishes rapidly during roasting (Yeager 2021; Jham 
2002). CHC establishes a minimum malic acid level of 150 mg/100 g to preserve its 
functional contributions to flavor modulation and metabolic support.
Quinic acid is a degradation product of CGA and an intermediate in the biosynthesis 
of phenolics. Though often associated with bitterness or astringency in excess, it has 
prebiotic effects and supports phenolic bioavailability. Average levels in green coffee 
range from 0.3–0.6 g/100 g (Yeager 2021; Jham 2002). CHC includes a minimum 
threshold of 300 mg/100 g for quinic acid to preserve its nutritional contribution 
while managing astringency through roast profile.
These organic acid thresholds offer another way to confirm the compound integrity 
of coffees labeled as health-grade. They do not replace CGA testing but add depth to 
the profile. While lab testing increases cost, it’s part of what sets CHC coffees apart, 
measurable quality, not just assumed.
ENTEROTOXIGENIC BACTERIA (E.G., BACILLUS CEREUS)
It may come as a surprise, but Bacillus cereus, B. thuringiensis, and other microorganisms 
have been found in both green and roasted coffee. Microbes are naturally present in 
soil, on coffee fruit, and throughout fermentation and processing. Good hygiene is the 
first line of defense. While most microbes are destroyed during roasting, the greater 
risk comes afterwards through cross-contamination during packaging, handling, or 
worker exposure. Many roasters don’t consider this, simply because it hasn’t been part 
of the conversation. But green coffee can easily introduce contaminants into roasting 
and production areas. Microbial testing helps assess the load on green coffee and the 
risk of transfer post-roast. And while roasted coffee has some natural antimicrobial 
properties, and hot brewing reduces risk further, the best protection starts upstream 
by monitoring and managing microbial levels in the green coffee itself.
The following microbial limits are recommended as part of CHC’s approach to food 
safety and hygiene:
