We never know what someone is going through. This level of vigilance matters for 
people who are immunocompromised, recovering from illness, or simply trying to 
reduce daily exposure to contaminants. While the risk in coffee is generally low, careful 
handling helps ensure it remains a clean, dependable part of a health-supportive 
routine.
THE STARTING LINE FOR HEALTH-DRIVEN COFFEE SOURCING
The following charts are proposed starting points for Health-Grade Green Arabica 
Coffee standards. This is still a new category, and the wider industry has not yet caught 
up to the level of food safety, compound preservation, and functional quality outlined 
in this book. But these standards are not theoretical. They are aspirational, yes, but 
also achievable. At Purity, all of our coffees now meet these benchmarks thanks to 
the commitment of producers who had already been working for years to grow clean, 

338 ♦ Ildi Revi
health-focused coffee. We were fortunate to find them, and they, in turn, were looking 
for buyers who shared their values. Reaching this level consistently requires deep 
collaboration not only with these producers, but also with dry mills and exporters, 
who play key roles in ensuring food safety and security.
Other companies may use different benchmarks, and that’s understandable. What 
matters most is having a clear strategy for sourcing coffee that supports human health, 
backed by thoughtful roasting and verified through lab results. As discussed in the 
lab testing section, it’s equally important to know how to read Certificates of Analysis 
and calculate compound values per cup using adult tolerable reference values (TRVs). 
Sharing your lab results and approach with customers is what will ultimately build 
trust in this category. The science is still evolving, and so are we. Where evidence 
exists, we follow it. Where it doesn’t, we apply the best judgment and remain open 
to refinement.
These standards are rooted in both aspiration and reality. While zero-detection of 
contaminants is the ideal, it isn’t always practical, or scientifically meaningful, given 
the precision of modern lab testing. Trace amounts of naturally occurring elements 
like cadmium or arsenic may be found even in coffees grown with great care. That 
doesn’t mean those coffees aren’t safe, it means the testing is honest. Transparency is 
more important than perfection, and CHC embraces that principle.
CHC testing protocols are aligned with globally accepted food safety frameworks, and 
results are shared with supply chain partners and consumers. This approach allows for 
informed decisions, continual improvement, and a clear baseline for what it means 
to produce truly health-focused coffee.

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦339

340 ♦ Ildi Revi

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦341

342 ♦ Ildi Revi

Green coffee sample for grading.



346 ♦ Ildi Revi
As discussed throughout this book, and especially in this chapter on farming and 
processing, circular health necessitates considering the entire system. Coffee is 
not just a product; it is the outcome of relationships between soil, water, plants, 
microbes, workers, and global trade. A truly health-focused standard must reflect 
this interconnectedness. CHC, guided by the principles of One Health and Circular 
Health, acknowledges that human wellness is dependent on the health of ecosystems, 
agricultural systems, and communities.
In addition to green grading and lab testing, CHC coffees should meet a broader set 
of proposed criteria that reflect these values. Coffees should be organically grown and, 
where possible, cultivated using regenerative or biodynamic methods. Aligning with 
initiatives that are working toward carbon neutrality and implementing methane-
reduction strategies are part of the long-term vision. Clean water management at 
the wet mill, hygienic handling and food safety/security at the dry mill, the use of 
intercropping or byproduct innovation, and protective packaging all support the 
integrity of the product and the well-being of those who grow and consume it.
These are not mandates but living standards meant to guide the conversation 
forward. We welcome collaborators across the supply chain: farmers, scientists, 
exporters, certifiers, and roasters. With more perspectives, better science, and shared 
commitment, these guidelines can evolve into something both rigorous and ready for 
the real world. Health-grade coffee isn’t just something we measure, but is something 
we build together.
Ultimately, health-grade green coffee represents a shift in how we define quality, 
moving from surface-level appearance and tradition to a deeper focus on safety, 
integrity, and measurable contributions to human health. These standards don’t 
claim to be final. They reflect our current best understanding based on food safety, 
nutritional biochemistry, agricultural integrity, and practical sourcing experience. 
As the science advances and more coffees are evaluated under this lens, we expect 
refinements. But the foundation is here: traceable, tested, safe, and supportive coffee 
that works in harmony with both the body and the environment.
Of course, setting standards is only one part of the equation. For health-grade coffee to 
function at scale, the contracts, pricing models, and buying strategies behind it must 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 347
evolve as well. It’s the only way the system works, ensuring producers are compensated 
fairly, roasters can manage their risk, and most importantly, consumers understand 
the value of what they’re drinking. Transparency, Certificates of Analysis, and shared 
standards build that bridge. In the next section, we’ll examine how CHC redefines 
its pricing logic to align with purpose and performance.

348
5.8 Economics and Pricing
The global coffee market was designed to move large volumes of standardized beans 
through an industrial supply chain. The Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) Coffee “C” 
contract, still the pricing foundation for most Arabica worldwide, was built for speed, 
scale, and risk management. Even in quality-focused segments, where premiums and 
certifications are added, pricing typically begins with the C-market base and is adjusted 
through differentials for origin, certification status, or traceable practices.
But these tools were never designed to reward coffees grown for health.
CHC coffees are different. Producers who meet these standards are investing in things 
that aren’t accounted for in the conventional pricing model: clean fermentation 
systems, air-powered dry mill vacuums, optical sorting, water quality monitoring, 
hygienic bagging, and lab testing for mycotoxins, heavy metals, CGA, and microbial 
contaminants. These are not cosmetic upgrades; they’re part of a system that delivers 
measurable health outcomes. And yet, under the futures-based model, such investments 
rarely result in higher compensation. Volatile markets, speculative trading, and rigid 
quality premiums often fail to capture the real value behind coffees intentionally 
grown to meet these standards.
That’s why CHC supports outright pricing: a fixed price agreed upon between buyer 
and producer, independent of the C market. This approach brings clarity, stability, 
and accountability. It allows producers to forecast, plan, and improve their systems 
without gambling on market swings. It also allows buyers to define quality on their 
own terms, including the bioactive, regenerative, and health-focused attributes that 
the C market doesn’t recognize.
At Purity, we’ve implemented outright pricing with the producers we work with, 
and the conversations are different. Producers present the real costs behind clean, 
health-focused coffee, including labor, infrastructure, and upgrades required to meet 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 349
CHC standards. We respond with multi-year contracts and clear expectations for lab 
verification, helping build the kind of consistency that supports long-term planning 
and resilience at origin. Both sides share the risks and rewards, and with transparency 
and trust, everyone understands the worth of the coffee.
Pricing can take many forms for other companies looking to work within CHC. But 
a few principles remain essential:
•	
Transparency: Buyers and producers must understand each other’s costs.
•	
Direct negotiation: The fewer intermediaries, the better the value retained 
at origin.
•	
Open costing and risk sharing: When prices are fixed, producers can plan, 
and buyers know exactly what they’re paying for.
•	
Consumer education: Trust comes from clear communication. Sharing 
lab results, price structures, and what it takes to produce this kind of coffee 
helps consumers understand the higher cost and the greater value.
Health-grade coffee is not a commodity. It isn’t priced by tonnage or market 
speculation. It’s priced by purpose, by science, and by mutual respect.
If we want CHC to thrive, we need more than new grading standards. We need a trade 
model that values knowledge, measures impact, and rewards those who make coffee 
that supports long-term human and planetary health.

350
6
Decaffeinated Coffee (Decaf)
HIGHLIGHTS
Despite removing caffeine, decaf retains many beneficial compounds, such as trigonelline, 
diterpenes, and CGA.
Health benefits of decaf have been shown to include:
Type 2 Diabetes: Decaf coffee, especially with high CGA content, has shown promising 
results in reducing diabetes risk. 
Liver Health: Decaf coffee has been found to reduce liver steatosis, inflammation, and 
fibrosis.
Colorectal Cancer: Drinking decaf coffee has been linked to a lower risk of colorectal 
cancer-specific mortality and all-cause mortality.
Neurodegenerative Diseases: Decaf contains neuroprotective compounds like CGA and 
lactones, providing antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. 
Historically, lower-quality or older coffee beans were often sent for decaffeination. However, with 
increasing demand for high-quality decaf, the industry is now focusing more on better-quality 
beans to meet consumer expectations. 
The decaf coffee industry increasingly focuses on higher-quality beans to satisfy the rising consumer 
demand for premium decaffeinated products. This shift reflects a broader trend towards enhancing 
decaf coffee’s overall quality and flavor profile, moving away from the historical practice of using 
lower-quality or older beans. As consumers become more discerning, the industry adapts to meet 
these expectations, ensuring that decaf coffee can compete with its caffeinated counterparts in 
taste and quality.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 351
Decaffeination Methods and Their Impacts:
Methylene Chloride: This method is effective but has raised health concerns due to its 
potential carcinogenic properties. Safety measures are crucial to protect workers from 
exposure. Environmental concerns include air and water pollution from producing and 
disposing of methylene chloride. 
Ethyl Acetate: Considered a safer alternative, this method uses a fruit-derived solvent. 
However, it still poses health risks to workers and requires careful disposal to avoid 
environmental impact. 
Supercritical Carbon Dioxide: This method uses CO2 in its supercritical state to extract 
caffeine. It is one of the safest methods for preserving coffee flavors and aromas while 
minimizing health and environmental impacts
Water Processing: This environmentally friendly method uses water and green coffee 
extract to remove caffeine without chemical solvents. It is considered safe for consumers 
but results in a slight loss of CGA.
Decaffeinated coffee (decaf coffee) allows people to enjoy the flavor and social aspects 
of coffee without the adverse effects of caffeine. If you review the information in the 
Caffeine section of this book, you can see that there can be many reasons related 
to health for people to avoid caffeine and instead drink decaf coffee. Individuals 
who metabolize caffeine slowly or have heightened sensitivity to it may experience 
symptoms such as insomnia and tachycardia. 
Additionally, caffeine’s interaction with certain medications is an important factor. 
Caffeine interacts with some medicines, causing various issues and side effects, while 
other medications change how people metabolize caffeine. People with certain medical 
conditions, such as heart arrhythmias or anxiety disorders, may need to limit their 
caffeine intake. Caffeine has been shown to agitate some behaviors of Alzheimer’s 
patients and those with other neurological disorders. While caffeinated coffee may 
help prevent Alzheimer’s, switching to decaf might be beneficial once the disease has 
developed. Some individuals prefer to avoid caffeine due to its psychoactive properties.

352 ♦ Ildi Revi
Whenever wellness professionals tell people not to drink coffee, I cannot help but 
wonder if it is simply because of the caffeine. Scientific research has proven that 
decaffeinated coffee offers several health benefits independent of caffeine’s effects. Due 
to decaffeination, many people can still benefit from potentially beneficial compounds, 
such as trigonelline, diterpenes, CGA, key minerals, and other bioactive compounds. 
Some of the information below reviews content covered at length earlier in this book 
in the chapter on coffee’s impact on health conditions. At the same time, we will review 
the information and compare regular coffee (caffeinated) with decaffeinated coffee.
DECAF AND TYPE 2 DIABETES
Type 2 diabetes, the most common form of the disease, involves two primary problems: 
impaired insulin production by the pancreas and reduced cellular sensitivity to insulin, 
a condition known as insulin resistance. Together, these factors disrupt glucose 
regulation and lead to chronically elevated blood sugar levels. Numerous observational 
studies have shown a consistent association between higher coffee consumption, both 
caffeinated and decaffeinated, and a lower risk of developing type 2 diabetes. Notably, 
the risk reduction tends to increase in a dose-dependent manner, meaning greater 
intake correlates with lower risk (van Dam and Hu 2005).
Emerging evidence suggests that the protective effects of coffee may be attributed less 
to caffeine and more to its bioactive compounds, particularly CGA, trigonelline, and 
magnesium. In a controlled human trial, van Dijk and colleagues found that acute 
supplementation with CGA and trigonelline significantly lowered early postprandial 
glucose and insulin spikes during an oral glucose tolerance test in overweight men, 
supporting their role in improving glucose metabolism independently of caffeine.
A study by Shearer et al. in 2007 demonstrated that decaffeinated coffee significantly 
improved whole-body insulin sensitivity and skeletal muscle glucose uptake in insulin-
resistant animals. Adding caffeine to the same coffee preparation neutralized these 
benefits, suggesting that caffeine may antagonize coffee’s positive metabolic effects.
Mechanistically, CGA has been shown in both in vitro and in vivo models to inhibit 
intestinal glucose absorption and enzymes such as glucose-6-phosphatase, potentially 
reducing hepatic glucose output and blunting postprandial blood sugar spikes.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 353
These findings support the idea that decaffeinated coffee rich in CGA and trigonelline 
may offer a safe and effective dietary strategy to improve glucose control and reduce 
type 2 diabetes risk, particularly for individuals sensitive to caffeine.
DECAF AND LIVER HEALTH
As discussed in the liver section, metabolic-associated fatty liver disease (MAFLD) 
prevalence has steadily increased over the past three decades, paralleling rising rates of 
diabetes, obesity, and other metabolic disorders. While caffeinated coffee provides liver 
benefits, research shows these protective effects aren’t exclusively caffeine-dependent. 
This means consumers of high-quality decaffeinated coffee may experience similar 
liver health advantages. 
A groundbreaking 2012 study demonstrated this principle: rats fed a high-fat diet 
for 12 weeks showed reduced liver steatosis, inflammation, and fibrosis when given 
decaffeinated espresso. This was the first study to prove that caffeine isn’t essential 
for coffee’s anti-fibrotic effects, indicating that other bioactive compounds drive the 
hepatoprotective benefits (Furtado 2012). 
Two independent 2013 studies reinforced these findings, showing that both regular 
and decaffeinated coffee reduce liver fibrosis in rats (Arauz 2013; Ma 2015). Research 
by Ma and colleagues specifically examined CGA, demonstrating both preventive and 
therapeutic effects in mice fed high-fat diets. The CGA treatment not only blocked 
fatty liver development but also reversed existing hepatic steatosis. 
These studies provide compelling evidence that decaffeinated coffee retains the liver-
protective benefits of regular coffee, making it a valuable option for those who need 
to avoid caffeine but want to support their liver health.
COLORECTAL CANCER
There is solid evidence that drinking decaf can also contribute to a protective effect 
against colorectal cancer, which is a cancer that starts in the colon or rectum. There 
are an estimated 3.5 million individuals living with colorectal cancer worldwide, and 
it is the third most common incidence of cancer in the world (Ferlay 2015). Multiple 
studies have pointed to an important association between drinking decaf or regular 

354 ♦ Ildi Revi
coffee and a decreased risk of colorectal cancer (Michels 2005, Galeone 2010, Sinha 
2012).
A large epidemiological study by the Harvard School of Public Health found that a 
higher intake of caffeinated and decaffeinated coffee (2 or more cups per day) was 
associated with a lower risk of colorectal cancer-specific death and death from any 
cause (Hu 2018). Specifically, they found that patients who consumed at least 4 
cups of coffee per day had a 52 percent lower risk of CRC-specific death and a 30 
percent reduced risk of all-cause death. This means that patients who maintained 
a high level of coffee consumption both before and after diagnosis had particularly 
favorable survival rates. Researchers hypothesize that the inverse association between 
coffee and colorectal cancer risk is due to several non-caffeine factors, including the 
anticarcinogenic properties of CGA and coffee diterpenes and coffee’s influence on 
various physical and biological processes in the gut.
NEURODEGENERATIVE DISEASES
Healthy decaf coffee should contain certain amounts of CGA, caffeic acid and 
ferulic acid, which offer several neurological benefits, such as antioxidant and anti-
inflammatory effects, and may protect against harmful proteins linked to brain 
diseases. Lactones found in coffee have been shown to penetrate brain tissue (de Paulis 
2004), exhibiting notable neuroprotective and antioxidant effects in experiments on 
neuron degeneration and cell death caused by oxidative stress. More research is needed 
on other compounds, such as quinic acid. Early evidence suggests that decaf coffee 
may help reduce neurodegeneration, primarily due to its CGA and lactone content. 
These compounds appear to target various brain-related pathways and disorders.
DECAF FOR THE HISTORY BUFFS
Decaf coffee has been around for a relatively short time. Ludwig Roselius’s work in 
the early 1900s led to the commercialization of decaffeinated coffee. Over the past 
century, decaf has evolved from a niche product to a distinct coffee category in its own 
right. Although Roselius is often credited with the invention of the first decaffeination 
process, various scientists had already been experimenting with caffeine extraction 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 355
from coffee. As early as 1819, Friedlieb Ferdinand Runge was credited with identifying 
caffeine and its properties.
Roselius, a German coffee merchant, accidentally discovered the decaffeination process 
after a shipment of coffee beans was soaked in seawater, as the story goes. Subsequent 
research led to the development of the first commercial process for removing caffeine 
from coffee beans, which was patented in 1905. The motivating force behind the 
creation of the decaffeination process was personal; Roselius blamed caffeine for the 
premature death of his father, who was a coffee taster. In 1906, Roselius founded 
Kaffee HAG (Kaffee-Handels-Aktien-Gesellschaft), a company that commercialized 
his decaffeination process and sold decaffeinated coffee in Germany. The company 
gained popularity and was eventually acquired by General Foods in 1979. 
Decaffeinated coffee was introduced to the American market in 1909 under the brand 
name “Dekafa” or “Dekofa” and was further popularized in 1923 under the brand 
name “Sanka,” derived from the French term “sans caffeine,” meaning without caffeine 
(Pendergrast 1999; Weinberg 2002).
The initial methods of decaffeination were rudimentary and hazardous. Early 
solvents like brine and highly flammable substances like benzene and chloroform 
made decaffeination plants prone to fires. By the 1960s and 1970s, the industry 
had transitioned to trichloroethylene, a less flammable solvent, but this method was 
controversial, as well. A study showed that lifetime inhalation of trichloroethylene 
by mice caused tumors, raising health concerns about workers in decaffeination 
plants and questions about the healthiness of the product in general. This research 
underscored the need for natural decaffeination methods.
In response to the demand for potentially safer and more natural methods, Kaffe 
HAG developed the supercritical carbon dioxide (CO2) decaffeination method in 
the 1970s. This process uses carbon dioxide under high pressure and temperature to 
extract caffeine, making it one of the first methods considered effective and the safest 
choice for consumers. 
Another natural decaffeination method, the Swiss ®, was invented in the 1930s and 
commercialized in the 1980s. This process uses water, green coffee extract, and 

356 ♦ Ildi Revi
activated charcoal filters to remove caffeine from unroasted coffee. The Mountain 
Water Process, which was developed independently, followed soon afterward.
QUALITY OF COFFEES THAT ARE SENT FOR DECAFFEINATION
Only green coffees are sent for decaffeination, which must happen before roasting. 
Although recent trends suggest this process may evolve in the coffee industry, the 
common practice has been to send lower-quality or older coffee beans for decaffeination. 
This includes beans with defects or those from past crop years. While I do not have 
scientific proof of this, in my years of buying coffee, I have experienced this, and it 
has not gone unnoticed by others (a quick internet search would corroborate this 
opinion). Often, when I needed to purchase green decaf coffee to roast, I would read 
the descriptions and notice that the crop year listed was one to three years prior. Many 
importers buy “good enough” coffee to send to decaffeination because they believe 
that the process will alter the flavor anyway and that most people will not notice… 
or they will drink whatever is available. 
Other common justifications are economic: decaffeination can mask some flavor 
defects in inferior beans, which would fetch a lower price otherwise, and decaffeinating 
coffee is a cost passed onto the roaster. Sometimes, coffee that has not been sold and 
is getting old in the warehouse is sent to be decaffeinated to add value to what would 
otherwise be a lower-revenue coffee.
Fortunately, some companies have realized that decaf drinkers are true coffee lovers. 
After all, so many people drink coffee for the caffeine buzz, but decaf drinkers enjoy 
drinking coffee or want to finish their evening with a cup without the threat of lying 
awake from the caffeine. There is an increasing demand for high-quality decaf. A 
market report by the International Coffee Organization (ICO) in 2020 noted that 
consumer expectations for flavor consistency in decaf have driven coffee producers 
to reassess the quality of beans they send for decaffeination.
A study published in the Journal of Food Quality examined various quality attributes of 
decaf. It indicated that defects in coffee beans can indeed affect the quality of the final 
decaffeinated product. The study emphasized the importance of starting with high-
quality beans to ensure a superior taste profile after decaffeination (Petracco 2005). 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 357
Furthermore, a study in Food Research International explored consumer preferences 
and found a significant preference for decaf that retained the nuanced flavors of high-
quality beans. This has encouraged producers to use better-quality beans (Farah 2012).
Among all coffee consumers, those who choose decaf may be the most devoted. 
They love coffee for what it is, not for the caffeine. Despite caffeine no longer being 
an option, they still want to keep coffee in their lives. Many are managing medical 
conditions, following healthcare guidance, or simply prioritizing their long-term 
health. Pregnant women and others more vulnerable to trace contaminants often turn 
to decaf, not because they want less from their coffee, but because their bodies demand 
more care. These are the people who deserve the cleanest and highest quality coffee 
we can offer. With so much research linking coffee to health outcomes, producers 
have a responsibility to raise the bar, especially for decaf. It’s not enough to remove 
the caffeine; we have to make healthy, defect-free coffee that honors the needs of those 
who need it most.
PRIMARY METHODS OF DECAFFEINATION
Caffeine is more soluble than many other key compounds found in coffee. During the 
decaffeination process, a specific amount of solvent must penetrate the porous structure 
of green coffee beans. The goal is to extract primarily caffeine while leaving other 
desirable compounds intact. This selective extraction is possible due to caffeine’s higher 
solubility than other coffee components. Green coffee beans are soaked in a heated 
solvent mixture, typically between 158-212°F (70-100°C). This process primarily 
extracts caffeine molecules, along with some other compounds. Decaffeinated coffee 
beans are ready for roasting once the solvent is removed and the beans are dried. Studies 
have shown that in any of these methods listed, the decaffeinated coffee retained more 
than 80 percent of the original antioxidant levels compared to regular coffee (Farah 
2005, Hall 2018, Ho 2012, Lestari 2023). 
Regarding government regulations and the caffeine content of decaf, the U.S. FDA 
requires decaffeinated coffee to be at least 97 percent caffeine-free, and the European 
EFSA regulation is 99.9 percent caffeine-free. Both the U.S. and EU permit the use 
of methylene chloride in the decaffeination process. 

358 ♦ Ildi Revi
In addition to the processing, as mentioned earlier, coffee roasters need to have 
strict process controls to ensure that the roasting machines are clear of any leftover 
regular coffee before roasting decaf. They should also have additional post-processing 
procedures to ensure that regular coffee is never confused with decaf in packaging or 
distribution. Roasters must meet government regulations or risk mandatory product 
recalls. In March 2025, a major U.S. coffee company recalled more than 4,000 
packages of a flavored decaf coffee after discovering it contained caffeine despite 
being labeled as decaffeinated. The FDA classified it as a Class II recall, meaning the 
health risk was considered not life-threatening. For individuals with fragile health, 
including severe anxiety disorders, delicate pregnancies, or those on medications 
interacting with caffeine, even small amounts of caffeine can cause serious, potentially 
life-threatening reactions. This incident highlights a deeper issue: the coffee industry 
rarely treats caffeine as a food safety concern. Yet for many decaf drinkers, it is exactly 
that. A casual approach to caffeine shows a general unawareness of its health impact 
on those avoiding it.
FOUR MAIN COMMERCIAL DECAFFEINATION METHODS
1. Methylene chloride
2. Ethyl acetate
3. Carbon dioxide
4. Water processing
These processes are explained below, although plenty of information about them 
is available online. My focus for this chapter is on consumers’ health, workers in 
decaf facilities, and the environment. More research needs to be done into these 
decaffeination methods’ health and environmental aspects. No matter which method is 
used, green coffee undergoes many chemical changes that depend on the temperature 
and solvent used, noticeable in the color of the unroasted coffee.
Since moisture is added to each decaffeination method, the processors must constantly 
monitor their water for purity, avoid contamination by microorganisms, and ensure 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 359
correct drying to prevent mold development. Because the processes involve washing, 
many contaminants found in coffee are washed off during the hydrating phases.
One last note: Additional methods are being developed for decaffeination, but I have 
chosen not to cover them here because not only is detailed information difficult to 
find, but they are not common practices yet. Another tactic for having caffeine-free 
coffee is breeding coffee plants that do not have caffeine. Some of these are being 
actively developed and may come on the market shortly. These would be ideal for 
researching eco-health coffees and studying levels of polyphenols, trigonelline and 
organic acids.
METHYLENE CHLORIDE DECAFFEINATION PROCESS
Methylene chloride (MC) decaffeination process, sometimes referred to as the 
European method, is currently the most widely used method to decaffeinate coffee. 
It involves using methylene chloride as a solvent to extract caffeine from green coffee 
beans. The FDA mandates that the remaining levels in decaf coffee cannot exceed ten 
parts per million. In my research, I could not find any FDA recalls of decaffeinated 
coffee due to methylene chloride (MC) levels exceeding the FDA limit.
Some organizations have raised concerns about the toxicity of MC, but not everyone 
agrees it should be banned for decaffeinating coffee. The National Coffee Association 
has taken the position that the overwhelming weight of scientific evidence shows 
that drinking MC decaf is still associated with benefits, which it very well may be, 
depending on the coffee quality. 
The method involves the following main steps:
•
The green coffee beans are first steamed to open their pores.
•
The beans are soaked in methylene chloride, which binds to the caffeine
molecules and extracts them from the beans.
•
The beans are thoroughly rinsed to remove residual methylene chloride.
•
The beans are dried to a food-safe level, usually around 10 percent moisture
content, but not to exceed 12 percent.

360 ♦ Ildi Revi
The high temperatures (typically 200-230°C) used in the coffee roasting process 
help evaporate residual methylene chloride, ensuring the final roasted coffee beans 
are safe for consumption.
A 2006 study by Toci et al. noted that up to 60 percent of sucrose can be lost in this 
process for Arabica coffee.
   
Green coffee reference (left) and methylene chloride decaffeinated coffee (right)
HEALTH IMPACT:
Methylene chloride has raised health concerns due to its classification as a potential 
carcinogen by several health organizations, including the National Toxicology Program 
and the WHO. There have been concerns about the safety of consuming trace amounts 
of methylene chloride, which is metabolized readily to carbon monoxide and has been 
linked to liver damage and cancer. Despite these concerns, the FDA and European 
food regulators permit its use in decaf. I have not found any recent studies in peer-
reviewed scientific journals that have measured the residues of methylene chloride 
left on roasted coffee. 
Although additional research on MC decaf is needed, the eco-health coffee category 
should involve everyone in the production chain. Studies on workers’ health in 
methylene chloride decaffeination plants have shown that they face health risks due 
to exposure to MC. The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) 
classifies methylene chloride as a potential occupational carcinogen, with long-term 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 361
exposure linked to liver and lung cancer; acute exposure can cause neurotoxicity 
and symptoms such as dizziness, headaches, and nausea. Chronic exposure has been 
associated with more severe health issues, including liver toxicity and heart problems. 
These risks highlight the importance of stringent safety measures and regulations to 
protect workers in these environments. 
Methylene chloride may be a particularly harmful safety concern during pregnancy for 
those who work in decaffeination plants, as it may have toxic effects on the developing 
fetus at various stages. In a study of over 13,000 pregnant women, researchers assessed 
the impact of maternal occupational exposure to solvents on fetal growth. Using 
job-exposure matrices and various regression analyses, they found that exposure to 
chlorinated solvents (MC being one of several) risked lower birth weights and reduced 
head circumference (HC) in their infants. These results were strongest among women 
who worked into the third trimester of pregnancy (Enderle 2023).
ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT: 
The production and disposal of methylene chloride can contribute to air and water 
pollution. The report “Toxicological Profile for Methylene” (Agency for Toxic 
Substances 2000) highlights that methylene chloride contamination primarily 
occurs through air emissions, leading to significant environmental exposure due to 
its volatility and ability to evaporate easily. This chemical can also contaminate water 
and soil, posing risks to ecosystems and potentially entering the food chain. Regulatory 
measures are essential to mitigate these environmental impacts and protect human 
health and the environment. Proper management practices are necessary to avoid 
contaminating the environment surrounding methylene chloride manufacturing 
facilities. 

ETHYL ACETATE (EA) DECAFFEINATION PROCESS
Ethyl acetate is a compound naturally found in fruits such as bananas and sugarcane. 
It is also a chemical used in the decaffeination process. This process is often called 
“natural decaffeination” when used for coffee. Although it is considered a natural 
solvent, to be regarded as healthy coffee, the EA used for coffee must be naturally 
produced. This is an important distinction. While synthetic EA has the same chemical 
formula as naturally produced EA, it is primarily used as a solvent for varnishes and 
dry cleaning, among other things. 
The EA decaffeination process, similar to the methylene chloride method, involves 
soaking the green coffee beans in water, which softens them and makes the caffeine 
more accessible. The beans are then rinsed in a solution containing ethyl acetate, which 
selectively bonds with the caffeine. After rinsing the beans with water to remove any 
residual solvent, the beans are dried to return to their original moisture content of 
around 10-12 percent. While effective, this process may leave the beans with a slightly 
fruity aroma and taste.
Green coffee reference (left) and ethyl acetate decaffeinated coffee (right) 
HEALTH IMPACT: 
Ethyl acetate is considered safe for decaffeination, although it is highly flammable. 
It is naturally found in many fruits and is generally recognized as safe by food safety 
authorities. According to The Pocket Guide to Chemical Hazards of the National 
362 ♦ Ildi Revi

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 363
Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH), Ethyl acetate has a low flash 
point of 24°F (-4.5°C) and a boiling point of 171°F (77°C), which is well below coffee 
roasting temperatures. Consequently, any residual ethyl acetate would completely 
evaporate during roasting, ensuring no detectable solvent remains in the final coffee 
product.
Again, health concerns must extend to workers exposed to EA, who can encounter 
it through inhalation, ingestion, or skin and eye contact. The primary target 
organs affected by ethyl acetate exposure are the eyes, skin, and respiratory system. 
Correspondingly, symptoms of exposure include irritation of the eyes, skin, nose, 
and throat, as well as dermatitis and narcosis, a condition of drowsiness (or even 
unconsciousness) that impairs cognitive and motor functions. 
Another potential food safety concern warrants further investigation: the suitability of 
packaging materials for shipping and storing decaffeinated green coffee at roasteries. 
While properly processed ethyl acetate (EA) decaf coffee should contain minimal to no 
residual EA, research is needed to determine if current packaging methods adequately 
protect decaffeinated beans from potential contaminants or if they introduce any 
unintended chemicals. This is particularly important given the unique processing 
that decaffeinated beans undergo.
A 2007 study by R.A. Auras examined different plastics’ ‘barrier properties’ to 
determine ethyl acetate (EA) penetrability in packaging materials. In experiments, 
scientists measured the absorption and permeability of ethyl acetate in various 
plastics at 30°C (86°F). While this temperature is far higher than roasters should 
warehouse their green coffee, some roasteries (being hot places by nature) may reach 
that temperature for extended periods. The researchers found that the plastics took 
over 30 days to achieve stable absorption. These findings are particularly relevant for 
roasters who may store their EA green decaf for several months. 
The study tested four types of plastic: PLA (polylactic acid), PET (polyethylene 
terephthalate), PP (polypropylene), and LDPE (low-density polyethylene). The PLA 
and PET had better barrier properties than PP and LDPE. Polystyrene (Styrofoam is 
one type of polystyrene) dissolved entirely in ethyl acetate. Out of an abundance of 
caution, avoiding any direct contact between ethyl acetate (EA) and green decaffeinated 

364 ♦ Ildi Revi
coffee beans during storage and shipping is advisable. If plastic is used to store EA 
green decaf in a warehouse, whether in bags or bins, roasters should determine what 
type is used for food safety due diligence and observe flavor or other changes in the 
EA decaf over time. We discuss this further in the food safety chapter.
ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT
Ethyl acetate is considered biodegradable and less environmentally harmful than 
methylene chloride. It breaks down relatively quickly in the atmosphere and water, 
reducing its long-term environmental impact. Of course, proper disposal of any 
chemical is crucial to protect the environment, particularly by avoiding seepage into 
groundwater.
SUPERCRITICAL CARBON DIOXIDE
(Left to right) Green coffee reference, Swiss Water Process, Mountain Water Process, Ethyl Acetate 
All coffees are Colombian. The green reference and the Swiss Water are the same farm.
The supercritical carbon dioxide (CO2) process uses CO2 in a supercritical state, 
which behaves like a liquid and a gas, to extract caffeine while maintaining the beans’ 
aromatic compounds. The method uses CO2 at high pressures and temperatures to 
dissolve caffeine, and the CO2 can be easily separated from the products through 
decompression. Since CO2 is stable and inert, it removes caffeine while keeping much 

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦365
of the aromas, flavors and other compounds intact (Zabot 2020). Steps in the Process 
(from Zabot 2020):
1. Green coffee beans are moistened with water or steam to expand their cellular
structure, making the caffeine more accessible for extraction.
2. The beans are placed in an extraction vessel, and supercritical carbon dioxide
(CO2) is introduced under pressure and at temperatures where CO2 exhibits
properties of both a liquid and a gas.
3. The supercritical CO2 acts as a solvent, selectively dissolving and extracting caffeine
molecules from the coffee beans.
4. The beans are transferred to another chamber, and the pressure is reduced, allowing
the CO2 to return to its gaseous state and separate from the caffeine.
5. The gaseous CO2 is then recycled back into the system to be used again for further
extraction cycles.
6. The decaffeinated coffee beans are dried to remove residual moisture, making
them ready for roasting.
Green coffee reference (left) and supercritical carbon dioxide decaffeinated coffee (right)
The 2006 study (Toci) noted that virtually no CGA is lost in this process for Arabica 
coffee.

366 ♦ Ildi Revi
HEALTH IMPACT
The supercritical CO2 method 
is considered one of the safest 
decaffeination processes for consumers. 
It leaves no harmful chemical residues 
in the coffee, and the CO2 used is food-
grade and does not react adversely with 
the coffee components. It is a top choice 
for eco-health coffee.
However, working with high-pressure 
CO2 systems can pose safety and health 
hazards to workers. Possible risks include exposure to supercritical CO2, which can 
displace oxygen and pose breathing-related risks in poorly ventilated areas. To combat 
this, strict safety protocols, such as regular equipment maintenance and proper 
ventilation, must be followed to prevent accidents or unhealthy conditions.
ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT
The supercritical CO2 process is designed to recycle CO2, minimizing waste and 
reducing the need for continuous CO2 replenishment. Efficient recycling mechanisms 
help mitigate the environmental impact of this process. Compared to solvent-based 
decaffeination processes, the supercritical CO2 method is relatively environmentally 
friendly, as it avoids potentially harmful organic solvents. 
WATER PROCESSES
The two main commercial decaffeination facilities that use water processes are the 
Swiss Water Process® [SWP] in Canada and the Descamex Mountain Water Process 
[MWP] in Mexico. They differ, but both use water, green coffee extracts, controlled 
temperature and pressure, filtering and drying. They are differentiated mainly by their 
water sources SWP uses water from British Columbia, Canada, but MWP uses pure 
water from the Pico de Orizaba mountain in Mexico. Both companies have online 
videos that explain the process visually and in detail.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 367
The water-based decaffeination process generally involves the following steps, although 
variations exist between the two processors:
1. Green coffee beans are soaked in water to hydrate them, making the caffeine more
accessible for extraction.
2. Now containing caffeine and other coffee compounds, the water becomes a green
coffee extract.
3. This extract is used to soak a new batch of green coffee beans. Since the extract is
already saturated with flavor compounds, it primarily removes caffeine from the
new beans, leaving other soluble compounds intact.
4. The caffeine-rich water is then passed through a carbon filter, which traps the
caffeine molecules.
5. Steps 3 and 4 are repeated until the beans contain 0.1 percent or less caffeine.
6. Finally, the decaffeinated beans are dried to their original moisture content,
retaining most of their original flavors.
HEALTH IMPACT
Because the water process does not use chemical solvents, it eliminates the risk of 
chemical residues in decaffeinated coffee, making it a top choice for eco-health coffee. 
That said, more CGA is lost in this method (up to 20 percent) than in the CO2 
method. Despite this, the water process is generally considered safe for both consumers 
and workers involved in production.
ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT
The water process uses only hot water and activated carbon filters, making it an 
environmentally friendly method. It does not involve ecologically harmful chemicals. 
Also, the facilities recycle the water used in the decaffeination process, reducing 
water waste and conserving resources. By avoiding chemical solvents, the water 
process minimizes the potential for soil and water pollution from the disposal of toxic 
chemicals, as seen in other methods.

368 ♦ Ildi Revi
FINAL THOUGHTS ON DECAF 
For coffee importers and roasters, it is essential to disclose clearly the decaffeination 
method used on your marketing materials and packaging. Conducting lab tests to 
determine any residual solvent compounds in your decaf coffee allows consumers to 
make informed choices. Additionally, recording the type of coffee used, including 
green grade and a COA, and gathering customer feedback on health impacts will help 
in assessing your coffee’s safety and health benefits. Investing in scientific research 
focused on decaf coffee’s physical and mental health effects is necessary. Providing 
comprehensive information will help consumers make informed decisions.

369
7
Roasting for Health 
HIGHLIGHTS
•
Roasting with health in mind means designing roast curves that preserve or transform
bioactive compounds linked to specific health outcomes—without sacrificing flavor or safety.
•
Key compounds affected by roasting include CGA, melanoidins, trigonelline,
N-methylpyridinium (NMP), diterpenes (cafestol and kahweol), acrylamide, and polycyclic 
aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH). Each is influenced by time, temperature, and roast style.
•
Lighter roasts may retain more CGA, while darker roasts generate beneficial derivatives like
CGA lactones and NMP—creating trade-offs that depend on the intended health benefit
(e.g., antioxidant support, digestive comfort, or liver detoxification).
•
Coffee roasting also involves managing undesirable byproducts such as acrylamide and PAH.
These can be minimized through controlled roasting profiles, origin-specific strategies, and
post-roast testing.
•
Scientific research, consumer feedback, and laboratory testing all inform the CHC approach 
to roasting. Spot-testing, roast curve documentation, and compound analysis are essential
for transparency and consistency.
•
Roasting for health is not about choosing health over flavor—but about roasting with
intentionality to optimize both. It invites innovation, collaboration, and a new definition of 
coffee quality grounded in measurable outcomes.
•
Shelf life and packaging are critical to maintaining health benefits. Oxidation degrades key
compounds, so roasters must protect freshness through thoughtful packaging, oxygen control,
and storage practices.

370 ♦ Ildi Revi
If you are a coffee roaster who has read this far, you may already be exploring how 
to create holistic coffees that support health without sacrificing taste. Roasting for 
health is a practice in its own right, and this chapter offers a practical framework for 
navigating it.
This is not a step-by-step roasting manual. It offers principles, science, and real-world 
strategies for applying health goals to roast decisions. It assumes you are already fluent 
in coffee roasting techniques and that you care not just about cup quality, but about 
outcomes. How does this coffee affect the people who drink it?
Here is the paradox at the heart of this work: every roast is just a snapshot in time. A 
different week, a different coffee lot, or a change in humidity can alter the outcome. 
And yet, consistency is possible. We can source coffees with strong compound 
potential, develop intentional roast curves, and verify our process through disciplined 
feedback loops.
Roasting for health is its own field. We are still at the beginning. We do not need to 
mimic pharmaceutical labs or overpromise exact compound levels. Coffee is not a 
drug. But it is a functional food, and many of its health-promoting compounds are 
sensitive to heat, time, and technique. When we roast with intention, we can optimize 
those compounds while creating a cup that people love to drink.
If you are roasting in this category, you are not alone. I am looking for collaborators—
roasters who want to share knowledge and advance this field together. There is room 
for all of us. This is not about competition. It is about building trust and doing the 
work with care.
Roasting coffee is like navigating a shifting landscape. The idea of proprietary roast 
curves makes me smile. With so many variables, what is often called “proprietary” just 
means someone does not fully understand what is happening inside their machine. 
Chemical reactions occur differently depending on coffee species, origin, moisture 
content, processing method, and equipment. Even with the same green coffee, small 
changes in airflow, gas pressure, or thermocouple placement or functionality can 
affect outcomes. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 371
Instead of pretending that precision alone can guarantee results, we focus on 
replicability. Can you repeat a roast? Can you verify it with data-logging, color readings 
and cupping? Can you correlate your approach with occasional lab testing to make 
sure you are still in range? These are the practical tools of roasting for health.
This chapter is structured around those tools. First, we will review foundational 
information about coffee and roasting as it relates to health. Then, we will cover 
decision-making strategies that support health outcomes. Appendix 3 includes a 
worksheet for creating a roast plan, especially useful if you do not have a digital roast 
log.
Testing every roast in a lab is not realistic. But roasting blindly is not being accountable 
either. If you have lab data on a green coffee and develop a roasting plan that prioritizes 
compound preservation or formation, you can follow that curve with confidence. You 
can monitor the outcome with color and sensory data, and you can spot-check with 
third-party analysis and affordable in-house equipment. If those results hold steady, 
you have created a reliable protocol.
Coffee is an agricultural product. It is not produced in a pharmaceutical manufacturing 
facility, and we cannot control every variable. But we can be honest about our process. 
We can tell our customers the truth, which is that our quality control is consistent 
and transparent. And we can share our lab results when appropriate. This builds trust 
over time.
Some consumers, influenced by confusing messages online, question lab tests or 
suspect bias. This adds to the complexity of our jobs and requires patience. Many 
people simply do not understand how third-party testing works or what realistic 
expectations look like. It is important not to react defensively. Instead, we can take 
the opportunity to explain, clarify, and build trust over time. Transparency matters 
more than perfection. Stay calm, stay consistent, and keep doing the work. The coffee 
industry does not have the infrastructure to meet all expectations, especially when it 
comes to pharmaceutical-level precision. That is why this work must be collaborative. 
We are trying our best with the tools available, and those tools are improving.
We know some things with confidence. Melanoidins increase with roast development. 
Acrylamide levels are highest in light roasts and degrade as the roast darkens, contrary 

372 ♦ Ildi Revi
to misinformation spread by some coffee roasters. Trigonelline breaks down into 
niacin and NMP, both of which have beneficial effects. These are patterns supported 
by dozens of studies.
Other compounds, such as the diterpenes cafestol and kahweol, are harder to measure 
consistently. They are found in the coffee oil fraction, along with triglycerides and 
free fatty acids like linoleic acid, which in cupping can equate to levels of mouthfeel 
or “body.” Recall that some studies have shown C&K to raise cholesterol. Even if a 
green coffee has low body, if it tests high for CGA and trigonelline, it may be a good 
candidate for heart health. In that case, we may recommend brewing with a paper 
filter to trap the small amount of oil present, especially for people monitoring their 
cholesterol. If the coffee has a rich mouthfeel and high CGA, we can strategize a coffee 
to promote liver health. We will prioritize both CGA levels and the retention of coffee 
oils, and may suggest brewing without a paper filter. These are examples of how we 
intentionally roast for health.
You might say something like, “We roasted this coffee using research-supported 
methods to optimize compounds linked to [insert benefit].” The lab results you 
display might include CGA, trigonelline, caffeine, organic acids, niacin, essential 
minerals, fatty acids, and vitamin E. Even listing CGA levels sets your product apart 
from commodity-grade coffees.
Yes, taste matters. No one wants a coffee that tastes terrible. But this category does 
not start with low-quality green coffee. It begins with high-integrity, defect-free beans 
that meet rigorous standards and have been grown and processed with “one health” 
in mind. If you avoid roast defects, you will not sacrifice flavor. Many of these coffees 
taste exceptional.
Still, some tasters will say, “I have had coffee that tastes better.” That may be true by 
certain measures. But this coffee was not roasted to chase flavor complexity. It was 
roasted to protect and highlight compounds with health benefits. Changing the roast 
to pursue a specific flavor note may reduce the compound you are trying to preserve. 
That is the trade-off.
To those in the coffee industry: the sky is in the jar. Look at coffee through a new lens. 
Keep tasting. Keep learning. Keep sharing.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 373
PRINCIPLES OF ROASTING FOR HEALTH: A NEW FIELD
If you are selecting a coffee with a specific health goal, begin with organic, regeneratively 
grown green coffee that has been tested for contaminants. Then, build your roast plan 
based on the compounds most relevant to your goal. Every choice of temperature and 
time will influence how those compounds behave.
In roasting education, it is often said that the producer’s work ends in the hands of 
the roaster. Our responsibility as holistic coffee roasters is not just to create a pleasing 
flavor, but to preserve or enhance the health potential of the coffee.
Roasting involves a series of chemical reactions, including the Maillard reaction and 
pyrolysis, along with all the processes in between. The Maillard reaction starts with 
amino acids and reducing sugars, which create aroma precursors. Strecker degradation 
then transforms some of these into aldehydes and other volatile compounds. 
Caramelization contributes color, while pyrolysis produces compounds that define 
mouthfeel and aroma.
Roast classification is often visual, using color to describe stages such as green, yellow, 
cinnamon, and brown, followed by terms like first crack, medium, or dark. Roasts are 
typically classified visually by color changes that correlate with specific temperature 
and time points. Common basic terms include green, grassy, bready, yellow, cinnamon, 
brown, first crack, light, medium, and dark, all of which are open to interpretation. 
Industry terms like Scandinavian, city, full city, Italian, or French roast also reflect 
various roast levels. These terms can be hotly debated across regions and cultures.
What does this mean for how you read this book? It means I use these terms in a 
practical and accessible way, not as rigid definitions. If you see a term that differs 
slightly from how you would use it, please read with an open mind. I probably mean 
something close to what you think it means. This is not a technical manual. It is a guide 
grounded in science, but flexible in language. There will be some ambiguity, and I ask 
for tolerance around that. If you need exact definitions, consult the scientific literature. 
I intended this book to be a starting point for deeper study, not the final word.
This chapter explores how roast level affects bioactive compounds. For example, total 
CGA levels decrease during roasting because CGA participates in multiple reactions. 

374 ♦ Ildi Revi
Some of these transformations produce melanoidins, which carry their own health 
benefits. I recommend the paper by Rakesh Jaiswal et al. in Food & Function for a 
deep dive into CGA transformations during roasting.
Acrylamide deserves special attention. It forms early in the roast and breaks down as 
temperature and time increase. I mentioned before that light roasts generally contain 
the most. Darker roasts contain less. This is well-established. Misunderstandings like 
this often arise from confusing acrylamide with other heat-related contaminants, such 
as PAHs, furans, alpha-dicarbonyls, and similar compounds (Silva da Costa 2023). 
These are often treated as frightening or toxic in abstract terms, but they are not 
mysterious invaders. They are natural byproducts of roasting—part of the chemical 
reality of transforming green beans into something aromatic and consumable. The goal 
is not to eliminate them entirely, but to understand and manage them. They form from 
precursors in the green bean, such as proteins, peptides, amino acids, carbohydrates, 
and reducing sugars (Silva da Costa 2023). The more we share accurate information, 
the better our control strategies become. One promising study showed that pretreating 
green coffee with organic selenium reduced acrylamide by over seventy percent (Alafeef 
2021). Asparaginase pretreatments have shown similar results (Silva da Costa 2023). 
These are opportunities for collaboration between roasters and producers.
In summary, roasting for health means following the science. We base our work on 
known reactions and adapt our plans as new research emerges. This includes: 
1.	 Understanding your green coffee and its compound potential. 
2.	 Making roast decisions that support health outcomes. 
3.	 Testing, tracking, and adjusting based on results.
Ultimately, lab results confirm whether a roast achieved its goal. While the process of 
sending samples to a laboratory can be time-consuming and expensive, it provides the 
clearest feedback on whether the compounds you aimed to preserve or enhance were 
actually affected by your roasting choices. This is why it is essential to keep meticulous 
records and save roast samples for comparison.
But consistency does not depend solely on constant lab validation. It can be maintained 
through disciplined tracking, careful logging, color analysis, and regular cupping. 
Following a well-developed roast curve, monitoring your data, and checking post-roast 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 375
color gives you the framework. Cupping keeps your senses tuned to subtle changes. 
These practices form the backbone of day-to-day quality control in health-focused 
roasting. When I have followed this process and spot-checked roasts through lab 
testing, the levels of key compounds vary only slightly, confirming that this method 
keeps the coffee within a reliable range of variance.
ROASTING FOR HEALTH: A PRACTICAL WORKFLOW
This workflow supports professional roasters working within the CHC framework. 
It offers a structured approach to optimizing health-related compounds while 
maintaining quality and sensory integrity.
Identify the Desired Health Impact
Clarify what benefit you want this coffee to support. Let that goal guide every step.
1.
Identify the Desired Health Impact
Clarify what benefit you want this coffee to support. Let that goal guide
every step
2.
Review Scientific Research
Study the literature to understand which compounds relate to your health
target.
3.
Choose Green Coffee with a Health Intention
Select certified organic and regeneratively grown coffees with tested
compound potential. Use health-grade green Arabica grading standards.
4.
Plan the Roast Curve
Design your time-temperature plan to preserve or enhance the desired
compounds.
5.
Execute the Roast
Document your curve. Check post-roast color. Cup for consistency.
6.
Conduct Laboratory Testing
Spot-check with a qualified third-party lab. Screen for beneficial compounds
and contaminants.

376 ♦ Ildi Revi
7.
Analyze, Adjust, Repeat
Compare your lab results with roast logs. Make informed adjustments.
Maintain samples.
8.
Be Transparent
Share test results with customers. Build trust by explaining your approach.
9.
Collaborate and Learn
Engage with others in the field. Open-source learning strengthens the entire
category.
This is not a fixed formula. It is a foundation. What follows in the remainder of this 
chapter are deeper insights into how roasting choices affect health, which compounds 
to watch, and what practices support the long-term goals of holistic coffee production.
ANALYZING YOUR GREEN COFFEE FOR ROASTING

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 377
MOISTURE CONTENT (FOR ROASTING PURPOSES)
Moisture content affects both food safety and the behavior of green coffee during 
roasting. For roasters focused on health outcomes, knowing the exact moisture level of 
green coffee helps guide energy input, airflow adjustments, and roast profile decisions. 
Higher-moisture beans require more heat to build and maintain momentum, while 
lower-moisture beans tend to heat more quickly and are prone to scorching, tipping, 
or uneven development.
Even when green coffee meets the CHC moisture standard of 9.0–11.5 percent, 
roasters still need to adjust their approach based on specific readings. If moisture 
content drops below 9.0 percent, it may indicate staling or over-drying, which can 
lead to the loss of volatile aromatics and flavor compounds. On the other hand, if 
green coffee exceeds 12.5 percent moisture, it becomes vulnerable to mold growth 
and microbial activity. In some cases, excess moisture can trigger germination, which 
depletes sugars and other stored nutrients, both of which are important to the flavor 
and health-supportive chemistry of the roasted coffee.
During roasting, moisture content naturally declines. Dark roasts can reach as little 
as one percent moisture by weight (Bonnlander 2005), while medium roasts typically 
retain around two to three percent (Eggers and Pietsch 2001). These changes influence 
not only flavor development but also the preservation or degradation of compounds 
such as CGA, trigonelline, and melanoidins.
In this chapter, we focus on how to apply moisture data in practice, not just record it. 
Use moisture readings to help plan your charge temperature, batch size, and airflow 
strategy, ensuring your roast curve reflects the physical condition of the beans and 
supports your desired health outcomes.
WATER ACTIVITY
Water activity (aw) is a measure used in the food industry primarily for shelf life and 
preservation to predict the survival and growth of microorganisms in food. If no 
water is available in food, aw=0; if water is completely available, aw=1. Water activity 
primarily measures the amount of water that is available for microorganisms to use. 

378 ♦ Ildi Revi
Every organism has a minimum and optimal water activity for its growth. Molds that 
produce mycotoxins (like Aspergillus) are inhibited below about 0.75 aw, general mold 
and yeast have been observed with 0.61 aw, and 0.60 aw is considered the threshold for 
no growth of microbes (Graintec 2006).
Average air humidity is roughly 50-80 percent (aw = 0.5-0.8), and foods with 
lower aw tend to gain water in high air humidity while those with higher aw tend 
to lose water in low air humidity. The SCA water activity standard for green coffee 
is <0.70 aw. Conditions of 10–12 percent moisture content (approx. 0.65–0.70 aw) 
and 50–70 percent relative humidity are appropriate for safely storing green coffee 
without quality loss (Taniwaki 2001). Most coffee warehouses keep the humidity 
and temperature at a level that helps coffee’s water activity stay as constant as 
possible, usually between 57 percent ± 5 (max. 70 percent- ICO/FAO).
Water that is not bound to food molecules influences foods’ chemical, microbiological, 
and enzymic stability and changes when temperature and pressure change. This can 
be seen in coffee when coffee is (1) in the warehouse and (2) when roasting. 
Water activity readings are one of several quality assurance measures that 
must be conducted regularly. Water activity here reads 0.567 Aw.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 379
(1) In the warehouse, the compounds not bound to water inside green coffee can 
evaporate and degrade, which we see/smell/hear/taste with green coffee as it 
ages. We also observe that green coffee is better preserved in the warehouse 
when packed in a green coffee-intended plastic bag lining the burlap sack (like 
GrainPro® or Ecotact®).
(2) When roasting, we observe a couple of things related to water activity:
•
Water vapor is released (not bound) up to and through the first crack. When 
salts and some sugars are present, water activity may reduce with temperature 
increase.
•
Water activity is believed to be one of the important factors in controlling the 
Maillard reaction. The maximum browning rate has generally been observed 
in most foods at aw 0.65-0.75, and the reaction will cease at aw below 0.3 
(Wong 2015) with an optimum roasting range around aw 0.50-0.60.
COLOR AND ODOR EVALUATION
As part of green grading, observe the color of the coffee and identify any issues that 
seem unusual for that processing method. Additionally, prepare to smell the green 
coffee sample before opening the container or bag. Taking time to focus before opening 
the bag is wise because the green coffee fragrance can be fleeting, yet it contains 
valuable sensory information. Sometimes, an arrival sample has a different scent than 
a pre-shipment sample, which can indicate whether quality may have been affected 
during transport from the origin.
Normal Green Coffee Aromas by Processing Method:
Washed/Wet Process: Should exhibit clean, grassy, herbaceous aromas with subtle 
floral or fruit notes. A fresh hay-like fragrance is typical, sometimes with hints of 
cucumber or stone fruit, like apricots or peaches. Any sour, vinegar-like, or overly 
fermented odors may indicate processing issues.
Natural/Dry Process: Often displays more pronounced fruity, wine-like, or berry 
aromas, along with earthy undertones. A slight sweetness reminiscent of dried fruit 

380 ♦ Ildi Revi
is normal. However, overly fermented, alcoholic, or moldy odors suggest over-
fermentation or contamination.
Honey/Pulped Natural Process: Typically combines characteristics of both methods, 
clean but with subtle fruity sweetness. May have honey-like or brown sugar notes with 
mild earthiness, maybe even a touch of molasses.
CAUTIONARY SIGNS IN GREEN COFFEE AROMA:
•
Musty, moldy, or dank odors: Indicate moisture damage, poor storage, or
mold contamination
•
Sour, vinegar-like smells: Suggest over-fermentation or bacterial
contamination
•
Chemical or petroleum odors: Point to contamination during transport
or storage
•
Smoky or burnt smells: May indicate fire damage or improper drying
•
Absence of aroma: Could signal old coffee or moisture loss
•
Overly intense fermented notes: Even in naturals, may indicate quality
issues
Transport-Related Changes: Coffee shipped in containers can absorb odors from fuel, 
chemicals, or other cargo. Samples may develop metallic, plastic, or chemical notes 
that weren’t present at origin. Moisture changes during shipping can also intensify 
or diminish normal aromas, sometimes creating off-flavors that will carry through 
to the cup.
This is also important for quality assurance: Anything could have been transported 
with the coffee on ships and in trucks, contaminating the coffee in the process. Sniff 
checks and visual inspections are legitimate quality control measures.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 381
DENSITY
Essential considerations for density when roasting include the following:
1.	 Denser beans usually absorb heat more efficiently. Soft beans, with less substance 
in their cells, may roast unevenly, with the outside developing faster than the inside 
if the roasting temperature is too high. 
2.	 Soft beans may be prone to tipping if too much heat is applied too quickly.
Because dense beans pack in more substance and have a more complex cell structure, 
more stuff (carbohydrates, acids, lipids, etc.) is needed to develop. Regardless of 
moisture content, very dense coffees often lose momentum toward the later parts of 
the Maillard reactions and the entrance to the first crack. This means they are likely 
to stall out if you do not apply extra heat, which can mean the coffee gets flat or even 
baked. Add additional heat before the first crack, but avoid scorching.

382 ♦ Ildi Revi
ORGANIC ACIDS IN GREEN AND ROASTED COFFEE
Many 3rd party labs have organic acid testing. This is a way to evaluate how your green 
coffee may be aging, or you can test levels to confirm roast profiles were met. These can 
also help us hypothesize how compounds change during roasting based on research.
Acid in 
Coffee
Summary Information
What happens during roasting
Acetic Acid
Sucrose is a major precursor to acetic 
acid; as sucrose and polysaccharides 
decrease, acetic acid increases (coffee’s 
content may triple from green to roast). 
Acetic acid is the main acid in vinegar.
Acetic acid continues to increase 
during roasting up to 464°F/240°C.
Butyric Acid
Butyric acid in coffee is a short-chain 
fatty acid formed during fermentation, 
known for its rancid, buttery taste, and it 
typically becomes noticeable at roasting 
temperatures around 200°C (392°F).
Butyric acid can form and degrade at 
temperatures typically between 160°C 
and 260°C (320°F and 500°F).
Citric Acid
Formed during the development of 
the seed as an intermediate product 
of cellular respiration. Can be more 
sour than tart: lemon or lime-like.
Decomposes during roasting, 
starting around 350°F/176°C. 
It is less stable than malic acid 
and may lose 30-60 percent.
Formic Acid
Formic acid is the simplest carboxylic 
acid formed during coffee processing, 
fermentation, or the Maillard reaction. 
High-temperature roasting causes the thermal 
breakdown of CGA and other compounds, 
forming formic acid. The sensory 
description is often pungent and bitter.
From about 140°C to 235°C (284°F 
to 455°F). The formation of formic 
acid becomes noticeable around 
180°C (356°F), peaks at medium 
roast levels, and then diminishes 
slightly as the roast becomes darker. 
Lactic Acid
During lactic acid fermentation, lactic 
acid bacteria (LAB) convert sugars in 
the coffee fruits into lactic acid. It may 
contribute to a smoother, creamier 
taste with sweet and floral notes,
As the temperature increases, lactic 
acid concentration begins around 
180°C (356°F) and increases 
continually during roasting.
