Malic Acid
Formed during the development of 
the seed as an intermediate product 
of cellular respiration. Can be more 
tart than sour. Green apple-like.
Decomposes during roasting, 
starting around 375°F/190°C. 
It is more stable than citric acid 
and may lose 15-40 percent.
Quinic Acid
The concentration increases as CGA 
breaks down. It is about 0.4g per 100g 
in green Arabica coffee. It is cranberry-
like. It can undergo isomerization to 
form quinide, which, when brewed 
(and sitting on a hot plate, especially), 
can hydrolyze back into quinic acid and 
increase overall acidity and astringency.
Roughly doubles during roasting (avg. 
0.8g per 100g). Quinic acid forms as 
CGA break down, typically between 
175°C-235°C (347°F-455°F). 
CGA
A family of esters formed between caffeic 
acid and quinic acid, and they possess acidic 
properties due to the presence of carboxyl 
groups in their molecular structure. Impart 
taste acidity due to caffeic acid moiety. 
CGA are significant compounds impacting 
coffee. CGA are a major group of phenolic 
compounds. They are bioactive and have 
antioxidant capacity and health benefits. 
Appx. 4g-8g per 100g of green Arabica 
coffee. Impart astringency and bitterness. 
Depending on the roast degree, 
total CGA content may be reduced 
to less than 1 percent of its original 
amount (range about 0.5g-3.0g in 
Arabica and lighter roasts have higher 
CGA). As precursors of phenols and 
catechols (antioxidants with anti-
inflammatory properties), roasting 
may form unpleasant sensory notes 
from CGA degradation. Declines 
starting at 149°C (300°F).
References: (1)Lentner C and Deatherage FE, 1959; (2) Ginz M, Balzer H, Bradbury A, Maier H, 2000; (3) Farah 
A, 2012; (3) Diviš P, Pořízka J, Kříkala J, 2019.
STAGES OF ROASTING
When discussing the roasting process, dividing it into different stages is useful. Some 
divide it into two stages: the drying phase and the roasting phase. Others divide it 
into three stages: drying, browning and development. We will use four phases in 
our discussion. There is a chart that provides more detail at the end of this chapter. 
Temperatures indicated are for the coffee bean, not the drum or roasting environment, 
and, of course, they are general because of the multitude of variables (roaster type, 
coffee type, sensors, gauges, etc.).

384 ♦ Ildi Revi
1. Drying: The goal is to remove moisture from the bean without causing defects 
like scorching (discussed later).
2. Initial Browning: The goal is to apply heat appropriately during various chemical 
and physical changes to target the coffee’s intended health qualities without 
creating off-flavors.
3. First Crack: The goal is to observe the roast development (color, shape, bean pop, 
volatiles, etc.) and control the heat during this phase and after the first crack. The 
roast may end at any time after this phase.
4. Second Crack: The goal is to end the roast at the best time for the health-targeted 
compounds without charring them and to cool the beans quickly to limit further 
reactions.
BROWNING REACTIONS: MAILLARD, 
STRECKER AND CARAMELIZATION
We have already discussed three crucial chemical changes during the roasting process: 
The Maillard reaction, caramelization and pyrolysis. Each of these contributes to 
coffee’s brown color and complex flavor. Initially, the beans undergo a drying phase 
until their moisture content reaches 8-12 percent. As roasting begins, the beans 
turn yellow due to pyrolysis, accompanied by moisture and mass loss. Sucrose starts 
breaking down into glucose and fructose, along with the degradation of carbohydrates 
and amino acids. The Maillard reaction then forms new molecules that develop the 
coffee’s color and aromatic properties, typically around 320°F (160°C). 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 385
Maillard and Caramelization Recap
Definitions
The Maillard Reaction is usually responsible for cooking raw foods and 
browning them to produce new flavors. It is a nonenzymatic browning 
reaction involving the carbonyl group of a sugar (a carbon atom double-
bonded to an oxygen atom, defining the carbohydrate’s structure and 
reactivity, like aldehydes and ketones), reducing sugars, and amino acids. 
CGA are involved, too. The reaction breaks apart those compounds and 
forms new aromas, flavors, and compounds, including melanoidins.
Strecker Degradation is a reaction that occurs during roasting as part 
of the broader Maillard reaction. In this process, amino acids react with 
dicarbonyl compounds, formed from sugars breaking down, to produce 
aromatic aldehydes like methional and methylbutanal. These contribute 
to coffee’s characteristic malty, nutty, and honey-like aromas. Strecker 
degradation products also participate in forming pyrazines and other 
nitrogenous volatiles, deepening the flavor complexity of roasted coffee.
Caramelization is the browning of sugars through the application 
of heat, occurring independently of the Maillard reaction. It begins 
when simple sugars like glucose and fructose are heated to the point 
where they break down and form new flavor and color compounds. 
This process contributes to the sweetness, depth, and complexity 
of roasted coffee, especially in medium and darker roasts.
Application 
to roasting 
How quickly or slowly we pass through the Maillard reaction 
temperature range affects a coffee’s perceived sweetness, 
acidity, and mouthfeel. In general, the faster we roast, we can 
retain higher acidity, especially if we end the roast “light.” The 
slower we roast, in general, the more rounded the perceived 
acidity, sweetness and body. Yet, going too slowly with too 
little heat input can create undesirable characteristics, too.
The temperature at which caramelization begins depends 
on the rate of temperature increase. Sucrose might begin 
caramelizing at 320°F/160°C when heat rises a few degrees per 
minute (roasting), but fast temperature rises may push sucrose 
caramelization to start above 356°F/180°C (Fernandes F 2019). 

386 ♦ Ildi Revi
Temp. and Notes
Maillard may begin around 280°F/140°C and 
usually is well underway by 330°F/165 °C. 
Strecker is well underway during first crack with peak activity 
~320–392 °F/160–200 °C, especially when you start smelling 
malty, bready, and nutty notes. Continues through the development 
phase, but may taper as precursors degrade or are consumed.
Specific sugars begin caramelizing at different temperatures. 
Fructose may start at 230°F/110°C and glucose at 320°F/160°C.
Controlling the coffee bean temperature at key stages is essential to roasting. The 
changes in chemicals in a coffee roasted at a fast, high temperature will differ from those 
in the coffee roasted more slowly at a lower temperature. Also, the time/temperature 
to end the roast is an essential decision for a roaster. The end temperature has a major 
impact on health compounds. In addition to overall time and end temperature, the 
roaster operator must consider how to adjust airflow, gas, and various controls to meet 
a profile’s time and temperature goals. These decisions make up the roast curve and 
profile, which will be discussed further. 
CHLOROGENIC ACIDS: CHANGES DURING 
ROASTING CRITICAL TO HEALTH
This book has discussed how roasting conditions greatly influence the amount and 
type of CGA in coffee. Here are some details to help coffee roasters plan their roasts 
for health:
When coffee is roasted at high temperatures, as CGA breaks apart, the pieces reconfigure 
into compounds that give coffee its flavor and aroma. CGA react with other chemicals 
in the coffee through five main processes: changing shape, releasing carbon dioxide, 
moving an acyl group (a functional group found in organic compounds), forming a 
ring structure, and losing water. These reactions also contribute to the formation of 
melanoidins, which began to appear in literature around the early 2000s and have 
become one of the most important aspects of coffee’s healthfulness.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 387
Remember that melanoidins largely remain intact throughout the digestive process 
and exert their effects primarily within the gastrointestinal tract (Morales 2012), and 
phenolics have antioxidant properties (del Castillo 2002). Initially, scientists suspected 
CGA were part of these melanoidins based on a method that measures total phenolics 
(Bekedam 2008). This was later confirmed by analyzing phenolic derivatives from 
coffee brews (Bekedam 2008). The breakdown of CGA during roasting depends on 
how long and hot the roasting process is (Moon 2009). The products formed during 
roasting can range from simple acids to more complex compounds like chlorogenic 
lactones, which form when CGA loses water and creates ester bonds under high heat, 
contributing to complex flavor compounds (Clifford 2000). Additionally, CGA reacts 
with amine groups, developing flavor compounds, and thiol groups (sulfur-containing 
chemicals), enhancing the antioxidant properties of the coffee, both originating from 
peptides (Delgado-Andrade 2005; Moreira 2012).
As coffee beans are roasted more, the total amount of CGA decreases. For example, 
Moon et al. found that lighter roasting (446°F (230°C) for 12 minutes) reduced CGA 
by about 45 percent-54 percent compared to green beans, while darker roasting (at 
482°F (250°C) for 17 minutes or French roast at 482°F (250°C) for 21 minutes) 
reduced CGA by more than 99 percent. Despite this, 5-CQA remains the main CGA 
isomer in roasted coffee (Moon 2009). 

THIS PICTURE IS SUGGESTIVE, NOT LITERAL, AND SHOWS GENERALLY THE 
COMPLEXITY OF WHAT MAY BE GOING ON DURING ROASTING:


390 ♦ Ildi Revi
TELL ME HOW TO ROAST THIS COFFEE
Unfortunately, it isn’t easy to provide exact instructions on roasting a certain coffee 
to reach an outcome intended for health reasons. Common questions include, “I 
just bought a coffee from Sumatra. How should I roast it to maximize the CGA?” or 
“How do I roast this Brazilian coffee for a stomach-friendly espresso base?” These are 
good questions, but there are too many variables to consider. The usual approach to 
roasting a new coffee is:
1.	 Analyze your green coffee for size, moisture and density.
2.	 Perform an initial roast using a general profile that will give an acceptable starting 
point.
3.	 Record all roasting parameters and observations
4.	 Send it to the lab to check your target compounds.
5.	 Provide samples to a test group of individuals who might benefit from your target 
health point.
6.	 Adjust your heat application and airflow at points you believe achieve the desired 
results. 
7.	 Taste the coffee by cupping and using the intended brewing method (like espresso 
or mechanical batch brew). Record the aromas and tastes of the final roast so you 
can use them to quality-check your roasts against your roast curve.
8.	 Record all data and observations. Test again and adjust as necessary.
Later in this section, the chart summarizes some key points that science (and 
experience) have demonstrated as common in the roasting process. Roasting defects 
are described later in this chapter. 

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦391
STRATEGIES FOR ROASTING COFFEE FOR HEALTH: OVERALL GOALS
LIGHTNESS OR DARKNESS OF COFFEE
The true test of great roasting in this category is learning to focus on health while 
making intentional, flavor-driven decisions that support it. The challenge is not 
choosing between taste and health, but harmonizing both through thoughtful craft. 
This approach also invites collaboration, as each roaster can tailor choices based on 
compound goals, origin character, and consumer needs.
Referring back to the chapter on compounds can help clarify these goals. For instance, 
CGA are more abundant in lighter roasts. NMP forms during roasting, and as the 
roast darkens, CGA lactones develop and later degrade. Although total CGA levels 
drop, these transformation products still offer antioxidant and metabolic benefits.
Beyond individual compounds, roasting also affects how molecules interact. Research 
shows that Strecker aldehydes can bind with CGA, quinic acid, and its lactone form 
during roasting. These reactions form new compounds, some of which increase 
bitterness and reduce aroma availability over time (Gigl 2021). This may explain 
why some coffees, especially light roasts, seem to lose vibrancy when held warm for 
too long.
Ultimately, a roast protocol depends on your health focus, taste preferences, and 
physiological response. Here are a few compounds more strongly associated with 
darker roasts, based on their increased presence or reaction products identified in 
roasted coffee:
•
Melanoidins: Prebiotic fiber, immune support, colon health
•
CGA lactones: Antioxidant, neuroprotective, blood-sugar regulating
•
β-carbolines: Potentially antidepressant, neuroprotective, anti-diabetic

392 ♦ Ildi Revi
•
Trigonelline, niacin (B3), and NMP: Antioxidant activity, metabolic
support
•
Quinic acid and quinic acid lactone: May influence antioxidant function.
Roasting for health doesn’t limit us; it expands what’s possible. It invites curiosity, 
collaboration, and deeper skill and understanding of our craft.
A sample roaster.
A STORY OF A ROASTING MACHINE TEST FOR HEALTH
The way heat is applied during roasting influences not only flavor but also health-
related compounds. Roasters use a mix of conduction, convection, and radiation, 
with each method affecting bean uniformity and chemical reactions differently. For 
instance, conduction-heavy systems can increase the risk of scorching, which may 
generate polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH). In contrast, fluid-bed roasters 
use convection to suspend beans in hot air, reducing contact with hot surfaces and 
minimizing smoke absorption.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 393
Clean-burning fuel and complete combustion are also essential to avoid the formation 
of harmful gases like carbon monoxide, nitrogen oxides (NOx), and formaldehyde, 
especially in poorly ventilated environments. While materials like copper or steel 
affect heat transfer, we still need more research to understand how roasting techniques 
and environmental controls impact compound transformation and health outcomes.
In 2019, we hosted the Southeastern Roasters Consortium (SERC) at Ally. I mentioned 
this event in my introduction. It is a regional gathering of coffee roasting professionals, 
and in 2019, twenty-six from the East Coast participated. At that point, I had been 
working for Purity for three years, and we were actively questioning how much the 
roasting machine impacted the health profile of the coffee. 
At that event, we ran a test using three different roasting machines:
•	
A Loring (Purity’s roaster) with smokeless, air-based technology
•	
A propane direct-flame drum roaster
•	
A natural gas drum roaster with ceramic plates that radiate heat
The day before SERC, Purity’s lead roaster opened a fresh sack of Colombian CHC 
coffee and roasted a batch on the Loring to our specifications. He sealed 5 pounds of 
his roasted coffee in a one-way valve bag and brought both it and the remainder of 
the same exact sack of green coffee to SERC for a challenge activity.
The process was as follows:
1.	 Analyze the Purity-roasted coffee for color (whole bean and ground) and taste 
it via cupping protocols.
2.	 Roast the same green coffee on Ally’s propane drum roaster, attempting to 
match the Purity roast as closely as possible.
3.	 Repeat the process on the natural gas roaster with ceramic radiative heat.
4.	 Measure the color of each roast and compare it to the Purity control.
5.	 Use triangulation cupping: each set contains two identical cups and one 
different. The standard roast must be in each set: either two controls and one 
test roast, or two test roasts and one control. Can the taster identify the odd 
cup? Can they tell which one is the control?

394 ♦ Ildi Revi
Triangle tests are standard in sensory analysis and focus on identifying differences 
rather than describing flavors. While descriptors help explain the differences, the 
goal is simply to detect them. Most triangulations use historic cupping protocols for 
grinding, evaluating fragrance, steeping, and then selecting the odd cup. Some labs 
even black out windows and use red light to mask color cues.
For SERC, I created custom triangulation forms, and we ensured facilitators followed 
protocols correctly. Then we ran the tests. The results were as follows:
•
Cupping. Twenty-six cuppers (coffee roasters, not Q graders) could not
taste the difference between the propane drum roaster and Loring roasts.
About 8 people could tell the difference between the ceramic plate gas roaster
and Loring roasts. In other words, the taste was essentially the same.
Triangle testing in a red light room.
•
Color: There was a 0.5-1.0 Agtron difference in the whole bean and ground
between the Atilla and Loring roasts, and two to three Agtron between the
Diedrich and Loring roasts. In other words, the color was essentially the
same.
We sent the best of the roasts to the lab for analysis, and we could say that if most 
people tasted these three coffees, they would not taste any difference, and the coffee 
looked the same. This supports my point earlier about “proprietary roast curves” 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 395
because they would have looked different graphically due to the differences in heat 
sources and machines, yet they tasted the same.
Here are the results we got back from the lab:
Acrylamide ppb/ w/w:
Loring:  280
Natural Gas: 260
Propane:  340 
Chlorogenic Acids (6 isomers) 
Total percent (w/w):
Loring:  2.60 percent
Natural Gas: 2.14 percent
Propane:  2.26 percent
The results are curious, particularly the higher retention of the CGA by the Loring. 
The Loring and natural gas roasters were statistically the same regarding acrylamide. 
Still, the propane machine was legitimately higher, even though the color analysis 
showed them almost identical, and the taste was nearly identical in the triangulation 
results.
The key takeaways from this test were more questions about roasting equipment and 
the need for scientific studies on different machine materials, structures, and fuel types. 
Additionally, while coffees may appear similar in taste or appearance, their underlying 
chemistry can differ significantly, and those differences matter for health. Lab testing 
is essential for verifying the presence of beneficial compounds and the absence of 
contaminants. Without this data, we can’t rely on type, flavor, or roast color alone to 
assess a coffee’s health profile.
PRODUCTS AND BYPRODUCTS OF ROASTING
COLUMN HEADINGS IN THE CHART ON THE NEXT TWO PAGES:
The following two pages contain a chart summarizing the roasting process and its 
key considerations. Understanding this process is essential when roasting for health, 
as every decision impacts the final product. While many roasters are familiar with 
how these phases affect taste and quality, it is equally important to consider their 

396 ♦ Ildi Revi
potential health implications. For instance, the roasting degree can influence flavor 
profile and certain bioactive compounds’ levels. The chart includes brief descriptions 
and definitions for each section, providing a comprehensive overview of the roasting 
process from quality and health perspectives. 
Phase
Common stages of note in the roasting process have generally distinctive 
activities.
Common Temperature Range
This range of temperatures relates to changes taking place in the beans 
that have been observed and recorded in the literature on roasting. 
This is bean temperature, not hot air temperature. Heat exchange 
is determined by combining hot air temperature and airflow speed.
Heat Notes
Understanding how heat behaves during roasting is key to mastering 
the process. Heat transfer is a complex subject, and I encourage 
deeper study through advanced courses or technical readings. For 
our purposes, here are a few essential concepts:
•
Heat is energy that moves from one object to another because of a difference
in temperature. In roasting, this energy flows from the hot environment
into the cooler coffee beans.
•
Latent heat refers to the energy absorbed or released during a phase change—
such as water turning into vapor—without a change in temperature. During
these changes, the energy doesn’t increase the speed (kinetic energy) of the
bean’s particles, but instead affects the bonds between them, releasing or
storing potential energy (Fadai, NT 2017).
•
When water evaporates from the coffee bean, energy is required to break
the molecular attractions between the water molecules. This process is
endothermic (it absorbs heat energy).
•
By contrast, an exothermic process releases heat. Some chemical reactions
during roasting, such as caramelization or parts of the Maillard reaction,

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 397
may be mildly exothermic, contributing small amounts of heat back into 
the system.
Physical Notes
Roasters can observe common changes in beans, including coffee’s color, 
bean size, sounds and smells. In the roasting system, we gain insight 
from reading gauges and charting the roast progress.
Changes that occur during a phase transform one substance into another. 
A substance’s molecular composition changes when it interacts with 
other substances and when heat is applied. 
Chemical Notes
Every chemical change has an activation energy to be reached and 
maintained for the reactions to ensue. Internal energy is based 
on temperature and pressure, activating the reactions. The bean 
structure and evolution of temperature with time influence pressure 
(Fernandes F 2019).
Regarding CGA, up to 45-54 percent of CGA can be lost in a light 
roast compared to its green form, and up to 99 percent can be lost 
in darker roasts (Farah 2019). 
Decisions/Challenges
These are questions that many roasters normally ask (and to which this 
book provides answers or suggestions), and some of the common 
challenges roasters face at this phase in the roasting process.

398 ♦ Ildi Revi
Simple notetaking with a photo can be an effective way to start a holistic coffee profile.

Challenges: (see p. 76)
Avoid baking.
Avoid scorching. 
Avoid tipping.
Caramelize the sugars optimally.

Exothermal reactions begin. 
Coffee beans start producing heat

becomes available to the beans. 
(Clarke, RJ and Macrae, R).
Beans continue to get darker. 
First crack increases the beans’ swelling. 
Expansion causes internal structures

Structural carbohydrates begin 
to degrade in part by pyrolysis

become more permeable. 
Some acids are forming (e.g.,

are degrading). Perceived

The Strecker reaction converts

produce bitterness. Byproducts 
of the Maillard reaction 
also produce bitterness.
The CGA degrade more 
significantly, forming quinic and 
phenolic compounds. This can 
result in bitter compounds.
Pyrolysis reactions (organic 
substances heated in a low-
oxygen environment) begin. 
Decisions:
When I change heat or airflow, 
how is the smell or appearance 
of the bean impacted?
Do I roast faster or slower during 
the caramelization stage?

by sound, smell, visual 
or a combination? 
Do I turn the heat down or off 
during first crack? If so, when 
do I put the heat back up?
How much acidity do I want in 
my finished coffee? How much 
CGA do I want to retain?
How much past first crack 
do I want to end the roast, or 
move into second crack?

403
7.1 Roast Defects
Avoiding roast defects is essential to preserving both the flavor and health benefits of 
coffee. Defects like tipping, scorching, and facing can alter chemical composition and 
increase the formation of potentially harmful compounds.
Tipping: Tipping occurs when the tips or edges of the coffee beans become 
burned or charred. Coffee is a seed, and the area where the root would emerge 
is softer and more vulnerable. This spot is particularly susceptible to tipping 
when exposed to excessive heat or aggressive airflow.
Scorching: Scorching results from direct contact between beans and 
overheated metal surfaces. This produces unevenly roasted beans with dark, 
burned patches—often on one side or along the surface that had the most 
exposure.
Facing: Facing refers to uneven roasting, usually where one side (typically the 
flat side) of the bean becomes more roasted, or even burnt, due to prolonged 
contact with the hot drum early in the roast.
Health Impact: These three defects can lead to the formation of burnt or charred 
compounds, which may include harmful substances like PAH (Kubo 2008) and 
furans. Additionally, tipping can degrade beneficial compounds such as CGA. Overall, 
these defects not only impair the flavor and aroma of the coffee, but also compromise 
its health benefits. 

404 ♦ Ildi Revi
While these defects are easy to identify, others, like baking, underdevelopment, and 
overdevelopment, are more complex.
Some roasters argue that overdevelopment isn’t a true defect. But from a health 
perspective, roasting past the point of structural and chemical integrity, where most 
compounds have broken down and only charred fiber and oil remain, is problematic. 
If a customer insists on very dark coffee, that’s a business decision, but such coffee 
should not be marketed as health-supportive unless validated by independent lab data.
A well-developed light roast can bring unique and pleasant characteristics to a high-
quality coffee. Lighter roasts retain CGA, but they also contain higher levels of 
acrylamide. If the coffee is undeveloped, the acidity will be too sharp and overpowering; 
also, there may be vegetative, peanut, grassy or green notes, plus very high levels of 
CGA and acrylamide. People with stomach or GI issues may find that this coffee 
makes them feel unwell.
Baked coffee is a subject of debate among roasters. Some believe it results from stalling 
the roast before first crack; others attribute it to a sharp decline in the rate of rise after 
first crack. Either way, the result is coffee with muted flavor, paper-like or bready 
notes, and dull acidity, despite a normal-looking roast color. For health, the extended 
heat exposure can degrade antioxidant compounds and diminish bioactive potential. 
It’s worth experimenting on your own roaster to understand how and when baking 
occurs—and to prevent it in future batches.

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦405
