QUALITY CONTROL OVERVIEW
Developing a quality control system requires more than checking the final product—
it involves establishing clear procedures, planning, documentation, training, and 
ongoing evaluation. A well-designed system ensures consistency, improves traceability, 
and supports the goals of health-focused coffee production. While quality is distinct 
from food safety, ensuring coffee safety is a foundational part of any quality system.
KEY COMPONENTS OF A QUALITY CONTROL SYSTEM:
•	
Quality Policy Statement
Start with a formal statement that defines your company’s commitment 
to quality. This sets the tone and expectations for the entire organization.
•	
Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)
Clearly document the processes involved in production, from green coffee 
intake through roasting and packaging. SOPs serve as the backbone of the 
system and ensure consistency across team members.
•	
Quality Manual
Develop a living document that includes specifications, visual references, 
and quality standards for each product. This manual should be updated 
as new green coffees arrive or products change. Keep it accessible to the 
production team for reference.
•	
Work Instructions and Tools
Provide detailed instructions, worksheets, and visual aids to guide the team 
through each product’s unique process. These tools give staff a roadmap to 
follow and help avoid errors or inconsistencies.
•	
Recordkeeping and Review
Maintain thorough documentation of all quality-related activities. Regularly 
review records with the team to reinforce standards, spot trends, and identify 
areas for improvement.
Although holistic coffee is a different category from “specialty coffee,” focused on taste, 
sensory evaluation is still a powerful quality assurance tool. When roasting to meet 

408 ♦ Ildi Revi
health-related compound targets, any deviation in the process will often manifest as 
a change in flavor. In this way, taste becomes an indicator that something may be out 
of spec, even when health is the primary focus.
A trained team that knows your standard roasts by taste is invaluable. That’s why it’s 
important to maintain a tasting outcomes database. Detailed recordkeeping helps 
verify quality, especially when it’s difficult to recall the nuances of past lots. Over time, 
these records become a valuable reference library for maintaining product consistency.
Documentation also helps connect what the team tastes to what happened during 
roasting. Once your roast profiles are well established, use a descriptive cupping 
or tasting method to assess whether each batch matches the control (spec) coffee. 
Differences in flavor can signal that something changed—whether it’s in the roast 
curve, green coffee, or environmental conditions.
Using the tasting notes section of the Roast Data Chart (in Appendix 2) is especially 
helpful. Over time, patterns emerge, such as a particular flavor note showing up after 
a specific adjustment in the roast. These observations help roasters fine-tune their 
craft and build a more responsive, data-informed approach to quality.
For this section of the Roast Data 
Chart, find one or two notes to 
articulate and record. As you change 
the roast, it helps to compare one 
curve to the other. Also, once you 
have your set profile for a coffee, 
you can keep checking back on your 
original notes to help you maintain 
quality.
If you want to document additional information about the coffee, such as origin, 
specifics about the farm, processing and drying method, exporter, bag numbers, 
importer, dates (harvest, shipping, and arrival), and other details, you could use the 
back of the Roast Data chart for this information. Create a grid on the back to fill in 
such data.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 409
Also, a comment about getting samples from importers: After evaluation, professional 
cuppers should give feedback to the person who supplied the coffee sample, 
generally within a few days. This will help improve communication between you 
and your supplier—it is best practice and good business. It also helps to develop 
your relationships with producers, who will trust your input and communication on 
health issues.
SHELF LIFE AND PACKAGING
The shelf life and packaging of roasted coffee are important factors that influence its 
flavor profile and health properties. Staling is a term used to describe the deterioration 
of coffee quality over time. Chemically and physically, roasted coffee undergoes several 
changes that lead to staling, affecting taste and health. One of such causes of staling 
is oxidation, which involves the reaction of oxygen with coffee compounds.
OXYGEN
Oxidation is a major factor in the staling of roasted coffee, with oxygen being the 
primary culprit, followed closely by temperature and moisture. Secondary factors 
include how coffee interacts with its environment, such as roast level, porosity, surface 
area (grind size), light exposure, and water activity.
Oxidation greatly affects the freshness and health properties of roasted coffee, engaging 
lipids, antioxidants, and various volatile aroma and flavor compounds. When these 
compounds are exposed to oxygen, they begin to degrade. This degradation can result 
in the formation of peroxides, which serve as precursors to rancid flavors. In coffee 
beans, lipids (or fats) are particularly susceptible to oxidation, generating peroxides 
and free radicals. This not only diminishes flavor, leading to off-flavors like cardboard 
or stale notes, but also risks the formation of unhealthy compounds. Consuming 
oxidized coffee can expose people to these degraded and harmful substances.

410 ♦ Ildi Revi
•
Reducing the oxygen content to 0.5 percent in packaging can extend shelf
life by 20 times. Each 1 percent increase in oxygen leads to a 10 percent rise
in the degradation rate.
•
Lipid oxidation can begin within 24 hours of roasting and grinding, resulting
in rancidity.
•
Oxidation reactions are accelerated by water, light, heat, and oxygen.
•
Grinding coffee increases its surface area, speeding up the degradation rate.
•
Darker roasts stale faster because the cells break open more, releasing lipids
that are exposed to oxygen. However, this does not mean you should under-
roast or bake coffee.
Temperature influences the staling of coffee through both chemical and physical forces. 
The storage temperature affects the beans as significantly as the roasting temperature, 
as chemical reactions persist. The rate of chemical reactions doubles with every 18°F 
(10°C) increase in temperature.
Also, the physical changes in coffee over time involve the loss of volatile oils and 
gases. Freshly roasted coffee beans release carbon dioxide in a process called degassing. 
Proper packaging is essential to manage this degassing while protecting the beans from 
oxygen and moisture. Common packaging solutions include one-way valve bags that 
allow carbon dioxide to escape without letting oxygen in, significantly slowing down 
the staling process. Vacuum-sealing or nitrogen-flushing can also eliminate or reduce 
oxygen exposure, thus preserving the coffee’s freshness and health benefits.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 411
Optimal packaging and storage conditions play a vital role in maintaining the 
health benefits of roasted coffee. By minimizing oxygen exposure, reducing light 
exposure, and controlling moisture, coffee’s shelf life can be extended, and its health-
promoting compounds, such as antioxidants, can be better preserved. This ensures that 
consumers enjoy the best possible flavor and the beneficial properties of fresh coffee. 
Understanding and mitigating the factors contributing to roasted coffee’s staling is 
essential for maintaining the coffee’s quality and health attributes. 
In grocery and food safety, shelf life varies considerably for different products. It 
determines when a product remains safe to consume and retains its desired sensory, 
chemical, physical, and microbiological properties. For roasted coffee, shelf life is 
typically determined based on staling rather than spoilage. Staling in coffee is a gradual 
process driven by chemical and physical changes that detract from its freshness and 
flavor profile. Staling is a measure based on time. Defining the science behind staling 
is problematic. 
After roasting, coffee undergoes a series of chemical and physical changes that make 
it very difficult to control or replicate conditions precisely. That’s why experimental 
repeatability and clean data analysis in coffee research are often more complicated 
than they seem.
When we talk about how coffee degrades over time, it’s helpful to distinguish between 
staling and spoilage. Staling is the gradual loss of pleasant, fresh flavors and the rise of 
flat or unpleasant notes. Spoilage, on the other hand, refers to more immediate and 
severe breakdown, usually involving microbial activity, that can make food unsafe to 
consume.
Roasted coffee, thanks to its low moisture content, is relatively resistant to spoilage 
compared to other foods. Most microorganisms simply can’t thrive in its dry 
environment. However, spoilage is not impossible. If roasted coffee is stored in high-
humidity conditions or the packaging is compromised, moisture can seep in. In rare 
cases, this could allow mold to grow, especially if the coffee sits in warm, uncontrolled 
warehouse environments for extended periods before reaching store shelves.
One possible contributor to this risk is a method some large roasters use called water-
quenching, spraying or misting the beans with water immediately after roasting to cool 

412 ♦ Ildi Revi
them quickly. When done properly, it can be safe and effective. But if not carefully 
monitored, it can add just enough moisture to the beans to accelerate staling or even 
promote mold growth, particularly in coffees bound for long-term storage or the 
supermarket supply chain. While it’s rare, as of this writing, there are no reports of 
mold-related coffee recalls in the FDA database; it’s a risk worth understanding and 
managing.
When it comes to shelf life, coffee is generally judged by staling, not spoilage. But 
staling is tricky to define. Unlike spoilage, which has obvious signs like visible mold or 
unpleasant odors, staling creeps in subtly. Oxidation, light exposure, and the natural 
loss of volatile compounds all contribute. You may not see it happening, but you’ll taste 
it: muted aromatics, dull flavors, and a general sense that the coffee has “lost its edge.”
The real challenge is that staling is a subjective attribute. What one person considers 
stale, another might find perfectly drinkable. There’s no clear threshold—no line 
where good becomes bad. That’s why sensory evaluation is so important. Tracking 
the taste and aroma of your coffee over time and documenting those changes is the 
most reliable way to understand how your coffee holds up. Without that empirical 
feedback, it’s nearly impossible to predict when a roast has passed its prime.
Research indicates that CGA in roasted coffee diminishes over time, particularly 
during storage in retail packaging. The Król study we cited earlier found that during 
a 12-month storage period, polyphenolic compounds, including CGA, significantly 
decreased due to degradation. This degradation impacts the total bioactivity of the 
coffee. Another study highlighted that CGA are thermolabile (can undergo chemical 
or physical changes in response to elevated temperatures) and degrade significantly 
during roasting. This degradation continues during storage, especially if the coffee is 
exposed to oxygen and light (Farah 2019). 
PACKAGING MATERIALS
Moisture and water activity (free and bound water) correlate positively with staling, 
meaning more free water leads to faster staling. This has also been linked to quicker 
degassing (CO2 release), which is generally associated with flavor loss. Higher moisture 
levels can also support microbial activity, so it is critical to control the residual humidity 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 413
of your final product, keeping it around 3 percent or less. This is one major reason 
why packaging materials matter.
Flexible packaging is designed to be an economical way to package products and 
minimize shelf and shipping space. It also provides a barrier to light, oxygen and the 
harmful effects of environmental conditions that may affect coffee flavor (like paint, 
fumes, outside smells, etc.). The advantage of flexible packaging can be found in 
shipping costs and shelf space taken. This can compensate for environmental costs 
when considering packing type and the ability to recycle. 
Gases released by freshly roasted coffee can inflate a bag without a valve, causing it 
to leak and allowing oxygen to enter, which stales the coffee. After roasting, coffee 
releases up to 1000 cc of CO2 per pound. Grinding releases about half of this gas. 
Fresh whole-bean coffee continues to release gas for several weeks, depending on the 
roast level. Bags of freshly roasted whole bean coffee without a valve will deform and 
are likely to breach, letting oxygen in and causing staling. The bags could burst if you 
pack at a high elevation and ship to sea level.
Flexible packaging, while convenient and versatile, can pose health concerns due to 
the presence of toxic chemicals that may leach into food and beverages. Any reputable 
packaging manufacturer will follow regulations for food-safe materials; a laboratory 
tests their packaging to demonstrate no harmful compounds are involved and disclose 
this information to a roaster as part of their food safety plan. The concern is that 
chemicals, such as phthalates and bisphenol A (BPA), are in the packaging, although 
they should not be in coffee packaging. These chemicals have been linked to various 
health issues, including cancer, congenital disabilities, immune system compromise, 
and endocrine disruption. 
Since the late 1990s, PFAS, or per- and poly-fluoroalkyl substances, have become a 
major concern. They comprise a large group of manufactured chemicals that have 
been widely used in various industrial applications and consumer products since the 
1940s. While they are valued for their resistance to heat, water, and oil (making them 
useful in a variety of contexts), their environmental persistence and potential health 
risks have raised significant alarm. PFAS have been utilized in numerous products, 

414 ♦ Ildi Revi
including non-stick cookware (e.g., Teflon), water-repellent fabrics, stain-resistant 
carpets, food packaging, firefighting foams, and certain industrial processes.
PFAS are characterized by their strong carbon-fluorine bonds, one of the strongest 
in organic chemistry. This makes them highly resistant to breakdown in the 
environment… and the human body. This has earned them the nickname “forever 
chemicals”.
Research has linked PFAS exposure to various health issues, including liver damage, 
thyroid disease, decreased fertility, developmental problems in children, increased 
risk of certain cancers, and immune system impairment. Given these potential health 
impacts, regulatory agencies increasingly focus on limiting their use and managing 
environmental impact. In the US, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has 
established health advisories and is working on regulatory standards for PFAS in 
drinking water.
Efforts to remediate PFAS-contaminated sites and water sources are complex. 
Techniques being investigated and implemented include advanced filtration methods 
such as activated carbon, ion-exchange resins, high-pressure membranes, and oxidation 
and thermal processes.
The environmental impact of plastic pollution from flexible packaging is significant, 
contributing to long-term ecological damage. Although potentially more 
environmentally friendly, non-flexible items like cans and jars are harder to ship, 
more likely to be damaged, take up shelf space and increase costs.
SUSTAINABLE PACKAGING
As we pursue sustainable solutions in coffee, we have to carefully balance product 
preservation and safety with environmental goals. Packaging must protect against 
contamination from both the packaging itself and the external environment, while 
maintaining the coffee’s freshness throughout modern supply chains. In other words, 
we want packaging that cares for the earth without creating secondary problems. 
Roasted coffee needs to arrive at its destination fresh, safe, and intact, whether it’s 
traveling across a city or around the globe.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 415
Sustainable packaging is an essential aspect of modern environmental stewardship, 
aiming to minimize the ecological footprint of packaging materials. One key strategy 
is source reduction, which involves using fewer materials to create packaging and 
reducing waste from the outset. This can be achieved by removing unnecessary 
packaging components, reducing the thickness of materials, and implementing a 15 
percent source reduction target. 
Biodegradable and compostable packaging materials offer another sustainable solution. 
These materials break down naturally over time, reducing the burden on landfills and 
the environment. Renewable packaging is another aspect of sustainability. It involves 
the use of materials made from renewable resources such as plant-based plastics, paper 
from sustainably managed forests or materials made from agricultural wastes. These 
materials could reduce dependence on finite resources and lower the carbon footprint 
associated with packaging production. It is important to note that proper disposal and 
processing facilities are necessary to realize their full benefits.
Recyclable and reusable packaging further contributes to sustainability by extending 
the life cycle of materials. Recycling involves processing used materials into new 
products, conserving resources, and reducing waste. Reusable packaging, by definition, 
can be used multiple times before disposal, minimizing the need for single-use 
products. Educating consumers and businesses about the benefits of sustainable 
packaging is vital, as informed choices can drive demand for eco-friendly options. 
Most roasters aim to achieve cost savings or at least cost neutrality with sustainability 
practices. 
Nevertheless, sustainable packaging solutions are not without issues. Using food 
crops for bioplastics can raise concerns about land use and food security. The massive 
corporate farms that grow the products that make these bioplastics (corn, sugarcane, 
cassava, potatoes) use conventional farming practices. The FAO has raised concerns 
about soil degradation, noting that significant portions of the world’s agricultural 
soils are affected by erosion, contamination, and loss of organic matter. They also 
emphasize the urgent need for the adoption of sustainable soil management practices, 
with a focus on farms like the ones that produce crops for bioplastics.

416 ♦ Ildi Revi
A 2015 FAO report warns that global topsoil could be significantly depleted if current 
soil degradation rates remain consistent over the next 50 years. Similarly, the United 
Nations Convention to Combat Desertification reports that 24 billion tonnes of 
fertile soil are lost annually due to unsustainable agriculture. Are we trading our need 
for biodegradable packaging at the expense of the soil and farm ecosystems? I look 
forward to seeing future studies and statistics on this.
Biodegradable flexible packaging offers clear environmental advantages, particularly 
for companies focused on sustainability. Materials like polylactic acid (PLA), derived 
from renewable sources such as corn starch or sugarcane, are among the most common 
options. These bioplastics are generally recognized as safe and are approved for food 
contact under many regulatory frameworks. However, the long-term safety of these 
materials—particularly under conditions specific to coffee storage—remains an area 
in need of further research.
While PLA and similar materials are inert under normal conditions, additives used 
to improve flexibility, heat resistance, or biodegradability may vary by manufacturer. 
Some of these compounds could theoretically leach into coffee, especially when 
exposed to elevated temperatures, humidity, or prolonged storage times. At this 
point, however, there is no published scientific evidence indicating that PLA or other 
biodegradable packaging materials pose a food safety risk in the context of roasted 
coffee.
I feel strongly about spreading unnecessary alarm, especially since much of the concern 
online is driven by speculation rather than data. Still, given the complexity of coffee’s 
chemical makeup and its sensitivity to oxygen, moisture, and light, more independent 
research is warranted to verify the integrity and safety of biodegradable packaging 
over time.
Another concern is that biodegradable packaging may not provide the same protection 
against oxygen and moisture as traditional packaging. This can affect the shelf life 
and quality of the coffee, potentially leading to spoilage or a degradation of flavor 
and aroma. Some biodegradable coffee bags include degassing valves to release carbon 
dioxide while preventing oxygen ingress. The valves should also be made from safe, 
biodegradable materials.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 417
Under certain conditions, such as high humidity or temperature fluctuations, the 
integrity of biodegradable packaging can be compromised. This can lead to premature 
degradation of the packaging material, affecting the coffee’s safety and quality. 
Mitigation strategies for biodegradable packaging include:
•
Ensuring materials are tested and certified for food safety by relevant
authorities
•
Storing coffee in cool, dry conditions to maintain packaging integrity
•
Educating consumers about proper disposal and storage to maximize
benefits and minimize risks
NITROGEN OR CARBON DIOXIDE FLUSHING
When storing coffee, the goal is to have less than 2 percent residual oxygen level. 
Flushing coffee with nitrogen or carbon dioxide before packing can preserve its 
freshness and extend its shelf life. This process, known as gas flushing, involves 
replacing the oxygen inside the packaging with an inert gas like nitrogen or carbon 
dioxide. Nitrogen is particularly effective because it is inert, non-reactive, and heavier 
than oxygen, which helps to displace oxygen and prevent oxidation. This method 
helps to maintain the coffee’s flavor and aroma by minimizing the chemical reactions 
that cause staling. Carbon dioxide can also be used, especially since freshly roasted 
coffee naturally releases CO2, which can help create a protective atmosphere inside 
the packaging.

418
Part of the coffee display at an American supermarket, 2016.
Packaging Recap
Packaging materials play an essential role in preserving coffee quality, with flexible packaging 
offering economic and practical advantages despite potential health and environmental concerns. 
Sustainable packaging solutions, including biodegradable and recyclable materials, aim to reduce 
environmental impact but face land use issues and potential food safety concerns. The industry 
continues to balance the need for effective coffee preservation with sustainability goals, exploring 
innovations in materials and techniques to address these complex challenges.

419
8
Food Safety
2
HIGHLIGHTS
This chapter looks at food safety in coffee production and roasting, focusing on legal requirements, 
best practices, and industry responsibility. Written primarily for coffee industry professionals, 
including roasters, green coffee buyers, quality control specialists, it also offers valuable insights 
for anyone interested in understanding the industry’s approach to food safety.
Food safety is not just a quality control measure—it is a legal and ethical obligation that impacts 
public health. While coffee is often considered low-risk, contamination can occur at multiple 
points along the supply chain, from farming and processing to roasting and distribution. The Food 
Safety Modernization Act (FSMA) mandates that food businesses implement preventive controls 
to mitigate risks associated with biological, chemical, and physical hazards. The chapter explores 
how these regulations apply specifically to coffee and how industry professionals can comply. 
Not only for CHC but all coffee businesses must stay ahead of regulatory changes, implement 
proactive safety measures, and foster a strong food safety culture. 
2	  This chapter originally was much longer—too long for this book. I’m planning to write a complete guide on food safety 
practices for coffee next. Also, I restated the acronyms for the agencies I’ve cited in the past for ease of reading, so you don’t 
have to go back and look up what they are.

420 ♦ Ildi Revi
In holistic coffee, every sip tells a story of care and quality control. For the community 
interested in this category, coffee enthusiasts, wellness professionals, and industry 
experts, it is no surprise that food safety is a clear and necessary goal as part of the 
whole picture of healthy coffee. Ensuring the safety and integrity of our products is 
both a responsibility and a legal obligation that directly impacts consumers’ health 
and well-being.
This chapter is written primarily for coffee industry professionals, from roasters and 
green coffee buyers to quality control specialists and business owners. When I say 
“you,” I am speaking directly to those involved in producing, handling, and selling 
coffee. As you read, I encourage you to think about someone in your life who has 
fragile health, perhaps an older adult, a friend with a compromised immune system, 
or someone battling a chronic illness. Imagine that this person will be drinking the 
coffee you produce. From the moment the green coffee arrives at your facility to the 
time it is boxed and shipped out, you are not only responsible for maintaining its 
quality but also its safety. Food safety is not just about following regulations, but is 
about taking ownership of the product you put into the world and ensuring that it is 
as safe as possible for everyone who consumes it.
Worker health, although equally important, falls under the purview of occupational 
safety laws, such as OSHA in the U.S. and similar agencies abroad. This chapter 
focuses on food safety, but it’s worth remembering that in a One Health model like 
Circular Health Coffee, the well-being of the people producing the coffee matters too. 
A truly health-focused coffee system cares for everyone involved.
An alarming number of foodborne illnesses have resulted in unnecessary injury and 
death, many of which could have been prevented by proper food safety practices. 
While coffee production and roasting have not been directly linked to major outbreaks, 
coffee is still a food product and must be treated as such. This means proactively 
implementing preventive controls to protect our products, staff, and consumers from 
biological, chemical (including allergens), and physical hazards.
Throughout my career, I have visited dozens of coffee farms and roasteries of varying 
sizes, and I have witnessed firsthand how food safety issues persist throughout the 
supply chain. I once watched as a cat freely roamed a coffee drying bed, using it as a 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 421
litter box. Then workers simply sifted out the solid waste, leaving no way to determine 
which coffee seeds had been contaminated by the cat’s urine. 
Cat peeing in coffee
On another farm, a motorcycle with rakes attached was used to turn drying coffee 
cherries, kicking up dust and potentially contaminating the cherries. I have lost count 
of the times I have seen chemical sprayers being washed near water reservoirs, allowing 
pesticide and herbicide runoff to flow directly into the same water used to wash and 
pulp harvested coffee. There are unavoidable environmental concerns, such as birds 
flying over drying patios or perching above processing areas.
At the roastery level, food safety lapses are just as concerning as they are at origin. I’ve 
witnessed roasters eating or drinking while working, then running their hands through 
coffee in the cooling tray without washing them. I’ve seen restrooms so unsanitary 

422 ♦ Ildi Revi
it’s hard to imagine they pass inspection. In trendy, repurposed industrial buildings, 
peeling paint, questionable water from aging pipes, and coffee sacks stored directly 
on the floor all raise red flags for contamination risk. These examples reflect a broader 
issue: the coffee industry still has work to do in meeting basic food safety standards.
While the industry has made significant strides in flavor development from green 
coffee selection to roast profile precision, those efforts can be undermined by lapses 
in food safety. A beautifully roasted coffee loses its value if it’s handled in unsanitary 
conditions or contains contaminants that could harm the consumer. Quality isn’t 
just what’s tasted in the cup; it’s also what’s tested behind the scenes. True excellence 
requires both: a sensory experience that delights and a chemical and microbial profile 
that protects.
We have a responsibility to protect the most vulnerable people who drink our coffee—
those with compromised immune systems, chronic illnesses, or anyone relying on safe, 
clean food to support their health. This chapter explores the core principles of food 
safety in roasting and production, from legal requirements and preventive controls to 
best practices that support both flavor integrity and consumer well-being.
A GLOBAL CONCERN
Food safety laws have evolved to ensure accountability in food production. In recent 
years, global food safety agencies have increasingly collaborated to create unified 
standards for food production, trade, and public health. As the world becomes more 
interconnected, contamination or unsafe practices in one region can quickly impact 
global trade, consumer health, and economic stability. In response to supply chain 
vulnerabilities and the growing demand for transparency, international agencies 
and governments have strengthened efforts to align food safety standards, improve 
compliance, and protect public health across borders.
The Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA), enacted by the FDA and U.S. Congress, 
holds food manufacturers, including coffee roasters and importers, responsible for 
preventing contamination. Some professionals assume coffee is inherently safe, while 
others are simply unaware of the laws that apply to them. But whether we acknowledge 
them or not, these laws exist, and compliance is mandatory. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 423
FSMA is built on seven core rules, each targeting a specific point in the food supply 
chain. These include the Produce Safety Rule (for farms), Preventive Controls Rules 
(for human and animal food processing), the Foreign Supplier Verification Program 
(for importers), the Sanitary Transportation Rule, and the Intentional Adulteration 
Rule (for food defense). Two additional rules address the accreditation of third-party 
auditors and the lab testing process for food safety compliance. While not every rule 
applies to coffee, together they form a comprehensive framework designed to prevent 
contamination and ensure accountability from farm to final product.
As FSMA governs food production in the U.S., similar regulations exist worldwide to 
ensure that coffee and other foods meet strict safety and quality standards. The FDA 
enforces FSMA rules for both domestic and imported goods, requiring hazard analysis, 
preventive controls, and supplier verification. In the European Union, EFSA oversees 
compliance with Regulation (EC) No 852/2004 on hygiene and contamination 
prevention, and Regulation (EC) No 178/2002, which sets traceability and general 
food law principles. 
Throughout this book, I have referenced the FAO and WHO, which are central in 
shaping international food safety policy. To create a global standard for food safety, the 
WHO and the FAO developed the Codex Alimentarius, a collection of internationally 
recognized food safety guidelines and standards. The Codex includes coffee-specific 
recommendations, such as risk assessments and control measures for contaminants 
like ochratoxin A (OTA). It emphasizes risk analysis, assessment, and management 
while outlining best practices for minimizing hazards throughout the supply chain. 
Many national food safety regulations, including those in the United States, European 
Union, and other major coffee markets, incorporate Codex guidelines into their 
frameworks, reinforcing its influence on global food safety policies. 
Many countries also maintain their own national food safety frameworks that align 
with FSMA principles and, in some areas, go further. In Canada, the Safe Food for 
Canadians Regulations (SFCR) require food businesses to develop written food safety 
plans, maintain full traceability, and implement preventive controls. Australia and 
New Zealand enforce food safety under the Australia New Zealand Food Standards 
Code, which mandates hygiene practices, food processing standards, and risk-based 
assessments for domestic production and imports.

424 ♦ Ildi Revi
In Asia, Japan enforces food safety through the Food Sanitation Act, with the aid 
of two regulatory bodies: the Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare (MHLW) 
and the Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries (MAFF). These agencies 
regulate pesticide residue limits, contaminant thresholds, and food import standards. 
South Korea maintains strict controls through the Ministry of Food and Drug Safety 
(MFDS), which applies Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP) 
protocols and enforces MRLs across food categories, including coffee.
In Latin America, Brazil regulates agricultural food safety through the National Health 
Surveillance Agency (ANVISA), which oversees testing for contaminants and ensures 
compliance for both domestic and export consumption. Colombia relies on the 
National Institute for Food and Drug Surveillance (INVIMA) to monitor microbial 
contamination, pesticide residues, and food processing safety. Peru and other coffee-
producing nations follow similar frameworks, often modeled on Codex Alimentarius 
guidelines to ensure alignment with international trade requirements.
Across Africa, Kenya enforces food and crop safety standards through the Agriculture 
and Food Authority (AFA), which regulates agricultural inputs, pesticide use, and 
post-harvest quality control. Like many export-oriented systems, the AFA places a 
strong emphasis on compliance for international markets.
Major coffee producers in Southeast Asia, including Vietnam and Indonesia, also 
maintain national agencies responsible for enforcing food safety standards for both 
export and domestic markets. These agencies establish pesticide residue limits, 
contamination thresholds, and hygiene standards in accordance with Codex guidelines 
and the regulatory requirements of key importing countries.
Despite these frameworks, enforcement remains inconsistent across many coffee-
producing regions. While countries such as Brazil, Colombia, and Vietnam have 
established relatively strong systems, numerous smaller or lower-income producers 
continue to confront ongoing challenges due to inadequate funding, infrastructure 
deficiencies, or understaffed agencies. In certain areas, informal markets function 
entirely outside the scope of regulatory oversight, complicating efforts to monitor food 
safety. Even where legislation is in place, enforcement may be impeded by political, 
technical, or financial constraints.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 425
To strengthen global compliance, many governments partner with international 
organizations. The FAO and WHO play key roles in helping countries build food 
safety capacity, develop emergency response plans, and train personnel. Targeted 
programs have supported regulatory development in countries such as Laos, Fiji, 
Thailand, Panama, and Bangladesh. Regardless of domestic capacity, however, 
exporters must still meet the food safety standards of their target markets. Coffee 
sold into the U.S. or the E.U. must comply with FDA or EFSA regulations or meet 
Codex standards where applicable.
Despite differences in regulatory strength, the shared global objective is clear: prevent 
contamination, ensure food safety, and protect public health. Understanding these 
structures is essential for coffee professionals operating in an international supply 
chain, not just for compliance, but for credibility, traceability, and long-term viability. 
As consumer demand for clean, transparent, and responsibly produced coffee grows, 
alignment with international food safety standards becomes a cornerstone of quality.
These global frameworks emphasize prevention over reaction. Good Agricultural 
Practices (GAPs) form the foundation of safe production, promoting hygiene, proper 
drying and storage, and the responsible use of inputs. During transport, sanitary 
handling helps reduce exposure to allergens, fuel fumes, and moisture. In the roastery, 
Good Manufacturing Practices (GMPs) ensure that processing and packaging occur 
in clean, well-managed environments.
By applying Codex guidance, GAPs, GMPs, and sound handling procedures 
throughout the supply chain, coffee producers and roasters not only meet regulatory 
expectations, but also demonstrate a meaningful commitment to consumer trust, 
health, and product integrity. The following sections explore how each category of 
food safety hazard can be proactively addressed to uphold these standards in practice.
FIRST: FOOD SAFETY BASICS
Ensuring food safety in coffee production requires a systematic approach that addresses 
biological, chemical, and physical hazards at every stage of the supply chain. From farm 
to roastery, food safety regulations and best practices exist to prevent contamination, 
mitigate risks, and maintain the highest standards for public health. While coffee is 

426 ♦ Ildi Revi
often considered a low-risk food, its long journey from cultivation to consumption 
introduces multiple points of vulnerability that must be controlled through science-
based preventive measures.
Before diving into the specific types of food safety risks in coffee production, it’s 
important to understand the regulatory framework that guides how these risks must be 
addressed. In the United States, FSMA requires most food facilities to adopt a system 
called HARPC, Hazard Analysis and Risk-Based Preventive Controls. HARPC is 
often seen as an evolution of the older HACCP system, which has long been used in 
food safety planning. But rather than replacing HACCP entirely, FSMA builds on 
it. Food safety trainers often say, “Don’t throw out your HACCP plan—convert it.” 
That means shifting from a focus on reactive control points to a broader, proactive 
system of preventive controls throughout the supply chain.
Under FSMA, the HARPC framework requires food facilities to:
•
Identify hazards through a comprehensive risk analysis.
•
Implement preventive controls for biological, chemical, and physical risks.
•
Monitor control effectiveness and take corrective actions if necessary.
•
Maintain verification records to document compliance.
•
Reassess food safety plans every three years or when significant changes
occur.
Unlike HACCP, which focuses primarily on Critical Control Points (CCPs) where 
hazards can be reduced or eliminated, HARPC covers a broader range of risks and 
applies preventive measures even before CCPs come into play. While HACCP tends 
to focus on specific production steps (e.g., pasteurization), HARPC requires facilities 
to evaluate risks from suppliers, storage, personnel practices, allergens, and facility 
layout, taking a system-wide view of food safety. In short, HACCP tells you where a 
problem might happen; HARPC pushes you to prevent it from happening at all.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 427
In coffee roasting facilities, food safety risks generally fall into three categories: 
biological, chemical, and physical hazards. Understanding each is essential to building 
an effective food safety plan. 
Biological hazards primarily arise from microbial contamination at the farm level and 
during post-harvest processing, where mold growth, bacteria, and mycotoxins can 
develop if conditions are not properly managed. Chemical hazards in coffee include 
pesticide residues, heavy metals from soil contamination, and processing-related 
contaminants such as acrylamide and PAH. Physical hazards pose a risk through 
foreign objects like stones, metal fragments, or packaging contaminants that enter 
the product at different handling stages.
Below is a risk assessment tool commonly used for HARPC. 
ASSESSMENT OF SIGNIFICANCE FOR COFFEE 
SAFETY AND QUALITY HAZARDS
Based on the Workplace Risk Assessment and Control system, the matrix below 
allows a more consistent approach to determining the significance or otherwise of any 
identified food safety hazard (chemical, biological, or physical) or quality hazard. This 
then allows the identification of CCP or QCP status control measures at a glance.
A Critical Control Point (CCP) is a position in the process where there is a potential 
for a serious hazard to exist and must be reduced or eliminated. If not, it can result in 
harm or injury to the consumer. A CCP requires a preventive control.

428 ♦ Ildi Revi
A value of 1-10 indicates a significant hazard, i.e., above the line, which signifies that 
control measures must be put in place. Hazards that are not significant will have values 
of 11-25, with values of 20-25 being the lowest risk and severity.
To better understand how these risks are assessed in real-world coffee systems, we will 
begin at the farm.
CURRENT GOOD AGRICULTURAL PRACTICES: FOR PRODUCERS
Under FSMA, the Produce Safety Rule establishes minimum standards for safe 
farming practices. Although coffee is not directly regulated under this rule, foreign 
producers are often expected to follow equivalent guidelines as part of U.S. importers’ 
food safety verification processes. This is where current Good Agricultural Practices 
(cGAPs) become relevant. While GAPs refer broadly to recommended on-farm safety 
measures, cGAPs reflect the newest, science-based protocols recognized by regulatory 
authorities. These practices help producers identify and manage risks related to water, 
soil, hygiene, and post-harvest handling. Because they are routinely updated, producers 
need to consult official resources regularly. This section reflects the cGAPs in effect 
as of September 2024.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 429
At the core of cGAPs are standards for water safety, soil management, worker hygiene, 
animal and pest control, sanitation, harvesting practices, and post-harvest handling. 
In coffee production, each of these areas requires specific attention.
Water quality is a critical control point. All water used for irrigation, processing, and 
post-harvest washing must be free from harmful pathogens and contaminants. Well 
water should be tested at least once per season, while surface water, more prone to 
contamination, should be tested three times. If contamination is detected, treatment 
and retesting are mandatory before continued usage. Biological hazards such as bacteria 
and parasites pose the greatest risk, though chemical contamination from runoff or 
heavy metals also requires monitoring and corrective action when necessary.
Soil amendments must also be handled with care. Organic farms are required to test 
soil for residual chemical contamination at least three years before planting. Compost 
and manure must be properly treated to eliminate pathogens. Raw manure, for 
instance, must be applied well before harvest, while composting processes must be 
documented to ensure adequate pathogen control. In some systems, biochar binds 
contaminants and improves soil fertility. These steps reduce the risk of biological, 
chemical, and physical hazards entering the food chain through the soil.
Worker hygiene is another cornerstone of food safety. All personnel involved in 
cultivation, harvest, or handling must be trained in sanitation protocols. Standard 
Operating Procedures (SOPs) should outline handwashing techniques, the use of 
protective clothing, and restricted areas for eating, drinking, or smoking. Clean 
drinking and washing water must be readily available, and sanitation facilities must 
be maintained in good condition. These practices reduce the risk of biological hazards 
from human pathogens and minimize physical contamination from tools, clothing, or 
personal care products. Even though we are discussing the farm here, proper hygiene 
is required in all parts of the system.
Animal and pest control involves managing the risks posed by wildlife, domestic 
animals, and farm service animals such as oxen or mules. These animals should be 
kept from drying beds, processing areas, and storage zones. Preventive measures like 
fencing, noise deterrents, or natural repellents help discourage wildlife intrusion. 

430 ♦ Ildi Revi
Rodent and pest management programs should avoid chemical treatments that could 
contaminate crops or water sources.
Land use history must also be assessed. Farmland near flood zones, livestock 
operations, or industrial areas may carry higher risks of contamination from runoff 
or airborne pollutants. Mitigation strategies, such as buffer zones, drainage controls, 
and frequent testing for heavy metals or chemical residues, help reduce these risks. 
Pre-planting assessments and periodic retesting are essential for long-term safety.
Harvesting and packing activities require strict sanitation. Containers and tools 
must be clean and in good condition. Gloves and protective gear should be used 
when handling coffee cherries, and only new or properly sanitized bags and containers 
should be used for packing. Traceability systems should be in place to identify each 
lot and track any issues.
Post-harvest transport and storage present additional food safety challenges. 
Contamination risks persist if coffee is not properly handled during transfer. Trucks 
and containers must be cleaned and sanitized, and tarps or enclosed trailers should be 
used to protect beans from environmental exposure. Storage bags such as GrainPro or 
other hermetic solutions help prevent mold, pest damage, and contamination from 
fuel fumes or airborne toxins.
Following cGAPs, holistic coffee producers ensure their coffee is cultivated, processed, 
and transported under the highest food safety standards. These measures protect 
public health and maintain the integrity of the coffee industry. Producers can 
consistently deliver a clean, high-quality product while complying with global food 
safety regulations through proper worker hygiene, soil and water testing, pest control, 
harvest sanitation, and safe transport.
FOREIGN SUPPLIER VERIFICATION PROGRAM (FSVP): FOR IMPORTERS
The FSVP is a central part of FSMA, designed to shift food safety from reaction 
to prevention. Under this rule, U.S. coffee importers must verify that their foreign 
suppliers produce and handle coffee in a way that ensures the same level of public 
health protection as required domestically. This includes evaluating food safety risks, 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 431
such as contamination by mycotoxins, pesticide residues, allergens, or physical hazards, 
and confirming appropriate preventive controls.
FSVP requires importers to develop and maintain a verification program for each 
product they bring into the country. This includes conducting a hazard analysis, 
evaluating supplier performance, approving suppliers based on risk, and outlining 
corrective actions if a supplier fails to meet safety standards. Importers must keep 
detailed records of these evaluations and reassess supplier compliance regularly. If 
an issue arises, such as unexpected allergens or chemical contaminants, importers 
are expected to take corrective actions, including ceasing purchases or requesting 
additional testing or remediation from the supplier.
Contrary to some assumptions, importers are not automatically required to test 
coffee for contaminants like mycotoxins or pesticide residues unless their own hazard 
analysis identifies these as risks requiring control. This has led to occasional confusion 
when roasters or buyers expect testing or a COA outside of what the law mandates. 
In contrast, EU regulations are more explicit: pesticide residues must comply with 
established MRLs, and mycotoxins are regulated under EU Commission Regulation 
1881/2006, with mandatory testing required at several points in the supply chain.
In addition to FSVP, these other FSMA rules also apply to coffee importers:
•
The Preventive Controls for Human Food Rule requires facilities receiving
or storing coffee to implement a food safety system that includes hazard
identification and risk-based preventive measures.
•
The Sanitary Transportation Rule mandates that coffee be transported in
ways that prevent contamination (e.g., clean vehicles, sealed packaging).
However, the importer’s responsibility begins once the coffee reaches the
U.S. port of entry.
•
The Intentional Adulteration Rule protects the food supply against
economically motivated fraud or deliberate contamination. For coffee, this
could include blending with lower-quality beans, falsifying origin, or adding
fillers such as corn or soy. Importers are not required to implement a full
food defense plan, but are expected to verify the authenticity of the coffee
and its documentation.

432 ♦ Ildi Revi
Importers typically rely on trusted shipping companies, written assurances, and 
periodic spot checks to ensure compliance with transportation and handling standards. 
While they may not control every step of the international journey, they are legally 
and ethically responsible for ensuring that the product they bring into the country is 
safe, traceable, and compliant with U.S. law.
For those participating in the holistic coffee movement, FSVP offers a regulatory 
framework that complements and supports CHC’s higher standards. While legal 
compliance is the baseline, CHC encourages importers to go further through third-
party testing, transparent communication with roasters and producers, and clear 
documentation that coffee has been grown and handled using best practices. In this 
way, FSVP becomes not just a compliance tool, but a vehicle for building trust, quality, 
and accountability throughout the health-focused coffee supply chain.
ROASTERS: CURRENT GOOD MANUFACTURING 
PRACTICES (CGMPS)
Just as GAPs provide the foundation for safe produce, GMPs do the same for safe food 
handling and processing in roasting facilities. Current Good Manufacturing Practices 
(cGMPs) refer to the most up-to-date, legally enforceable standards that regulatory 
agencies require. Meanwhile, GMP is the broader term often used to describe good 
manufacturing practices, whether mandated or voluntarily adopted.
Based on what I’ve seen, it’s worth stating outright: Coffee roasting is food 
manufacturing. You might call it craft roasting, and it may feel like culinary performance 
art, but it’s food manufacturing. And food manufacturing comes with rules.
Regardless of size, U.S. coffee roasters are required to follow the cGMPs and Hazard 
Analysis and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food, as outlined in 21 CFR 
Part 117 under the Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA). These regulations are not 
optional. They mandate that food manufacturing facilities, including coffee roasteries, 
maintain strict hygiene, assess and control hazards, and document preventive actions 
to protect public health. As of September 2024, the cGMPs described here reflect the 
most up-to-date federal guidance, but roasters should regularly check for updates or 
region-specific requirements.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 433
A first step for any roaster selling coffee in the U.S., regardless of size, is registering 
with the FDA. Each facility must also designate a Preventive Controls Qualified 
Individual (PCQI) responsible for conducting hazard analyses and implementing 
food safety protocols. While general PCQI training is helpful, roasters should ideally 
seek instruction specific to coffee processing, which presents distinct risks compared 
to other food industries.
At a minimum, roasters are required to maintain sanitary operations, conduct hazard 
analyses for biological, chemical, and physical threats, and develop and document a 
food safety plan with appropriate monitoring. This includes verifying that green coffee 
suppliers meet safety expectations, properly storing chemicals and allergens, and using 
equipment such as magnets, sieves, and destoners to remove contaminants. Employee 
training, especially on visual inspections and food contact hygiene, plays a major role 
in preventing contamination post-roast.
While FSMA governs U.S. operations, roasters elsewhere must comply with similar 
frameworks. The European Union enforces Regulation (EC) No 852/2004, which sets 
hygiene standards for food production. Canada’s Safe Food for Canadians Regulations 
(SFCR) address hazard control and traceability. Australia and New Zealand follow 
the Food Standards Code, which outlines contamination prevention, hygiene, and 
packaging requirements. Across all these systems, the underlying goal remains the 
same: to protect consumers by proactively managing risk.
BIOLOGICAL HAZARDS IN ROASTING
Biological hazards are primarily linked to green coffee and post-roast handling. Roasting 
kills most microbes, but risks remain from poor hygiene and cross-contamination. 
Bacillus cereus can survive roasting and contaminate the final product if not properly 
managed(Souza and Abrantes 2011). This bacterium can cause foodborne illnesses: 
vomiting syndrome and diarrheal syndrome, with symptoms like abdominal cramps 
and diarrhea. Contamination may occur during harvesting, processing, or storage 
under conditions that support bacterial growth. 
In 2017, when we were testing a range of coffees at Purity, a couple of green sample 
results came back “B. cereus presumptive”—so I’ve seen this firsthand. Roasters: 

434 ♦ Ildi Revi
Imagine handling this coffee, forgetting to wash your hands, and then eating a 
sandwich... You might end up with a stomachache and blame the food, when really, 
it was the green coffee. Also, general hygiene and worker health are critical when 
touching the finished product to avoid spreading any illnesses.
CHEMICAL HAZARDS IN ROASTING
Chemical hazards in coffee go 
beyond just acrylamide and 
PAH (discussed earlier). While 
those are often in the spotlight, 
other compounds formed during 
roasting, and even those naturally 
present in green coffee, can pose 
risks that are less talked about but 
equally important, especially in the 
context of long-term exposure in 
roasting facilities.
Furan and furfuryl alcohol are two 
examples. These compounds form 
during roasting through the thermal 
degradation of carbohydrates, 
oxidation of polyunsaturated fatty 
acids, and also from the Maillard 
reaction. Furan is classified as a 
possible human carcinogen, and 
furfuryl alcohol has demonstrated 
neurotoxic potential in animal studies. Darker roasts tend to have higher levels due 
to longer roasting times and elevated temperatures (Lachenmeier 2018). While levels 
in brewed coffee are generally low and within regulatory limits, these compounds are 
still important to monitor in production settings focused on health.
Then, there’s diacetyl and 2,3-pentanedione, naturally occurring compounds that 
also develop during roasting and are released during grinding. They contribute to the 
I didn’t know any of this when I started roasting. 
Once my business got going, I was roasting coffee 
for hours every day. I hadn’t really needed my inhaler 
since childhood, but that changed in the roastery. 
Whenever I roasted in the mornings and the sun was 
coming through the windows, I could see it—the 
dust billowing in the air as I dumped green coffee 
into the hopper, swirling in the light.
What was in that dust? I didn’t know then. I still 
don’t know entirely. But after about five years of 
roasting, I was using my inhaler every time I worked. 
And it wasn’t just my lungs—sometimes, I’d break 
out in hives or develop a rash along the inside of 
my forearms, right where they brushed against the 
inside of a burlap sack (Rob did not pack in GrainPro 
bags at the time, it was just green coffee in hessian). 
At first, I assumed it was nothing. But now I see it 
differently. The evidence was there all along. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 435
warm, buttery notes of roasted coffee, but have been linked to respiratory damage 
when inhaled chronically, especially in enclosed workspaces. Studies have shown that 
exposure levels in some roasting and packaging facilities can exceed occupational 
safety guidelines, particularly during grinding and degassing (Hawley 2018; LeBouf 
2022). While worker safety falls outside FSMA’s direct scope, it’s worth a reminder 
here: in a One Health model like CHC, the well-being of those who produce the 
coffee matters too.
PHYSICAL HAZARDS IN ROASTING
Physical hazards in coffee are usually the visible and tangible, and sometimes the most 
surprising. Of course, every roaster has a story of what they’ve found in green coffee, 
which is such a common experience that it’s a sort of conversation starter in roasting 
circles. I once opened a sack of coffee, and sitting right on top was a dried-out frog, 
flattened and shriveled, like a piece of biltong. Ask enough roasters, and you’ll hear 
about bolts, chunks of cement, paperclips, you name it. As entertaining as these stories 
are, they highlight why green coffee inspection, destoning, sieving, and final checks 
before packaging are part of food safety.
These foreign objects make their way into coffee during harvesting, processing, 
transportation, or roasting. They include things like stones, sticks, bits of plastic, 
and especially metal fragments, which can come from damaged equipment, worn 
parts, or fasteners shaken loose during transport or milling. Roasters typically use a 
combination of magnets and “visual inspection,” and the big companies can afford 
metal detectors at the end of the packing line. Magnets can catch a lot of iron and 
steel fragments, especially from milling or grinding machinery, but they won’t catch 
plastic, aluminum, brass, copper, or some types of stainless steel.
DEVELOPING A FOOD SAFETY PLAN
As the final step in the supply chain before coffee reaches the consumer, roasters carry 
a critical food safety responsibility. Under FSMA, facilities that manufacture, process, 
pack, or hold food, including coffee roasteries, are required to develop a written food 

436 ♦ Ildi Revi
safety plan. This plan must be created and overseen by a Preventive Controls Qualified 
Individual (PCQI) and should reflect the specific risks and operations of the facility.
This requirement is often misunderstood. A food safety plan under FSMA is a legal 
requirement, distinct from third-party certification. While certification may support 
marketing or buyer assurance, FSMA compliance is about regulatory accountability 
and public health protection. For any roaster operating in the U.S., having a compliant 
food safety plan isn’t optional; it’s the foundation of safe food manufacturing.
A PCQI is someone who has either completed training under an FDA-recognized 
curriculum or has equivalent job experience and knowledge. While formal certification 
is not mandatory, the FDA evaluates the adequacy of your food safety plan, not your 
résumé. If your plan is found deficient, the agency may determine that your PCQI 
needs further training, regardless of previous experience. For roasters unfamiliar with 
these requirements, the FDA’s Food Safety Plan Builder is a free tool that can help 
structure a compliant system.
The PCQI is responsible for designing, implementing, and maintaining the facility’s 
food safety system. This includes:
•	
Hazard Analysis – Identifying all reasonably foreseeable risks, including 
biological, chemical, and physical hazards.
•	
Preventive Controls – Implementing tailored procedures like sanitation, 
allergen management, and environmental monitoring.
•	
Monitoring Procedures – Ensuring preventive controls are consistently 
followed and documented.
•	
Corrective Actions – Establishing steps to take when safety controls fail or 
deviations occur.
•	
Verification and Validation – Confirming that food safety measures are 
working as intended.
•	
Supply Chain Program – Ensuring that ingredients and raw materials come 
from approved, verified suppliers.
•	
Recall Plan – Preparing for product recalls, including traceability, customer 
notifications, and product removal.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 437
Coffee roasting is the final step in a long and complex journey, where everything either 
holds together or unravels. That’s why preventive controls at this stage are so critical. 
When producers follow cGAPs, importers uphold FSVP, and roasters implement 
cGMPs backed by a solid food safety plan, the entire system works in harmony.
Holistic coffee production puts food safety at the center, not as a box to check, but as 
a reflection of the care and precision that define truly high-quality coffee. FSMA was 
designed to prevent harm but also supports something deeper: trust, resilience, and 
responsibility throughout the supply chain.
Food safety goes beyond compliance for those of us in the health-focused coffee 
community; it’s truly a part of our craft. It’s the unseen discipline behind every clean, 
safe, and exceptional cup. And it’s one of the ways we show respect for the people who 
drink our coffee, and for those who help us produce it. 

438
9
Brewing Coffee for Health
HIGHLIGHTS
Understanding brewing methods, water quality, microbiome interactions, and storage enhances 
coffee’s flavor and health benefits, catering to personal taste and health goals.
Brewing Coffee: Brewing coffee involves extracting flavors from ground beans using clean water, 
influenced by factors like brew time, temperature, and pressure. Hydrolysis enhances antioxidant 
potential, and various methods impact bioactive compounds, affecting flavor and health benefits. 
Taste experiences vary based on personal, genetic, and health factors.
Human Microbiome and Coffee Perception: The nasal and oral microbiomes influence flavor 
perception through interactions with smell and taste receptors. Professional tasters develop refined 
sensory skills to enhance their coffee-tasting experience.
Psychology of Coffee Consumption: Cultural and personal factors shape coffee’s psychological 
impact, with aroma linking to memory and emotion. Anticipation affects flavor perception, with 
tasters trained to reduce biases.
Water Quality in Brewing: As the primary component of coffee, water quality greatly impacts 
its taste. Pure water with essential minerals is crucial, with guidelines helping optimize flavor.
Brewing Techniques: Methods like drip, pour-over, and espresso affect coffee’s taste and health, 
such as antioxidant content. Brewing factors like grind size and temperature play significant roles.
Cold Brew: This method involves steeping coffee in cool water, resulting in unique flavors. 
Emphasizing cleanliness and proper storage is essential for safety and quality.
Storing Roasted Coffee: For freshness, store coffee away from moisture, heat, and odors. Whole 
beans can be frozen for short-term storage, requiring warming before use.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 439
When I first opened my coffee shop, I chased my tail when it came to brewing, trying 
to make everyone happy. I had a row of brewed coffee choices lined up on the counter 
in self-serve “airpots” (insulated containers that keep coffee hot), and customers 
dispense the coffee by pushing down on a pump lever. The exact same airpot would 
generate different reactions: One person would ask me to add hot water because it was 
too strong, another person would comment it wasn’t strong enough, another person 
would say it was bitter, another person would think it was perfect, another person 
would blend it with a darker coffee.
The experience quickly taught me that taste is deeply personal and subjective, 
with people harboring diverse opinions on what makes a flavor enjoyable. While 
one individual might savor the bitterness of dark chocolate, another might find it 
unpalatable, preferring the sweetness of milk chocolate instead. This variation in taste 
preferences can be attributed to cultural background, individual sensory perception, 
and personal experiences.
When discussing coffee brewing, we refer to the meticulous process of preparing the 
beverage using clean water, which extracts soluble solids from optimally ground coffee 
beans tailored for specific brewing methods. This involves a range of techniques, like 
how vigorously the coffee and water are mixed, the type of filter used, and controlling 
variables such as brew time, water temperature, and pressure applied to the grounds, 
all contributing to the final flavor and quality of the brew. I’ll go through ten variables 
of brewing in this chapter.
Each brewing method seeks to extract a harmonious blend of pleasant flavors and a few 
less desirable ones, producing a balanced and delightful beverage. What one person 
finds “pleasant” in coffee flavor can differ significantly from another’s preference. 
The same batch of roasted beans can yield diverse taste experiences when brewed by 
different people with varying methods, highlighting the importance of understanding 
different brewing techniques.
Ultimately, coffee brewing is a matter of personal preference, heavily influenced by 
individual sensory perception. It begins with the aromatic experience when inhaling 
the fragrance of ground coffee, which evolves as it is brewed. The olfactory system 
detects thousands of volatile compounds released, sending signals to the brain to 

440 ♦ Ildi Revi
discern floral, fruity, or spicy notes. Similarly, the gustatory system engages when coffee 
touches the tongue, with taste buds discerning sweet, sour, bitter, salty, and umami 
flavors. Factors like age, genetics, and even what you consumed prior can influence 
taste sensitivity, making coffee a uniquely personal experience.
How are these sensory aspects related to health, though? A person’s health conditions 
can influence their sensory experience; conversely, sensory experiences can affect their 
health. The sense of smell is critical for detecting environmental hazards, and also 
plays a role in social communication, and the enjoyment of food and life (Doty 2009); 
and the integration of sensory perceptions with health outcomes underscores the 
importance of maintaining robust sensory systems for overall well-being (Croy 2014). 
Many people experienced anosmia (loss of smell) and ageusia (loss of taste) during 
COVID-19 (Parma 2020). This affected many people’s enjoyment of eating, which 
may have affected general health and nutrition since the interplay between the olfactory, 
gustatory, and gastrointestinal systems is essential for maintaining homeostasis and 
overall wellness (Proserpio 2017). Also, stress can alter taste perception, often leading 
to changes in appetite and food preferences (Dallman 2005).
OUR MICROBIOME: NOT JUST IN THE GUT
Earlier in this book, we explored how coffee interacts with the gut microbiome and 
how that affects digestion, inflammation, and gastrointestinal health. But the human 
microbiome extends far beyond the gut, and so does its influence. Our microbial 
communities also affect how we taste, smell, and perceive flavor, which makes them 
influential in how we experience coffee.
Flavor perception starts with aroma, and recent studies suggest that the nasal 
microbiome can shape how well we smell. Changes in the diversity and composition 
of nasal microbial communities have been linked to altered olfactory performance. 
For instance, individuals with reduced sense of smell often show different microbial 
signatures than those with normal olfaction (Wang 2020). These microbial differences 
may help explain why some people perceive coffee aromas more vividly, or differently, 
than others. A recent study confirmed this relationship in more detail, showing that 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 441
certain bacterial genera like Corynebacterium and Faecalibacterium are more abundant 
in people with diminished olfactory sensitivity (Kumpitsch 2024).
The mouth is home to complex microbiota, bacteria, viruses, fungi, and archaea, 
which interact with taste receptors on the tongue and other oral tissues. Some oral 
microbes can enhance or dull our perception of bitterness, sweetness, and different 
taste qualities. They can also influence how we perceive polyphenols and other key 
coffee flavor compounds. When the balance of microorganisms is disrupted, known as 
dysbiosis, it can affect both oral and systemic health, as well as alter flavor perception 
(Zaura 2009).
Interestingly, taste receptors aren’t limited to the mouth. They’re also found throughout 
the gastrointestinal tract and other tissues. These “extra-oral” receptors interact with 
gut microbiota and metabolic signaling pathways, influencing digestion, satiety, and 
food preferences. This means that flavor isn’t just about the tongue—it’s a whole-body, 
microbiome-mediated experience (Behrens and Meyerhof 2011, Moran 2010).
INDUSTRY INFLUENCE 
The environment in which coffee is consumed and evaluated represents another area of 
potential interest when exploring how the microbiome interacts with our olfactory and 
gustatory systems. While limited research specifically connects the professional coffee 
world to changes in the sensory microbiome, insights from other industries can help 
us make informed hypotheses. For example, studies suggest that individuals working 
in the wine industry may develop distinct nasal microbiomes, which in turn influence 
their perception of aroma and flavor. Repeated exposure to specific environments, 
especially those rich in volatile compounds, may shift microbial communities in ways 
that affect sensory performance (Leclercq 2014).
From my experience as a former Q Arabica Instructor and Authorized SCA Trainer 
for Sensory Skills, I can confidently say that professional coffee tasters are trained to 
sharpen their perception through deliberate practice. This includes structured sensory 
exercises and repeated exposure to a range of coffee aromas and flavor profiles designed 
to build consistent sensory memory and calibration. But this kind of exposure might 

442 ♦ Ildi Revi
not just shape perception through learning alone; it could also affect the biology of 
taste through microbiome shifts.
Learners practicing cupping in one of my courses a few years ago.
Earlier in this book, we explored how moderate coffee consumption increases the 
abundance of beneficial gut bacteria like Bifidobacterium and reduces harmful species 
such as Enterobacteria. A 2024 review published in Nutrients confirmed this trend, 
noting that habitual coffee intake, particularly at levels below four cups per day, was 
consistently associated with increased microbiome diversity and a measurable rise 
in Bifidobacterium abundance (Saygili 2024). There is no direct evidence linking 
microbiome changes to sensory skills in coffee professionals. Still, I often wonder about 
coffee professionals’ health and internal systems, since they interact with coffee more 
than the general public. Regardless, these studies highlight the intricate relationships 
between microbial communities and sensory perception. It may even help explain 
why identical twins, despite having nearly identical DNA, may have very different 
preferences for foods and flavors. The microbiome, shaped by environment, diet, and 
experience, adds another layer to the biology of taste, one that the coffee industry is 
only beginning to understand.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 443
PSYCHOLOGY OF SMELL AND TASTE IN COFFEE
Cultural and individual factors deeply influence the psychological aspect of tasting and 
smelling coffee. Coffee has a unique ability to evoke memories and emotions, largely 
due to its complex aroma profile. The brain regions responsible for processing smells 
are closely linked to the areas that handle memory and emotion, the hippocampus 
and the amygdala. This connection can explain why the smell of coffee might remind 
someone of a cozy morning at home or an invigorating break at the office. Odors can 
evoke strong emotional responses and memories, making them a powerful tool for 
psychological research and therapy (Herz 2016).
In terms of psychology, anticipation and expectation heavily affect how we perceive the 
taste of coffee. Psychological factors from prior experiences or emotional situations can 
influence flavor perception. The training of coffee tasters, like Q Arabica and Robusta 
Graders, includes discussions on minimizing biases by teaching tasters to approach 
each sample objectively and consistently (Spence 2015). 
Coffee tasters develop mental skills to recognize, identify, isolate, and categorize 
flavors. To help new professional tasters describe the sensory attributes of coffee 
consistently, formal coffee taster training should include standardized vocabularies, 
such as the Coffee Taster’s Flavor Wheel and World Coffee Research Lexicon. Since 
language affects culture and psychology, it can also influence perception. Research 
indicates that factors such as the environment where coffee is consumed, as well as 
the material and color of the coffee cup, can affect the perceived flavor and overall 
satisfaction. For instance, a beautifully presented cup with latte art in a well-designed 
café can heighten the perceived sweetness and richness of the coffee. Moreover, 
habitual coffee drinkers often develop a psychological dependence, where the mere 
smell of coffee can induce a sense of alertness and readiness, even before the first sip.
This intricate dance between biology and psychology makes the simple act of drinking 
coffee a profoundly intricate multisensory experience. This is important to the category 
of healthy coffee because how we perceive our coffee affects how much of it we 
consume and, consequently, its effect on our health.

444
9.1 Water
Water is key to coffee brewing,  the process of turning ground beans into flavorful 
coffee. Water usually makes up 98-99 percent of what we drink as coffee. The quality 
of water can greatly affect the final taste. Good brewing water should be pure and 
rich in essential minerals, and should be devoid of chemicals or impurities. Whether 
it comes from mountain springs or is filtered, the water must be clean and odor-free 
to extract the flavors from the coffee beans properly. 
Also, ensure all equipment and containers are clean and odor-free. This can prevent 
unwanted flavors or ingredients from contaminating your coffee. Water labeled 
“spring water” will work, but any water you use needs to meet various government 
standards for food safety (in the chart below, 2024 data).  Remember, water is not just 
an ingredient, but is critical for making great coffee. Paying attention to water quality 
can significantly enhance your coffee brewing experience.
