The Specialty Coffee Association of Europe (SCAE) conducted research to quantify 
the impact of water quality on coffee flavor. They used two types of coffee (Colombian 
and Brazilian) and three types of water (low, medium, and high mineral content). The 
results showed that coffee brewed with low mineral content water scored highest in 
flavor, acidity, balance, and overall quality. This highlights the role of water quality in 
brewing for optimum taste, the base standard for healthy coffee.
Various factors, such as processing method, roast degree, grind size, temperature, 
pressure, and extraction time, also influence coffee’s flavor profile. Since brewed coffee 
is 98-99 percent water, the water quality can significantly impact the final taste.
Water without minerals (distilled water) extracts different substances from coffee 
grounds compared to mineral-rich water. Water’s overall mineralization and alkalinity 
(carbonate hardness) affect coffee taste. Key minerals in drinking water include 
calcium, magnesium, sodium, potassium, chloride, sulfate, and hydrogen carbonate.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 447
•
Calcium, Magnesium, Sodium and Potassium: These minerals enhance
coffee extraction and improve the flavor profile, but in high concentrations,
they can lead to hard water, resulting in an unbalanced, bitter, and flat
taste. Also, healthy coffees often contain measurable levels of calcium
and magnesium. Research suggests that moderate levels of calcium and
magnesium are beneficial for health, contributing to strong bones and
metabolic functions. Sodium works with potassium in the body to power
nerve impulses and muscle contractions. Sodium-potassium pumps,
embedded in cell membranes, maintain this balance by moving sodium
out and potassium in. This creates a concentration gradient essential for
nerve function.
When a nerve cell fires, sodium floods in through special channels, triggering
a chain reaction that carries the signal to the brain or muscles. Muscle cells
also rely on the alteration of their sodium/potassium balance to contract.
These pumps are so vital that they consume about a third of our cells’
main energy source, adenosine triphosphate (ATP). Although potassium is
essential for heart health, excessive amounts can affect blood pressure and
kidney health (NIH Fact Sheets 2024). Coffee can have high potassium
levels, and this is exactly where we need to give consumers certificates of
analysis with potassium levels. At Purity, we had a customer support inquiry
about potassium from someone who had to watch intake for health reasons,
and we were able to provide the information, and he ended up not buying
the coffee because it was too high in potassium for him.
•
Chloride and Sulfate: These ions can contribute to the saltiness and
bitterness of coffee. While necessary in small amounts for flavor complexity,
high levels can be undesirable. Both have roles in bodily functions. Chloride
aids digestion, and sulfate supports metabolism (WHO 2004).
•
Hydrogen Carbonate: This bicarbonate acts as a buffer, neutralizing acids
and affecting coffee’s flavor balance. Naturally present in water through
the dissolution of carbon dioxide, hydrogen carbonate forms carbonic
acid, which dissociates into hydrogen carbonate ions. These ions neutralize
coffee acids like CGA, citric acid, and malic acid, reducing coffee’s acidity
and forming salt, water, and carbon dioxide gas. Also, excessive hydrogen

448 ♦ Ildi Revi
carbonate can make coffee taste less acidic and potentially unbalanced if 
the water is too hard.
Substances added to treat water, such as chlorine for disinfection, can interact with 
coffee’s extracted substances and affect its taste. When I owned my coffee shop, every 
year at some point, the city would flush the water lines with extra chlorine to kill the 
buildup of pathogens. When this happened, it was not uncommon for people to come 
into my shop complaining about the taste of their coffee. Chlorine can impart an 
unpleasant odor and taste to water and coffee even in small quantities. Even if chlorine 
is undetectable in water, it can still react with coffee aromas, altering its flavor profile. 
There are health concerns associated with long-term exposure to chlorine by-products, 
which may include increased risks of bladder cancer and heart disease (EPA 2020).
Natural substances like algae metabolites, which enter the water supply primarily due 
to harmful algal blooms (HABs) driven by excess nutrients from agricultural runoff 
and wastewater, can significantly impact water quality. To mitigate these metabolites, 
strategies include reducing nutrient inputs, using advanced water treatments such as 
activated carbon filtration and ozonation, employing physical methods like mixing 
and barriers, exploring biological controls, and implementing early detection and 
monitoring systems. 
Algae metabolites, such as geosmin, produce an earthy-musty taste similar to beetroot. 
These organic contaminants can impart off-flavors to both water and coffee. Geosmin, 
specifically, is produced by certain algae, especially cyanobacteria, responsible for 
HABs. When these algae decompose, they release geosmin and other metabolites 
into the water, causing an unpleasant taste and odor even at very low concentrations. 
High levels of geosmin are also linked to nausea and gastrointestinal distress (Water 
Research Institute 2016).
Even with high-quality coffee beans, the wrong kind of water can lead to unwanted 
coffee flavors. Ensuring water quality preserves coffee’s taste and safeguards consumer 
health. The total dissolved solids (TDS) should generally fall within the range of 75 to 
250 mg/L to ensure proper extraction. A moderate level of calcium hardness—typically 
between 17 and 85 mg/L—helps with flavor clarity and prevents excessive scaling. 
Similarly, total alkalinity should hover around 40 mg/L to maintain balance without 
flattening acidity. The water’s pH should remain close to neutral, between 6.5 and 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 449
7.5, and sodium levels are best kept low, ideally near 10 mg/L, to avoid off-flavors or 
interference with the coffee’s natural taste. For those who prefer more alkaline water 
for health reasons, be aware that while it may support personal wellness goals, it can 
subtly mute acidity and brightness in coffee, affecting the overall flavor profile.
PRE-BREWING INFORMATION
There is not much literature on coffee brewing and health. Most brewing literature is 
based on taste, and in epidemiological research on coffee, they mostly do not define 
what “coffee” is. We do not know if it was espresso, ibrik-brewed, electric drip brewed, 
or another method. We also do not see how the coffee was ground. 
For coffee to be brewed, it must first be wetted, meaning the coffee grounds must 
absorb water. This generally refers to applying enough liquid to moisten a surface or 
material. It can range from a light mist to enough liquid to produce visible moisture on 
the surface. This is a physical, not a chemical, process that involves many interacting 
variables. Grinding coffee into various particle sizes affects the wetting speed, which is 
influenced by the grounds’ size, density, and placement, as well as the water addition 
method. Stirring during wetting also impacts the speed and evenness of the process. 
The coffee bed can be compared to sand or soil, where the water infiltration rate 
depends on particle size and shape (e.g., gravel vs. sand), initial moisture content, 
porosity, gas solubility, pressure, and particle swelling.
During wetting, coffee grounds swell and retain some water dispensed onto them, 
depending on particle size. As air, volatile compounds, and other gases (primarily 
carbon dioxide) are displaced, we might notice effervescence or coffee “blooming” 
(when freshly ground coffee bubbles up as water is added). Some gases dissolve into 
the water or coffee slurry. The wetting period is responsible for the coffee’s bloom in 
certain brewing methods. In immersion methods, wetting may occur quickly, while 
in espresso, some machines include a pre-infusion to wet the coffee bed before the 
main extraction.
When you start brewing, try to wet the coffee uniformly and with a regular flow and 
extraction time, which can be more challenging than it initially seems. Baristas try to 
minimize variation in grind size and wetting time, which is ideal, though the merits 

450 ♦ Ildi Revi
of a normal grind particle distribution are still debated. The final step in the coffee 
chain, brewing, allows the coffee’s unique and favorable flavor characteristics to shine 
or be destroyed. The barista’s role in extraction is the final act in the coffee opera.
When wetting coffee grounds, gases and volatile compounds dissolve and are released, 
while soluble compounds are drawn out through extraction. Coffee grounds are our 
brewing product, and water is the solvent. Without extraction, coffee grounds would 
remain in water indefinitely. Extraction involves chemical and physical reactions that 
transfer coffee solubles from the grounds to the water, creating the coffee beverage. 
The coffee industry measures extraction as the percentage of soluble yield (or brewing 
yield) from the coffee grounds used, aiming for an ideal range of 18-22 percent. 
Generally speaking, under-extraction impacts sour and sweet flavors, while over-
extraction produces bitter, astringent flavors. Flavors are compounds that may have 
certain bioactivity—in other words, what we taste may indicate the healthy compounds 
we consume.
Heat is a key factor influencing coffee extraction. Higher temperatures increase 
extraction rates and the solubility of compounds. When compounds are broken 
down due to heat, it is called thermal degradation, which largely occurs during 
roasting. Brewing with hot water (195-205°F) accelerates chemical reactions and 
increases the solubility of certain compounds. Cold brewing requires an extended brew 
time to achieve similar extraction due to lower solubility at room temperature. The 
final beverages (hot and cold brewed) are not expected to exhibit the same chemical 
composition. I discuss cold brewing in detail at the end of this chapter.
Multiple forces synergize during extraction. Each coffee particle has its own brew time, 
and the solute concentration of the liquid passing over coffee grounds changes as it 
flows through the coffee bed, affecting the diffusion rate via Fick’s Law. The brewed 
coffee undergoes many chemical reactions during brewing, even after filtration. 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 451
Soluble compounds leave the coffee grounds through several reactions dependent on 
temperature, time, and agitation:
•
Hydrolysis: Water reacts with compounds to alter or break them down.
This loosens insoluble or large soluble coffee compounds, contributing to
the extraction of larger organic acids.
•
Dissolution: Water solubility allows compounds like chlorogenic, acetic,
malic, and other acids, caffeine, and trigonelline to dissolve into the coffee
beverage.
•
Diffusion: Solutes move from high-concentration areas (coffee grounds)
to low-concentration areas (water), driven by concentration gradients. This
can occur via osmosis and follows Fick’s Law, which considers factors like
time, temperature, and solute concentration.
When we brew coffee, if we extract about 20 percent of the ground coffee, we have 
about 80 percent left over for compost (roses and gardenias enjoy spent grounds) 
or biochar. Coffee’s larger, insoluble molecules are not extracted; they stay in the 
coffee grounds. These include structural compounds in the coffee cell walls, such 
as polysaccharides like cellulose and lignin, which also survive the roasting process. 
Hemicellulose molecules (mannose, galactose, and arabinose) are integral to coffee 
cell walls. Although some may be extracted during a long, hot brew cycle, they remain 
in the coffee grounds if not hydrolyzed. High molecular weight melanoidins typically 
remain in the spent coffee bed, but their smaller counterparts, which give our coffee 
brew its recognizable color, are the soluble fibers we discussed earlier in this book. 
Additionally, minerals like potassium, phosphorus, and magnesium are partially 
extracted during brewing, with some remaining.

452 ♦ Ildi Revi
10 Variables that Impact Taste and Health
1.
Time: When coffee is exposed to water for longer periods, more substances are extracted.
2.
Grind: The coarseness or fineness of your coffee grind, which matches your brew method,
significantly impacts the extraction and flavor. The finer the grind, the more the coffee’s
compounds are made available for extraction.
3.
Brew methods: French press, electric drip brewer, manual pour-over (like Chemex,
AeroPress®, or espresso), cold brewers, etc. Brew methods that allow more oils in the finished 
brew will have more diterpenes (oils with healthy properties, particularly for the liver).
4.
Quantity of coffee to water: The less water you use for the same amount of coffee, the
stronger the brew.
5.
Water temperature: Hotter water extracts more soluble substances from the beans and can 
change the chemical composition of the finished brew.
6.
Stirring: Any stirring, mixing, agitation/turbulence during brewing will increase the
extraction speed.
7.
Pressure: The higher the pressure during brewing (such as with espresso or the AeroPress®), 
the faster the extraction.
8.
Filter: The type of filter can impact the flavor. Wire, metal or plastic woven filters let more 
oils into the brew than paper filters. The oils are aromatic and add flavor and texture to the
coffee.
9.
Water quality: The amount of minerals and chemicals in the water impacts coffee flavor, as 
most brewed coffee is approximately 98 percent water (espresso may be 97 percent).
10. Equipment cleanliness: When equipment collects oils and residue from past brews, it builds
up, imparting stale and rancid notes due to the presence of moisture, bacteria, enzymes,
light, heat, air, and even certain types of metals. Rancid oil forms harmful free radicals in
the body, which are known to cause cellular damage and have been associated with diabetes, 
Alzheimer’s disease and other conditions. Rancid oils can also cause digestive distress and
deplete the body of vitamins B and E. In some severe cases, mold can creep into the nooks
and crannies of brewers, which is also unhealthy. Use your nose! If you can smell old, stale
coffee on your equipment, chances are that aroma will also taint your cup.

453
9.2 Brewing 
Choices in the variables of brew time, water temperature and pressure (used to pass 
the water through the grounds) also influence the resulting compounds and flavors. 
The usual goal of any brew method is to find the perfect combination of variables that 
yields the most pleasant flavors and avoids producing unpleasant flavors, creating a 
delicious beverage. 
Of course, “pleasant” and “delicious” are subjective, and the fascination of coffee is that 
the same batch of roasted beans, brewed by different people using different methods, 
could taste completely different yet be pleasing to the preparer and displeasing to 
others.
Coffee gets most of its flavor from the chemical compounds released when dissolved 
in water. As mentioned before, the water temperature has one of the most significant 
impacts on the brew. The laws of thermodynamics apply here, with the energy (heat) 
used to break apart the chemical bonds within the coffee (Lee, 1992). Of the hundreds 
of chemical constituents in coffee, many have different extraction rates and different 
flavors that will contribute to the cup (Lee 1992). Some compounds may remain 
unaffected because there is insufficient heat energy for reactions. This, of course, is 
the biggest difference between hot and cold-brewed coffees.
“Adjust to taste.” Everyone has their taste preferences for how they like their coffee 
brewed, and by changing the variables of brewing coffee, the same beans could taste 
like totally different coffees. If you want your coffee to taste different, start by only 
adjusting one variable, maybe either the grind or the quantity of coffee you use. Brew 
fresh each time. Do not let the coffee steep on a hot plate for more than a few minutes, 
and please avoid microwaving coffee unless you really need a coffee and do not care 
what it tastes like or how it affects your health. We will discuss heat and chemical 
changes momentarily.

454 ♦ Ildi Revi
It is important to remember that brewing methods can impact flavor and the coffee’s 
health properties. For example, unfiltered coffee (like French press) retains more 
cafestol and kahweol, compounds linked to increased cholesterol levels. On the other 
hand, filtered coffee may remove some beneficial antioxidants.
Understanding these variables allows you to optimize your coffee brewing for both 
taste and potential health benefits. Remember, the perfect cup of coffee is what you 
enjoy and that aligns with your health goals.
TIME IN EXTRACTION
Brewing time affects the concentration of polyphenols and flavonoids in coffee. Longer 
brewing times are associated with lower levels of total polyphenols and, specifically, 
CGA. Conversely, brewing coffee for six instead of three minutes produces a final 
product significantly higher in total flavonoids (Górecki 2020).
The contact time between coffee grounds and water affects extraction:
•	
Shorter Contact Time: Use a finer grind. (Espresso extracted under high 
pressure [9-10 bars] is generally acceptable at a 20-30 second cycle time).
•	
Longer Contact Time: Use a coarser grind. (Ideally, a French press brews 
for 3-5 minutes.)
GRINDING COFFEE
As we will discuss, different brewing methods extract compounds differently. In 
the chapter on compounds, we looked at cholesterol and diterpenes. Diterpene 
concentration also varies based on the coffee preparation method. Boiled and cafetière 
(French Press) coffee has the highest amounts, followed by espresso. Instant and filtered 
coffee have negligible amounts, though (Poole 2017). The average cup of unfiltered 
coffee provides 3-5 milligrams of diterpenes (Ren 2019).
Some people avoid grinders altogether and buy ground coffee. That is an option. As 
we discussed before, though, coffee stales when exposed to oxygen, and ground coffee 
will stale faster than whole-bean coffee. Please see the discussion in the section on 
coffee packaging in the chapter on roasting for more information.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 455
Some people buy whole-bean coffee and use a blender, which can be problematic, so 
resist that temptation. A blender is a blade grinder type that chops the coffee only to 
a certain particle size. Although some particles may be the same size, there is usually 
a wide variation, which makes it hard to make the coffee taste good. You would need 
to keep the blender running for a while, and afterward, you would still have unevenly 
ground coffee, but it would also be heated by friction, which alters the coffee fragrance, 
aroma and flavor compounds.
If you want a grinder that will help you get the best possible flavor out of your coffee, 
you are better off getting a burr grinder. A burr grinder has two metal or ceramic 
discs with sharp upward fins that crush the beans. Some discs are flat, and others are 
cone-shaped (often the choice for espresso). Grinding coffee fresh from whole beans 
before each brew usually provides the best sensory experience. Burr grinders produce 
more uniform grinds than blade grinders, but they also retain residue and small coffee 
particles within the teeth of both the upper and lower burrs. These can impact flavor 
and health.
If you have had a coffee in the past that gave you a histamine-type reaction, and you 
used a burr grinder, you will need to purge your grinder. The easiest way to do this 
is to send a small amount (25 grams) of coffee that does NOT give you a reaction 
through the grinder and throw those grounds in the compost. This practice will have 
seasoned the grinder with good coffee. It is the same issue with decaf. If you drink 
decaf but share a grinder with someone who drinks regular coffee, purge your grinder 
with decaf before grinding for yourself. By following these steps, you can clean or 
maintain your grinder to keep your coffee tasting good. If you have had non-holistic 
coffee in your grinder, you can get rid of most of the residue or leftovers of old coffee 
through the same process

456 ♦ Ildi Revi
BURR GRINDER CLEANING
To clean a burr grinder simply but effectively, 
start by adding anywhere from a tablespoon 
to a half cup (depending on how much old 
residue may be present) of the fresh whole-bean 
coffee you plan to drink. This small amount of 
coffee will help season the burrs, removing any 
residual flavors from previous grinds. Grind the 
beans and then discard the grounds. Next, if it 
is straightforward to do so, use a small brush 
to remove coffee particles from the burrs, the 
grinder’s interior and the chute. If you want 
to be thorough, a vacuum can help suck out 
any remaining fines and dust from hard-to-reach areas, including the chute. After 
brushing and vacuuming, use a dry cloth to wipe down the burrs and the interior of 
the grinder and then purge again. Do not use water, as it can cause rust, damage the 
grinder or introduce moisture into a dry system (a food safety no-no). Grinders often 
have disassembly and reassembly instructions for cleaning. If you are good at that sort 
of thing and want to be super clean, you may go with that alternative. I must admit, 
every time I’ve tried to do that, the grinder never works the same again…
BLADE GRINDER CLEANING
To clean a blade grinder, start by carefully wiping out the interior using a clean cloth. 
Be cautious of the sharp blades to avoid injury. Instead of using your fingers, wrap 
a paper towel or clean cloth around the handle of a spoon to reach into the grinder 
safely. I find a paper towel is effective at getting rid of old oils pretty well.
GRIND YOUR COFFEE TO MATCH YOUR BREWING METHOD. 
•	
Grind fresh within 15 minutes of brewing. 
•	
Grind coarser for French press (cafetiere), medium-to-fine for drip (like 
coarse decorator’s sugar), and fine for espresso. 
Burr in need of cleaning]

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 457
•
Changing the grind particle size can significantly impact the taste of the
final brew, so play around with it. The finer the grind, the more coffee
components will be extracted. Therefore, the more precise the grinder, the
greater the chance of finding just the right grind for your taste.
•
The longer the coffee grounds are in contact with water, the more it will
extract. Having fine grinds mixed with coarse grinds in ratios that are too
large will make it more difficult to find the best taste. It is fine that all
grinders produce variation, which adds complexity to the finished brew.
•
One tablespoon of a fine grind weighs more than one tablespoon of coarse
grind, so scoop or spoonful recommendations are too imprecise.
Experiment with grind size to adjust taste: the finer the grind, the more extraction 
occurs. 
BREWING METHODS WITH HEALTH NOTES
Below are general descriptions of a few brewing methods. Of course, there are 
many methods and many manufacturers of equipment. Extraction of compounds 
is discussed.
ELECTRIC DRIP BREW
Electric drip brewing involves adding ground coffee to a filter 
placed within an automatic electric coffee maker. Hot water at 
around 200°F (93 °C) passes through the grounds by gravity. Some 
machines are designed to release hot water in cycles, allowing for 
better extraction and preventing overflow of fresh coffee. Advanced 
commercial coffee makers can also adjust the water temperature to 
refine brewing.
FULL-IMMERSION BREW 
The most common form of full-immersion brewing is the French 
press. Coarsely ground coffee is placed directly into the vessel, and 

458 ♦ Ildi Revi
hot water around 200°F (93 °C) is poured over the grounds. After stirring the coffee 
to break the surface crust, a metal mesh filter attached to a disk presses the ground 
to the bottom. The finished beverage contains fine particles and coffee oils, adding 
to its mouthfeel. The longer you steep, the more compounds you will extract up to 
a certain point. 
Another device is a Clever Dripper® that combines immersion and drip brewing. Coffee 
grounds are steeped in hot water before releasing the brewed coffee through a valve at 
the bottom when placed on top of a cup. (Clever Dripper® is a registered trademark 
of Clever Coffee Brewers, distributed by Eight Ounce Coffee. )
EXTRACTION EQUILIBRIUM IN FULL-IMMERSION BREWING
Full-immersion brewing methods, such 
as French press, cold brew, and cupping, 
rely on soaking coffee grounds in water 
for extended periods, allowing soluble 
compounds to diffuse into the liquid. But 
this process does not go on indefinitely. 
Scientific research confirms that these 
methods eventually reach extraction 
equilibrium, a point at which the 
concentration of dissolved compounds 
in the water stabilizes and no significant 
further extraction occurs.
In a 2021 Scientific Reports study, 
researchers proposed a pseudo-
equilibrium desorption model for full-immersion brewing. Their experiments showed 
that total extraction yield (E) reaches a stable value of approximately 21%, regardless 
of the brew ratio, grind size, or even temperature within typical ranges. In other 
words, once the coffee and water reach equilibrium, increasing steep time will not 
appreciably increase the amount of coffee extracted, unless you alter other conditions 
like agitation, particle size, or pressure.
Extraction equilibrium: The point at which 
the concentration of extracted compounds in 
the brew stabilizes.
Steady-state concentration: A practical term 
describing when compound levels in the liquid 
phase remain constant over time.
Pseudo-equilibrium desorption: A modeling 
framework that treats coffee brewing as a 
reversible process where solubles reach a near-
equilibrium with the brewing water.

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦459
Cold brew coffee follows a similar pattern. A 2017 study found that caffeine and 
3-chlorogenic acid (3-CGA) follow first-order kinetics during extraction, with 
concentrations rising early and plateauing between 6 and 7 hours, well before the 
12- to 24-hour steeping times often recommended in recipes. This plateau reflects a 
steady-state concentration, where the rate of compound diffusion into the liquid is 
balanced by the system’s physical and chemical limits.
Understanding extraction equilibrium can help avoid over-brewing and unnecessary 
steeping times, while also informing choices around brew ratio, grind size, and 
temperature. It reinforces the idea that longer isn’t always better, and that delicious 
coffee is the result of balance, not excess.
Below is an example of lab results for coffees brewed by full immersion following 
the manufacturer’s instructions. You can see how caffeine, trigonelline, and CGA are 
extracted in an example of an immersion brew. The coffee was ground about the same 
as drip coffee, brewed at 205ºF (96.1ºC) and steeped for 3 minutes:

460 ♦ Ildi Revi
The AeroPress® is considered another type of full-immersion coffee brewer. In the AeroPress® 
brewing process, coffee grounds are fully immersed in hot water for a period of time before 
being pressed through a filter. AeroPress is a registered trademark of AeroPress, Inc.
POUR OVER BREW 
Pour-over brewing is a manual version of 
drip brewing. A filter within a brew basket 
or funnel is placed on top of a decanter. The 
funnel and filter are often pre-heated, rinsed 
with near-boiling water, and discarded from 
the decanter. Ground coffee is added to the filter, and the preparer carefully pours 
water over it while timing the process from when the water contacts the grounds. 
Various pouring techniques can be used to ensure optimal extraction.
ESPRESSO
Espresso is a concentrated coffee drink, typically about 
30 ml (1 ounce), made using around 8 grams of finely 
ground coffee per shot. Water at around 202°F 
(94.4°C) is forced through the coffee at about 9 to 10 
atmospheres of pressure. There is a debate about 
whether the term “espresso” originates from the high 
pressure or the quick extraction time (20-35 seconds), 

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦461
which makes it an express version of brewing, or “expresso”, a term some people cringe 
at when they hear it. So, perhaps it is not altogether incorrect. Though many associate 
a dark roast with espresso, beans of any roast level can be used. The rich, reddish-brown 
foam on top of an espresso is called “crema.” 
Although a Moka pot is often called a “stovetop espresso 
maker,” it does not produce true espresso because it 
operates at a lower pressure (1-2 bars) than espresso 
machines. Instead, it brews a strong, concentrated coffee 
that resembles espresso but lacks the crema and intensity 
of a true espresso shot. The brewed coffee that ends up in 
the top portion of the moka pot can overheat, too, affecting 
the coffee’s compounds like CGA, which degrade at high temperatures. 
SIPHON BREW
Also known as vacuum pot brewing, siphon brewing uses an open 
flame to combine immersion brewing with vacuum pressure. 
Water is placed in a glass globe at the bottom and heated to rapid 
boiling. The resulting pressure forces the water upward through a 
filter into the upper chamber, where medium-ground coffee awaits. 
After a brief stirring, the flame is reduced or turned off, causing the 
brewed coffee to be drawn down through the filter back into the 
globe due to pressure changes and gravity. The stages are depicted 
below in the photo.

462 ♦ Ildi Revi
COLD BREW 
Cold brewing involves steeping ground coffee with water at room temperature or 
cooler. This is fully discussed in its own section at the end of this chapter.
SAMPLE EXTRACTIONS
The total CGA in coffee brews varies with the degree of roasting. Light-roasted coffee 
brews contain between 187.7 and 295.6 mg of CGA per 100 mL, while dark-roasted 
coffee brews contain between 24.2 and 41.3 mg per 100 mL. Another study found that 
espresso made from light, medium, and dark roasted beans contained 1060 mg, 517 
mg, and 340 mg of CGA per 100 mL, respectively. Regardless of the roasting level, the 
total CGA in espresso from different sources ranged from 89 to 811 mg per 100 mL. 
Below are some examples from Purity Coffee testing of starting levels of compounds 
in dry coffee grounds before brewing, as well as what was extracted in the brewing 
method. Note that this is just one snapshot of one day of brewing one type of coffee. I 
present this to demonstrate how difficult it is to tell consumers how much of a certain 
substance may be in their cup of coffee. 

The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦463
FILTERS
Use a type of filter that matches your brew method and is made from a material that 
does not trigger your allergies. Three common filter materials are noted below, with 
the point being that you choose one that meets your health needs:
Paper: Review the materials from which your paper filters 
are made and note how they are packaged. Are they bleached 
or brown paper? Are they made of natural fibers that may 
contain allergens? Are they in packaging that is sealed 
properly, or can contaminants get into the package? Paper 
filters can also filter out most of the coffee oils that contain 
diterpenes, which are considered beneficial to liver health. 
Metal: Metal filters may also be used in some brewing 
equipment, but they should be non-reactive and made of 
high-quality stainless steel. Avoid double metal filters, which 
are difficult to clean and retain oils and particles that can go 
rancid. Espresso machines rely on metal brew baskets in their 
portafilters, which must also be kept clean at home or in a 
café. Not only does oil residue lurk in metal filters, but microparticles of coffee may 
get stuck in the mesh or holes. Some people are sensitive to metals or have health issues 
related to metals. In that case, certain metals, such as nickel, copper and chromium, 
could cause problems. Also, as mentioned before, metal coffee filters can allow higher 
levels of cafestol to pass into the brewed coffee, potentially raising cholesterol levels 
in frequent coffee drinkers. For those not concerned with cholesterol nor metal 
sensitivity and drinking coffee for liver health, metal filters are a good choice to let 
through compounds that paper filters block. 
Cloth: These are good options for getting the healthy coffee 
oils into your cup while capturing micro-grounds and 
keeping your cup sediment-free if you prefer it that way. Of 
course, washing and drying cloth filters involve factors that 
can affect health too, and at some point, they will start to 
infuse off-flavors into the brew.

464 ♦ Ildi Revi
COFFEE-TO-WATER RATIOS
Using volume measurements can be imprecise because grind sizes vary. For more 
accuracy, weigh your coffee.
Professional baristas use a balance (mass scale) to measure the brew’s water amount. 
Water’s weight is the same regardless of temperature, as its mass does not change. 
However, its density slightly changes, meaning the volume might differ slightly for a 
given mass of water at different temperatures.
For example:
•
Cold Water (around 39.2°F [4°C]): The temperature at which water is
most dense. 1 liter (1000 milliliters) of cold water weighs exactly 1000
grams.
•
Boiling Water (212°F [100°C]): The density decreases slightly, so 1 liter of
boiling water would weigh slightly less than 1000 grams. Although minor
for most practical purposes, it makes a difference in precision brewing.
For precise calculations, the density of water at 100°C is about 0.95865 grams per 
milliliter:
•
1 liter of boiling water weighs approximately 958.65 grams.
For basic brewing purposes, cold and boiling water weigh nearly the same, with a 
very slight reduction in weight for boiling water. So, when I put milliliters of water, 
the milliliters can be thought of as grams, too, since 1 gram of water is 1 milliliter. I 
mention this considering that you may you use a scale to measure your water instead 
of a measuring flask. Suggested ratios:
•
General: 18-22 grams of ground coffee per 350 ml of water (about 4-5
tablespoons of whole beans).
•
Large Quantities: 3.75 oz. of ground coffee per half gallon (64 fl. oz.) water;
55-70 grams per liter.
•
Espresso: 7-9 grams of fine-grind coffee per 25-35 ml.
•
Ibrik: 2.5-3 grams of powdery-grind coffee per 30 ml (1 fl. oz.).

BREWING TEMPERATURE AND TIME
Brew using water temperatures between 195°F-205°F (92°C-96°C) for 3 to 5 minutes. 
Factors like temperature, time, and turbulence (water agitation through coffee 
grounds) all influence extraction. In a brewing machine, the showerhead provides 
agitation. For manual brewing, you can stir or control the water pouring.
To test water temperature, you can get a kettle or brewer that shows temperature, or 
invest in a thermometer. These are easy to find online or at cooking supply stores. 
CLEAN EQUIPMENT
Always discard used grounds immediately and clean your equipment according to the 
manufacturer’s instructions. If your equipment smells like stale coffee, it will affect 
your brew’s taste. Using spirit vinegar to clean is a tried-and-true healthy way to get 
rid of coffee oils and off-smells… just be sure to rinse it all away.
Molds and microorganisms can lurk in damp, dark, wet crevices of machines, and it 
is important to wash parts of the brewer and allow them to air dry. Manual pourover 
brew methods, such as Chemex, are the simplest equipment and are easy to maintain 
regarding sanitation. Also, be aware of the material into which your coffee is brewed. 
Some metal carafes or travel mugs may contain nickel, known to cause reactions, and 
some may have BPA or phthalates. Glass or ceramic containers may be a better bet.
Don’t forget your grinder. Do a sniff check on your grinder to detect any signs of 
staleness or rancidity. This simple step usually tells you what you need to know. While 

466 ♦ Ildi Revi
commercial grinder cleaners are available, the purge method of using fresh beans and 
careful cleaning has proven highly effective. 
COFFEE ADDITIVES
When discussing the additives in coffee regarding health, it mainly revolves around 
polyphenols, such as CGA. Polyphenols can interact with proteins, altering their 
structural, functional, and nutritional properties. Factors such as temperature, pH, and 
the specific types of proteins and phenolic compounds play a role in these interactions. 
These interactions can lead to protein solubility, stability, and digestibility changes, 
potentially changing their bioavailability, bioactivity, nutritional value and antioxidant 
capacity. In other words, the stuff we add to coffee, including milk or protein-rich 
additives, whether concocting nutrition shakes or just having a cup of coffee, can 
significantly affect its health benefits and how the body processes it. Understanding 
these interactions can help optimize coffee preparation to maximize health advantages.
Many people add dairy products to coffee, such as half and half or milk. These 
products naturally contain calcium, protein, saturated fats, and the natural sugar 
lactose. Based on the principle of “like dissolves like,” it is reasonable to assume that 
adding some fats to coffee could enhance the absorption of lipophilic compounds, 
including cafestol and kahweol, which present as fatty esters (Eastep and Chen 2015). 
Unfortunately, I could not find where this effect has been researched, specifically 
regarding diterpenes and dietary fat. Since dairy cream is a popular coffee additive, it 
is unclear whether the effects of saturated fat on insulin sensitivity would counter or 
promote any potential benefits (Rice Bradley 2018). 
Research shows that how you consume your coffee affects your health, particularly 
affecting microbiota populations in the small and large intestines, and creating 
variations in the bioavailability of CGA and CGL. Some studies show that adding milk 
to coffee reduces the bioavailability and activity of CGA because of the whey proteins 
(β-lactoglobulin) and casein. The amount of CGA and its metabolites recovered in 
urine after coffee with milk is consistently lower than in plain coffee, suggesting that 
milk alters the absorption and metabolism of these antioxidant compounds. Soy 
milk did not affect coffee’s CGA bioavailability (Farah 2015). Nut milk or creamers 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 467
containing unsaturated fats could be a better alternative to dairy to help optimize 
coffee’s health benefits, as long as you do not have allergies to any ingredients.
Sugar (sucrose) is also commonly added to coffee to reduce bitterness. While sugar 
consumption is not the sole cause of diabetes, high-sugar diets promote obesity and 
inflammation, leading to insulin resistance (Walbolt 2020). Depending on the amount 
of sugar added to coffee and the number of sweetened coffees consumed daily, coffee 
can become a significant source of added sugar for some individuals.
To avoid sugar, many people with diabetes choose to sweeten their coffee with non-
nutritive or artificial sweeteners, sometimes referred to as sugar substitutes. These 
products range from chemical sweeteners (aspartame and sucralose) to natural herbs 
like stevia. Research on artificial sweeteners has not established definitive benefits for 
diabetes prevention, blood sugar control, or weight management. Recent research 
suggests that certain non-nutritive sweeteners, particularly saccharine, may induce 
insulin resistance (Walbolt 2020).
One study on non-nutritive sweeteners provided participants with sucralose or water 
before performing a glucose tolerance test. The sucralose group experienced a spike in 
insulin, supporting the theory that regular consumption promotes hyperinsulinemia 
and decreased insulin sensitivity. Additionally, observational studies have identified 
a dose-dependent correlation between type 2 diabetes risk and artificially sweetened 
beverage intake (Mathur 2020).
While more research is needed to confirm the suspected link between artificial 
sweeteners and insulin resistance, recommending increased artificial sweetener use for 
those at risk of diabetes remains controversial. Despite that, stevia is gaining popularity 
and may serve as a better alternative (Walbolt 2020). In one study, healthy adults 
who consumed a stevia-sweetened versus a sugar-sweetened beverage with a meal 
experienced lower postprandial insulin and glucose levels (Stamataki 2020). In another 
12-week randomized trial, participants were instructed to add daily stevia drops to 
their beverages. The stevia group showed no change in glycemic control or body 
weight compared to the control group, which experienced gradual weight gain during 
the study period. Despite promising observations, longer-term, placebo-controlled 
interventions would be helpful to expand on these early findings (Stamataki 2020).

468 ♦ Ildi Revi
Beyond stevia and sucralose, the world of alternative sweeteners is expanding rapidly. 
Newer options such as monk fruit extract, allulose, erythritol, tagatose, and various 
proprietary blends have entered the market, each with its own claims around glycemic 
neutrality, gut health, or antioxidant potential. These sweeteners differ widely in their 
metabolic pathways, impact on insulin sensitivity, and influence on the microbiome, 
and research is still catching up. Some compounds show promise for reducing glucose 
spikes or caloric load, while others raise concerns about gastrointestinal tolerance or 
long-term effects. The landscape is dynamic and complex, and the science behind 
these alternatives is far too broad for me to cover in this chapter. For now, I would 
encourage consumers to prioritize labeling transparency, monitor their responses, and 
recognize that the “best” sweetener may differ depending on individual health goals 
and tolerances.
Other natural herbs and spices with established anti-diabetic effects could bring 
new flavors and additional health benefits to coffee. For example, there are several 
mechanisms by which cinnamon controls blood sugar. Some evidence demonstrates 
that a natural compound in cinnamon, cinnamaldehyde, is superior to metformin 
in reducing plasma glucose levels. (Sharma 2020) Since cinnamon is well-liked and 
generally recognized as safe by the FDA, it easily complements other functional foods, 
like coffee. Unfortunately, as I write this, cinnamon is in the news for being recalled 
due to toxic levels of lead found in certain brands that a particular food processor 
intentionally adulterated.
Lead in contaminated cinnamon is a stark reminder that heavy metals can find their 
way into even the most natural and health-promoting foods. While cinnamon has long 
been recognized for its blood sugar-lowering properties, its benefits are meaningless 
if tainted with harmful substances. This highlights the importance of vigilance in 
food sourcing, laboratory testing, and transparency, which are principles that apply 
as much to coffee as to spices and other functional foods. Lead contamination is not 
isolated to one ingredient or industry; it is a widespread concern affecting the air we 
breathe, the water we drink, and the soil our food grows in.
For those prioritizing health, it is essential to be aware of potential sources of lead 
exposure and take proactive steps to minimize risks. Whether through selecting 
laboratory-tested products, choosing reputable brands, or advocating for stricter 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 469
food safety regulations, awareness is the first step toward meaningful change. As 
research continues to uncover new ways to enhance coffee’s health benefits, from 
natural sweeteners like stevia to blood sugar-regulating spices, it is equally important 
to ensure that these additions meet the highest purity and safety standards. In this 
pursuit, knowledge is power, and transparency is the key to making informed, health-
conscious choices.
 STORING ROASTED COFFEE
Coffee beans are like little, hard sponges that absorb odors into their little cells and 
contain the aromatic oils we love. The basic principles of keeping roasted coffee fresh 
are to keep your coffee away from:
1. Moisture
2. Heat
3. Direct Sunlight
4. Oxygen (as much as possible)
5. Odors
Once opened, coffee should be stored in a well-sealed, opaque container on your 
pantry shelf. 
FREEZER OR FRIDGE
The Purity Coffee customer support team is often asked if it is sensible to store coffee 
in the freezer or refrigerator. While we do not recommend refrigerator storage, freezer 
storage can be beneficial when done correctly.
Refrigerator storage (~5°C/41°F) creates several problems for coffee preservation. 
While this temperature slows the degradation of key aroma compounds like aldehydes, 
pyrazines, and furans compared to room temperature storage, it introduces more 
serious issues. The refrigerator environment exposes coffee to temperature fluctuations, 
odor absorption, and most critically, condensation cycles.
If you take your coffee out of the freezer or refrigerator daily, you expose the coffee 
beans to condensation as warm, humid air contacts the cold bean surface. This creates 

470 ♦ Ildi Revi
the same effect as moisture forming on a glass of ice water, except it happens on each 
individual coffee bean. This moisture penetration accelerates staling reactions and can 
make your brew taste flat or cardboard-like within days. Additionally, coffee readily 
absorbs odors from other foods stored nearby, compromising its flavor integrity.
Freezing coffee below -18°C (0°F) nearly halts the loss of volatile aroma compounds 
and significantly slows oxidation reactions that degrade coffee quality. At these 
temperatures, the molecular movement responsible for staling reactions is dramatically 
reduced, potentially extending coffee freshness for several months rather than weeks.
The key to successful freezer storage lies in moisture exclusion and airtight sealing. 
When coffee is properly protected from air and moisture, freezing suppresses both 
oxidation and moisture-driven staling mechanisms that rapidly degrade coffee at 
higher temperatures.
PROPER FREEZER STORAGE PROTOCOL
It is acceptable to freeze well-protected, unopened packages of whole bean coffee for 
up to one month. For optimal results:
•	
Ensure airtight packaging - The original sealed bag or vacuum-sealed 
container can prevent moisture infiltration and odor absorption
•	
Freeze only whole beans - Ground coffee has exponentially more surface 
area exposed to degradation
•	
Single-use portions - Divide large quantities into single-use portions before 
freezing to avoid repeated freeze-thaw cycles
The thawing process is the most critical. When you remove frozen beans from the 
freezer, allow them to reach room temperature completely before opening the package. 
This equilibration process will help prevent condensation from forming on the bean 
surface (and moisture getting into your grinder) when the package is opened. Once 
the coffee reaches room temperature, remove it from the freezer permanently and use 
it within two weeks, treating it as you would freshly roasted coffee.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 471
THE BOTTOM LINE FOR HEALTH-FOCUSED COFFEE
For CHC where compound preservation is critical, proper freezer storage can help 
maintain beneficial compounds like CGA and antioxidants that are sensitive to 
oxidation. However, the best practice remains purchasing smaller quantities more 
frequently and storing them in a cool, dark, dry place at room temperature in airtight 
containers.

472
9.3 Cold Brew
Cold-brew coffee, which involves steeping ground coffee in cool or room-temperature 
water for an extended period, offers a distinct flavor profile compared to traditional 
hot brewing. Cold-brew taste differs noticeably from that of coffee brewed at higher 
temperatures.
The concept of cold brew is that ground coffee is extracted with water at room 
temperature (“ambient” = not chilled or heated) or cooler and steeped for an extended 
period to extract flavor compounds and soluble solids from the coffee. The key point 
is that the water is not hot. The brewing process swaps time for temperature. Instead 
of brewing with near-boiling water for a few minutes, cold brewing typically uses 
cool water and extended periods (12–24 hours) to extract an optimal amount of 
flavor compounds and solids from the ground beans. Research indicates that optimal 
extraction may occur sooner than traditionally thought, with significant compound 
extraction achieved within 6-9 hours for most brewing methods (Fuller 2017, Cerca 
2023). However, both papers note that sensory attributes, such as flavor, body, and 
aroma, may continue to evolve beyond this point, likely due to the slower extraction 
of other compounds. This helps explain why cold brew methods often recommend 
steeping times of 12 to 24 hours, even after the major bioactive compounds have 
reached a plateau.
GROUNDBREAKING GUT HEALTH RESEARCH: 
COLD BREW POLYSACCHARIDES
The most remarkable research on cold brew and gut health was conducted in 2017 by 
Kwang-Soon Shin in South Korea, revolving around crude polysaccharides (CCP) and 
their immune-stimulating activities. CCPs are carbohydrates that boost the immunity 
of your digestive system and are mainly galactose, mannose, arabinose, and uronic acid.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 473
Results indicate that CCPs isolated from cold-brew coffee can serve as an inducer 
of the systemic immune system and activate macrophage function and intestinal 
immunity. Macrophages and dendritic cells are distributed throughout the body and 
play important roles in pathogen detection and tissue homeostasis (Cain 2013).
Shin’s study found that these polysaccharides significantly increased cell proliferation in 
murine peritoneal macrophages (immune cells found in the abdominal cavity of mice) 
and splenocytes (a mixture of immune cells derived from the spleen). Additionally, 
they enhanced the production of nitric oxide, reactive oxygen species, and various 
cytokines, suggesting potential benefits for macrophage functions and the intestinal 
immune system.
The research also demonstrated effects on Peyer’s patches, which are lymphoid 
tissues distributed throughout the small intestine and ileum containing lymphocytes, 
macrophages, dendritic cells and microfold cells (important for antigen uptake). 
Peyer’s patches serve as the gut’s immune system, where foods and potential pathogens 
are first absorbed into the body.
Studies have reported the role of CCP in activating the systemic immune system by 
activating the intestinal immune system (Shin 2017).
Immune System Benefits from CCP:
•
Granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor production increased
significantly
•
Bone-marrow-cell proliferation increased by more than 40 percent
•
Enhanced cell proliferation in immune cells
•
Increased production of nitric oxide and cytokines
•
Activation of Peyer’s patches in the small intestine
Optimal Parameters for Gut Health:
•
Coarse grinding for better polysaccharide extraction
•
Extended extraction times (18-24 hours) specifically for CCP benefits
•
Room temperature extraction for maximum compound availability

474 ♦ Ildi Revi
COLD BREWING HEALTH NOTES
It is essential to prioritize food safety to prevent microbial contamination during 
brewing. Studies confirm that cold brew coffee may support the survival of harmful 
bacteria such as Listeria monocytogenes, Salmonella spp., and E. coli, even when 
refrigerated (Daeschel 2017).  Viable cells of all of these were found between 7 to 14 
days after inoculation. L. monocytogenes survived up to three weeks after inoculation 
with refrigerated cold brew coffee. It is the pathogen of greatest concern for cold brew 
coffee production and can be controlled with a thorough cleaning of equipment and 
the environment (Lopez 2020).
Antioxidant degradation and aroma loss are also noted during extended storage 
(Bellumori 2023), so it highlights the need for refrigeration, airtight storage, and 
strict sanitation protocols throughout brewing and packaging. Potential sources of 
contamination include water quality, containers, equipment, environment, personnel, 
and ingredients.  
IMPORTANT SAFETY FACTORS:
Water Quality: Use only potable water meeting WHO, EPA, or local drinking 
water standards we discussed earlier. Even low levels of bacterial contamination 
in source water can multiply during extended cold extraction periods. The water 
serves as the primary vehicle for any microorganisms present in the coffee grounds 
or equipment.
Temperature Control: Cold brewing at room temperature increases the risk of 
microbial development, as yeast, bacteria, and mold thrive in the 40º to 140ºF 
zone, known as the “danger zone” for optimal microbial growth. Brewing in the 
refrigerator is considered safer, but certain microorganisms can still survive at these 
temperatures.
Equipment Sanitization: All brewing equipment must be thoroughly cleaned 
and sanitized before each use. Research has identified Bacillus subtilis and Bacillus 
licheniformis in coffee equipment, emphasizing the importance of proper cleaning 
protocols (Zuniga 2016). These organisms can survive on surfaces and potentially 
contaminate subsequent brews.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 475
A risky argument used by some in the coffee industry points to the concept that coffee 
is antiseptic. While coffee does contain natural antimicrobial compounds that we have 
discussed throughout this book, including caffeic acid, trigonelline, and CGA, it’s 
vital to understand that these compounds are preventive rather than actively curative 
and should never be relied upon as a primary food safety strategy. Just leave a cup of 
coffee on your desk for a few days or forget to empty your brew basket full of grounds 
before you go on vacation for two weeks, and you will see that coffee allows organisms 
to grow. Proper sanitation, temperature control, and hygiene practices remain the 
foundation of safe cold brew preparation.
One antimicrobial compound worth highlighting for cold brew, which we haven’t 
discussed earlier, is protocatechuic acid, a phenolic compound formed primarily during 
roasting as CGA break down. Although it appears in relatively low concentrations 
in coffee (typically between 0.01–0.51 mg/kg depending on roast level and bean 
origin), protocatechuic acid has drawn scientific interest for its antioxidant, anti-
inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. It has demonstrated inhibitory activity 
against various pathogens, including Salmonella enterica, Enterobacter cloacae, and 
Serratia marcescens (Almeida 2006, Kamiyama 2015). Cold brew coffee may preserve 
more of this compound, making it especially relevant in conversations around gut 
health and microbiome support. 
OPTIMAL EXTRACTION TIMES FOR SAFETY AND HEALTH
Research by Cerca et al. (2023) provides specific guidance on extraction timing for 
both safety and optimal compound extraction:
•
For E. coli protection: 9 hours of extraction at room temperature (25°C)
provides sufficient antimicrobial activity
•
For B. cereus protection: Just 3 hours is adequate for effective antimicrobial
action
•
For compound extraction: Shorter extraction times may be both safer and
more efficient than commonly practiced extended brewing periods, with
6-7 hours achieving equilibrium for caffeine and CGA extraction. Although

476 ♦ Ildi Revi
this is contrary to traditional 12-24 hour recommendations, you’ll still get 
the taste compounds to make it nice to drink.
COLD BREWING PRACTICE
Store freshly made cold brew concentrate in the refrigerator for no more than a 
week. Mark the expiration date on the container and follow thorough cleaning and 
sanitation procedures for all brewing equipment. It is a way of life for those with 
histamine intolerance to consider what other things they are ingesting along with 
their cups of coffee that may be high in histamine. Impurities may also slip in during 
beverage preparation, not to mention if anything is added to the final cup (sweeteners 
and creamers).
HEALTHY COLD BREWING POINT 
1:OPTIMIZE CONCENTRATION
Research demonstrates that more concentrated cold brews (15% coffee-to-water 
ratio) extracted at room temperature show superior antimicrobial activity compared 
to weaker concentrations (Cerca 2023). The higher concentration of bioactive 
compounds provides:
•	
Enhanced antimicrobial protection
•	
Better extraction efficiency
•	
Concentrated product suitable for dilution
•	
Improved shelf stability when refrigerated
Recommendation: Prepare concentrated cold brew (15% ratio) for later dilution 
rather than weaker ready-to-drink preparations.
Drinking a clean, safely prepared glass of cold brew could improve gastrointestinal 
well-being. With this in mind, an especially healthy coffee roasted conscientiously for 
gut health would make an exceptional tonic.

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 477
HEALTHY COLD BREWING POINT 2: CHOOSING 
AND PREPARING YOUR COFFEE
COFFEE SPECIES SELECTION FOR HEALTH BENEFITS
We looked at the health compounds of Robusta and Arabica in the farming chapter. 
Robusta cold brews consistently showed higher levels of bioactive compounds and 
stronger antimicrobial activity compared to Arabica due to their higher caffeine and 
chlorogenic acid content (Cerca 2023):
Robusta Advantages:
•
Higher caffeine content provides enhanced antimicrobial activity
•
Increased CGA levels for superior antioxidant benefits
•
Higher melanoidin content for gut health benefits
Arabica Benefits:
•
Higher trigonelline content for neuroprotective effects
•
Generally preferred flavor profile
•
Better compound retention during processing
Recommendation: Consider Robusta-based cold brews or Robusta-Arabica blends 
for maximum health benefits while balancing flavor preferences.
BREWING METHOD SELECTION
Drip and Immersion Methods: Both dripping and immersion (with or without filter) 
methods demonstrate excellent compound extraction and antimicrobial properties, 
preventing multiplication of tested bacterial strains (Cerca 2023).
Vacuum Method: Shows significantly lower compound extraction and minimal 
antimicrobial activity. This method is less suitable for health-focused cold brewing.

478 ♦ Ildi Revi
GRIND SIZE OPTIMIZATION 
Particle size significantly impacts both extraction efficiency and health compound 
availability. Research indicates that particles of 0.65mm show substantially higher 
antioxidant potential than coarser grinds of 1.15mm and 1.65mm (Morrisey 2019).
Grind size distribution uniformity is crucial for optimal compound extraction, a 
narrow particle size distribution (more uniform grinding) allows for better control 
over extracted compounds and avoids over-extraction of bitter compounds while 
under-extracting beneficial ones. Córdoba noted that particle size distribution from 
the grinder played a significant role in the chemical composition of cold brew coffee, 
with narrow grind size distribution being important for evenly transferring soluble 
compounds into the brew (Córdoba 2019).
Cold brew coffees prepared within 14 hours using a coarse grind scored the highest 
taste points with stronger perceived sweetness, medium bitterness and acidity. The 
pH values of cold and hot brew samples were comparable, ranging from 4.85 to 5.13, 
though all cold brew coffee samples had lower titratable acidity values than their hot 
counterparts (Córdoba 2019).
Recommendation: Use a high-quality burr grinder capable of producing uniform 
particle sizes. Avoid blade grinders that create inconsistent particle sizes. The more 
accurate your grinder is, where it has a narrow range of size particles (more uniform, 
not having some like powder while others are like chunks), the more you can focus 
on adjusting for the compounds that you want to bring out in the coffee.
HEALTHY COLD BREWING POINT 3:  
MAXIMIZE ANTIOXIDANT ACTIVITY AND GUT HEALTH
ROAST LEVEL AND ANTIOXIDANTS
CGA and caffeine are found at higher concentrations in cold brew coffee made with 
medium roast coffees rather than dark roast (Fuller 2017). However, grind size had 
more impact than roast level on antioxidant potential. A 2024 study further supports 
that lighter roast levels and elevated brewing temperatures enhance antioxidant 
extraction (Morrisey 2024).

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 479
STORAGE AND SERVING RECOMMENDATIONS
Temperature Control: Store finished cold brew at 4°C (39°F) or below immediately 
after brewing. Even with antimicrobial compounds present, cold brew requires 
refrigeration for safety.
Container Selection: Use airtight, food-grade containers to prevent contamination 
and preserve compound integrity.
Duration Guidelines:
•
Concentrated cold brew (15%): Up to 2 weeks refrigerated
•
Ready-to-drink strength: 7-10 days refrigerated
•
Signs of spoilage: Off-odors, visible mold, sour taste, or slimy texture
Antioxidant Preservation: Research shows antioxidant degradation occurs during 
storage, especially after three months, with aroma compound losses accelerating by 
the ninth month (Bellumori 2023). For maximum health benefits, consume cold 
brew fresh and within recommended timeframes.
SERVING FOR HEALTH BENEFITS
Dilution Ratios: When using concentrated cold brew (15%), dilute with clean water, 
milk, or milk alternatives according to taste preferences while maintaining beneficial 
compound levels.
pH Considerations: Cold brew coffee typically has pH values ranging from 4.85 to 
5.13, comparable to hot brew. While perceived as less acidic, this is due to different 
acid profiles rather than lower acid content (Córdoba 2019).

480 ♦ Ildi Revi
CONCLUSION
Cold brew coffee offers unique opportunities for health optimization through careful 
attention to brewing parameters, safety protocols, and compound preservation. By 
understanding the science behind extraction timing, antimicrobial activity, and gut 
health benefits, coffee enthusiasts can maximize both the safety and health benefits 
of their cold brew while enjoying exceptional flavor.
The key to healthy cold brewing lies in balancing optimal extraction conditions with 
rigorous food safety practices, choosing appropriate coffee species and processing 
methods, and maintaining proper storage and serving protocols. Through this 
scientific approach, cold brew becomes not just a refreshing beverage, but a functional 
food that supports overall health and wellness.
PUTTING IT ALL TOGETHER: BASIC RECIPE 
FOR COLD BREW COFFEE
GOAL:
The goal is to extract as many pleasing flavors as possible from the ground coffee beans 
into the cup and create a balanced and delicious beverage.
HOW:
There are numerous methods of preparing cold brew. They all involve immersing 
coffee in cold water for a certain amount of time, depending on how important the 
extraction of different compounds is to you. The research indicates that about 24 
hours is good for maximum antioxidant extraction.
Below is a basic recipe you can apply to any cold brew device. Using a French press 
most easily demonstrates the basic principles of cold brewing that can be applied to 
other methods.
Device:
French press with about 1.5-liter capacity 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 481
Filter:
Stainless steel mesh is included with the French press (some presses have filters 
made of other materials). 
Coffee:
Medium-coarsely ground. 125g (4.5 oz or approximately 1.5 measuring cups 
firmly packed coffee grounds. 
Water:
1000ml (approx. 32 oz) ,  cold or room temperature, clean. 
For a more concentrated cold brew, try 100g of coffee with 500 ml of water. 
For a less concentrated brew, try 100g of coffee with 1000 ml of water. 
Time:
6-7 hours
PROCESS:
•
Grind the coffee fresh just before mixing it with water in the carafe. Ensure
that all the grounds are thoroughly saturated with water.
•
The grounds will have risen to the carafe’s top, called the “crust.” Stir again
after 5 minutes using your spoon to break the crust and submerge the
grounds.
•
Place the French press plunger in the carafe and gently push it slightly below
the surface to submerge the grounds. The press cover should be positioned
close to the top of the carafe to prevent foreign odors or particles from
entering the cold brew.
•
Place the French press in the refrigerator with the top sealing the container
so that it is not sitting open, and let it sit for 6-7 hours.
•
Filter the cold brew concentrate by slowly pushing the plunger down. The
coarser grinds will act like a net for the finer particles, most of which would
have likely fallen to the bottom of the flask by this time anyway.

482 ♦ Ildi Revi
■
If you are watching your cholesterol, you may want to take an
additional step by filtering your coffee through a funnel with a paper
filter. This will trap much of the oils that are of concern for those
with high cholesterol. Although there is some debate about this.
•
Create your final beverage by diluting it with water or ice.
•
Store the cold brew concentrate in a closed container in the fridge for up to
7 days, preferably shorter. Mark the expiration date yourself with masking
tape and a marker.
•
When the cold brew is finished, thoroughly clean the French press carafe,
filter (if it’s removable), and lid, sanitizing each tiny nook and cranny. Air-
dry and store in a clean, hygienic area.

the more it will extract.

10
Final Thoughts
As we close this journey into the world of Circular Health Coffee, I invite you to 
reflect on what coffee has the potential to become beyond a beverage: a bridge between 
health, ecology, culture, and daily ritual.
We’ve seen that coffee, when grown and processed intentionally, can support liver 
health, reduce the risk of type 2 diabetes and neurodegenerative diseases, and offer 
protective benefits against cardiovascular disease and some cancers. These outcomes 
depend on more than caffeine. They require clean, nutrient-dense beans grown in 
living soil, harvested with care, and roasted with precision.
We’ve also seen that farming methods matter deeply. Circular, regenerative practices 
not only maintain flavor but also ensure the future of Coffea arabica. Producers are 
not just farming coffee; they are farming health. Soil health, biodiversity, and water 
purity influence the chemistry of every cup.
To organic-regenerative producers: You are cultivating vitality, not just crops. You are 
stewards of the plant, the land, and the communities that depend on both. Your work 
is the foundation of CHC, and your leadership is essential to its success.
To consumers: Every cup is a choice. You are not passive participants, but co-creators 
in this cycle. As you reflect on how coffee makes you feel, experiment with different 
roast levels, or pay attention to markers like energy, mood, or liver enzyme levels, 
you become a participant in something much larger: a regenerative feedback loop 
grounded in trust, data, and care.
Now, imagine a future where every cup of coffee reflects our shared commitment to 
wellness, equity, and ecological integrity. A world where coffee plays a leadership role 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 485
in the “food as medicine” movement, proving that food safety, health optimization, 
and environmental stewardship don’t have to be trade-offs. They can be the standard.
Let’s reframe how we think about food safety and quality, not as hurdles to clear, but as 
catalysts for innovation and connection. The coffee industry has incredible potential 
to lead, and Circular Health Coffee is one model for how to begin. It’s not a niche. 
It’s a blueprint.
Thank you for joining this journey. Let’s carry what we’ve learned forward and keep 
building a future where coffee continues to nourish, inspire, and heal.
Here’s to making better choices for our bodies, our communities, and the planet, one 
intentional cup at a time.

Afterword 
by Dr. Sanjiv Chopra
For decades, I have recommended coffee to my patients based on robust clinical 
and epidemiological evidence, particularly for its benefits to liver health, metabolic 
function, and chronic disease prevention. Coffee drinkers have a lower risk of several 
common cancers and lower total and cause-specific mortality. But as our understanding 
of nutrition and toxicology has evolved, so too has my perspective: the type and quality 
of coffee we consume is far more important than once believed.
My journey as a hepatologist and medical educator at Harvard Medical School has 
taught me that what we consume routinely can profoundly influence our bodies, 
especially organs like the liver. Coffee’s transformation from a mere stimulant to 
a substance with measurable effects on chronic disease represents one of the most 
significant shifts in nutritional science of our time.
In The Coffee Guide to Better Health, Ildi Revi takes this understanding even further, 
presenting a scientifically rigorous framework for what she terms Circular Health 
Coffee (CHC). This emerging category represents the bold idea that coffee can be 
a true functional beverage, with quantified benefits, ethical foundations, and clean 
sourcing practices.
Revi’s comprehensive exploration of coffee chemistry reveals how roasting, processing, 
and bean quality dramatically influence levels of chlorogenic acids (CGA), antioxidants, 
and other bioactive compounds. Her analysis demonstrates that lighter roasts preserve 
CGA at significantly higher levels than darker roasts, presenting a clear scientific 
opportunity to optimize coffee for health benefits consciously.
The CHC approach demands precision at every level: quantified CGA and antioxidant 
levels verified through laboratory testing, roasting techniques that protect beneficial 

 The Coffee Guide to Better Health ♦ 487
nutrients, and complete supply chain transparency to ensure harmful contaminants 
never reach the consumer.
As a clinical hepatologist, I have observed coffee’s benefits for patients with liver 
disease, metabolic syndrome, and other chronic conditions through real-world 
outcomes and controlled studies. Revi’s literature reviews add crucial mechanistic 
clarity: by protecting polyphenols during processing and eliminating toxins during 
sourcing, we can amplify coffee’s inherent protective qualities.
This evidence-based approach creates coffee that is functionally designed to deliver 
benefits beyond taste, standing on quantifiable chemistry rather than marketing claims, 
and sourced through regenerative agricultural practices that eliminate contaminants 
known to disrupt metabolism and health.
TRANSPARENCY AND VERIFICATION
One of the biggest barriers to confidently recommending coffee in clinical practice 
has always been quality variability. Without transparency, how can practitioners guide 
patients?
CHC solves this problem by incorporating lab verification of antioxidant levels, batch-
level testing, and full traceability from soil to cup. It introduces the kind of integrity 
that health professionals and their patients can rely on.
This level of verification allows coffee to be recommended as confidently as any 
other evidence-based dietary intervention, particularly for liver and metabolic health 
protocols, without concerns about variability or hidden toxins that could undermine 
therapeutic goals.
A NEW STANDARD FOR DAILY WELLNESS
Today’s health-conscious consumers seek more than “less harmful” options; they want 
everyday choices that meaningfully support their well-being and affirmation that their 
daily rituals actively support long-term healthiness. CHC addresses this evolution by 
transforming each cup into part of a measurable health strategy, backed by scientific 
rigor and clinical transparency.

Coffee grown organically and regeneratively, meeting the green grading standards 
outlined in this book, has the potential to significantly impact the health landscape for 
those who drink it regularly. This represents not just better coffee, but a new category 
of functional nutrition.
The clarity provided by CHC standards enables healthcare practitioners to integrate 
coffee recommendations into preventative and therapeutic protocols confidently. 
When we can verify antioxidant levels, confirm clean sourcing, and understand precise 
processing methods, coffee becomes a reliable component of evidence-based wellness 
strategies.
THE FUTURE OF FUNCTIONAL COFFEE
Coffee will likely remain one of humanity’s most beloved beverages, especially when 
grown organically and regeneratively, which Revi shows is the path of survival for the 
Coffee arabica species. Thanks to the rigorous scientific work presented in this book, 
we now have the framework to move beyond commodity beverages toward functional 
nutrition aligned with both human wellness and environmental stewardship.
As a proponent of evidence-based medicine and preventative care, I encourage readers 
to consider not just whether to drink coffee, but what kind of coffee deserves a place in 
their daily health routine. The standards and insights presented here offer a roadmap 
for making that choice with both scientific confidence and clinical wisdom.
This is the promise of Circular Health Coffee: intentional nourishment that honors 
both the complexity of human health and our responsibility to the planet that sustains 
us.
—Sanjiv Chopra, MBBS, MACP, FRCP
Professor of Medicine, Harvard Medical School
Sought-after Inspirational Speaker
Author of Multiple Books, Including Coffee! The Magical Elixir
488 ♦ Ildi Revi

Measurement of viable bacterial or fungal cells

Group of phenolic compounds found in coffee with antioxidant properties
CHC - Circular Health Coffee 
Coffee category defined by health integrity, ecological accountability, and scientific verification
cGAPs - Current Good Agricultural Practices 
Science-based standards for safe food production
cGMPs - Current Good Manufacturing Practices 
Most up-to-date, legally enforceable standards for food processing
COA - Certificate of Analysis 
Third-party laboratory report documenting compound levels and contaminant testing
COD - Chemical Oxygen Demand 
Measure of water quality and organic pollution
Codex Alimentarius – Sometimes referred to as simply “Codex”
International food safety standards developed by WHO and FAO
CYP1A2 - Cytochrome P450 1A2 
Enzyme involved in caffeine metabolism

Trade association representing the European coffee industry

EU agency providing scientific advice on food safety

Maximum safe daily intake level for specific compounds

495
APPENDIX 2: 
THE CIRCULAR HEALTH COFFEE CYCLE
Circular Health Coffee follows a systems-based approach I call the Circular Health 
Coffee Cycle, a model made up of nine interconnected stages. At the center of this cycle 
is the coffee drinker, who both initiates and completes the loop by choosing coffee 
aligned with personal health goals and offering feedback that shapes future practices.
1. Wellness Motivation – The cycle begins with individual and collective
motivations to improve well-being through everyday choices.
2. Health Research – CHC production is informed by evolving scientific
evidence on coffee’s health effects.
3. Organic Regenerative Farming – Coffee must be grown in ways that heal
the land and protect biodiversity.
4. Equitable Partnerships – Fair value and transparent relationships enable
producers to sustain these practices.
5. Product Optimization – Roasters and producers collaborate to preserve and
enhance health-supportive compounds.
6. Product Transparency – Lab testing and Certificates of Analysis empower
consumers with verified information.
7. Personalized Choices – Coffee is selected and consumed with individual
needs in mind, from decaf options to specific compounds.
8. Learning and Feedback – Producers and consumers share knowledge, closing
the loop and driving continual improvement.

496 ♦ Ildi Revi
9. Standards Development – CHC relies on evolving, science-based standards
grounded in inclusivity and transparency. – Producers and consumers share
knowledge, closing the loop and driving continual improvement.
Research, field-tested practices, and shared values support each of these stages. 
Together, they define a regenerative model of production and consumption rooted 
in health, science, and accountability.

497
APPENDIX 3: 
ROAST PLAN AND DATA POINTS
RECORDING YOUR ROASTING INFORMATION FOR 
ANALYSIS, EVALUATION AND CONSISTENCY
The chart on the next page is to assist roasters who do not have digital data logging 
software installed with their roasting machines. Manual data logging can also help 
you focus and be more involved in understanding as much as you can about a coffee. 
Here are the pieces:
1.
Overall roasting timeline: Time zero is the charge temperature. Every 30 seconds (or 60 
if you prefer), mark the time relative to the bean temperature Plot a dot or “X” at each 
interval where time and temperature meet.
2.
Coffee: Record the coffee name and any identifiers (lot number, offer/PO codes).
3.
Date & roasted by: Write the roast date and the roaster’s name/initials.
4.
Green coffee data: Note green moisture (%) and water activity (aw). Record room 
conditions at roast (ambient temp and relative humidity).
5.
Key compounds to target: Specify targets such as chlorogenic acids (CGA), trigonelline, 
melanoidins (and others you track). Keep comparable readings over time to see aging 
and seasonal shifts.
6.
Roasting benchmarks: For each benchmark, record both time and bean temp using 
consistent criteria: charge, turning point, color change/dry end, first crack (start/end), 
second crack (if used), and drop. Be consistent in how you define each event across roasts.
7.
Weight: Log green weight and finished roasted weight. Calculate weight loss.
8.
Development time: DT = drop time − first crack start.
9.
Roast color: Record the measured final color value (specify the instrument/scale used).
10. Tasting notes: Jot concise descriptors for aroma, flavor, acidity, body, and finish. Note 
any defects or off-notes and suggested adjustments for the next roast.

498 ♦ Ildi Revi

528 ♦ Ildi Revi

ABOUT THE AUTHOR
Ildi Revi, M.Ad.Ed., CPT, PCQI, former Q Instructor, Chief Learning Officer at Purity 
Coffee, Inc., brings nearly thirty years of experience at the intersection of education, 
agriculture, and coffee. 
Her career in education began in rural Zimbabwe, then continued in Chicago, 
where she founded an alternative high school and created literacy and job readiness 
initiatives for adult learners for a social service agency. She then spent two years in 
Beira, Mozambique, with Universidade Católica de Moçambique under a USIS 
contract. She designed and taught business computing courses and founded the 
National English Language Theatre Competition. She also trained instructors at the 
Instituto de Línguas to create workplace-specific language programs at the port, flour 
mill, sugar mill and national electric company. She assisted with the US Embassy’s 
democracy-building conference in Maputo, conducted teacher training and evaluation of 
the first Peace Corps Volunteers to Mozambique, and developed an intensive English 
program for agricultural extensionists with Food for the Hungry International.
Ildi lived and worked for nearly a decade on a coffee farm in Zimbabwe’s Vumba 
Mountains. While there, she created an agricultural extensionist coffee production 
curriculum for a World Vision and USAID project. She also founded Leopard Forest 
Coffee Company in South Carolina, a vertically integrated roastery and café she 
owned for twelve years, traveling back and forth between Zimbabwe and the US.
From 2010 to 2016, she served as Education Manager at the Specialty Coffee 
Association of America, where she designed and launched certificate and instructor 
development programs that became the foundation of the SCA’s education 
system. She then joined Ally Coffee as Director of Performance, developing 
employee learning systems, building FDA-compliant food safety protocols as staff 
PCQI, and teaching courses in FSMA, FSVP, and market dynamics. Since 2018, she has 
served as a Q Arabica Instructor, Grader, Lecturer, and Processing Generalist.
At Purity Coffee, she leads Research and Development and Learning, overseeing 
laboratory testing, cupping, literature reviews, and health and environmental research. She 
develops training for staff and customers and collaborates with farms, production teams, 
scientists, and industry partners. Her work includes extensive travel to farms in Colombia, 
Brazil, Nicaragua, and Honduras, and she directs the longitudinal biochar research for the 
Purity Cool Farm initiative.
